1. Choose sound fruit, not too ripe; pick off the stalks, weigh it, and to every lb. allow 6 oz. of pounded sugar
2. Put the fruit into large dry stone jars, sprinkling the sugar amongst it
3. Cover the jars with saucers, place them in a rather cool oven, and bake the fruit until it is quite tender
4. When cold, cover the top of the fruit with a piece of white paper cut to the size of the jar
5. Pour over this melted mutton suet about an inch thick, and cover the tops of the jars with thick brown paper, well tied down
6. Keep the jars in a cool dry place, and the fruit will remain good till the following Christmas, but not much longer

title: Baked Fillets of Turbot
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Remains of cold turbot
- Lobster sauce left from the preceding day
- Egg
- Bread crumbs
- Cayenne
- Salt
- Minced parsley
- Nutmeg
- Lemon-juice
- Butter

## Directions

1. After having cleared the fish from all skin and bone, divide it into square pieces of an equal size
2. Brush them over with egg, sprinkle with bread crumbs mixed with a little minced parsley and seasoning
3. Lay the fillets in a baking-dish, with sufficient butter to baste with
4. Bake for 1/4 hour, and keep them well moistened with the butter
5. Put a little lemon-juice and grated nutmeg to the cold lobster sauce; make it hot, and pour over the fish, which must be well drained from the butter
6. Garnish with parsley and cut lemon

title: Baked Gooseberry Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Gooseberries
- 3 eggs
- 1-1/2 oz. of butter
- 1/2 pint of bread crumbs
- sugar to taste
- puff-paste

## Directions

1. Put the gooseberries into a jar, previously cutting off the tops and tails
2. Place this jar in boiling water, and let it boil until the gooseberries are soft enough to pulp
3. Beat them through a coarse sieve
4. To every pint of pulp add 3 well-whisked eggs, 1-1/2 oz. of butter, 1/2 pint of bread crumbs, and sugar to taste
5. Beat the mixture well
6. Put a border of puff-paste round the edge of a pie-dish
7. Put in the pudding
8. Bake for about 40 minutes
9. Strew sifted sugar over, and serve

title: Baked Haddocks
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- haddock
- forcemeat
- butter
- egg
- bread crumbs
- parsley
- lemon
- brown gravy
- anchovy sauce

## Directions

1. Scale and clean the fish, without cutting it open much
2. put in a nice delicate forcemeat
3. sew up the slit
4. brush it over with egg
5. sprinkle over bread crumbs
6. baste frequently with butter
7. garnish with parsley and cut lemon
8. serve with a nice brown gravy, plain melted butter, or anchovy sauce

title: Baked Lemon Pudding I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The yolks of 4 eggs
- 4 oz. of pounded sugar
- 1 lemon
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- puff-crust

## Directions

1. Beat the eggs to a froth
2. Mix with them the sugar and warmed butter
3. Stir these ingredients well together, putting in the grated rind and strained juice of the lemon-peel
4. Line a shallow dish with puff-paste
5. Put in the mixture, and bake in a moderate oven for 40 minutes
6. Turn the pudding out of the dish, strew over it sifted sugar, and serve

title: Baked Lemon Pudding II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 10 oz. of bread crumbs
- 2 pints of milk
- 2 oz. of butter
- 1 lemon
- 1/4 lb. of pounded sugar
- 4 eggs
- 1 tablespoonful of brandy

## Directions

1. Bring the milk to the boiling point, stir in the butter, and pour these hot over the bread crumbs
2. Add the sugar and very finely-minced lemon-peel
3. Beat the eggs, and stir these in with the brandy to the other ingredients
4. Put a paste round the dish, and bake for 3/4 hour

title: Baked Mackerel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 middling-sized mackerel
- a nice delicate forcemeat
- 3 oz. of butter
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Clean the fish, take out the roes, and fill up with forcemeat, and sew up the slit.
2. Flour, and put them in a dish, heads and tails alternately, with the roes; and, between each layer, put some little pieces of butter, and pepper and salt.
3. Bake for 1/2 an hour, and either serve with plain melted butter or a maître d'hôtel sauce.

title: Baked Minced Mutton (Cold Meat Cookery)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- remains of any joint of cold roast mutton
- 1 or 2 onions
- 1 bunch of savoury herbs
- pepper and salt to taste
- 2 blades of pounded mace or nutmeg
- 2 tablespoonfuls of gravy
- mashed potatoes

## Directions

1. Mince an onion rather fine, and fry it a light-brown colour
2. Add the herbs and mutton, both of which should be also finely minced and well mixed
3. Season with pepper and salt, and a little pounded mace or nutmeg, and moisten with the gravy
4. Put a layer of mashed potatoes at the bottom of a dish
5. Add the mutton layer
6. Add another layer of potatoes
7. Bake for about 1/2 hour

title: Baked Mushrooms
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 16 to 20 mushroom-flaps
- butter
- pepper to taste

## Directions

1. Cut off a portion of the stalk, peel the top, and wipe the mushrooms carefully with a piece of flannel and a little fine salt
2. Put them into a tin baking-dish, with a very small piece of butter placed on each mushroom
3. Sprinkle over a little pepper, and let them bake for about 20 minutes, or longer should the mushrooms be very large
4. Have ready a very hot dish, pile the mushrooms high in the centre, pour the gravy round, and send them to table quickly, with very hot plates

title: Baked or Boiled Arrowroot Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 tablespoonfuls of arrowroot
- 1-1/2 pint of milk
- 1 oz. of butter
- rind of 1/2 lemon
- 2 heaped tablespoonfuls of moist sugar
- a little grated nutmeg

## Directions

1. Mix the arrowroot with as much cold milk as will make it into a smooth batter, moderately thick
2. Put the remainder of the milk into a stewpan with the lemon-peel, and let it infuse for about 1/2 hour
3. When it boils, strain it gently to the batter, stirring it all the time to keep it smooth
4. Add the butter and beat this well in until thoroughly mixed
5. Sweeten with moist sugar
6. Put the mixture into a pie-dish, round which has been placed a border of paste
7. Grate a little nutmeg over the top
8. Bake the pudding from 1 to 1-1/4 hour in a moderate oven, or boil it the same length of time in a well-buttered basin

title: Baked or Boiled Carrot Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of bread crumbs
- 4 oz. of suet
- 1/4 lb. of stoned raisins
- 3/4 lb. of carrot
- 1/4 lb. of currants
- 3 oz. of sugar
- 3 eggs
- milk
- 1/4 nutmeg

## Directions

1. Boil the carrots until tender enough to mash to a pulp
2. Add the remaining ingredients, and moisten with sufficient milk to make the pudding of the consistency of thick batter
3. If to be boiled, put the mixture into a buttered basin, tie it down with a cloth, and boil for 2-1/2 hours
4. If to be baked, put it into a pie-dish, and bake for nearly an hour; turn it out of the dish, strew sifted sugar over it, and serve

title: Baked or Boiled Ground Rice Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 pints of milk
- 6 tablespoonfuls of ground rice
- sugar to taste
- 4 eggs
- flavouring of lemon-rind, nutmeg, bitter almonds or bay-leaf

## Directions

1. Put 1-1/2 pint of the milk into a stewpan, with any of the above flavourings, and bring it to the boiling-point
2. With the other 1/2 pint of milk, mix the ground rice to a smooth batter
3. Strain the boiling milk to this, and stir over the fire until the mixture is tolerably thick
4. Pour it into a basin, leave it uncovered, and when nearly or quite cold, sweeten it to taste, and add the eggs, which should be previously well beaten, with a little salt
5. For boiled pudding: Put the pudding into a well-buttered basin, tie it down with a cloth, plunge it into boiling water, and boil for 1-1/2 hour
6. For baked pudding: Use half the above proportion of ground rice, with the same quantity of all the other ingredients, and bake in a moderate oven for an hour
7. Stewed fruit, preserves, or marmalade may be served with either the boiled or baked pudding

title: Baked or Boiled Marrow Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of bread crumbs
- 1-1/2 pint of milk
- 6 oz. of marrow
- 4 eggs
- 1/4 lb. of raisins or currants, or 2 oz. of each
- sugar and grated nutmeg to taste

## Directions

1. Make the milk boiling, pour it hot on to the bread crumbs, and let these remain covered for about 1/2 hour
2. shred the marrow, beat up the eggs, and mix these with the bread crumbs
3. add the remaining ingredients
4. beat the mixture well, and either put it into a buttered mould and boil it for 2-1/2 hours, or put it into a pie-dish edged with puff-paste, and bake for rather more than 3/4 hour
5. Before sending it to table, sift a little pounded sugar over, after being turned out of the mould or basin

title: Baked or Stewed Calf's Foot
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 calf's foot
- 1 pint of milk
- 1 pint of water
- 1 blade of mace
- the rind of 1/4 lemon
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Well clean the foot, and either stew or bake it in the milk-and-water with the other ingredients from 3 to 4 hours
2. To enhance the flavour, an onion and a small quantity of celery may be added, if approved
3. 1/2 a teacupful of cream, stirred in just before serving, is also a great improvement to this dish

title: Baked Orange Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of stale sponge cake or bruised ratafias
- 6 oranges
- 1 pint of milk
- 6 eggs
- 1/2 lb. of sugar
- puff-paste
- sifted sugar

## Directions

1. Bruise the sponge cake or ratafias into fine crumbs, and pour upon them the milk, which should be boiling.
2. Rub the rinds of 2 of the oranges on sugar, and add this, with the juice of the remainder, to the other ingredients.
3. Beat up the eggs, stir them in, sweeten to taste, and put the mixture into a pie-dish previously lined with puff-paste.
4. Bake for rather more than 1/2 hour; turn it out of the dish, strew sifted sugar over, and serve.

title: Baked Pears
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 12 pears
- rind of 1 lemon
- 6 cloves
- 10 whole allspice
- to every pint of water allow 1/2 lb. of loaf sugar

## Directions

1. Pare and cut the pears into halves, and, should they be very large, into quarters; leave the stalks on, and carefully remove the cores.
2. Place them in a clean baking-jar, with a closely-fitting lid; add to them the lemon-rind cut in strips, the juice of 1/2 lemon, the cloves, pounded allspice, and sufficient water just to cover the whole, with sugar in the above proportion.
3. Cover the jar down closely, put it into a very cool oven, and bake the pears from 5 to 6 hours, but be very careful that the oven is not too hot.
4. To improve the colour of the fruit, a few drops of prepared cochineal may be added; but this will not be found necessary if the pears are very gently baked.

title: Baked Pike
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 or 2 pike
- a nice delicate stuffing
- 1 egg
- bread crumbs
- 1/4 lb. butter

## Directions

1. Scale the fish, take out the gills, wash, and wipe it thoroughly dry.
2. Stuff it with forcemeat, sew it up, and fasten the tail in the mouth by means of a skewer.
3. Brush it over with egg, sprinkle with bread crumbs, and baste with butter.
4. Put it in a well-heated oven.
5. When the pike is of a nice brown colour, cover it with buttered paper, as the outside would become too dry.
6. If 2 are dressed, a little variety may be made by making one of them green with a little chopped parsley mixed with the bread crumbs.
7. Serve anchovy or Dutch sauce, and plain melted butter with it.

title: Baked Plum-Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of flour
- 1 lb. of currants
- 1 lb. of raisins
- 1 lb. of suet
- 2 eggs
- 1 pint of milk
- a few slices of candied peel

## Directions

1. Chop the suet finely; mix with it the flour, currants, stoned raisins, and candied peel
2. moisten with the well-beaten eggs, and add sufficient milk to make the pudding of the consistency of very thick batter
3. Put it into a buttered dish, and bake in a good oven from 2-1/4 to 2-1/2 hours
4. turn it out, strew sifted sugar over, and serve

title: Baked Potatoes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Potatoes
- Cold butter

## Directions

1. Choose large potatoes, as much of a size as possible
2. Wash them in lukewarm water, and scrub them well
3. Put them into a moderate oven, and bake them for about 2 hours, turning them three or four times whilst they are cooking
4. Serve them in a napkin immediately they are done, as, if kept a long time in the oven, they have a shrivelled appearance
5. Send to table with them a piece of cold butter

title: Baked Raisin Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of flour
- 3/4 lb. of stoned raisins
- 1/2 lb. of suet
- a pinch of salt
- 1 oz. of sugar
- a little grated nutmeg
- milk

## Directions

1. Chop the suet finely
2. Stone the raisins and cut them in halves
3. Mix these with the suet, add the salt, sugar, and grated nutmeg, and moisten the whole with sufficient milk to make it of the consistency of thick batter
4. Put the pudding into a buttered pie-dish, and bake for 1-1/2 hour, or rather longer
5. Turn it out of the dish, strew sifted sugar over, and serve

title: Baked Rice Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 small teacupful of rice
- 4 eggs
- 1 pint of milk
- 2 oz. of fresh butter
- 2 oz. of beef marrow
- 1/4 lb. of currants
- 2 tablespoonfuls of brandy
- nutmeg
- 1/4 lb. of sugar
- the rind of 1/2 lemon
- puff-paste

## Directions

1. Put the lemon-rind and milk into a stewpan, and let it infuse till the milk is well flavoured with the lemon
2. Boil the rice until tender in water, with a very small quantity of salt, and, when done, let it be thoroughly drained
3. Beat the eggs, stir to them the milk, which should be strained, the butter, marrow, currants, and remaining ingredients
4. Add the rice, and mix all well together
5. Line the edges of the dish with puff-paste, put in the pudding, and bake for about 3/4 hour in a slow oven

title: Baked Rice Pudding (Plain and Economical)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 teacupful of rice
- 2 tablespoonfuls of moist sugar
- 1 quart of milk
- 1/2 oz. of butter or 2 small tablespoonfuls of chopped suet
- 1/2 teaspoonful of grated nutmeg

## Directions

1. Wash the rice, put it into a pie-dish with the sugar, pour in the milk, and stir these ingredients well together
2. Add the butter cut up into very small pieces, or, instead of this, the above proportion of finely-minced suet
3. Grate a little nutmeg over the top, and bake the pudding, in a moderate oven, from 1-1/2 to 2 hours

title: Baked Sea-Bream
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 bream
- salt, pepper, and cayenne to taste
- 1/4 lb. of butter

## Directions

1. Well wash the bream, but do not remove the scales, and wipe away all moisture with a nice dry cloth
2. Season it inside and out with salt, pepper, and cayenne
3. lay it in a baking-dish
4. Place the butter, in small pieces, upon the fish
5. bake for rather more than 1/2 an hour

title: Baked Semolina Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 oz. of semolina
- 1-1/2 pint of milk
- 1/4 lb. of sugar
- 12 bitter almonds
- 3 oz. of butter
- 4 eggs

## Directions

1. Flavour the milk with the bitter almonds, by infusing them in it by the side of the fire for about 1/2 hour
2. Strain it, and mix with it the semolina, sugar, and butter
3. Stir these ingredients over the fire for a few minutes
4. Take them off, and gradually mix in the eggs, which should be well beaten
5. Butter a pie-dish
6. Line the edges with puff-paste
7. Put in the pudding
8. Bake in rather a slow oven from 40 to 50 minutes
9. Serve with custard sauce or stewed fruit, a little of which may be poured over the pudding

title: Baked Soles
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 soles
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- egg, and bread crumbs
- minced parsley
- 1 glass of sherry
- lemon-juice
- cayenne and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Clean, skin, and well wash the fish, and dry them thoroughly in a cloth.
2. Brush them over with egg, sprinkle with bread crumbs mixed with a little minced parsley, lay them in a large flat baking-dish, white side uppermost; or if it will not hold the two soles, they may each be laid on a dish by itself; but they must not be put one on the top of the other.
3. Melt the butter, and pour it over the whole, and bake for 20 minutes.
4. Take a portion of the gravy that flows from the fish, add the wine, lemon-juice, and seasoning, give it one boil, skim, pour it under the fish, and serve.

title: Baked Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of any kind of meat, any trimmings or odd pieces
- 2 onions
- 2 carrots
- 2 oz. of rice
- 1 pint of split peas
- pepper and salt to taste
- 4 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Cut the meat and vegetables in slices
2. Add to them the rice and peas
3. Season with pepper and salt
4. Put the whole in a jar
5. Fill up with the water
6. Cover very closely
7. Bake for 4 hours

title: Baked Spanish Onions
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 or 5 Spanish onions
- salt
- water
- cold butter
- pepper and salt
- brown gravy

## Directions

1. Put the onions, with their skins on, into a saucepan of boiling water slightly salted, and let them boil quickly for an hour
2. Take them out, wipe them thoroughly, wrap each one in a piece of paper separately, and bake them in a moderate oven for 2 hours, or longer, should the onions be very large
3. They may be served in their skins, and eaten with a piece of cold butter and a seasoning of pepper and salt; or they may be peeled, and a good brown gravy poured over them

title: Baked Sturgeon
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 small sturgeon
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 small bunch of herbs
- juice of 1/2 lemon
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 1 pint of white wine

## Directions

1. Cleanse the fish thoroughly, skin it, and split it along the belly without separating it
2. Have ready a large baking-dish, in which lay the fish
3. Sprinkle over the seasoning and herbs very finely minced
4. Moisten it with the lemon-juice and wine
5. Place the butter in small pieces over the whole of the fish
6. Put it in the oven, and baste frequently
7. Brown it nicely, and serve with its own gravy

title: Baked Sweetbreads
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 sweetbreads
- Egg and bread crumbs
- Oiled butter
- 3 slices of toast
- Brown gravy

## Directions

1. Choose large white sweetbreads
2. Put them into warm water to draw out the blood and to improve their colour
3. Let them remain for rather more than 1 hour
4. Put them into boiling water and allow them to simmer for about 10 minutes
5. Take them up and drain them
6. Brush over with egg
7. Sprinkle with bread crumbs
8. Dip them in egg again and then into more bread crumbs
9. Drop on them a little oiled butter
10. Put the sweetbreads into a moderately-heated oven and let them bake for nearly 3/4 hour
11. Make 3 pieces of toast
12. Place the sweetbreads on the toast
13. Pour round, but not over them, a good brown gravy

title: Baked Tomatoes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 8 or 10 tomatoes
- pepper and salt to taste
- 2 oz. of butter
- bread crumbs

## Directions

1. Take off the stalks from the tomatoes
2. cut them into thick slices, and put them into a deep baking-dish
3. add a plentiful seasoning of pepper and salt, and butter in the above proportion
4. cover the whole with bread crumbs
5. drop over these a little clarified butter
6. bake in a moderate oven from 20 minutes to 1/2 hour, and serve very hot

title: Baked Tomatoes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 8 or 10 tomatoes
- pepper and salt to taste
- 2 oz. of butter
- bread crumbs
- clarified butter

## Directions

1. Take off the stalks from the tomatoes; cut them into thick slices, and put them into a deep baking-dish
2. Add a plentiful seasoning of pepper and salt, and butter in the above proportion
3. Cover the whole with bread crumbs
4. Drop over these a little clarified butter
5. Bake in a moderate oven from 20 minutes to 1/2 hour, and serve very hot

title: Baked Veal (Cold Meat Cookery)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of cold roast veal
- a few slices of bacon
- 1 pint of bread crumbs
- 1/2 pint of good veal gravy
- 1/2 teaspoonful of minced lemon-peel
- 1 blade of pounded mace
- cayenne and salt to taste
- 4 eggs

## Directions

1. Mince finely the veal and bacon; add the bread crumbs, gravy, and seasoning, and stir these ingredients well together.
2. Beat up the eggs thoroughly; add these, mix the whole well together, put into a dish, and bake from 3/4 to 1 hour.
3. When liked, a little good gravy may be served in a tureen as an accompaniment.

title: Baked White Herrings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 12 herrings
- 4 bay-leaves
- 12 cloves
- 12 allspice
- 2 small blades of mace
- cayenne pepper and salt to taste
- sufficient vinegar to fill up the dish

## Directions

1. Take the herrings, cut off the heads, and gut them.
2. Put them in a pie-dish, heads and tails alternately, and between each layer, sprinkle over the above ingredients.
3. Cover the fish with the vinegar, and bake for 1/2 hour, but do not use it till quite cold.
4. The herrings may be cut down the front, the backbone taken out, and closed again.

title: Baked White Herrings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 12 herrings
- 4 bay-leaves
- 12 cloves
- 12 allspice
- 2 small blades of mace
- cayenne pepper
- salt
- vinegar

## Directions

1. Take the herrings
2. cut off the heads
3. gut them
4. put them in a pie-dish, heads and tails alternately
5. between each layer, sprinkle over the bay-leaves, cloves, allspice, mace, cayenne pepper and salt
6. cover the fish with the vinegar
7. bake for 1/2 hour
8. do not use it till quite cold

title: Bakewell Pudding (Very Rich) - I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of puff-paste
- 5 eggs
- 6 oz. of sugar
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 1 oz. of almonds
- jam

## Directions

1. Cover a dish with thin paste
2. Put over this a layer of any kind of jam, 1/2 inch thick
3. Put the yolks of 5 eggs into a basin with the white of 1, and beat these well
4. Add the sifted sugar, the butter, which should be melted, and the almonds, which should be well pounded
5. Beat all together until well mixed
6. Pour it into the dish over the jam
7. Bake for an hour in a moderate oven

title: Bakewell Pudding (Very Rich) - II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 pint of bread crumbs
- 1 pint of milk
- 4 eggs
- 2 oz. of sugar
- 3 oz. of butter
- 1 oz. of pounded almonds
- jam

## Directions

1. Put the bread crumbs at the bottom of a pie-dish
2. Over them put a layer of jam of any kind that may be preferred
3. Mix the milk and eggs together
4. Add the sugar, butter, and pounded almonds
5. Beat all well together
6. Pour it into the dish and bake in a moderate oven for 1 hour

title: Bandoline
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 oz. of gum-tragacanth
- 1/4 pint of cold water
- 3 pennyworth of essence of almonds
- 2 teaspoonfuls of old rum

## Directions

1. Put the gum-tragacanth into a wide-mouthed bottle with the cold water
2. Let it stand till dissolved, then stir into it the essence of almonds
3. Let it remain for an hour or two, when pour the rum on the top
4. When any is required for use, dilute it with a little cold water until the desired consistency is obtained, and keep it in a small bottle, well corked, for use

title: Barbel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of port wine
- a saltspoonful of salt
- 2 tablespoonfuls of vinegar
- 2 sliced onions
- a faggot of sweet herbs
- nutmeg and mace to taste
- the juice of a lemon
- 2 anchovies
- 1 or 2 barbels according to size

## Directions

1. Boil the barbels in salt and water till done
2. Pour off some of the water
3. To the remainder, put the port wine, salt, vinegar, sliced onions, sweet herbs, nutmeg, mace, lemon juice, and anchovies
4. Simmer gently for 1/2 hour or rather more
5. Strain
6. Put in the fish
7. Heat it gradually but do not let it boil or it will be broken

title: Barberries in Bunches
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of syrup No. 1513
- Barberries

## Directions

1. Prepare some small pieces of clean white wood, 3 inches long and 1/4 inch wide, and tie the fruit on to these in nice bunches.
2. Have ready some clear syrup, made by recipe No. 1513; put in the barberries, and simmer them in it for 2 successive days, boiling them for nearly 1/2 hour each day, and covering them each time with the syrup when cold.
3. When the fruit looks perfectly clear, it is sufficiently done, and should be stored away in pots, with the syrup poured over, or the fruit may be candied.

title: Barberry Tart
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- barberries
- 3/4 lb. of lump sugar per lb. of barberries
- paste

## Directions

1. Pick the barberries from the stalks, and put the fruit into a stone jar
2. Place this jar in boiling water, and let it simmer very slowly until the fruit is soft
3. Put it into a preserving-pan with the sugar, and boil gently for 15 minutes
4. Line a tartlet-pan with paste and bake it
5. When the paste is cold, fill with the barberries
6. Ornament the tart with a few baked leaves of paste, cut out as shown in the engraving

title: Barley Gruel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. of Scotch or pearl barley
- 1/2 pint of port wine
- the rind of 1 lemon
- 1 quart and 1/2 pint of water
- sugar to taste

## Directions

1. After well washing the barley, boil it in 1/2 pint of water for 1/4 hour
2. Pour this water away
3. Put to the barley the quart of fresh boiling water, and let it boil until the liquid is reduced to half
4. Strain it off
5. Add the wine, sugar, and lemon-peel
6. Simmer for 5 minutes, and put it away in a clean jug
7. It can be warmed from time to time, as required

title: Barley Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of shin of beef
- 1/4 lb. of pearl barley
- a large bunch of parsley
- 4 onions
- 6 potatoes
- salt and pepper
- 4 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Put in all the ingredients
2. Simmer gently for 3 hours

title: Baroness Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 lb. of suet
- 3/4 lb. of raisins weighed after being stoned
- 3/4 lb. of flour
- 1/2 pint of milk
- 1/4 saltspoonful of salt

## Directions

1. Prepare the suet, by carefully freeing it from skin, and chop it finely
2. Stone the raisins, and cut them in halves
3. Mix both these ingredients with the salt and flour
4. Moisten the whole with the above proportion of milk
5. Stir the mixture well
6. Tie the pudding in a floured cloth, which has been previously wrung out in boiling water
7. Put the pudding into a saucepan of boiling water, and let it boil, without ceasing, 4-1/2 hours
8. Serve merely with plain sifted sugar, a little of which may be sprinkled over the pudding

title: Bechamel Maigre, or Without Meat
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 onions
- 1 blade of mace
- mushroom trimmings
- a small bunch of parsley
- 1 oz. of butter
- flour
- 1/2 pint of water
- 1 pint of milk
- salt
- the juice of 1 lemon
- 2 eggs

## Directions

1. Put in a stewpan the milk, and 1/2 pint of water, with the onions, mace, mushrooms, parsley, and salt. Let these simmer gently for 20 minutes.
2. In the mean time, rub on a plate 1 oz. of flour and butter; put it to the liquor, and stir it well till it boils up; then place it by the side of the fire, and continue stirring until it is perfectly smooth.
3. Now strain it through a sieve into a basin, after which put it back in the stewpan, and add the lemon-juice.
4. Beat up the yolks of the eggs with about 4 dessertspoonfuls of milk; strain this to the sauce, keep stirring it over the fire, but do not let it boil, lest it curdle.

title: Bechamel, or French White Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 small bunch of parsley
- 2 cloves
- 1/2 bay-leaf
- 1 small faggot of savoury herbs
- salt
- 3 or 4 mushrooms
- 2 pints of white stock
- 1 pint of cream
- 1 tablespoonful of arrowroot

## Directions

1. Put the stock into a stewpan, with the parsley, cloves, bay-leaf, herbs, and mushrooms; add a seasoning of salt, but no pepper, as that would give the sauce a dusty appearance, and should be avoided.
2. When it has boiled long enough to extract the flavour of the herbs, etc., strain it, and boil it up quickly again, until it is nearly half-reduced.
3. Now mix the arrowroot smoothly with the cream, and let it simmer very gently for 5 minutes over a slow fire; pour to it the reduced stock, and continue to simmer slowly for 10 minutes, if the sauce be thick.
4. If, on the contrary, it be too thin, it must be stirred over a sharp fire till it thickens.

title: Beef a la Mode
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 or 7 lbs. of the thick flank of beef
- a few slices of fat bacon
- 1 teacupful of vinegar
- black pepper
- allspice
- 2 cloves well mixed and finely pounded, making altogether 1 heaped teaspoonful
- salt to taste
- 1 bunch of savoury herbs, including parsley, all finely minced and well mixed
- 3 onions
- 2 large carrots
- 1 turnip
- 1 head of celery
- 1-1/2 pint of water
- 1 glass of port wine

## Directions

1. Slice and fry the onions of a pale brown, and cut up the other vegetables in small pieces.
2. Choose a fine piece of beef, cut the bacon into long slices, about an inch in thickness, dip them into vinegar, and then into a little of the above seasoning of spice, &c., mixed with the same quantity of minced herbs.
3. With a sharp knife make holes deep enough to let in the bacon.
4. Rub the beef over with the remainder of the seasoning and herbs, and bind it up in a nice shape with tape.
5. Have ready a well-tinned stewpan into which put the beef, with the vegetables, vinegar, and water.
6. Let it simmer very gently for 5 hours, or rather longer, should the meat not be extremely tender, and turn it once or twice.
7. When ready to serve, take out the beef, remove the tape, and put it on a hot dish.
8. Skim off every particle of fat from the gravy, add the port wine, just let it boil, and pour it over the beef.

title: Beef a la Mode (Economical)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- About 3 lbs. of clod or sticking of beef
- 2 oz. of clarified dripping
- 1 large onion
- flour
- 2 quarts of water
- 12 berries of allspice
- 2 bay-leaves
- 1/2 teaspoonful of whole black pepper
- salt to taste

## Directions

1. Cut the beef into small pieces, and roll them in flour.
2. Put the dripping into a stewpan with the onion, which should be sliced thin.
3. Let it get quite hot; lay in the pieces of beef, and stir them well about.
4. When nicely browned all over, add by degrees boiling water in the above proportion, and, as the water is added, keep the whole well stirred.
5. Put in the spice, bay-leaves, and seasoning, cover the stewpan closely, and set it by the side of the fire to stew very gently, till the meat becomes quite tender, which will be in about 3 hours.
6. Remove the bay-leaves before it is sent to table.

title: Beef Cake
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Remains of cold roast beef (any amount)
- Bacon or ham (1/4 lb. per pound of cold meat)
- Pepper and salt to taste
- 1 small bunch of minced savoury herbs
- 1 or 2 eggs
- Hot dripping for frying
- Good gravy for serving

## Directions

1. Mince the beef very finely (if underdone it will be better).
2. Add to it the bacon, which must also be chopped very small, and mix well together.
3. Season, stir in the herbs, and bind with an egg, or 2 should 1 not be sufficient.
4. Make it into small square cakes, about 1/2 inch thick.
5. Fry them in hot dripping, and serve in a dish with good gravy poured round them.
6. Fry for 10 minutes.

title: Beef Fritters
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- remains of cold roast beef
- pepper
- salt
- 3/4 lb. of flour
- 1/2 pint of water
- 2 oz. of butter
- whites of 2 eggs
- boiling lard
- finely-minced onions

## Directions

1. Mix very smoothly, and by degrees, the flour with the proportion of water.
2. Stir in 2 oz. of butter, which must be melted, but not oiled.
3. Just before it is to be used, add the whites of two well-whisked eggs.
4. Should the batter be too thick, add more water.
5. Pare down the cold beef into thin shreds, season with pepper and salt, and mix it with the batter.
6. A small quantity of finely-minced onions, mixed with the batter, is an improvement.
7. Drop a small quantity at a time into a pan of boiling lard, and fry from 7 to 10 minutes, according to the size.
8. When done on one side, turn and brown them on the other.
9. Let them dry for a minute or two before the fire, and serve on a folded napkin.

title: Beef Olives
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of rump-steak
- 1 egg
- 1 tablespoonful of minced savoury herbs
- pepper and salt to taste
- 1 pint of stock
- 2 or 3 slices of bacon
- 2 tablespoonfuls of any store sauce
- butter and flour for thickening

## Directions

1. Have the steaks cut rather thin, slightly beat them to make them level, cut them into 6 or 7 pieces
2. Brush over with egg, and sprinkle with herbs, which should be very finely minced
3. Season with pepper and salt, and roll up the pieces tightly, and fasten with a small skewer
4. Put the stock in a stewpan that will exactly hold them
5. Lay in the rolls of meat, cover them with the bacon, cut in thin slices, and over that put a piece of paper
6. Stew them very gently for full 2 hours
7. Take them out, remove the skewers, thicken the gravy with butter and flour, and flavour with any store sauce that may be preferred
8. Give one boil, pour over the meat, and serve

title: Beef Olives (Economical)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- remains of underdone cold roast beef
- bread crumbs
- 1 shalot finely minced
- pepper and salt to taste
- gravy made from the beef bones
- butter and flour for thickening
- 1 tablespoonful of mushroom ketchup

## Directions

1. Cut some slices of underdone roast beef about half an inch thick
2. Sprinkle over them some bread crumbs, minced shalot, and a little of the fat and seasoning
3. Roll them, and fasten with a small skewer
4. Have ready some gravy made from the beef bones; put in the pieces of meat
5. Stew them till tender, which will be in about 1-1/4 hour, or rather longer
6. Arrange the meat in a dish, thicken and flavour the gravy, and pour it over the meat, when it is ready to serve

title: Beef Pickle
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 lbs. of salt
- 2 lbs. of fine sugar
- 3 oz. of powdered saltpetre
- 3 gallons of spring water

## Directions

1. Boil all the ingredients gently together, so long as any scum or impurity arises, which carefully remove
2. When quite cold, pour it over the meat, every part of which must be covered with the brine
3. This may be used for pickling any kind of meat, and may be kept for some time, if boiled up occasionally with an addition of the ingredients

title: Beef Ragout (Cold Meat Cookery)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- About 2 lbs. of cold roast beef
- 6 onions
- pepper
- salt
- mixed spices to taste
- 1/2 pint of boiling water
- 3 tablespoonfuls of gravy

## Directions

1. Cut the beef into rather large pieces, and put them into a stewpan with the onions, which must be sliced
2. Season well with pepper, salt, and mixed spices, and pour over about 1/2 pint of boiling water, and gravy in the above proportion
3. Let the whole stew very gently for about 2 hours
4. Serve with pickled walnuts, gherkins, or capers, just warmed in the gravy

title: Beef Rissoles
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of cold roast beef
- to each pound of meat allow 3/4 lb. of bread crumbs
- salt and pepper to taste
- a few chopped savoury herbs
- 1/2 a teaspoonful of minced lemon-peel
- 1 or 2 eggs, according to the quantity of meat

## Directions

1. Mince the beef very fine, which should be rather lean, and mix with this bread crumbs, herbs, seasoning, and lemon-peel, in the above proportion, to each pound of meat
2. Make all into a thick paste with 1 or 2 eggs
3. Divide into balls or cones, and fry a rich brown
4. Garnish the dish with fried parsley, and send with them to table some good brown gravy in a tureen
5. Instead of garnishing with fried parsley, gravy may be poured in the dish, round the rissoles: in this case, it will not be necessary to send any in a tureen

title: Beef Rolls
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of cold roast or boiled beef
- seasoning to taste of salt, pepper, and minced herbs
- puff paste

## Directions

1. Mince the beef tolerably fine with a small amount of its own fat
2. Add a seasoning of pepper, salt, and chopped herbs
3. Put the whole into a roll of puff paste, and bake for 1/2 hour, or rather longer, should the roll be very large
4. Beef patties may be made of cold meat, by mincing and seasoning beef as directed above, and baking in a rich puff paste in patty-tins

title: Beef Sausages
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of lean beef per lb. of suet
- salt, pepper, and mixed spices to taste

## Directions

1. Clear the suet from skin, and chop that and the beef as finely as possible.
2. Season with pepper, salt, and spices, and mix the whole well together.
3. Make it into flat cakes, and fry of a nice brown.

title: Beef-Collops
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of rump-steak
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 1 pint of gravy (water may be substituted for this)
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 shalot finely minced
- 1/2 pickled walnut
- 1 teaspoonful of capers

## Directions

1. Have the steak cut thin, and divide it in pieces about 3 inches long.
2. Beat these with the blade of a knife, and dredge with flour.
3. Put them in a frying-pan with the butter, and let them fry for about 3 minutes.
4. Then lay them in a small stewpan, and pour over them the gravy.
5. Add a piece of butter, kneaded with a little flour, put in the seasoning and all the other ingredients, and let the whole simmer, but not boil, for 10 minutes.
6. Serve in a hot covered dish.

title: Beef-Steak and Kidney Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of rump-steak
- 2 kidneys
- salt
- black pepper
- suet crust made with milk
- 6 oz. of suet to each 1 lb. of flour
- water

## Directions

1. Procure some tender rump steak (that which has been hung a little time), and divide it into pieces about an inch square, and cut each kidney into 8 pieces.
2. Line the dish with crust made with suet and flour in the proportion of 6 oz. of suet to each 1 lb. of flour, leaving a small piece of crust to overlap the edge.
3. Cover the bottom with a portion of the steak and a few pieces of kidney.
4. Season with salt and pepper.
5. Add another layer of steak, kidney, and seasoning.
6. Proceed in this manner till the dish is full.
7. Pour in sufficient water to come within 2 inches of the top of the basin.
8. Moisten the edges of the crust, cover the pudding over, press the two crusts together, that the gravy may not escape, and turn up the overhanging paste.
9. Wring out a cloth in hot water, flour it, and tie up the pudding.
10. Put it into boiling water, and let it boil for at least 4 hours.
11. Replenish with hot water in a jug if the water diminishes, as the pudding should be kept covered all the time, and not allowed to stop boiling.
12. When the cloth is removed, cut out a round piece in the top of the crust, to prevent the pudding bursting.
13. Send it to table in the basin, either in an ornamental dish, or with a napkin pinned round it.
14. Serve quickly.

title: Beef-Steak Pie
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 lbs. of rump-steak
- salt
- cayenne
- black pepper
- crust
- water
- the yolk of an egg

## Directions

1. Have the steaks cut from a rump that has hung a few days, that they may be tender, and be particular that every portion is perfectly sweet.
2. Cut the steaks into pieces about 3 inches long and 2 wide, allowing a small piece of fat to each piece of lean, and arrange the meat in layers in a pie-dish.
3. Between each layer sprinkle a seasoning of salt, pepper, and, when liked, a few grains of cayenne.
4. Fill the dish sufficiently with meat to support the crust, and to give it a nice raised appearance when baked, and not to look flat and hollow.
5. Pour in sufficient water to half fill the dish, and border it with paste.
6. Brush it over with a little water, and put on the cover.
7. Slightly press down the edges with the thumb, and trim off close to the dish.
8. Ornament the pie with leaves, or pieces of paste cut in any shape that fancy may direct.
9. Brush it over with the beaten yolk of an egg.
10. Make a hole in the top of the crust, and bake in a hot oven for about 1-1/2 hour.

title: Beef-Steak, Rolled, Roasted, and Stuffed
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of rump-steak
- forcemeat No. 417
- pepper and salt to taste
- clarified butter

## Directions

1. Have the steaks cut rather thick from a well-hung rump of beef, and sprinkle over them a seasoning of pepper and salt.
2. Make a forcemeat by recipe No. 417; spread it over half of the steak.
3. Roll it up, bind and skewer it firmly, that the forcemeat may not escape.
4. Roast it before a nice clear fire for about 1-1/2 hour, or rather longer, should the roll be very large and thick.
5. Keep it constantly basted with butter.
6. Serve with brown gravy, some of which must be poured round the steak, and the remainder sent to table in a tureen.

title: Beef-Steaks and Oyster Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 dozen oysters
- ingredients for oyster sauce
- 2 lbs. of rump-steak
- seasoning to taste of pepper and salt

## Directions

1. Make the oyster sauce by recipe No. 492, and when that is ready, put it by the side of the fire, but do not let it keep boiling.
2. Have the steaks cut of an equal thickness, broil them over a very clear fire, turning them often, that the gravy may not escape.
3. In about 8 minutes they will be done, then put them on a very hot dish; smother with the oyster sauce, and the remainder send to table in a tureen.
4. Serve quickly.

title: Beef-Steaks With Fried Potatoes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of steak
- 8 potatoes
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 teaspoonful of minced herbs

## Directions

1. Put the butter into a frying or sauté pan, set it over the fire, and let it get very hot.
2. Peel, and cut the potatoes into long thin slices.
3. Put them into the hot butter, and fry them till of a nice brown colour.
4. Broil the steaks over a bright clear fire, turning them frequently, that every part may be equally done; 5 minutes will broil them.
5. Put the herbs and seasoning in the butter the potatoes were fried in, pour it under the steak, and place the fried potatoes round, as a garnish.

title: Bengal Recipe for Making Mango Chetney
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 lbs. of moist sugar
- 3/4 lb. of salt
- 1/4 lb. of garlic
- 1/4 lb. of onions
- 3/4 lb. of powdered ginger
- 1/4 lb. of dried chilies
- 3/4 lb. of mustard-seed
- 3/4 lb. of stoned raisins
- 2 bottles of best vinegar
- 30 large unripe sour apples

## Directions

1. Make the sugar into syrup
2. Pound the garlic, onions, and ginger finely in a mortar
3. Wash the mustard-seed in cold vinegar, and dry it in the sun
4. Peel, core, and slice the apples, and boil them in a bottle and a half of the vinegar
5. When all this is done and the apples are quite cold, put them into a large pan
6. Gradually mix the whole of the rest of the ingredients, including the remaining half-bottle of vinegar
7. Stir well until the whole is thoroughly blended
8. Put into bottles for use
9. Tie a piece of wet bladder over the mouths of the bottles, after they are well corked

title: Benton Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 tablespoonful of scraped horseradish
- 1 teaspoonful of made mustard
- 1 teaspoonful of pounded sugar
- 4 tablespoonfuls of vinegar

## Directions

1. Grate or scrape the horseradish very fine, and mix it with the other ingredients, which must be all well blended together; serve in a tureen.

title: Beurre Noir, or Browned Butter
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 1 tablespoonful of minced parsley
- 3 tablespoonfuls of vinegar
- salt and pepper to taste

## Directions

1. Put the butter into a fryingpan over a nice clear fire, and when it smokes, throw in the parsley, and add the vinegar and seasoning.
2. Let the whole simmer for a minute or two, when it is ready to serve.

title: Black or Red Currant Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 quart of red or black currants, measured with the stalks
- 1/4 lb. of moist sugar
- 3/4 lb. of flour
- suet crust or butter crust
- butter
- raspberries (optional, about 1/2 pint)

## Directions

1. Make, with 3/4 lb. of flour, either a suet crust or butter crust
2. Butter a basin, and line it with part of the crust
3. Put in the currants, which should be stripped from the stalks, and sprinkle the sugar over them
4. Put the cover of the pudding on; make the edges very secure, that the juice does not escape
5. Tie it down with a floured cloth, put it into boiling water, and boil from 2-1/2 to 3 hours
6. If boiled without a basin, allow 1/2 hour less
7. If desired, brown the crust with a salamander, or put into a very hot oven for a few minutes to colour it

title: Black-Currant Jam
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of fruit, weighed before being stripped from the stalks
- 3/4 lb. of loaf sugar per lb. of fruit
- 1 gill of water per lb. of fruit

## Directions

1. Let the fruit be very ripe, and gathered on a dry day
2. Strip it from the stalks, and put it into a preserving-pan, with a gill of water to each lb. of fruit
3. Boil these together for 10 minutes
4. Add the sugar, and boil the jam again for 30 minutes, reckoning from the time when the jam simmers equally all over, or longer, should it not appear to set nicely when a little is poured on to a plate
5. Keep stirring it to prevent it from burning
6. Carefully remove all the scum
7. When done, pour it into pots
8. Let it cool, cover the top of the jam with oiled paper, and the top of the jars with a piece of tissue-paper brushed over on both sides with the white of an egg

title: Black-Currant Jelly
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Black currants
- 1/4 pint of water per pint of juice
- 1 lb. of loaf sugar per pint of juice

## Directions

1. Strip the currants from the stalks by holding the bunch in one hand, and passing a small silver fork down the currants
2. Put them into a jar, place this jar in a saucepan of boiling water, and simmer them until their juice is extracted
3. Strain them, and to every pint of juice allow the above proportion of sugar and water
4. Stir these ingredients together cold until the sugar is dissolved
5. Place the preserving-pan on the fire, and boil the jelly for about 1/2 hour, reckoning from the time it commences to boil all over
6. Carefully remove the scum as it rises
7. When the jelly becomes firm when a little is put on a plate, it is done
8. Put it into small pots, and cover

title: Blanc-Mange
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of new milk
- 1-1/4 oz. of isinglass
- rind of 1/2 lemon
- 1/4 lb. of loaf sugar
- 10 bitter almonds
- 1/2 oz. of sweet almonds
- 1 pint of cream

## Directions

1. Put the milk into a saucepan, with the isinglass, lemon-rind, and sugar, and let these ingredients stand by the side of the fire until the milk is well flavoured
2. Add the almonds, which should be blanched and pounded in a mortar to a paste, and let the milk just boil up
3. Strain it through a fine sieve or muslin into a jug, add the cream, and stir the mixture occasionally until nearly cold
4. Let it stand for a few minutes, then pour it into the mould, which should be previously oiled with the purest salad-oil, or dipped in cold water
5. Omit the sediment at the bottom of the jug when pouring into the mould
6. Turn it out carefully on a dish by loosening the edges from the mould, placing a dish on it, and turning it quickly over
7. Garnish as fancy dictates

title: Boiled Aitch-Bone of Beef
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Beef
- Water
- Carrots
- Turnips
- Suet dumplings (optional)

## Directions

1. After the joint has been in salt 5 or 6 days, it will be ready for use.
2. Wash the meat, and if too salt, soak it for a few hours, changing the water once or twice, till the required freshness is obtained.
3. Put into a saucepan or boiling-pot sufficient water to cover the meat.
4. Set it over the fire, and when it boils, plunge in the joint and let it boil up quickly.
5. Draw the pot to the side of the fire, and let the process be very gradual, as the water must only simmer, or the meat will be hard and tough.
6. Carefully remove the scum from the surface of the water, and continue doing this for a few minutes after it first boils.
7. Boil for 2-1/2 hours if the aitch-bone is 10 lbs., or 4 hours if 20 lbs.
8. Garnish with a few of the carrots and turnips, and serve the remainder in a vegetable-dish.

title: Boiled Apple Dumplings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 apples
- 3/4 lb. of suet-crust
- sugar to taste

## Directions

1. Pare and take out the cores of the apples without dividing them
2. Sweeten
3. Roll each apple in a piece of crust
4. Be particular that the paste is nicely joined
5. Put the dumplings into floured cloths
6. Tie them securely
7. Put them into boiling water
8. Keep them boiling from 1/2 to 3/4 hour
9. Remove the cloths
10. Send them hot and quickly to table

title: Boiled Apple Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Crust No. 1215
- apples
- sugar to taste
- 1 small teaspoonful of finely-minced lemon-peel
- 2 tablespoonfuls of lemon-juice

## Directions

1. Make a butter-crust by recipe No. 1213, or a suet one by recipe No. 1215, using for a moderate-sized pudding from 3/4 to 1 lb. of flour, with the other ingredients in proportion
2. Butter a basin
3. Line it with some of the paste
4. Pare, core, and cut the apples into slices
5. Fill the basin with these apples
6. Add the sugar, the lemon-peel and juice
7. Cover with crust
8. Pinch the edges together
9. Flour the cloth, place it over the pudding, and tie it securely
10. Put it into plenty of fast-boiling water
11. Let it boil from 1-1/2 to 2-1/2 hours, according to the size
12. Turn it out of the basin and send to table quickly

title: Boiled Artichokes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- To each 1/2 gallon of water, 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- a piece of soda the size of a shilling
- artichokes

## Directions

1. Wash the artichokes well in several waters; see that no insects remain about them, and trim away the leaves at the bottom.
2. Cut off the stems and put them into boiling water, to which have been added salt and soda in the above proportion.
3. Keep the saucepan uncovered, and let them boil quickly until tender; ascertain when they are done by thrusting a fork in them, or by trying if the leaves can be easily removed.
4. Take them out, let them drain for a minute or two, and serve in a napkin, or with a little white sauce poured over.
5. A tureen of melted butter should accompany them.

title: Boiled Asparagus
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 gallon of water
- 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- asparagus

## Directions

1. Asparagus should be dressed as soon as possible after it is cut
2. Scrape the white part of the stems, beginning from the head, and throw them into cold water
3. Tie them into bundles of about 20 each, keeping the heads all one way
4. Cut the stalks evenly, that they may all be the same length
5. Put them into boiling water, with salt in the above proportion
6. Keep them boiling quickly until tender, with the saucepan uncovered
7. When the asparagus is done, dish it upon toast, which should be dipped in the water it was cooked in
8. Leave the white ends outwards each way, with the points meeting in the middle
9. Serve with a tureen of melted butter

title: Boiled Bacon
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- bacon
- water

## Directions

1. As bacon is frequently excessively salt, let it be soaked in warm water for an hour or two previous to dressing it; then pare off the rusty parts, and scrape the under-side and rind as clean as possible.
2. Put it into a saucepan of cold water, let it come gradually to a boil, and as fast as the scum rises to the surface of the water, remove it.
3. Let it simmer very gently until it is thoroughly done; then take it up, strip off the skin, and sprinkle over the bacon a few bread raspings, and garnish with tufts of cauliflower or Brussels sprouts.
4. When served alone, young and tender broad beans or green peas are the usual accompaniments.

title: Boiled Batter Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 eggs
- 1 oz. of butter
- 1 pint of milk
- 3 tablespoonfuls of flour
- a little salt

## Directions

1. Put the flour into a basin, and add sufficient milk to moisten it
2. carefully rub down all the lumps with a spoon, then pour in the remainder of the milk, and stir in the butter, which should be previously melted
3. keep beating the mixture, add the eggs and a pinch of salt
4. when the batter is quite smooth, put it into a well-buttered basin, tie it down very tightly, and put it into boiling water
5. move the basin about for a few minutes after it is put into the water, to prevent the flour settling in any part, and boil for 1-1/4 hour
6. Send these puddings very quickly to table, and serve with sweet sauce, wine sauce, stewed fruit, or jam of any kind

title: Boiled Beetroot
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Beetroot
- boiling water

## Directions

1. Wash the beets thoroughly
2. Do not prick or break the skin before they are cooked, or they would lose their beautiful colour in boiling
3. Put them into boiling water and let them boil until tender, keeping them well covered
4. If to be served hot, remove the peel quickly, cut the beetroot into thick slices, and send to table with melted butter
5. For salads, pickle, etc., let the root cool, then peel and cut it into slices

title: Boiled Bread Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 pint of milk
- 3/4 pint of bread crumbs
- sugar to taste
- 4 eggs
- 1 oz. of butter
- 3 oz. of currants
- 1/4 teaspoonful of grated nutmeg

## Directions

1. Make the milk boiling, and pour it on the bread crumbs
2. Let these remain till cold
3. Add the other ingredients, taking care that the eggs are well beaten and the currants well washed, picked, and dried
4. Beat the pudding well, and put it into a buttered basin
5. Tie it down tightly with a cloth
6. Plunge it into boiling water, and boil for 1-1/4 hour
7. Turn it out of the basin, and serve with sifted sugar
8. Any odd pieces or scraps of bread answer for this pudding; but they should be soaked overnight, and, when wanted for use, should have the water well squeezed from them

title: Boiled Breast of Mutton and Caper Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Breast of mutton
- bread crumbs
- 2 tablespoonfuls of minced savoury herbs (put a large proportion of parsley)
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Cut off the superfluous fat
2. Bone it
3. Sprinkle over a layer of bread crumbs, minced herbs, and seasoning
4. Roll, and bind it up firmly
5. Boil gently for 2 hours
6. Remove the tape
7. Serve with caper sauce No. 382, a little of which should be poured over the meat

title: Boiled Broad or Windsor Beans
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 gallon of water
- 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- broad beans
- parsley and butter

## Directions

1. Shell the beans
2. Put them into boiling water, salted in the above proportion
3. Let them boil rapidly until tender
4. Drain them well in a colander
5. Dish and serve with them separately a tureen of parsley and butter

title: Boiled Broccoli
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 gallon of water
- 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- broccoli

## Directions

1. Strip off the dead outside leaves, and the inside ones cut off level with the flower
2. Cut off the stalk close at the bottom
3. Put the broccoli into cold salt and water, with the heads downwards
4. Let them remain in this for about 3/4 hour until perfectly free from insects
5. Put them into a saucepan of boiling water, salted in the above proportion
6. Keep them boiling quickly over a brisk fire, with the saucepan uncovered
7. Take them up with a slice the moment they are done
8. Drain them well
9. Serve with a tureen of melted butter, a little of which should be poured over the broccoli

title: Boiled Brussels Sprouts
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 gallon of water
- 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- a very small piece of soda
- brussels sprouts
- melted butter

## Directions

1. Clean the sprouts from insects and nicely wash them
2. Pick off any dead or discoloured leaves from the outsides
3. Put them into a saucepan of boiling water, with salt and soda in the above proportion
4. Keep the pan uncovered and let them boil quickly over a brisk fire until tender
5. Drain and dish
6. Serve with a tureen of melted butter, or with a maître d'hôtel sauce poured over them
7. Alternatively, when dished, stir in about 1-1/2 oz. of butter and a seasoning of pepper and salt

title: Boiled Cabbage
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 gallon of water
- 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- a very small piece of soda
- cabbages

## Directions

1. Pick off all the dead outside leaves
2. cut off as much of the stalk as possible
3. cut the cabbages across twice, at the stalk end; if they should be very large, quarter them
4. Wash them well in cold water
5. place them in a colander, and drain
6. put them into plenty of fast-boiling water, to which have been added salt and soda in the above proportions
7. Stir them down once or twice in the water
8. keep the pan uncovered
9. let them boil quickly until tender
10. The instant they are done, take them up into a colander
11. place a plate over them
12. let them thoroughly drain
13. dish, and serve

title: Boiled Calf's Feet and Parsley and Butter
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 calf's feet
- 2 slices of bacon
- 2 oz. of butter
- 2 tablespoonfuls of lemon-juice
- salt and whole pepper to taste
- 1 onion
- a bunch of savoury herbs
- 4 cloves
- 1 blade of mace
- water
- parsley and butter No. 493

## Directions

1. Procure 2 white calf's feet; bone them as far as the first joint, and put them into warm water to soak for 2 hours.
2. Put the bacon, butter, lemon-juice, onion, herbs, spices, and seasoning into a stewpan; lay in the feet, and pour in just sufficient water to cover the whole.
3. Stew gently for about 3 hours.
4. Take out the feet, dish them, and cover with parsley and butter, made by recipe No. 493.
5. Strain the liquor they were boiled in and put by in a clean basin for use.

title: Boiled Calf's Head (with the Skin on)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's head
- boiling water
- bread crumbs
- 1 large bunch of parsley
- butter
- white pepper and salt to taste
- 4 tablespoonfuls of melted butter
- 1 tablespoonful of lemon-juice
- 2 or 3 grains of cayenne

## Directions

1. Put the head into boiling water, and let it remain by the side of the fire for 3 or 4 minutes.
2. Take it out, hold it by the ear, and with the back of a knife, scrape off the hair (should it not come off easily, dip the head again into boiling water).
3. When perfectly clean, take the eyes out, cut off the ears, and remove the brain, which soak for an hour in warm water.
4. Put the head into hot water to soak for a few minutes, to make it look white, and then have ready a stewpan, into which lay the head.
5. Cover it with cold water, and bring it gradually to boil.
6. Remove the scum, and add a little salt, which assists to throw it up.
7. Simmer it very gently from 2-1/2 to 3 hours.
8. When nearly done, boil the brains for 1/4 hour; skin and chop them, not too finely, and add a tablespoonful of minced parsley which has been previously scalded.
9. Season with pepper and salt, and stir the brains, parsley, &c., into about 4 tablespoonfuls of melted butter; add the lemon-juice and cayenne, and keep these hot by the side of the fire.
10. Take up the head, cut out the tongue, skin it, put it on a small dish with the brains round it.
11. Sprinkle over the head a few bread crumbs mixed with a little minced parsley; brown these before the fire, and serve with a tureen of parsley and butter, and either boiled bacon, ham, or pickled pork as an accompaniment.

title: Boiled Calf's Head (without the Skin)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's head
- water
- a little salt
- 4 tablespoonfuls of melted butter
- 1 tablespoonful of minced parsley
- pepper and salt to taste
- 1 tablespoonful of lemon-juice

## Directions

1. After the head has been thoroughly cleaned, and the brains removed, soak it in warm water to blanch it.
2. Lay the brains also into warm water to soak, and let them remain for about an hour.
3. Put the head into a stewpan, with sufficient cold water to cover it, and when it boils, add a little salt.
4. Take off every particle of scum as it rises, and boil the head until perfectly tender.
5. Boil the brains, chop them, and mix with them melted butter, minced parsley, pepper, salt, and lemon-juice in the above proportion.
6. Take up the head, skin the tongue, and put it on a small dish with the brains round it.
7. Have ready some parsley and butter, smother the head with it, and the remainder send to table in a tureen.
8. Serve with bacon, ham, pickled pork, or a pig's cheek as accompaniment.

title: Boiled Calf's Udder for French Forcemeats
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- calf's udder
- water

## Directions

1. Put the udder into a stewpan with sufficient water to cover it
2. Let it stew gently till quite done
3. Take it out to cool
4. Trim all the upper parts
5. Cut it into small pieces
6. Pound well in a mortar, till it can be rubbed through a sieve

title: Boiled Carrots
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 gallon of water
- 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- carrots

## Directions

1. Cut off the green tops
2. wash and scrape the carrots
3. should there be any black specks, remove them
4. If very large, cut them in halves, divide them lengthwise into four pieces
5. put them into boiling water, salted in the above proportion
6. let them boil until tender, which may be ascertained by thrusting a fork into them
7. dish, and serve very hot

title: Boiled Cauliflowers
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- cauliflowers
- salt
- water
- plain melted butter

## Directions

1. Choose cauliflowers that are close and white
2. Trim off the decayed outside leaves, and cut the stalk off flat at the bottom
3. Open the flower a little in places to remove the insects
4. Let the cauliflowers lie in salt and water for an hour previous to dressing them, with their heads downwards
5. Put them into fast-boiling water, with salt in proportion of 1 heaped tablespoonful to each 1/2 gallon of water
6. Let them boil briskly over a good fire, keeping the saucepan uncovered
7. Skim the water well
8. When the cauliflowers are tender, take them up with a slice and let them drain
9. If large enough, place them upright in the dish
10. Serve with plain melted butter, a little of which may be poured over the flower

title: Boiled Currant Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of flour
- 1/2 lb. of suet
- 1/2 lb. of currants
- milk
- lemon
- cold butter
- sifted sugar

## Directions

1. Wash the currants, dry them thoroughly, and pick away any stalks or grit
2. Chop the suet finely
3. Mix all the ingredients together, and moisten with sufficient milk to make the pudding into a stiff batter
4. Tie it up in a floured cloth, put it into boiling water, and boil for 3-1/2 hours
5. Serve with a cut lemon, cold butter, and sifted sugar

title: Boiled Custard Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of milk
- 1 tablespoonful of flour
- 4 eggs
- lemon-rind or cinnamon for flavouring
- butter

## Directions

1. Flavour the milk by infusing in it a little lemon-rind or cinnamon
2. Whisk the eggs
3. Stir the flour gradually to these
4. Pour over them the milk, and stir the mixture well
5. Butter a basin that will exactly hold it
6. Put in the custard, and tie a floured cloth over
7. Plunge it into boiling water, and turn it about for a few minutes, to prevent the flour from settling in one part
8. Boil it slowly for 1/2 hour
9. Turn it out of the basin, and serve

title: Boiled Custards
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of milk
- 5 eggs
- 3 oz. of loaf sugar
- 3 laurel-leaves, or the rind of 4 lemon, or a few drops of essence of vanilla
- 1 tablespoonful of brandy

## Directions

1. Put the milk into a lined saucepan, with the sugar, and whichever of the above flavourings may be preferred, and let the milk steep by the side of the fire until it is well flavoured.
2. Bring it to the point of boiling, then strain it into a basin.
3. Whisk the eggs well, and, when the milk has cooled a little, stir in the eggs, and strain this mixture into a jug.
4. Place this jug in a saucepan of boiling water over the fire; keep stirring the custard one way until it thickens; but on no account allow it to reach the boiling-point, as it will instantly curdle and be full of lumps.
5. Take it off the fire, stir in the brandy, and, when this is well mixed with the custard, pour it into glasses, which should be rather more than three-parts full.
6. Grate a little nutmeg over the top, and the dish is ready for table.

title: Boiled Eels
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 small eels
- sufficient water to cover them
- a large bunch of parsley

## Directions

1. Choose small eels for boiling; put them in a stewpan with the parsley, and just sufficient water to cover them; simmer till tender.
2. Take them out, pour a little parsley and butter over them, and serve some in a tureen.

title: Boiled Flounders
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Sufficient water to cover the flounders
- salt in the proportion of 6 oz. to each gallon
- a little vinegar
- plain melted butter or parsley and butter

## Directions

1. Pat on a kettle with enough water to cover the flounders, lay in the fish, add salt and vinegar in the above proportions, and when it boils, simmer very gently for 5 minutes
2. They must not boil fast, or they will break
3. Serve with plain melted butter, or parsley and butter

title: Boiled Fowl and Rice
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 fowl
- mutton broth
- 2 onions
- 2 small blades of pounded mace
- pepper and salt to taste
- 1/4 pint of rice
- parsley and butter

## Directions

1. Truss the fowl as for boiling, and put it into a stewpan with sufficient clear well-skimmed mutton broth to cover it
2. Add the onion, mace, and a seasoning of pepper and salt; stew very gently for about 1 hour, should the fowl be large
3. About 1/2 hour before it is ready put in the rice, which should be well washed and soaked
4. When the latter is tender, strain it from the liquor, and put it on a sieve reversed to dry before the fire, and keep the fowl hot
5. Dish it, put the rice round as a border, pour a little parsley and butter over the fowl, and the remainder send to table in a tureen

title: Boiled Fowl with Oysters
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 young fowl
- 3 dozen oysters
- yolks of 2 eggs
- 1/4 pint of cream
- a blade of pounded mace

## Directions

1. Truss a young fowl as for boiling
2. Fill the inside with oysters which have been bearded and washed in their own liquor
3. Secure the ends of the fowl
4. Put it into a jar, and plunge the jar into a saucepan of boiling water
5. Keep it boiling for 1-1/2 hour, or rather longer
6. Take the gravy that has flowed from the oysters and fowl
7. Stir in the cream and yolks of eggs
8. Add a few oysters scalded in their liquor
9. Let the sauce get quite hot, but do not allow it to boil
10. Pour some of it over the fowl, and the remainder send to table in a tureen
11. A blade of pounded mace added to the sauce, with the cream and eggs, will be found an improvement

title: Boiled Fowls a la Bechamel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A pair of fowls
- 1 pint of Béchamel
- a few bunches of boiled brocoli or cauliflower

## Directions

1. Truss and boil the fowls by recipe No. 938
2. Make a pint of Béchamel sauce by recipe No. 367
3. Pour some of this over the fowls, and the remainder send to table in a tureen
4. Garnish the dish with bunches of boiled cauliflowers or brocoli, and serve very hot
5. The sauce should be made sufficiently thick to adhere to the fowls; that for the tureen should be thinned by adding a spoonful or two of stock

title: Boiled Fowls or Chickens
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A pair of fowls
- water

## Directions

1. In choosing fowls for boiling, those that are not black-legged are generally much whiter when dressed.
2. Pick, draw, singe, wash, and truss them as follows: Cut off the neck, leaving sufficient skin to skewer back.
3. Cut the feet off to the first joint, tuck the stumps into a slit made on each side of the belly.
4. Twist the wings over the back of the fowl, and secure the top of the leg and the bottom of the wing together by running a skewer through them and the body.
5. Do the other side in the same manner.
6. Should the fowl be very large and old, draw the sinews of the legs before tucking them in.
7. Make a slit in the apron of the fowl, large enough to admit the parson's nose, and tie a string on the tops of the legs to keep them in their proper place.
8. When they are firmly trussed, put them into a stewpan with plenty of hot water.
9. Bring it to boil, and carefully remove all the scum as it rises.
10. Simmer very gently until the fowl is tender; the slower it boils, the plumper and whiter will the fowl be.
11. Many cooks wrap them in a floured cloth to preserve the colour, and to prevent the scum from clinging to them; in this case, a few slices of lemon should be placed on the breasts, over these a sheet of buttered paper, and then the cloth.
12. Boiled ham, bacon, boiled tongue, or pickled pork are the usual accompaniments to boiled fowls, and they may be served with Béchamel, white sauce, parsley and butter, oyster, lemon, liver, celery, or mushroom sauce.
13. Pour a little sauce over the fowls after the skewers are removed, and send the remainder in a tureen to table.
14. Large fowl, 1 hour; moderate-sized one, 3/4 hour; chicken, from 20 minutes to 1/2 hour.

title: Boiled French Beans
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 gallon of water
- 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- a very small piece of soda
- French beans

## Directions

1. Cut off the heads and tails, and a thin strip on each side of the beans, to remove the strings
2. Divide each bean into 4 or 6 pieces, according to size, cutting them lengthways in a slanting direction
3. Put them into cold water with a small quantity of salt dissolved in it
4. Have ready a saucepan of boiling water, with salt and soda in the above proportion
5. Put in the beans and keep them boiling quickly with the lid uncovered
6. Be careful that they do not get smoked
7. When tender, which may be ascertained by their sinking to the bottom of the saucepan, take them up and throw them into a colander
8. When drained, dish and serve with plain melted butter

title: Boiled Gooseberry Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 lb. of suet crust
- 1-1/2 pint of green gooseberries
- 1/4 lb. of moist sugar

## Directions

1. Line a pudding-basin with suet crust, rolled out to about 1/2 inch in thickness
2. With a pair of scissors, cut off the tops and tails of the gooseberries
3. Fill the basin with the fruit
4. Put in the sugar
5. Cover with crust
6. Pinch the edges of the pudding together
7. Tie over it a floured cloth
8. Put it into boiling water
9. Boil from 2-1/2 to 3 hours
10. Turn it out of the basin, and serve with a jug of cream

title: Boiled Green Peas
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Green peas
- Water
- Moist sugar
- Salt
- Butter
- Mint (optional)

## Directions

1. Shell the peas, wash them well in cold water, and drain them
2. Put them into a saucepan with plenty of fast-boiling water, to which salt and moist sugar have been added in the proportion of 1 small teaspoonful of moist sugar and 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt to each 1/2 gallon of water
3. Let them boil quickly over a brisk fire, with the lid of the saucepan uncovered, being careful that the smoke does not draw in
4. When tender, pour them into a colander
5. Put them into a hot vegetable-dish, and quite in the centre of the peas place a piece of butter, the size of a walnut
6. Many cooks boil a small bunch of mint with the peas, or garnish them with it, by boiling a few sprigs in a saucepan by themselves
7. For very old peas that are difficult to boil a good colour, a very tiny piece of soda may be thrown in the water previous to putting them in, but this must be very sparingly used

title: Boiled Haddock
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- haddock
- water
- salt

## Directions

1. Scrape the fish
2. take out the inside
3. wash it thoroughly
4. lay it in a kettle, with enough water to cover it and salt in the proportion of 1/4 lb. of salt to each gallon of water
5. simmer gently from 15 to 20 minutes, or rather more, should the fish be very large
6. For small haddocks, fasten the tails in their mouths, and put them into boiling water for 10 to 15 minutes
7. serve with plain melted butter, or anchovy sauce

title: Boiled Indian Wheat or Maize
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- ears of young and green Indian wheat
- 1/2 gallon of water
- 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- melted butter
- toast

## Directions

1. Take off the outside sheath and remove the waving fibres from the ears
2. Place the ears in boiling water, where they should remain for about 25 minutes (a longer time may be necessary for larger ears than ordinary)
3. When sufficiently boiled and well drained, send them to table whole, and with a piece of toast underneath them
4. Serve melted butter with them

title: Boiled Jerusalem Artichokes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- To each 1 gallon of water allow 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- artichokes
- melted butter or cream sauce

## Directions

1. Wash, peel, and shape the artichokes in a round or oval form, and put them into a saucepan with sufficient cold water to cover them, salted in the above proportion.
2. Let them boil gently until tender.
3. Take them up, drain them, and serve them in a napkin, or plain, whichever mode is preferred.
4. Send to table with them a tureen of melted butter or cream sauce, a little of which may be poured over the artichokes when they are not served in a napkin.

title: Boiled Leg of Lamb a la Bechamel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Leg of lamb
- Béchamel sauce, No. 367

## Directions

1. Do not choose a very large joint, but one weighing about 5 lbs.
2. Have ready a saucepan of boiling water, into which plunge the lamb
3. When it boils up again, draw it to the side of the fire, and let the water cool a little
4. Then stew very gently for about 1-1/4 hour, reckoning from the time that the water begins to simmer
5. Make some Béchamel by recipe No. 367, dish the lamb, pour the sauce over it, and garnish with tufts of boiled cauliflower or carrots
6. When liked, melted butter may be substituted for the Béchamel
7. Send to table with it some of the sauce in a tureen, and boiled cauliflowers or spinach

title: Boiled Leg of Mutton
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Mutton
- water
- salt

## Directions

1. Cut off the shank-bone, trim the knuckle, and wash and wipe it very clean
2. Plunge it into sufficient boiling water to cover it
3. Let it boil up, then draw the saucepan to the side of the fire, where it should remain till the finger can be borne in the water
4. Place it sufficiently near the fire, that the water may gently simmer, and be very careful that it does not boil fast, or the meat will be hard
5. Skim well, add a little salt
6. In about 2-1/4 hours after the water begins to simmer, a moderate-sized leg of mutton will be done
7. Serve with carrots and mashed turnips, which may be boiled with the meat, and send caper sauce No. 382 to table with it in a tureen

title: Boiled Leg of Pork
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Leg of pork
- salt
- Carrots, turnips, or parsnips

## Directions

1. Choose a small, compact, well-filled leg, and rub it well with salt; let it remain in pickle for a week or ten days, turning and rubbing it every day.
2. An hour before dressing it, put it into cold water for an hour, which improves the colour.
3. If the pork is purchased ready salted, ascertain how long the meat has been in pickle, and soak it accordingly.
4. Put it into a boiling-pot, with sufficient cold water to cover it; let it gradually come to a boil, and remove the scum as it rises.
5. Simmer it very gently until tender, and do not allow it to boil fast, or the knuckle will fall to pieces before the middle of the leg is done.
6. Carrots, turnips, or parsnips may be boiled with the pork, some of which should be laid round the dish as a garnish, and a well-made pease-pudding is an indispensable accompaniment.

title: Boiled Leg of Pork
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Leg of pork
- salt

## Directions

1. For boiling, choose a small, compact, well-filled leg, and rub it well with salt; let it remain in pickle for a week or ten days, turning and rubbing it every day.
2. An hour before dressing it, put it into cold water for an hour, which improves the colour.

title: Boiled Lemon Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of chopped suet
- 3/4 lb. of bread crumbs
- 2 small lemons
- 6 oz. of moist sugar
- 1/4 lb. of flour
- 2 eggs
- milk

## Directions

1. Mix the suet, bread crumbs, sugar, and flour well together, adding the lemon-peel, which should be very finely minced, and the juice, which should be strained.
2. When these ingredients are well mixed, moisten with the eggs and sufficient milk to make the pudding of the consistency of thick batter; put it into a well-buttered mould, and boil for 3-1/2 hours; turn it out, strew sifted sugar over, and serve with wine sauce, or not, at pleasure.

title: Boiled Marrow-Bones
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- bones
- a small piece of common paste
- a floured cloth
- dry toast

## Directions

1. Have the bones neatly sawed into convenient sizes, and cover the ends with a small piece of common crust, made with flour and water.
2. Over this tie a floured cloth, and place them upright in a saucepan of boiling water, taking care there is sufficient to cover the bones.
