3. Boil them for 2 hours, remove the cloth and paste, and serve them upright on a napkin with dry toast.

title: Boiled Neck of Mutton
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 lbs. of the middle, or best end of the neck of mutton
- a little salt
- turnips
- caper sauce
- carrots

## Directions

1. Trim off a portion of the fat, should there be too much, and if it is to look particularly nice, the chine-bone should be sawn down, the ribs stripped halfway down, and the ends of the bones chopped off; this is, however, not necessary.
2. Put the meat into sufficient boiling water to cover it; when it boils, add a little salt and remove all the scum.
3. Draw the saucepan to the side of the fire, and let the water get so cool that the finger may be borne in it; then simmer very slowly and gently until the meat is done, which will be in about 1-1/2 hour, or rather more, reckoning from the time that it begins to simmer.
4. Serve with turnips and caper sauce, and pour a little of it over the meat.
5. The turnips should be boiled with the mutton; and, when at hand, a few carrots will also be found an improvement.
6. If very large and thick, cut carrots into long thinnish pieces, or they will not be sufficiently done by the time the mutton is ready.
7. Garnish the dish with carrots and turnips placed alternately round the mutton.

title: Boiled Neck of Mutton
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 lbs. of the middle, or best end of the neck of mutton
- salt

## Directions

1. Trim off a portion of the fat, should there be too much
2. If it is to look particularly nice, the chine-bone should be sawn down, the ribs stripped halfway down, and the ends of the bones chopped off
3. Put the meat into sufficient boiling water to cover it
4. When it boils, add a little salt and remove all the scum
5. Draw the saucepan to the side of the fire, and let the water get so cool that the finger may be borne in it
6. Simmer very slowly and gently

title: Boiled Parsnips
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Parsnips
- 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt to each gallon of water

## Directions

1. Wash the parsnips, scrape them thoroughly, and, with the point of the knife, remove any black specks about them, and, should they be very large, cut the thick part into quarters
2. Put them into a saucepan of boiling water salted in the above proportion
3. Boil them rapidly until tender, which may be ascertained by thrusting a fork in them
4. Take them up, drain them, and serve in a vegetable-dish

title: Boiled Perch
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of salt to each gallon of water
- parsley
- plain melted butter or Dutch sauce

## Directions

1. Scale the fish, take out the gills and clean it thoroughly.
2. Lay it in boiling water, salted as above, and simmer gently for 10 minutes.
3. If the fish is very large, longer time must be allowed.
4. Garnish with parsley, and serve with plain melted butter, or Dutch sauce.

title: Boiled Pike
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of salt to each gallon of water
- a little vinegar

## Directions

1. Scale and clean the pike, and fasten the tail in its mouth by means of a skewer.
2. Lay it in cold water, and when it boils, throw in the salt and vinegar.
3. The time for boiling depends on the size of the fish; a middling-sized pike will take about 1/2 an hour.
4. Serve with Dutch or anchovy sauce, and plain melted butter.

title: Boiled Rabbit
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Rabbit
- water

## Directions

1. For boiling, choose rabbits with smooth and sharp claws, as that denotes they are young: should these be blunt and rugged, the ears dry and tough, the animal is old.
2. After emptying and skinning it, wash it well in cold water, and let it soak for about 1/4 hour in warm water, to draw out the blood.
3. Bring the head round to the side, and fasten it there by means of a skewer run through that and the body.
4. Put the rabbit into sufficient hot water to cover it, let it boil very gently until tender, which will be in from 1/2 to 3/4 hour, according to its size and age.
5. Dish it, and smother it either with onion, mushroom, or liver sauce, or parsley-and-butter; the former is, however, generally preferred to any of the last-named sauces.
6. When liver-sauce is preferred, the liver should be boiled for a few minutes, and minced very finely, or rubbed through a sieve before it is added to the sauce.

title: Boiled Raisin Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of flour
- 1/2 lb. of stoned raisins
- 1/2 lb. of chopped suet
- 1/2 saltspoonful of salt
- milk

## Directions

1. Stone the raisins and chop the suet finely
2. Mix them with the flour, add the salt, and when these dry ingredients are thoroughly mixed, moisten the pudding with sufficient milk to make it into rather a stiff paste
3. Tie it up in a floured cloth, put it into boiling water, and boil for 4 hours
4. Serve with sifted sugar

title: Boiled Rhubarb Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 or 5 sticks of fine rhubarb
- 1/4 lb. of moist sugar
- 3/4 lb. of suet-crust No. 1215

## Directions

1. Make a suet-crust with 3/4 lb. of flour, by recipe No. 1215, and line a buttered basin with it.
2. Wash and wipe the rhubarb, and, if old, string it--that is to say, pare off the outside skin.
3. Cut it into inch lengths, fill the basin with it, put in the sugar, and cover with crust.
4. Pinch the edges of the pudding together, tie over it a floured cloth, put it into boiling water, and boil from 2 to 2-1/2 hours.
5. Turn it out of the basin, and serve with a jug of cream and sifted sugar.

title: Boiled Rice for Curries
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 lb. of rice
- water
- salt

## Directions

1. Pick, wash, and soak the rice in plenty of cold water
2. Have ready a saucepan of boiling water, drop the rice into it, and keep it boiling quickly, with the lid uncovered, until it is tender, but not soft
3. Take it up, drain it, and put it on a dish before the fire to dry
4. Do not handle it much with a spoon, but shake it about a little with two forks, that it may all be equally dried, and strew over a little salt
5. It may be heaped lightly on a dish by itself, or be laid round the dish as a border, with a curry or fricassee in the centre

title: Boiled Rice Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of rice
- 1-1/2 pint of new milk
- 2 oz. of butter
- 4 eggs
- 1/2 saltspoonful of salt
- 4 large tablespoonfuls of moist sugar
- flavouring to taste

## Directions

1. Stew the rice very gently in the above proportion of new milk, and, when it is tender, pour it into a basin
2. Stir in the butter, and let it stand to cool
3. Beat the eggs, add these to the rice with the sugar, salt, and any flavouring that may be approved, such as nutmeg, powdered cinnamon, grated lemon-peel, essence of bitter almonds, or vanilla
4. When all is well stirred, put the pudding into a buttered basin, tie it down with a cloth, plunge it into boiling water, and boil for 1-1/4 hour

title: Boiled Rice Pudding with Dried or Fresh Fruit
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of rice
- 1 pint of any kind of fresh fruit that may be preferred, or 1/2 lb. of raisins or currants

## Directions

1. Wash the rice, tie it in a cloth, allowing room for it to swell, and put it into a saucepan of cold water
2. Let it boil for an hour, then take it up, untie the cloth, stir in the fruit, and tie it up again tolerably tight, and put it into the water for the remainder of the time
3. Boil for another hour, or rather longer, and serve with sweet

title: Boiled Round of Beef
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Beef (12 to 16 lbs. from the silver side)
- Water
- Carrots
- Turnips
- Parsnips
- Suet dumplings (optional)

## Directions

1. Take from 12 to 16 lbs. of silver side of the round after it has been in salt about 10 days.
2. Just wash off the salt, skewer it up in a nice round-looking form, and bind it with tape to keep the skewers in their places.
3. Put it in a saucepan of boiling water, set it upon a good fire, and when it begins to boil, carefully remove all scum from the surface.
4. When it is well skimmed, draw the pot to the corner of the fire, and let it simmer very gently until done.
5. Remove the tape and skewers, which should be replaced by a silver one.
6. Pour over a little of the pot-liquor, and garnish with carrots.
7. Boil for about 3 hours after the water boils.

title: Boiled Salad
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 heads of celery
- 1 pint of French beans
- lettuce
- endive

## Directions

1. Boil the celery and beans separately until tender
2. Cut the celery into pieces about 2 inches long
3. Put these into a salad-bowl or dish
4. Pour over either of the sauces No. 506, 507, or 508
5. Garnish the dish with a little lettuce finely chopped, blanched endive, or a few tufts of boiled cauliflower

title: Boiled Salmon
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of salt to each gallon of water
- sufficient water to cover the fish

## Directions

1. Scale and clean the fish, and be particular that no blood is left inside.
2. Lay it in the fish-kettle with sufficient cold water to cover it, adding salt in the above proportion.
3. Bring it quickly to a boil, take off all the scum, and let it simmer gently till the fish is done, which will be when the meat separates easily from the bone.
4. Drain it, and if not wanted for a few minutes, keep it warm by means of warm cloths laid over it.
5. Serve on a hot napkin, garnish with cut lemon and parsley, and send lobster or shrimp sauce, and plain melted butter to table with it.

title: Boiled Sea-Kale
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Sea-kale
- water
- salt

## Directions

1. Well wash the kale
2. Cut away any wormeaten pieces
3. Tie it into small bunches
4. Put it into boiling water, salted in the above proportion
5. Let it boil quickly until tender
6. Take it out and drain
7. Untie the bunches
8. Serve with plain melted butter or white sauce, a little of which may be poured over the kale

title: Boiled Tongue
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 tongue
- a bunch of savoury herbs
- water

## Directions

1. In choosing a tongue, ascertain how long it has been dried or pickled, and select one with a smooth skin
2. If a dried one and rather hard, soak it at least for 12 hours previous to cooking it; if fresh from the pickle, 2 or 3 hours will be sufficient
3. Put the tongue in a stewpan with plenty of cold water and a bunch of savoury herbs
4. Let it gradually come to a boil, skim well and simmer very gently until tender
5. Peel off the skin, garnish with tufts of cauliflowers or Brussels sprouts, and serve

title: Boiled Turbot
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of salt to each gallon of water
- a middling-sized turbot
- lemon
- lobster spawn
- parsley
- lobster or shrimp sauce
- plain melted butter

## Directions

1. Choose a middling-sized turbot; for they are invariably the most valuable: if very large, the meat will be tough and thready.
2. Three or four hours before dressing, soak the fish in salt and water to take off the slime; then thoroughly cleanse it, and with a knife make an incision down the middle of the back, to prevent the skin of the belly from cracking.
3. Rub it over with lemon, and be particular not to cut off the fins.
4. Lay the fish in a very clean turbot-kettle, with sufficient cold water to cover it, and salt in the above proportion.
5. Let it gradually come to a boil, and skim very carefully; keep it gently simmering, and on no account let it boil fast, as the fish would have a very unsightly appearance.
6. When the meat separates easily from the bone, it is done; then take it out, let it drain well, and dish it on a hot napkin.
7. Rub a little lobster spawn through a sieve, sprinkle it over the fish, and garnish with tufts of parsley and cut lemon.
8. Lobster or shrimp sauce, and plain melted butter, should be sent to table with it.

title: Boiled Turkey
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- turkey
- hot water

## Directions

1. Put the turkey into sufficient hot water to cover it
2. Let it come to a boil, then carefully remove all the scum
3. Let it simmer very gently for about 1-1/2 hour to 1-3/4 hour, according to the size
4. Serve with either white, celery, oyster, or mushroom sauce, or parsley-and-butter, a little of which should be poured over the turkey
5. Boiled ham, bacon, tongue, or pickled pork should always accompany this dish
6. When oyster sauce is served, the turkey should be stuffed with oyster forcemeat

title: Boiled Turkey
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Turkey
- forcemeat No. 417

## Directions

1. Pluck the bird, carefully draw, and singe it with a piece of white paper
2. Wash it inside and out, and wipe it thoroughly dry with a cloth
3. Cut off the head and neck, draw the strings or sinews of the thighs, and cut off the legs at the first joint
4. Draw the legs into the body, fill the breast with forcemeat made by recipe No. 417
5. Run a skewer through the wing and the middle joint of the leg, quite into the leg and wing on the opposite side
6. Break the breastbone, and make the bird look as round and as compact as possible
7. Put the turkey into sufficient hot water to cover it
8. Let it come to a boil, then carefully remove all the scum
9. Let it simmer very gently for about 1-1/2 hour to 1-3/4 hour, according to the size
10. Serve with either white, celery, oyster, or mushroom sauce, or parsley-and-butter, a little of which should be poured over the turkey
11. Accompany with boiled ham, bacon, tongue, or pickled pork
12. If oyster sauce is served, the turkey should be stuffed with oyster forcemeat

title: Boiled Turnip Greens
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 gallon of water
- 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- turnip-greens

## Directions

1. Wash the greens well in two or three waters, and pick off all the decayed and dead leaves
2. Tie them in small bunches, and put them into plenty of boiling water, salted in the above proportion
3. Keep them boiling quickly, with the lid of the saucepan uncovered
4. When tender, pour them into a colander
5. Let them drain, arrange them in a vegetable-dish, remove the string that the greens were tied with, and serve

title: Boiled Turnips
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Turnips
- to each 1/2 gallon of water allow 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt

## Directions

1. Pare the turnips, and, should they be very large, divide them into quarters; but, unless this is the case, let them be cooked whole.
2. Put them into a saucepan of boiling water, salted in the above proportion, and let them boil gently until tender.
3. Try them with a fork, and, when done, take them up in a colander; let them thoroughly drain, and serve.

title: Boiled Vegetable Marrow
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 gallon of water
- 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- vegetable marrows
- melted butter or salt butter
- toast

## Directions

1. Have ready a saucepan of boiling water, salted in the above proportion
2. Put in the marrows after peeling them, and boil them until quite tender
3. Take them up with a slice, halve, and, should they be very large, quarter them
4. Dish them on toast, and send to table with them a tureen of melted butter, or, in lieu of this, a small pat of salt butter

title: Boned Leg of Mutton Stuffed
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A small leg of mutton, weighing 6 or 7 lbs.
- forcemeat, No. 417
- 2 shalots finely minced

## Directions

1. Make a forcemeat by recipe No. 417, to which add 2 finely-minced shalots.
2. Bone the leg of mutton, without spoiling the skin, and cut off a great deal of the fat.
3. Fill the hole up whence the bone was taken, with the forcemeat, and sew it up underneath, to prevent its falling out.
4. Bind and tie it up compactly, and roast it before a nice clear fire for about 2-1/2 hours or rather longer.
5. Remove the tape and send it to table with a good gravy. It may be glazed or not, as preferred.

title: Boot Polish
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- ivory-black 4 oz.
- treacle 4 oz.
- sulphuric acid 1 oz.
- best olive-oil 2 spoonfuls
- best white-wine vinegar 3 half-pints

## Directions

1. Mix the ivory-black and treacle well in an earthen jar
2. Add the sulphuric acid, continuing to stir the mixture
3. Pour in the oil
4. Add the vinegar, stirring it in by degrees, until thoroughly incorporated

title: Boot Polish with Ivory-Black and Treacle
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- ivory-black 4 oz.
- treacle 4 oz.
- sulphuric acid 1 oz.
- best olive-oil 2 spoonfuls
- best white-wine vinegar 3 half-pints

## Directions

1. Mix the ivory-black and treacle well in an earthen jar
2. Add the sulphuric acid, continuing to stir the mixture
3. Pour in the oil
4. Add the vinegar, stirring it in by degrees, until thoroughly incorporated

title: Boudin à la Reine
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of cold roast fowls
- 1 pint of Béchamel No. 367
- salt and cayenne to taste
- egg and bread crumbs
- flour
- stock

## Directions

1. Take the breasts and nice white meat from the fowls; cut it into small dice of an equal size, and throw them into some good Béchamel, made by recipe No. 367
2. Season with salt and cayenne, and put the mixture into a dish to cool
3. When this preparation is quite cold, cut it into 2 equal parts, which should be made into boudins of a long shape, the size of the dish they are intended to be served on
4. Roll them in flour, egg and bread-crumb them, and be careful that the ends are well covered with the crumbs, otherwise they would break in the frying-pan
5. Fry them a nice colour, put them before the fire to drain the greasy moisture from them
6. Serve with the remainder of the Béchamel poured round; this should be thinned with a little stock

title: Braised Fillet of Mutton with French Beans
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The chump end of a loin of mutton
- buttered paper
- French beans
- a little glaze
- 1 pint of gravy

## Directions

1. Roll up the mutton in a piece of buttered paper, roast it for 2 hours, and do not allow it to acquire the least colour.
2. Have ready some French beans, boiled, and drained on a sieve.
3. Remove the paper from the mutton, glaze it.
4. Just heat up the beans in the gravy, and lay them on the dish with the meat over them.
5. The remainder of the gravy may be strained, and sent to table in a tureen.

title: Braised Leg of Mutton
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 small leg of mutton
- 4 carrots
- 3 onions
- 1 faggot of savoury herbs
- a bunch of parsley
- seasoning to taste of pepper and salt
- a few slices of bacon
- a few veal trimmings
- 1/2 pint of gravy or water

## Directions

1. Line the bottom of a braising-pan with a few slices of bacon, put in the carrots, onions, herbs, parsley, and seasoning, and over these place the mutton.
2. Cover the whole with a few more slices of bacon and the veal trimmings, pour in the gravy or water, and stew very gently for 4 hours.
3. Strain the gravy, reduce it to a glaze over a sharp fire, glaze the mutton with it, and send it to table, placed on a dish of white haricot beans boiled tender, or garnished with glazed onions.

title: Braised Loin of Lamb
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 loin of lamb
- a few slices of bacon
- 1 bunch of green onions
- 5 or 6 young carrots
- a bunch of savoury herbs
- 2 blades of pounded mace
- 1 pint of stock
- salt to taste

## Directions

1. Bone a loin of lamb, and line the bottom of a stewpan just capable of holding it, with a few thin slices of fat bacon
2. Add the remaining ingredients, cover the meat with a few more slices of bacon, pour in the stock, and simmer very gently for 2 hours
3. Take it up, dry it, strain and reduce the gravy to a glaze, with which glaze the meat, and serve it either on stewed peas, spinach, or stewed cucumbers

title: Braised Neck of Veal
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The best end of the neck of veal (from 3 to 4 lbs.)
- bacon
- 1 tablespoonful of minced parsley
- salt, pepper, and grated nutmeg to taste
- 1 onion
- 2 carrots
- a little celery (when this is not obtainable, use the seed)
- 1/2 glass of sherry
- thickening of butter and flour
- lemon-juice
- 1 blade of pounded mace

## Directions

1. Prepare the bacon for larding, and roll it in minced parsley, salt, pepper, and grated nutmeg; lard the veal, put it into a stewpan with a few slices of lean bacon or ham, an onion, carrots, and celery; and do not quite cover it with water.
2. Stew it gently for 2 hours, or until it is quite tender; strain off the liquor; stir together over the fire, in a stewpan, a little flour and butter until brown; lay the veal in this, the upper side to the bottom of the pan, and let it remain till of a nice brown colour.
3. Place it in the dish; pour into the stewpan as much gravy as is required, boil it up, skim well, add the wine, pounded mace, and lemon-juice; simmer for 3 minutes, pour it over the meat, and serve.

title: Bread and Butter Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- butter
- raisins, stoned
- bread
- eggs
- milk
- sugar
- grated nutmeg

## Directions

1. Butter a pudding-basin, and line the inside with a layer of raisins that have been previously stoned
2. Nearly fill the basin with slices of bread and butter with the crust cut off
3. Beat the eggs; add to them the milk, sugar, and grated nutmeg
4. Mix all well together, and pour the whole on to the bread and butter
5. Let it stand 1/2 hour
6. Tie a floured cloth over it
7. Boil for 1 hour, and serve with sweet sauce
8. Care must be taken that the basin is quite full before the cloth is tied over

title: Bread Sauce I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of milk
- 3/4 of the crumb of a stale loaf
- 1 onion
- pounded mace to taste
- cayenne to taste
- salt to taste
- 1 oz. of butter

## Directions

1. Peel and quarter the onion, and simmer it in the milk till perfectly tender.
2. Break the bread, which should be stale, into small pieces, carefully picking out any hard outside pieces; put it in a very clean saucepan, strain the milk over it, cover it up, and let it remain for an hour to soak.
3. Now beat it up with a fork very smoothly, add a seasoning of pounded mace, cayenne, and salt, with 1 oz. of butter; give the whole one boil, and serve.
4. To enrich this sauce, a small quantity of cream may be added just before sending it to table.

title: Bread Sauce II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Giblets of poultry
- 3/4 lb. of the crumb of a stale loaf
- 1 onion
- 12 whole peppers
- 1 blade of mace
- salt to taste
- 2 tablespoonfuls of cream or melted butter
- 1 pint of water

## Directions

1. Put the giblets, with the head, neck, legs, &c., into a stewpan; add the onion, pepper, mace, salt, and rather more than 1 pint of water.
2. Let this simmer for an hour, when strain the liquor over the bread, which should be previously grated or broken into small pieces.
3. Cover up the saucepan, and leave it for an hour by the side of the fire; then beat the sauce up with a fork until no lumps remain, and the whole is nice and smooth.
4. Let it boil for 3 or 4 minutes; keep stirring it until it is rather thick; when add 3 tablespoonfuls of good melted butter or cream, and serve very hot.

title: Bread Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of bread crusts
- 2 oz. butter
- 1 quart of common stock
- salt

## Directions

1. Boil the bread crusts in the stock with the butter
2. Beat the whole with a spoon
3. Keep it boiling till the bread and stock are well mixed
4. Season with a little salt

title: Bread-and-Butter Fritters
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Batter
- 8 slices of bread and butter
- 3 or 4 tablespoonfuls of jam

## Directions

1. Make a batter, the same as for apple fritters No. 1393
2. Cut some slices of bread and butter, not very thick
3. Spread half of them with any jam that may be preferred, and cover with the other slices
4. Slightly press them together, and cut them out in square, long, or round pieces
5. Dip them in the batter, and fry in boiling lard for about 10 minutes
6. Drain them before the fire on a piece of blotting-paper or cloth
7. Dish them, sprinkle over sifted sugar, and serve

title: Breast of Lamb and Green Peas
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 breast of lamb
- a few slices of bacon
- 1/4 pint of stock No. 105
- 1 lemon
- 1 onion
- 1 bunch of savoury herbs
- green peas

## Directions

1. Remove the skin from a breast of lamb, put it into a saucepan of boiling water, and let it simmer for 5 minutes
2. Take it out and lay it in cold water
3. Line the bottom of a stewpan with a few thin slices of bacon; lay the lamb on these
4. peel the lemon, cut it into slices, and put these on the meat, to keep it white and make it tender
5. cover with 1 or 2 more slices of bacon
6. add the stock, onion, and herbs, and set it on a slow fire to simmer very gently until tender
7. Have ready some green peas, put these on a dish, and place the lamb on the top of these
8. The appearance of this dish may be much improved by glazing the lamb, and spinach may be substituted for the peas when variety is desired

title: Brill
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- brill
- 1/4 lb. of salt to each gallon of water
- a little vinegar
- lemon-juice
- cut lemon
- parsley
- horseradish
- lobster coral
- lobster or shrimp sauce
- plain melted butter

## Directions

1. Clean the brill and cut off the fins
2. Rub it over with a little lemon-juice to preserve its whiteness
3. Set the fish in sufficient cold water to cover it
4. Throw in salt in the proportion of 1/4 lb. to each gallon and a little vinegar
5. Bring it gradually to boil
6. Simmer very gently till the fish is done, about 10 minutes depending on size
7. Serve it on a hot napkin
8. Garnish with cut lemon, parsley, horseradish, and a little lobster coral sprinkled over the fish
9. Send lobster or shrimp sauce and plain melted butter to table with it

title: Brilla Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 lbs. of shin of beef
- 3 carrots
- 2 turnips
- a large sprig of thyme
- 2 onions
- 1 head of celery
- salt and pepper to taste
- 4 quarts water

## Directions

1. Take the beef, cut off all the meat from the bone, in nice square pieces, and boil the bone for 4 hours
2. Strain the liquor, let it cool, and take off the fat
3. Put the pieces of meat in the cold liquor
4. Cut small the carrots, turnips, and celery; chop the onions, add them with the thyme and seasoning, and simmer till the meat is tender
5. If not brown enough, colour it with browning

title: Brillat Savarin's Fondue
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Eggs
- Gruyère cheese
- Butter
- Pepper
- Salt

## Directions

1. Take the same number of eggs as there are guests; weigh the eggs in the shell, allow a third of their weight in Gruyère cheese, and a piece of butter one-sixth of the weight of the cheese.
2. Break the eggs into a basin, beat them well; add the cheese, which should be grated, and the butter, which should be broken into small pieces.
3. Stir these ingredients together with a wooden spoon; put the mixture into a lined saucepan, place it over the fire, and stir until the substance is thick and soft.
4. Put in a little salt, according to the age of the cheese, and a good sprinkling of pepper, and serve the fondue on a very hot silver or metal plate.
5. Do not allow the fondue to remain on the fire after the mixture is set, as, if it boils, it will be entirely spoiled.

title: Brillat Savarin's Recipe for Roast Pheasant, a la Sainte Alliance
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- pheasant
- two snipes
- beef
- lard
- truffles
- pepper and salt to taste
- bread
- liver
- anchovy
- fresh butter
- Florida oranges

## Directions

1. Pluck the pheasant
2. Draw the two snipes, putting the bodies on one plate, and the livers, &c., on another
3. Take off the flesh, and mince it finely with a little beef, lard, a few truffles, pepper and salt to taste
4. Stuff the pheasant carefully with this mixture
5. Cut a slice of bread, larger considerably than the bird
6. Cover it with the liver, &c., and a few truffles
7. Add an anchovy and a little fresh butter to these
8. Put the bread, &c., into the dripping-pan
9. Roast the bird
10. Place it on the preparation
11. Surround it with Florida oranges

title: Brisket of Beef, a la Flamande
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- About 6 or 8 lbs. of the brisket of beef
- 4 or 5 slices of bacon
- 2 carrots
- 1 onion
- a bunch of savoury herbs
- salt and pepper to taste
- 4 cloves
- 4 whole allspice
- 2 blades of mace
- weak stock or water

## Directions

1. Choose that portion of the brisket which contains the gristle, trim it, and put it into a stewpan with the slices of bacon, which should be put under and over the meat
2. Add the vegetables, herbs, spices, and seasoning, and cover with a little weak stock or water
3. Close the stewpan as hermetically as possible, and simmer very gently for 4 hours
4. Strain the liquor, reserve a portion of it for sauce, and the remainder boil quickly over a sharp fire until reduced to a glaze, with which glaze the meat
5. Garnish the dish with scooped carrots and turnips, and when liked, a little cabbage; all of which must be cooked separately
6. Thicken and flavour the liquor that was saved for sauce, pour it round the meat, and serve

title: Broad Beans a la Poulette
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 pints of broad beans
- 1/2 pint of stock or broth
- a small bunch of savoury herbs, including parsley
- a small lump of sugar
- yolk of 1 egg
- 1/4 pint of cream
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Procure some young and freshly-gathered beans and shell sufficient to make 2 pints
2. Boil them until nearly done
3. Drain them and put them into a stewpan with the stock, finely-minced herbs, and sugar
4. Stew the beans until perfectly tender and the liquor has dried away a little
5. Beat up the yolk of an egg with the cream and add this to the beans
6. Let the whole get thoroughly hot and when on the point of simmering, serve

title: Broiled Beef and Mushroom Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 or 3 dozen small button mushrooms
- 1 oz. of butter
- Salt and cayenne to taste
- 1 tablespoonful of mushroom ketchup
- Mashed potatoes
- Slices of cold roast beef

## Directions

1. Wipe the mushrooms free from grit with a piece of flannel, and salt
2. Put them in a stewpan with the butter, seasoning, and ketchup
3. Stir over the fire until the mushrooms are quite done
4. Pour it in the middle of mashed potatoes, browned
5. Place round the potatoes slices of cold roast beef, nicely broiled over a clear fire

title: Broiled Beef and Oyster Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 dozen oysters
- 3 cloves
- 1 blade of mace
- 2 oz. of butter
- 1/2 teaspoonful of flour
- Cayenne and salt to taste
- Mashed potatoes
- A few slices of cold roast beef

## Directions

1. Put the oysters in a stewpan, with their liquor strained
2. Add the cloves, mace, butter, flour, and seasoning
3. Let them simmer gently for 5 minutes
4. Have ready in the centre of a dish round walls of mashed potatoes, browned
5. Into the middle pour the oyster sauce, quite hot
6. Round the potatoes place, in layers, slices of the beef, which should be previously broiled over a nice clear fire

title: Broiled Beef-Bones
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The bones of ribs or sirloin
- Salt, pepper, and cayenne

## Directions

1. Separate the bones, taking care that the meat on them is not too thick in any part
2. Sprinkle them well with salt, pepper, and cayenne
3. Broil over a very clear fire
4. When nicely browned they are done; but do not allow them to blacken

title: Broiled Beef-Steaks or Rump-Steaks
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Steaks
- A piece of butter the size of a walnut
- Salt to taste
- 1 tablespoonful of good mushroom ketchup or Harvey's sauce

## Directions

1. Ensure the fire is bright and clear, and perfectly free from smoke, and do not add any fresh fuel just before you require to use the gridiron
2. Sprinkle a little salt over the fire and put on the gridiron for a few minutes to get thoroughly hot through
3. Rub it with a piece of fresh suet to prevent the meat from sticking
4. Lay on the steaks, which should be cut of an equal thickness, about 3/4 of an inch, or rather thinner
5. Level them by beating them as little as possible with a rolling-pin
6. Turn them frequently with steak-tongs (if these are not at hand, stick a fork in the edge of the fat, that no gravy escapes)
7. Cook for from 8 to 10 minutes until done
8. Have ready a very hot dish, into which put the ketchup, and, when liked, a little minced shalot
9. Dish up the steaks, rub them over with butter, and season with pepper and salt
10. Garnish with scraped horseradish, or slices of cucumber

title: Broiled Fowl and Mushroom Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A large fowl
- pepper and salt to taste
- 2 handfuls of button mushrooms
- 1 slice of lean ham
- 3/4 pint of thickened gravy
- 1 teaspoonful of lemon-juice
- 1/2 teaspoonful of pounded sugar
- butter

## Directions

1. Cut the fowl into quarters, roast it until three-parts done, and keep it well basted whilst at the fire
2. Take the fowl up, broil it for a few minutes over a clear fire, and season it with pepper and salt
3. Put the mushrooms into a stewpan with a small piece of butter, the ham, a seasoning of pepper and salt, and the gravy; simmer these gently for 1/2 hour
4. Add the lemon-juice and sugar
5. Dish the fowl, and pour the sauce round them

title: Broiled Hare (a Supper or Luncheon Dish)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The leg and shoulders of a roast hare
- cayenne and salt to taste
- a little butter

## Directions

1. Cut the legs and shoulders of a roast hare, season them highly with salt and cayenne, and broil them over a very clear fire for 5 minutes.
2. Dish them on a hot dish, rub over them a little cold butter, and send to table very quickly.

title: Broiled Kidneys
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Kidneys
- butter
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Skin the kidneys and pass a skewer under the white part of each half to keep them flat
2. Broil over a nice clear fire, placing the inside downwards
3. Turn them when done enough on one side, and cook them on the other
4. Remove the skewers and place the kidneys on a very hot dish
5. Season with pepper and salt, and put a tiny piece of butter in the middle of each
6. Serve very hot and quickly, and send very hot plates to table

title: Broiled Kidneys
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Sheep kidneys
- pepper and salt to taste
- butter

## Directions

1. Ascertain that the kidneys are fresh, and cut them open very evenly, lengthwise, down to the root, but do not separate them.
2. Skin them, and pass a skewer under the white part of each half to keep them flat.
3. Broil over a nice clear fire, placing the inside downwards.
4. Turn them when done enough on one side, and cook them on the other.
5. Remove the skewers, place the kidneys on a very hot dish, season with pepper and salt, and put a tiny piece of butter in the middle of each.
6. Serve very hot and quickly, and send very hot plates to table.

title: Broiled Mackerel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- pepper and salt to taste
- a small quantity of oil

## Directions

1. Mackerel should never be washed when intended to be broiled, but merely wiped very clean and dry, after taking out the gills and insides.
2. Open the back, and put in a little pepper, salt, and oil; broil it over a clear fire, turn it over on both sides, and also on the back.
3. When sufficiently cooked, the flesh can be detached from the bone, which will be in about 15 minutes for a small mackerel.
4. Chop a little parsley, work it up in the butter, with pepper and salt to taste, and a squeeze of lemon-juice, and put it in the back.
5. Serve before the butter is quite melted, with a maître d'hôtel sauce in a tureen.

title: Broiled Mushrooms
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Mushroom-flaps
- pepper and salt to taste
- butter
- lemon-juice

## Directions

1. Cleanse the mushrooms by wiping them with a piece of flannel and a little salt
2. Cut off a portion of the stalk, and peel the tops
3. Broil them over a clear fire, turning them once, and arrange them on a very hot dish
4. Put a small piece of butter on each mushroom, season with pepper and salt, and squeeze over them a few drops of lemon-juice
5. Place the dish before the fire, and when the butter is melted, serve very hot and quickly

title: Broiled Mutton and Tomato Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A few slices of cold mutton
- tomato sauce (No. 529)
- pepper and salt

## Directions

1. Cut some nice slices from a cold leg or shoulder of mutton
2. Season them with pepper and salt
3. Broil over a clear fire
4. Make some tomato sauce by recipe No. 529
5. Pour it over the mutton
6. Serve very hot

title: Broiled Mutton Chops
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Loin of mutton
- pepper and salt
- a small piece of butter

## Directions

1. Cut the chops from a well-hung tender loin of mutton
2. Remove a portion of the fat
3. Trim them into a nice shape
4. Slightly beat and level them
5. Place the gridiron over a bright clear fire
6. Rub the bars with a little fat
7. Lay on the chops
8. Whilst broiling, frequently turn them
9. In about 8 minutes they will be done
10. Season with pepper and salt
11. Dish them on a very hot dish
12. Rub a small piece of butter on each chop
13. Serve very hot and expeditiously

title: Broiled Ox-Tail (an Entree)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 tails
- 1-1/2 pint of stock
- salt and cayenne to taste
- bread crumbs
- 1 egg

## Directions

1. Joint and cut up the tails into convenient-sized pieces, and put them into a stewpan, with the stock, cayenne, and salt
2. Let them simmer gently for about 2-1/2 hours
3. Take them out, drain them, and let them cool
4. Beat an egg upon a plate; dip in each piece of tail, and afterwards, throw them into a dish of bread crumbs
5. Broil them over a clear fire, until of a brownish colour on both sides, and serve with a good gravy, or any sauce that may be preferred

title: Broiled Partridge (a Luncheon, Breakfast, or Supper Dish)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 partridges
- salt
- cayenne
- a small piece of butter
- brown gravy or mushroom sauce

## Directions

1. Pluck, draw, and cut the partridges in half, and wipe the inside thoroughly with a damp cloth.
2. Season them with salt and cayenne, broil them over a very clear fire, and dish them on a hot dish.
3. Rub a small piece of butter over each half, and send them to table with brown gravy or mushroom sauce.

title: Broiled Pheasant
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pheasant
- a little lard
- egg and bread crumbs
- salt and cayenne to taste
- mushroom-sauce, sauce piquante, or brown gravy
- game-bones and trimmings

## Directions

1. Cut the legs off at the first joint
2. Cut the remainder of the bird into neat pieces
3. Put them into a frying pan with a little lard
4. When browned on both sides, and about half done, take them out and drain them
5. Brush the pieces over with egg
6. Sprinkle with bread crumbs with which has been mixed a good seasoning of cayenne and salt
7. Broil them over a moderate fire for about 10 minutes, or rather longer
8. Serve with mushroom-sauce, sauce piquante, or brown gravy, in which a few game-bones and trimmings have been stewed

title: Broiled Pigeons
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Pigeons
- 3 oz. of butter
- pepper and salt to taste
- mushroom-sauce or good gravy

## Directions

1. Take care that the pigeons are quite fresh, and carefully pluck, draw, and wash them.
2. Split the backs, rub the birds over with butter, season them with pepper and salt.
3. Broil them over a moderate fire for 1/4 hour or 20 minutes.
4. Serve very hot, with either mushroom-sauce or a good gravy.

title: Broiled Veal Cutlets a l'Italienne
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Neck of veal
- salt and pepper to taste
- the yolk of 1 egg
- bread crumbs
- 1/2 pint of Italian sauce No. 453
- clarified butter
- rashers of broiled ham

## Directions

1. Cut the veal into cutlets, flatten and trim them nicely
2. Powder over them a little salt and pepper
3. Brush them over with the yolk of an egg
4. Dip them into bread crumbs, then into clarified butter, and afterwards in the bread crumbs again
5. Broil or fry them over a clear fire, that they may acquire a good brown colour
6. Arrange them in the dish alternately with rashers of broiled ham
7. Pour the sauce, made by recipe No. 453, in the middle

title: Broiled Whiting
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Salt and water
- Flour

## Directions

1. Wash the whiting in salt and water, wipe them thoroughly, and let them remain in the cloth to absorb all moisture
2. Flour them well, and broil over a very clear fire
3. Serve with maître d'hôtel sauce, or plain melted butter
4. Be careful to preserve the liver, as by some it is considered very delicate

title: Brown Apple Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 good-sized apples
- 1/2 pint of brown gravy
- cayenne to taste

## Directions

1. Put the gravy in a stewpan, and add the apples, after having pared, cored, and quartered them.
2. Let them simmer gently till tender; beat them to a pulp, and season with cayenne.

title: Brown Chestnut Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of chestnuts
- 1/2 pint of stock
- 2 lumps of sugar
- 4 tablespoonfuls of Spanish sauce

## Directions

1. Prepare the chestnuts by scalding and peeling them.
2. Put them in a stewpan with the stock and sugar, and simmer them till tender.
3. When done, add Spanish sauce in the above proportion, and rub the whole through a tammy.
4. Keep this sauce rather liquid, as it is liable to thicken.

title: Brown Gravy
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. of butter
- 2 large onions
- 2 lbs. of shin of beef
- 2 small slices of lean bacon (if at hand)
- salt and whole pepper to taste
- 3 cloves
- 2 quarts of water
- 2 oz. of butter for thickening
- 3 oz. of flour

## Directions

1. Put the butter into a stewpan; set this on the fire, throw in the onions cut in rings, and fry them a light brown; then add the beef and bacon, which should be cut into small square pieces; season, and pour in a teacupful of water; let it boil for about ten minutes, or until it is of a nice brown colour, occasionally stirring the contents.
2. Now fill up with water in the above proportion; let it boil up, when draw it to the side of the fire to simmer very gently for 1-1/2 hour; strain, and when cold, take off all the fat.
3. In thickening this gravy, melt 3 oz. of butter in a stewpan, add 2 oz. of flour, and stir till of a light-brown colour; when cold, add it to the strained gravy, and boil it up quickly.
4. This thickening may be made in larger quantities, and kept in a stone jar for use when wanted.

title: Brown Gravy Without Meat
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 large onions
- 1 large carrot
- 2 oz. of butter
- 3 pints of boiling water
- 1 bunch of savoury herbs
- a wineglassful of good beer
- salt and pepper to taste

## Directions

1. Slice, flour, and fry the onions and carrots in the butter until of a nice light-brown colour
2. Add the boiling water and the remaining ingredients
3. Let the whole stew gently for about an hour
4. Strain, and when cold, skim off all the fat
5. Thicken it in the same manner as recipe No. 436, and, if thought necessary, add a few drops of colouring No. 108

title: Brown Mushroom Sauce, to Serve with Roast Meat, &c.
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of button mushrooms
- 1/2 pint of good beef gravy
- 1 tablespoonful of mushroom ketchup
- thickening of butter and flour

## Directions

1. Put the gravy into a saucepan, thicken it, and stir over the fire until it boils
2. Prepare the mushrooms by cutting off the stalks and wiping them free from grit and dirt
3. Put them into the gravy, and let them simmer very gently for about 10 minutes
4. Add the ketchup, and serve

title: Brown Onion Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 large onions
- rather more than 1/2 pint of good gravy
- 2 oz. of butter
- salt and pepper to taste

## Directions

1. Slice and fry the onions of a pale brown in a stewpan, with the above quantity of butter, keeping them well stirred, that they do not get black
2. When a nice colour, pour over the gravy, and let them simmer gently until tender
3. Now skim off every particle of fat, add the seasoning, and rub the whole through a tammy or sieve
4. Put it back in the saucepan to warm, and when it boils, serve

title: Brown Roux, a French Thickening for Gravies and Sauces
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of butter
- 9 oz. of flour

## Directions

1. Melt the butter in a stewpan over a slow fire
2. Dredge in very gradually the flour
3. Stir it till of a light-brown colour, doing it very slowly so the flour will not burn and impart a bitter taste to the sauce it is mixed with
4. Pour it in a jar and keep it for use

title: Brown Stock
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 lb. of good lean ham
- any poultry trimmings
- 3 small onions
- 3 small carrots
- 3 turnips
- 1 head of celery
- a few chopped mushrooms
- 1 tomato
- a bunch of savoury herbs
- parsley
- 1-1/2 oz. of salt
- 12 white peppercorns
- 6 cloves
- 3 small blades of mace
- 4 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Line a delicately clean stewpan with the ham cut in thin broad slices, carefully trimming off all its rusty fat
2. Cut up the beef and veal in pieces about 3 inches square, and lay them on the ham
3. Set it on the stove, and draw it down, and stir frequently
4. When the meat is equally browned, put in the beef and veal bones, the poultry trimmings, and pour in the cold water
5. Skim well, and occasionally add a little cold water, to stop its boiling, until it becomes quite clear
6. Put in all the other ingredients, and simmer very slowly for 5 hours
7. Do not let it come to a brisk boil, that the stock be not wasted, and that its colour may be preserved
8. Strain through a very fine hair sieve, or tammy, and it will be fit for use

title: Brown-Bread Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 lb. of brown-bread crumbs
- 1/2 lb. of currants
- 1/2 lb. of suet
- 1/4 lb. of moist sugar
- 4 eggs
- 2 tablespoonfuls of brandy
- 2 tablespoonfuls of cream
- grated nutmeg to taste

## Directions

1. Grate 3/4 lb. of crumbs from a stale brown loaf
2. Add to these the currants and suet, and be particular that the latter is finely chopped
3. Put in the remaining ingredients
4. Beat the pudding well for a few minutes
5. Put it into a buttered basin or mould
6. Tie it down tightly, and boil for nearly 4 hours
7. Send sweet sauce to table with it

title: Browning for Stock
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. of powdered sugar
- 1/2 a pint of water

## Directions

1. Place the sugar in a stewpan over a slow fire until it begins to melt, keeping it stirred with a wooden spoon until it becomes black
2. Then add the water, and let it dissolve
3. Cork closely, and use a few drops when required

title: Brunswick Black
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of common asphaltum
- 1/2 pint of linseed oil
- 1 quart of oil of turpentine

## Directions

1. Melt the asphaltum, and add gradually to it the other two ingredients.
2. Apply this with a small painter's brush, and leave it to become perfectly dry.
3. The grate will need no other cleaning, but will merely require dusting every day, and occasionally brushing with a dry black-lead brush.
4. When fires are required, the bars, cheeks, and back of the grate will need black-leading in the usual manner.

title: Bubble-and-Squeak
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A few thin slices of cold boiled beef
- Butter
- Cabbage
- 1 sliced onion
- Pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Fry the slices of beef gently in a little butter, taking care not to dry them up
2. Lay them on a flat dish
3. Cover with fried greens
4. The greens may be prepared from cabbage sprouts or green savoys
5. Boil them till tender
6. Well drain them
7. Mince them and place over the beef

title: Bubble-and-Squeak (Cold Meat Cookery)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A few thin slices of cold boiled beef
- butter
- cabbage
- 1 sliced onion
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Fry the slices of beef gently in a little butter, taking care not to dry them up.
2. Lay them on a flat dish, and cover with fried greens.
3. The greens may be prepared from cabbage sprouts or green savoys.
4. They should be boiled till tender, well drained, minced, and placed, till quite hot, in a frying-pan, with butter, a sliced onion, and seasoning of pepper and salt.
5. When the onion is done, it is ready to serve.

title: Burnt Onions for Gravies
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of onions
- 1/3 pint of water
- 1/2 lb. of moist sugar
- 1/3 pint of vinegar

## Directions

1. Peel and chop the onions fine, and put them into a stewpan (not tinned), with the water
2. Let them boil for 5 minutes, then add the sugar, and simmer gently until the mixture becomes nearly black and throws out bubbles of smoke
3. Have ready the above proportion of boiling vinegar, strain the liquor gradually to it, and keep stirring with a wooden spoon until it is well incorporated
4. When cold, bottle for use

title: Butter Crust for Boiled Puddings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb flour
- 6 oz butter
- 1/2 pint water

## Directions

1. With a knife, work the flour to a smooth paste with 1/2 pint of water
2. Roll the crust out rather thin
3. Place the butter over it in small pieces
4. Dredge lightly over it some flour
5. Fold the paste over
6. Repeat the rolling once more, and the crust will be ready for use

title: Buttered Apples
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Apple marmalade No. 1395
- 6 or 7 good boiling apples
- 1/2 pint of water
- 6 oz. of sugar
- 2 oz. of butter
- a little apricot jam
- sifted sugar

## Directions

1. Pare the apples and take out the cores without dividing them
2. Boil up the sugar and water for a few minutes
3. Lay in the apples and simmer them very gently until tender, taking care not to let them break
4. Have ready sufficient marmalade made by recipe No. 1395 and flavoured with lemon to cover the bottom of the dish
5. Arrange the apples on this with a piece of butter placed in each
6. Place a few spoonfuls of apricot jam or marmalade in between them
7. Place the dish in the oven for 10 minutes
8. Sprinkle over the top sifted sugar
9. Either brown it before the fire or with a salamander and serve hot

title: Buttered Eggs
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 new-laid eggs
- 2 oz. of butter

## Directions

1. Procure the eggs new-laid if possible; break them into a basin, and beat them well.
2. Put the butter into another basin, which place in boiling water, and stir till the butter is melted.
3. Pour the butter and the eggs into a lined saucepan.
4. Hold it over a gentle fire, and, as the mixture begins to warm, pour it two or three times into the basin, and back again, that the two ingredients may be well incorporated.
5. Keep stirring the eggs and butter one way until they are hot, without boiling.
6. Serve on hot buttered toast.
7. If the mixture is allowed to boil, it will curdle, and so be entirely spoiled.

title: Buttered Prawns or Shrimps
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of picked prawns or shrimps
- 3/4 pint of stock No. 104
- thickening of butter and flour
- salt, cayenne, and nutmeg to taste

## Directions

1. Pick the prawns or shrimps, and put them in a stewpan with the stock
2. Add a thickening of butter and flour
3. Season, and simmer gently for 3 minutes
4. Serve on a dish garnished with fried bread or toasted sippets

title: Buttered Rice
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of rice
- 1-1/2 pint of milk
- 2 oz. of butter
- sugar to taste
- grated nutmeg or pounded cinnamon

## Directions

1. Wash and pick the rice, drain and put it into a saucepan with the milk
2. Let it swell gradually, and, when tender, pour off the milk
3. Stir in the butter, sugar, and nutmeg or cinnamon
4. When the butter is thoroughly melted, and the whole is quite hot, serve
5. After the milk is poured off, be particular that the rice does not burn: to prevent this, do not cease stirring it

title: Cabbage Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 large cabbage
- 3 carrots
- 2 onions
- 4 or 5 slices of lean bacon
- salt and pepper to taste
- 2 quarts of medium stock No. 105

## Directions

1. Scald the cabbage, exit it up and drain it.
2. Line the stewpan with the bacon, put in the cabbage, carrots, and onions; moisten with skimmings from the stock, and simmer very gently, till the cabbage is tender.
3. Add the stock, stew softly for half an hour, and carefully skim off every particle of fat.
4. Season and serve.

title: Cabinet or Chancellor's Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 oz. of candied peel
- 4 oz. of currants
- 4 dozen sultanas
- a few slices of Savoy cake
- sponge cake
- a French roll
- 4 eggs
- 1 pint of milk
- grated lemon-rind
- 1/4 nutmeg
- 3 table-spoonfuls of sugar
- butter

## Directions

1. Melt some butter to a paste, and with it, well grease the mould or basin in which the pudding is to be boiled, taking care that it is buttered in every part
2. Cut the peel into thin slices, and place these in a fanciful device at the bottom of the mould, and fill in the spaces between with currants and sultanas
3. Add a few slices of sponge cake or French roll
4. Drop a few drops of melted butter on these, and between each layer sprinkle a few currants
5. Proceed in this manner until the mould is nearly full
6. Flavour the milk with nutmeg and grated lemon-rind; add the sugar, and stir to this the eggs, which should be well beaten
7. Beat this mixture for a few minutes; then strain it into the mould, which should be quite full
8. Tie a piece of buttered paper over it, and let it stand for 2 hours
9. Tie it down with a cloth, put it into boiling water, and let it boil slowly for 1 hour
10. Let it stand for a minute or two before the cloth is removed; then quickly turn it out of the mould or basin, and serve with sweet sauce separately
11. The flavouring of this pudding may be varied by substituting for the lemon-rind essence of vanilla or bitter almonds; and it may be made much richer by using cream

title: Cafe Au Lait
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 tablespoonfuls of strong coffee
- a breakfast-cupful of good hot milk
- a little cream

## Directions

1. Add 6 tablespoonfuls of strong coffee to a large proportion of good hot milk
2. If cream is available and the milk cannot be depended on for richness, mix a little cream with the milk to improve the taste

title: Cafe Noir
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- very strong coffee
- a little brandy or liqueurs

## Directions

1. Make the coffee very strong
2. Serve in very small cups, well sweetened, with the addition of a little brandy or liqueurs, which may be added or not at pleasure
3. Never mix with milk or cream
4. Alternatively, pour a tablespoonful of coffee essence into each cup and fill it up with boiling water

title: Calf's Head a la Maitre d'Hotel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of a cold calf's head
- rather more than 1/2 pint of Maitre d'hôtel sauce

## Directions

1. Make the sauce by recipe No. 466, and have it sufficiently thick that it may nicely cover the meat; remove the bones from the head, and cut the meat into neat slices.
2. When the sauce is ready, lay in the meat; let it gradually warm through, and, after it boils up, let it simmer very gently for 5 minutes, and serve.

title: Calf's Head Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- calf's head
- salt
- stock or pot-liquor or water
- onion
- sweet herbs
- rice-flour
- ketchup
- white pepper
- mace

## Directions

1. Rub the head with salt, soak it for 6 hours, and clean it thoroughly
2. Put it in the stewpan, and cover it with the stock, or pot-liquor, or water, adding the onion and sweet herbs
3. When well skimmed and boiled for 1-1/2 hour, take out the head, and skim and strain the soup
4. Mix the rice-flour with the ketchup, thicken the soup with it, and simmer for 5 minutes
5. Cut up the head into pieces about two inches long, and simmer them in the soup till the meat and fat are quite tender
6. Season with white pepper and mace finely pounded, and serve very hot

title: Calf's Liver and Bacon
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 or 3 lbs. of liver
- bacon
- pepper and salt to taste
- a small piece of butter
- flour
- 2 tablespoonfuls of lemon-juice
- 1/4 pint of water

## Directions

1. Cut the liver in thin slices, and cut as many slices of bacon as there are of liver
2. Fry the bacon first, and put that on a hot dish before the fire
3. Fry the liver in the fat which comes from the bacon, after seasoning it with pepper and salt and dredging over it a very little flour
4. Turn the liver occasionally to prevent its burning, and when done, lay it round the dish with a piece of bacon between each
5. Pour away the bacon fat, put in a small piece of butter, dredge in a little flour, add the lemon-juice and water, give one boil, and pour it in the middle of the dish

title: Calf's Liver aux Fines Herbes & Sauce Piquante
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A calf's liver
- flour
- a bunch of savoury herbs, including parsley
- 2 minced shalots
- 1 teaspoonful of flour
- 1 tablespoonful of vinegar
- 1 tablespoonful of lemon-juice
- pepper and salt to taste
- 1/4 pint water
- butter

## Directions

1. Procure a calf's liver as white as possible, and cut it into slices of a good and equal shape
2. Dip them in flour, and fry them of a good colour in a little butter
3. When they are done, put them on a dish, which keep hot before the fire
4. Mince the herbs very fine, put them in the frying-pan with a little more butter
5. Add the remaining ingredients, simmer gently until the herbs are done, and pour over the liver

title: Calf's Liver Larded and Roasted (an Entree)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A calf's liver
- vinegar
- 1 onion
- 3 or 4 sprigs of parsley and thyme
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 bay-leaf
- lardoons
- brown gravy

## Directions

1. Take a fine white liver, and lard it the same as a fricandeau
2. Put it into vinegar with an onion cut in slices, parsley, thyme, bay-leaf, and seasoning in the above proportion
3. Let it remain in this pickle for 24 hours
4. Roast and baste it frequently with the vinegar, &c.
5. Glaze it, serve under it a good brown gravy, or sauce piquante, and send it to table very hot

title: Calf's-Feet Jelly
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 quart of calf's-feet stock
- 1/2 lb. of sugar
- 1/2 pint of sherry
- 1 glass of brandy
- shells and whites of 5 eggs
- rind and juice of 2 lemons
- 1/2 oz. of isinglass

## Directions

1. Prepare the stock as directed, taking care to leave the sediment, and to remove all the fat from the surface.
2. Put it into a saucepan, cold, without clarifying it; add the remaining ingredients, and stir them well together before the saucepan is placed on the fire.
3. Simmer the mixture gently for 1/4 hour, but do not stir it after it begins to warm.
4. Throw in a teacupful of cold water, boil for another 5 minutes, and keep the saucepan covered by the side of the fire for about 1/2 hour, but do not let it boil again.
5. In simmering, the head or scum may be carefully removed as it rises; but particular attention must be given to the jelly, that it be not stirred in the slightest degree after it is heated.
6. The isinglass should be added when the jelly begins to boil: this assists to clear it, and makes it firmer for turning out.
7. Wring out a jelly-bag in hot water; fasten it on to a stand, or the back of a chair; place it near the fire with a basin underneath it, and run the jelly through it.
8. Should it not be perfectly clear the first time, repeat the process until the desired brilliancy is obtained.
9. Soak the moulds in water, drain them for half a second, pour in the jelly, and put it in a cool place to set.
10. If ice is at hand, surround the moulds with it, and the jelly will set sooner, and be firmer when turned out.
11. When wanted for table, dip the moulds in hot water for a minute, wipe the outside with a cloth, lay a dish on the top of the mould, turn it quickly over, and the jelly should slip out easily.

title: Calf's-Foot Broth
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 calf's foot
- 3 pints of water
- 1 small lump of sugar
- nutmeg to taste
- the yolk of 1 egg
- a piece of butter the size of a nut

## Directions

1. Stew the foot in the water, with the lemon-peel, very gently, until the liquid is half wasted, removing any scum, should it rise to the surface
2. Set it by in a basin until quite cold, then take off every particle of fat
3. Warm up about 1/2 pint of the broth, adding the butter, sugar, and a very small quantity of grated nutmeg
4. Take it off the fire for a minute or two, then add the beaten yolk of the egg
5. Keep stirring over the fire until the mixture thickens, but do not allow it to boil again after the egg is added, or it will curdle, and the broth will be spoiled

title: Calf's-Head Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 a calf's head
- 1 onion stuck with cloves
- a very small bunch of sweet herbs
- 2 blades of mace
- salt and white pepper to taste
- 6 oz. of rice-flour
- 3 tablespoonfuls of ketchup
- 3 quarts of white stock, No. 107, or pot-liquor, or water

## Directions

1. Rub the head with salt, soak it for 6 hours, and clean it thoroughly
2. Put it in the stewpan, and cover it with the stock, or pot-liquor, or water, adding the onion and sweet herbs
3. When well skimmed and boiled for 1-1/2 hour, take out the head, and skim and strain the soup
4. Mix the rice-flour with the ketchup, thicken the soup with it, and simmer for 5 minutes
5. Cut up the head into pieces about two inches long, and simmer them in the soup till the meat and fat are quite tender
6. Season with white pepper and mace finely pounded, and serve very hot
7. When the calf's head is taken out of the soup, cover it up, or it will discolour

title: Camp Vinegar
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 head of garlic
- 1/2 oz. cayenne
- 2 teaspoonfuls of soy
- 2 teaspoonfuls of walnut ketchup
- 1 pint of vinegar
- cochineal to colour

## Directions

1. Slice the garlic, and put it, with all the above ingredients, into a clean bottle.
2. Let it stand to infuse for a month, when strain it off quite clear, and it will be fit for use.
3. Keep it in small bottles well sealed, to exclude the air.

title: Canary Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- sugar and butter, the weight of 3 eggs
- flour, the weight of 2 eggs
- rind of 1 small lemon
- 3 eggs

## Directions

1. Melt the butter to a liquid state, but do not allow it to oil
2. Stir to this the sugar and finely-minced lemon-peel
3. Gradually dredge in the flour, keeping the mixture well stirred
4. Whisk the eggs; add these to the pudding
5. Beat all the ingredients until thoroughly blended
6. Put them into a buttered mould or basin
7. Boil for 2 hours, and serve with sweet sauce

title: Candied Holly Leaves
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- sprigs of holly
- oiled butter
- coarsely-powdered sugar

## Directions

1. Procure some nice sprigs of holly; pick the leaves from the stalks, and wipe them with a clean cloth free from all moisture
2. Place them on a dish near the fire, to get thoroughly dry, but not too near to shrivel the leaves
3. Dip them into oiled butter, sprinkle over them some coarsely-powdered sugar
4. Dry them before the fire
5. Keep in a dry place, as the least damp would spoil their appearance

title: Cannelons, or Fried Puffs
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of puff-paste
- apricot or any kind of preserve
- hot lard
- white of egg
- sifted sugar

## Directions

1. Make some good puff-paste; roll it out very thin, and cut it into pieces of an equal size, about 2 inches wide and 8 inches long.
2. Place upon each piece a spoonful of jam, wet the edges with the white of egg, and fold the paste over twice.
3. Slightly press the edges together, that the jam may not escape in the frying.
4. When all are prepared, fry them in boiling lard until of a nice brown, letting them remain by the side of the fire after they are coloured, that the paste may be thoroughly done.
5. Drain them before the fire, dish on a d'oyley, sprinkle over them sifted sugar, and serve.

title: Caper Sauce for Boiled Mutton
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of melted butter
- 3 tablespoonfuls of capers or nasturtiums
- 1 tablespoonful of their liquor

## Directions

1. Chop the capers twice or thrice, and add them, with their liquor, to 1/2 pint of melted butter, made very smoothly.
2. Keep stirring well.
3. Let the sauce just simmer, and serve in a tureen.

title: Caper Sauce for Fish
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of melted butter
- 3 dessertspoonfuls of capers
- 1 dessertspoonful of their liquor
- a small piece of glaze
- 1/4 teaspoonful of salt
- 1/4 teaspoonful of pepper
- 1 tablespoonful of anchovy essence

## Directions

1. Cut the capers across once or twice, but do not chop them fine.
2. Put them in a saucepan with 1/2 pint of good melted butter, and add all the other ingredients.
3. Keep stirring the whole until it just simmers, when it is ready to serve.

title: Carrot Jam to Imitate Apricot Preserve
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Carrots
- Pounded sugar, 1 lb. to every lb. of carrot pulp
- Grated rind of 1 lemon
- Strained juice of 2 lemons
- Chopped bitter almonds, 6
- Brandy, 2 tablespoonfuls

## Directions

1. Select young carrots; wash and scrape them clean, cut them into round pieces, put them into a saucepan with sufficient water to cover them, and let them simmer until perfectly soft; then beat them through a sieve.
2. Weigh the pulp, and to every lb. allow the above ingredients.
3. Put the pulp into a preserving-pan with the sugar, and let this boil for 5 minutes, stirring and skimming all the time.
4. When cold, add the lemon-rind and juice, almonds and brandy; mix these well with the jam; then put it into pots, which must be well covered and kept in a dry place.

title: Carrot Soup I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 quarts of liquor in which a leg of mutton or beef has been boiled
- a few beef-bones
- 6 large carrots
- 2 large onions
- 1 turnip
- salt and pepper to taste
- cayenne

## Directions

1. Put the liquor, bones, onions, turnip, pepper, and salt, into a stewpan, and simmer for 3 hours.
2. Scrape and cut the carrots thin, strain the soup on them, and stew them till soft enough to pulp through a hair sieve or coarse cloth.
3. Then boil the pulp with the soup, which should be of the consistency of pea-soup.
4. Add cayenne.
5. Pulp only the red part of the carrot, and make this soup the day before it is wanted.

title: Carrot Soup II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of carrots
- 3 oz. of butter
- salt and cayenne to taste
- 2 quarts of stock or gravy soup

## Directions

1. Scrape and cut out all specks from the carrots, wash, and wipe them dry, and then reduce them into quarter-inch slices.
2. Put the butter into a large stewpan, and when it is melted, add 2 lbs. of the sliced carrots, and let them stew gently for an hour without browning.
3. Add to them the soup, and allow them to simmer till tender,--say for nearly an hour.
4. Press them through a strainer with the soup, and add salt and cayenne if required.
5. Boil the whole gently for 5 minutes, skim well, and serve as hot as possible.

title: Cauliflowers a la Sauce Blanche
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 cauliflowers
- 1/2 pint of sauce blanche or French melted butter
- 3 oz. of butter
- salt and water

## Directions

1. Cleanse the cauliflowers and cut the stalks off flat at the bottom
2. Boil them until tender in salt and water, to which the butter has been added
3. Be careful to take them up the moment they are done, or they will break
4. Drain them well, and dish them in the shape of a large cauliflower
5. Have ready 1/2 pint of sauce made by recipe No. 378
6. Pour it over the flowers, and serve hot and quickly

title: Cauliflowers with Parmesan Cheese
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 or 3 cauliflowers
- rather more than 1/2 pint of white sauce
- 2 tablespoonfuls of grated Parmesan cheese
- 2 oz. of fresh butter
- 3 tablespoonfuls of bread crumbs

## Directions

1. Cleanse and boil the cauliflowers by recipe No. 1104, and drain them
2. Dish them with the flowers standing upright
3. Have ready the white sauce
4. Pour sufficient of it over the cauliflowers just to cover the top
5. Sprinkle over this some rasped Parmesan cheese and bread crumbs
6. Drop on these the melted butter
7. Brown with a salamander, or before the fire
8. Pour round, but not over, the flowers the remainder of the sauce

title: Cauliflowers with White Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 or 3 cauliflowers
- more than 1/2 pint of white sauce No. 378
- 2 tablespoonfuls of grated Parmesan cheese
- 2 oz. of fresh butter
- 3 tablespoonfuls of bread crumbs

## Directions

1. Cleanse and boil the cauliflowers by recipe No. 1104, and drain them and dish them with the flowers standing upright.
2. Have ready the above proportion of white sauce; pour sufficient of it over the cauliflowers just to cover the top.
3. Sprinkle over this some rasped Parmesan cheese and bread crumbs, and drop on these the butter, which should be melted, but not oiled.
4. Brown with a salamander, or before the fire, and pour round, but not over, the flowers the remainder of the sauce, with which should be mixed a small quantity of grated Parmesan cheese.

title: Cayenne Cheeses
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of butter
- 1/2 lb. of flour
- 1/2 lb. of grated cheese
- 1/3 teaspoonful of cayenne
- 1/3 teaspoonful of salt
- water

## Directions

1. Rub the butter in the flour
2. Add the grated cheese, cayenne, and salt
3. Mix these ingredients well together
4. Moisten with sufficient water to make the whole into a paste
5. Roll out, and cut into fingers about 4 inches in length
6. Bake them in a moderate oven a very light colour
7. Serve very hot

title: Cayenne Vinegar, or Essence of Cayenne
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 oz. of cayenne pepper
- 1/2 pint of strong spirit, or 1 pint of vinegar

## Directions

1. Put the vinegar, or spirit, into a bottle, with the above proportion of cayenne, and let it steep for a month, when strain off and bottle for use.

title: Celery Sauce (a More Simple Recipe)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 heads of celery
- 1/2 pint of melted butter, made with milk
- 1 blade of pounded mace
- Salt and white pepper to taste

## Directions

1. Wash the celery, boil it in salt and water till tender, and cut it into pieces 2 inches long.
2. Make 1/2 pint melted butter by recipe No. 380.
3. Put in the celery, pounded mace, and seasoning.
4. Simmer for three minutes, when the sauce will be ready to serve.

title: Celery Sauce, for Boiled Turkey, Poultry, &c.
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 heads of celery
- 1 pint of white stock
- 2 blades of mace
- 1 small bunch of savoury herbs
- Thickening of butter and flour, or arrowroot
- 1/2 pint of cream
- Lemon-juice
- Salt and white pepper

## Directions

1. Boil the celery in salt and water, until tender, and cut it into pieces 2 inches long.
2. Put the stock into a stewpan with the mace and herbs, and let it simmer for 1/2 hour to extract their flavour.
3. Then strain the liquor, add the celery and a thickening of butter kneaded with flour, or, what is still better, with arrowroot.
4. Just before serving, put in the cream, boil it up and squeeze in a little lemon-juice.
5. If necessary, add a seasoning of salt and white pepper.

title: Celery Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 9 heads of celery
- 1 teaspoonful of salt
- nutmeg to taste
- 1 lump of sugar
- 1/2 pint of strong stock
- a pint of cream
- 2 quarts of boiling water

## Directions

1. Cut the celery into small pieces; throw it into the water, seasoned with the nutmeg, salt, and sugar.
2. Boil it till sufficiently tender; pass it through a sieve, add the stock, and simmer it for half an hour.
3. Now put in the cream, bring it to the boiling point, and serve immediately.

title: Celery Vinegar
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 oz. of celery-seed
- 1 pint of vinegar

## Directions

1. Crush the seed by pounding it in a mortar.
2. Boil the vinegar, and when cold, pour it to the seed.
3. Let it infuse for a fortnight, when strain and bottle off for use.

title: Champagne-Cup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 quart bottle of champagne
- 2 bottles of soda-water
- 1 liqueur-glass of brandy or Curaçoa
- 2 tablespoonfuls of powdered sugar
- 1 lb. of pounded ice
- a sprig of green borage

## Directions

1. Put all the ingredients into a silver cup; stir them together, and serve the same as claret-cup.
2. Should the above proportion of sugar not be found sufficient to suit some tastes, increase the quantity.
3. When borage is not easily obtainable, substitute for it a few slices of cucumber-rind.

title: Chantilly Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 quart of young green peas
- a small bunch of parsley
- 2 young onions
- 2 quarts of medium stock No. 105

## Directions

1. Boil the peas till quite tender, with the parsley and onions; then rub them through a sieve, and pour the stock to them.
2. Do not let it boil after the peas are added, or you will spoil the colour.
3. Serve very hot.

title: Charlotte Russe
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- About 18 Savoy biscuits
- 3/4 pint of cream
- flavouring of vanilla, liqueurs, or wine
- 1 tablespoonful of pounded sugar
- 1/2 oz. of isinglass

## Directions

1. Procure about 18 Savoy biscuits, or ladies'-fingers; brush the edges of them with the white of an egg, and line the bottom of a plain round mould, placing them like a star or rosette.
2. Stand them upright all round the edge; carefully put them so closely together that the white of the egg connects them firmly, and place this case in the oven for about 5 minutes, just to dry the egg.
3. Whisk the cream to a stiff froth, with the sugar, flavouring, and melted isinglass.
4. Fill the charlotte with it, cover with a slice of sponge-cake cut in the shape of the mould.
5. Place it in ice, where let it remain till ready for table.
6. Turn it on a dish, remove the mould, and serve.

title: Charlotte-Aux-Pommes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- a few slices of rather stale bread 1/2 inch thick
- clarified butter
- apple marmalade made with about 2 dozen apples
- 1/2 glass of sherry
- white of egg
- sifted sugar
- melted apricot jam

## Directions

1. Cut a slice of bread the same shape as the bottom of a plain round mould, which has been well buttered, and a few strips the height of the mould, and about 1-1/2 inch wide.
2. Dip the bread in clarified butter or spread it with cold butter.
3. Place the round piece at the bottom of the mould, and set the narrow strips up the sides of it, overlapping each other a little, that no juice from the apples may escape, and that they may hold firmly to the mould.
4. Brush the interior over with white of egg (this will assist to make the case firmer).
5. Fill it with apple marmalade with the addition of a little sherry, and cover with a round piece of bread, also brushed over with egg, the same as the bottom.
6. Slightly press the bread down, to make it adhere to the other pieces.
7. Put a plate on the top, and bake the charlotte in a brisk oven, of a light colour.
8. Turn it out on the dish, strew sifted sugar over the top, and pour round it a little melted apricot jam.

title: Cheap Blanc-Mange
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of sugar
- 1 quart of milk
- 1-1/2 oz. of isinglass
- rind of 1/2 lemon
- 4 laurel-leaves

## Directions

1. Put all the ingredients into a lined saucepan, and boil gently until the isinglass is dissolved
2. Taste it occasionally, to ascertain when it is sufficiently flavoured with the laurel-leaves
3. Take out the laurel-leaves, and keep stirring the mixture over the fire for about 10 minutes
4. Strain it through a fine sieve into a jug
5. When nearly cold, pour it into a well-oiled mould, omitting the sediment at the bottom
6. Turn it out carefully on a dish, and garnish with preserves, bright jelly, or a compote of fruit

title: Cheap Gravy for Minced Veal
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Bones and trimmings of cold roast or boiled veal
- 1-1/2 pint of water
- 1 onion
- 1/4 teaspoonful of minced lemon-peel
- 1/4 teaspoonful of salt
- 1 blade of pounded mace
- the juice of 1/4 lemon
- thickening of butter and flour

## Directions

1. Put all the ingredients into a stewpan, except the thickening and lemon-juice, and let them simmer very gently for rather more than 1 hour, or until the liquor is reduced to a pint
2. Strain through a hair-sieve
3. Add a thickening of butter and flour, and the lemon-juice
4. Set it on the fire, and let it just boil up, when it will be ready for use
5. It may be flavoured with a little tomato sauce, and, where a rather dark-coloured gravy is not objected to, ketchup, or Harvey's sauce, may be added at pleasure

title: Cheap Onion Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 8 middling-sized onions
- 3 oz. of butter
- a tablespoonful of rice-flour
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 teaspoonful of powdered sugar
- thickening of butter and flour
- 2 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Cut the onions small, put them in the stewpan with the butter, and fry them well.
2. Mix the rice-flour smoothly with the water, add the onions, seasoning, and sugar, and simmer till tender.
3. Thicken with butter and flour, and serve.

title: Cheese Sandwiches
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Slices of brown bread-and-butter
- Thin slices of cheese

## Directions

1. Cut from a nice fat Cheshire, or any good rich cheese, some slices about 1/2 inch thick
2. Place them between some slices of brown bread-and-butter, like sandwiches
3. Place them on a plate in the oven
4. When the bread is toasted, serve on a napkin very hot and very quickly

title: Cherry Jam
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Fruit, weighed before stoning
- Sugar, 1/2 lb. to every lb. of fruit
- Red-currant juice, 1 pint to every 6 lbs. of fruit
- Sugar, 1 lb. to every pint of juice

## Directions

1. Weigh the fruit before stoning, and allow half the weight of sugar.
