1. Bone the mutton, and cut the meat into steaks all of the same thickness, and leave but very little fat.
2. Cut up the kidneys, and arrange these with the meat neatly in a pie-dish.
3. Sprinkle over them the minced parsley and a seasoning of pepper and salt.
4. Pour in the gravy, and cover with a tolerably good puff crust.
5. Bake for 1-1/2 hour, or rather longer, should the pie be very large, and let the oven be rather brisk.

title: Mutton Pie (Cold Meat Cookery)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- the remains of a cold leg, loin, or neck of mutton
- pepper and salt to taste
- 2 blades of pounded mace
- 1 dessertspoonful of chopped parsley
- 1 teaspoonful of minced savoury herbs
- a little minced onion or shalot
- 3 or 4 potatoes
- 1 teacupful of gravy
- crust

## Directions

1. Cold mutton may be made into very good pies if well seasoned and mixed with a few herbs; if the leg is used, cut it into very thin slices; if the loin or neck, into thin cutlets.
2. Place some at the bottom of the dish; season well with pepper, salt, mace, parsley, and herbs; then put a layer of potatoes sliced, then more mutton, and so on till the dish is full.
3. Add the gravy, cover with a crust, and bake for 1 hour.

title: Mutton Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- About 2 lbs. of the chump end of the loin of mutton, weighed after being boned
- pepper and salt to taste
- suet crust made with milk in the proportion of 6 oz. of suet to each pound of flour
- a very small quantity of minced onion

## Directions

1. Cut the meat into rather thin slices, and season them with pepper and salt.
2. Line the pudding-dish with crust.
3. Lay in the meat, and nearly, but do not quite, fill it up with water.
4. When the flavour is liked, add a small quantity of minced onion.
5. Cover with crust, and proceed in the same manner as directed in recipe No. 605, using the same kind of pudding-dish as there mentioned.

title: Negus
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of port wine
- 1/4 lb. of sugar
- 1 lemon
- grated nutmeg
- boiling water

## Directions

1. Rub lumps of sugar on the lemon-rind until all the yellow part of the skin is absorbed
2. Squeeze the juice and strain it
3. Add the sugar and lemon-juice to the port wine, with the grated nutmeg
4. Pour over it the boiling water
5. Cover the jug
6. When the beverage has cooled a little, it will be fit for use

title: Nesselrode Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 40 chestnuts
- 1 lb. of sugar
- vanilla
- 1 pint of cream
- the yolks of 12 eggs
- 1 glass of Maraschino
- 1 oz. of candied citron
- 2 oz. of currants
- 2 oz. of stoned raisins
- 1/2 pint of whipped cream
- 3 eggs

## Directions

1. Blanch the chestnuts in boiling water, remove the husks, and pound them in a mortar until perfectly smooth, adding a few spoonfuls of syrup.
2. Rub them through a fine sieve, and mix them in a basin with a pint of syrup made from 1 lb. of sugar, clarified, and flavoured with vanilla, 1 pint of cream, and the yolks of 12 eggs.
3. Set this mixture over a slow fire, stirring it without ceasing, and just as it begins to boil, take it off and pass it through a tammy.
4. When it is cold, put it into a freezing-pot, adding the Maraschino, and make the mixture set.
5. Add the sliced citron, the currants, and stoned raisins (these two latter should be soaked the day previously in Maraschino and sugar pounded with vanilla).
6. Add a plateful of whipped cream mixed with the whites of 3 eggs, beaten to a froth with a little syrup.
7. When the pudding is perfectly frozen, put it into a pineapple-shaped mould; close the lid, place it again in the freezing-pan, covered over with pounded ice and saltpetre, and let it remain until required for table.
8. Turn the pudding out, and serve.

title: Nice Breakfast Cakes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of flour
- 1/2 teaspoonful of tartaric acid
- 1/2 teaspoonful of salt
- 1/2 teaspoonful of carbonate of soda
- 1-1/2 breakfast-cupful of milk
- 1 oz. of sifted loaf sugar
- 2 eggs

## Directions

1. Mix the flour, tartaric acid, and salt well together, taking care that the two latter ingredients are reduced to the finest powder, and stir in the sifted sugar, which should also be very fine.
2. Dissolve the soda in the milk, add the eggs, which should be well whisked.
3. With this liquid work the flour, &c. into a light dough.
4. Divide it into small cakes, put them into the oven immediately, and bake for about 20 minutes.

title: Nourishing Lemonade
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 pint of boiling water
- the juice of 4 lemons
- the rinds of 2
- 1/2 pint of sherry
- 4 eggs
- 6 oz. of loaf sugar

## Directions

1. Pare off the lemon-rind thinly, put it into a jug with the sugar, and pour over the boiling water
2. Let it cool, then strain it
3. add the wine, lemon-juice, and eggs, previously well beaten, and also strained, and the beverage will be ready for use

title: Noyeau Cream
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 oz. of isinglass
- juice of 2 lemons
- noyeau to taste
- pounded sugar to taste
- 1-1/2 pint of cream

## Directions

1. Dissolve the isinglass in a little boiling water
2. Add the lemon-juice, and strain this to the cream
3. Put in sufficient noyeau and sugar to flavour and sweeten the mixture nicely
4. Whisk the cream well
5. Put it into an oiled mould, and set the mould in ice or in a cool place
6. Turn it out, and garnish the dish to taste

title: Nutritious Coffee
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 oz. of ground coffee
- 1 pint of milk

## Directions

1. Let the coffee be freshly ground; put it into a saucepan, with the milk, which should be made nearly boiling before the coffee is put in, and boil both together for 3 minutes
2. Clear it by pouring some of it into a cup, and then back again, and leave it on the hob for a few minutes to settle thoroughly
3. This coffee may be made still more nutritious by the addition of an egg well beaten, and put into the coffee-cup

title: Oeufs au Plat, or au Miroir
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 eggs
- 1 oz. of butter
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Butter a dish rather thickly with good fresh butter; melt it
2. break the eggs into it the same as for poaching, sprinkle them with white pepper and fine salt
3. put the remainder of the butter, cut into very small pieces, on the top of them
4. Put the dish on a hot plate, or in the oven, or before the fire, and let it remain until the whites become set, but not hard
5. serve immediately, placing the dish they were cooked in on another
6. To hasten the cooking of the eggs, a salamander may be held over them for a minute; but great care must be taken that they are not too much done

title: Omelet
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 or 3 eggs
- 2 oz. of butter
- 1 teaspoonful of flour
- 1/2 teacupful of milk
- salt
- cayenne
- chopped herbs
- shreds of onion

## Directions

1. Make a thin cream of the flour and milk
2. Beat up the eggs and mix all together
3. Add a pinch of salt and a few grains of cayenne
4. Melt the butter in a small frying-pan
5. When very hot, pour in the batter
6. Let the pan remain for a few minutes over a clear fire
7. Sprinkle upon the omelet some chopped herbs and a few shreds of onion
8. Double the omelet dexterously and shake it out of the pan onto a hot dish

title: Omelette au Thon
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- roes of 2 carp
- piece of fresh tunny about the size of a hen's egg
- small shalot, chopped
- very good butter

## Directions

1. Bleach the carp roes by putting them for 5 minutes in boiling water slightly salted
2. Hash up together the roe and the tunny, so as to mix them well
3. Throw the whole into a saucepan with a sufficient quantity of very good butter
4. Whip it up until the butter is melted

title: Omelette au Thon
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- roes of 2 carp
- piece of fresh tunny about the size of a hen's egg
- small shalot, chopped
- very good butter
- butter à discrétion
- parsley and herbs
- lemon
- 12 eggs

## Directions

1. Bleach the roes by putting them for 5 minutes in boiling water slightly salted.
2. Hash up together the roe and the tunny so as to mix them well, and throw the whole into a saucepan with a sufficient quantity of very good butter.
3. Whip it up until the butter is melted.
4. Mix a second piece of butter with parsley and herbs, place it in a long-shaped dish destined to receive the omelet.
5. Squeeze the juice of a lemon over it, and place it on hot embers.
6. Beat up 12 eggs, the fresher the better.
7. Throw up the sauté of roe and tunny, stirring it so as to mix all well together.
8. Make your omelet in the usual manner, endeavouring to turn it out long, thick, and soft.
9. Spread it carefully on the dish prepared for it, and serve at once.
10. Note: The roe and the tunny must be beaten up without allowing them to boil, to prevent their hardening.

title: Omelette Aux Confitures, or Jam Omelet
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 eggs
- 4 oz. of butter
- 3 tablespoonfuls of apricot, strawberry, or any jam that may be preferred

## Directions

1. Make the omelet by recipe No. 1459, only instead of doubling it over, leave it flat in the pan
2. When quite firm, and nicely brown on one side, turn it carefully on to a hot dish
3. Spread over the middle of it the jam, and fold the omelet over on each side
4. Sprinkle sifted sugar over, and serve very quickly
5. A pretty dish of small omelets may be made by dividing the batter into 3 or 4 portions, and frying them separately
6. Spread each one with a different kind of preserve, and roll the omelets over
7. Always sprinkle sweet omelets with sifted sugar before being sent to table

title: Omelette Soufflé
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 eggs
- 5 oz. of pounded sugar
- flavouring of vanilla, orange-flower water, or lemon-rind
- 3 oz. of butter
- 1 dessert-spoonful of rice-flour

## Directions

1. Separate the yolks from the whites of the eggs
2. Add to the yolks the sugar, the rice-flour, and either of the above flavourings that may be preferred, and stir these ingredients well together
3. Whip the whites of the eggs, mix them lightly with the batter
4. Put the butter into a small frying-pan
5. As soon as it begins to bubble, pour the batter into it, and set the pan over a bright but gentle fire
6. When the omelet is set, turn the edges over to make it an oval shape, and slip it on to a silver dish, which has been previously well buttered
7. Put it in the oven, and bake from 12 to 15 minutes
8. Sprinkle finely-powdered sugar over the soufflé, and serve it immediately

title: Onion Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 large onions
- 2 oz. of butter
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1/4 pint of cream
- 1 quart of stock No. 105

## Directions

1. Chop the onions, put them in the butter, stir them occasionally, but do not let them brown.
2. When tender, put the stock to them, and season.
3. Strain the soup, and add the boiling cream.

title: Open Jelly With Whipped Cream
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 pint of jelly
- 1/2 pint of cream
- 1 glass of sherry
- sugar to taste

## Directions

1. Make the above proportion of calf's-feet or isinglass jelly, colouring and flavouring it in any way that may be preferred
2. Soak a mould, open in the centre, for about 1/2 hour in cold water
3. Fill it with the jelly, and let it remain in a cool place until perfectly set
4. Turn it out on a dish
5. Fill the centre with whipped cream, flavoured with sherry and sweetened with pounded sugar
6. Pile this cream high in the centre, and serve

title: Open Tart Of Strawberry Or Any Other Kind Of Preserve
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Trimmings of puff-paste
- any kind of jam

## Directions

1. Butter a tart-pan of the shape shown in the engraving
2. Roll out the paste to the thickness of 1/2 an inch, and line the pan with it
3. Prick a few holes at the bottom with a fork
4. Bake the tart in a brisk oven from 10 to 15 minutes
5. Let the paste cool a little
6. Fill it with preserve
7. Place a few stars or leaves on it, which have been previously cut out of the paste and baked
8. The tart is ready for table

title: Orange Batter Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 eggs
- 1 pint of milk
- 1-1/4 oz. of loaf sugar
- 3 tablespoonfuls of flour

## Directions

1. Make the batter with the above ingredients, put it into a well-buttered basin, tie it down with a cloth, and boil for 1 hour
2. As soon as it is turned out of the basin, put a small jar of orange marmalade all over the top, and send the pudding very quickly to table

title: Orange Brandy
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 gallon of brandy
- 3/4 pint of Seville orange-juice
- 1-1/4 lb. of loaf sugar
- 2 or 3 unpared oranges
- rinds of 6 oranges pared very thin

## Directions

1. Rub a few lumps of the sugar on 2 or 3 unpared oranges, and put these lumps to the rest
2. Mix the brandy with the orange-juice, strained, the rinds of 6 of the oranges pared very thin, and the sugar
3. Let all stand in a closely-covered jar for about 3 days, stirring it 3 or 4 times a day
4. When clear, bottle and closely cork for a year; it will then be ready for use, but will keep any length of time
5. This may be taken pure in small quantities or diluted with water

title: Orange Cream
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 oz. of isinglass
- 6 large oranges
- 1 lemon
- sugar to taste
- water
- 1/2 pint of good cream

## Directions

1. Squeeze the juice from the oranges and lemon and strain it
2. Put it into a saucepan with the isinglass and sufficient water to make in all 1-1/2 pint
3. Rub the sugar on the orange and lemon-rind and add it to the other ingredients
4. Boil all together for about 10 minutes
5. Strain through a muslin bag
6. When cold, beat up with it 1/2 pint of thick cream
7. Wet a mould or soak it in cold water
8. Pour in the cream and put it in a cool place to set

title: Orange Creams
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Seville orange
- 1 tablespoonful of brandy
- 1/4 lb. of loaf sugar
- yolks of 4 eggs
- 1 pint of cream
- candied orange-peel or preserved chips

## Directions

1. Boil the rind of the Seville orange until tender and beat it in a mortar to a pulp
2. Add to it the brandy, the strained juice of the orange, and the sugar
3. Beat all together for about 10 minutes, adding the well-beaten yolks of eggs
4. Bring the cream to the boiling-point and pour it very gradually to the other ingredients
5. Beat the mixture till nearly cold
6. Put it into custard-cups and place the cups in a deep dish of boiling water
7. Let them remain till quite cold
8. Take the cups out of the water and wipe them
9. Garnish the tops of the creams with candied orange-peel or preserved chips

title: Orange Fritters
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of flour
- 1/2 oz. of butter
- 1/2 saltspoonful of salt
- 2 eggs
- milk
- oranges
- hot lard or clarified dripping
- pounded sugar

## Directions

1. Make a nice light batter with the flour, butter, salt, eggs, and sufficient milk to make it the proper consistency
2. Peel the oranges and remove as much of the white skin as possible
3. Divide each orange into eight pieces, without breaking the thin skin, unless it be to remove the pips
4. Dip each piece of orange in the batter
5. Have ready a pan of boiling lard or clarified dripping
6. Drop in the oranges and fry them a delicate brown from 8 to 10 minutes
7. Lay them on a piece of blotting-paper before the fire to drain away the greasy moisture
8. Dish them on a white d'oyley
9. Sprinkle over them plenty of pounded sugar and serve quickly

title: Orange Gravy, for Wildfowl, Widgeon, Teal, &c.
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of white stock
- 1 small onion
- 3 or 4 strips of lemon or orange peel
- a few leaves of basil
- juice of a Seville orange or lemon
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 glass of port wine

## Directions

1. Put the onion, cut in slices, into a stewpan with the stock, orange-peel, and basil, and let them simmer very gently for 1/4 hour or rather longer, should the gravy not taste sufficiently of the peel
2. Strain it off, and add to the gravy the remaining ingredients
3. Let the whole heat through, and, when on the point of boiling, serve very hot in a tureen which should have a cover to it

title: Orange Jelly
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of water
- 1-1/2 to 2 oz. of isinglass
- 1/2 lb. of loaf sugar
- 1 Seville orange
- 1 lemon
- about 9 China oranges

## Directions

1. Put the water into a saucepan, with the isinglass, sugar, and the rind of 1 orange, and the same of 1/2 lemon
2. Stir these over the fire until the isinglass is dissolved, and remove the scum
3. Add the juice of the Seville orange, the juice of the lemon, and sufficient juice of China oranges to make in all 1 pint
4. Stir all together over the fire until it is just on the point of boiling
5. Skim well
6. Strain the jelly through a very fine sieve or jelly-bag
7. When nearly cold, put it into a mould previously wetted
8. When quite set, turn it out on a dish, and garnish it to taste

title: Orange Jelly Moulded With Slices of Orange
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 pint of orange jelly
- 4 oranges
- 1 pint of clarified syrup

## Directions

1. Boil 1/2 lb. of loaf sugar with 1/2 pint of water until there is no scum left, carefully removing scum as it rises
2. Carefully peel the oranges
3. Divide them into thin slices, without breaking the thin skin
4. Put these pieces of orange into the syrup, where let them remain for about 5 minutes
5. Take them out, and use the syrup for the jelly, which should be made by recipe No. 1454
6. When the oranges are well drained, and the jelly is nearly cold, pour a little of the jelly into the bottom of the mould
7. Lay in a few pieces of orange
8. Pour a little jelly over these, and when this is set, place another layer of oranges, proceeding in this manner until the mould is full
9. Put it in ice, or in a cool place
10. Before turning it out, wrap a cloth round the mould for a minute or two, which has been wrung out in boiling water

title: Orange Marmalade I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Equal weight of fine loaf sugar and Seville oranges
- 1 pint of water to every 12 oranges

## Directions

1. Peel the oranges carefully, remove a little of the white pith, and boil the rinds in water 2 hours, changing the water three times to take off a little of the bitter taste
2. Break the pulp into small pieces, take out all the pips, and cut the boiled rind into chips
3. Make a syrup with the sugar and water; boil this well, skim it, and, when clear, put in the pulp and chips
4. Boil all together from 20 minutes to 1/2 hour; pour it into pots, and, when cold, cover down with bladders or tissue-paper brushed over on both sides with the white of an egg

title: Orange Marmalade II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Equal weight of Seville oranges and sugar
- 1/2 pint of water to every lb. of sugar

## Directions

1. Weigh the sugar and oranges, score the skin across, and take it off in quarters
2. Boil these quarters in a muslin bag in water until they are quite soft, and they can be pierced easily with the head of a pin
3. Cut them into chips about 1 inch long, and as thin as possible
4. Should there be a great deal of white stringy pulp, remove it before cutting the rind into chips
5. Split open the oranges, scrape out the best part of the pulp, with the juice, rejecting the white pith and pips
6. Make a syrup with the sugar and water; boil it until clear
7. Put in the chips, pulp, and juice, and boil the marmalade from 20 minutes to 1/2 hour, removing all the scum as it rises

title: Orange Marmalade Made with Honey
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 quart of the juice and pulp of Seville oranges
- 2 lbs. of honey
- 1 lb. of the rind

## Directions

1. Peel the oranges and boil the rind in water until tender, and cut it into strips
2. Take away the pips from the juice and pulp, and put it with the honey and chips into a preserving-pan
3. Boil all together for about 1/2 hour, or until the marmalade is of the proper consistency
4. Put it into pots, and, when cold, cover down with bladders

title: Orange Salad
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oranges
- 1/4 lb. of muscatel raisins
- 2 oz. of pounded sugar
- 4 tablespoonfuls of brandy

## Directions

1. Peel 5 of the oranges; divide them into slices without breaking the pulp, and arrange them on a glass dish
2. Stone the raisins, mix them with the sugar and brandy, and mingle them with the oranges
3. Squeeze the juice of the other orange over the whole
4. A little pounded spice may be put in when the flavour is liked; but this ingredient must be added very sparingly

title: Ox-Cheek Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- An ox-cheek
- 2 oz. of butter
- 3 or 4 slices of lean ham or bacon
- 1 parsnip
- 3 carrots
- 2 onions
- 3 heads of celery
- 3 blades of mace
- 4 cloves
- a faggot of savoury herbs
- 1 bay-leaf
- a teaspoonful of salt
- half that of pepper
- 1 head of celery
- browning
- the crust of a French roll
- 6 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Lay the ham in the bottom of the stewpan, with the butter
2. break the bones of the cheek, wash it clean, and put it on the ham
3. Cut the vegetables small, add them to the other ingredients, and set the whole over a slow fire for 1/4 of an hour
4. Now put in the water, and simmer gently till it is reduced to 4 quarts
5. take out the fleshy part of the cheek, and strain the soup into a clean stewpan
6. thicken with flour, put in a head of sliced celery, and simmer till the celery is tender
7. If not a good colour, use a little browning
8. Cut the meat into small square pieces, pour the soup over, and serve with the crust of a French roll in the tureen
9. A glass of sherry much improves this soup

title: Ox-Tail Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 ox-tails
- 2 slices of ham
- 1 oz. of butter
- 2 carrots
- 2 turnips
- 3 onions
- 1 leek
- 1 head of celery
- 1 bunch of savoury herbs
- 1 bay-leaf
- 12 whole peppercorns
- 4 cloves
- a tablespoonful of salt
- 2 tablespoonfuls of ketchup
- 1/2 glass of port wine
- 3 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Cut up the tails, separating them at the joints; wash them, and put them in a stewpan, with the butter
2. Cut the vegetables in slices, and add them, with the peppercorns and herbs
3. Put in 1/2 pint of water, and stir it over a sharp fire till the juices are drawn
4. Fill up the stewpan with the water, and, when boiling, add the salt
5. Skim well, and simmer very gently for 4 hours, or until the tails are tender
6. Take them out, skim and strain the soup, thicken with flour, and flavour with the ketchup and port wine
7. Put back the tails, simmer for 5 minutes, and serve

title: Oyster Forcemeat, for Roast or Boiled Turkey
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of bread crumbs
- 1-1/2 oz. of chopped suet or butter
- 1 faggot of savoury herbs
- 1/4 saltspoonful of grated nutmeg
- salt and pepper to taste
- 2 eggs
- 18 oysters

## Directions

1. Grate the bread very fine, and be careful that no large lumps remain; put it into a basin with the suet, which must be very finely minced, or, when butter is used, that must be cut up into small pieces
2. Add the herbs, also chopped as small as possible, and seasoning; mix all these well together, until the ingredients are thoroughly mingled
3. Open and beard the oysters, chop them, but not too small, and add them to the other ingredients
4. Beat up the eggs, and, with the hand, work altogether, until it is smoothly mixed
5. Do not stuff the turkey too full: if there should be too much forcemeat, roll it into balls, fry them, and use them as a garnish

title: Oyster Ketchup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- oysters to fill a pint measure
- 1 pint of sherry
- 3 oz. of salt
- 1 drachm of cayenne
- 2 drachms of pounded mace

## Directions

1. Procure the oysters very fresh, and open sufficient to fill a pint measure; save the liquor, and scald the oysters in it with the sherry
2. Strain the oysters, and put them in a mortar with the salt, cayenne, and mace; pound the whole until reduced to a pulp, then add it to the liquor in which they were scalded
3. Boil it again five minutes, and skim well
4. Rub the whole through a sieve, and, when cold, bottle and cork closely
5. The corks should be sealed

title: Oyster Patties
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 dozen oysters
- 2 oz. butter
- 3 tablespoonfuls of cream
- a little lemon-juice
- 1 blade of pounded mace
- cayenne to taste
- flour

## Directions

1. Scald the oysters in their own liquor, beard them, and cut each one into 3 pieces.
2. Put the butter into a stewpan, dredge in sufficient flour to dry it up.
3. Add the strained oyster-liquor with the other ingredients.
4. Put in the oysters, and let them heat gradually, but not boil fast.
5. Make the patty-cases as directed for lobster patties, No. 277.
6. Fill with the oyster mixture, and replace the covers.

title: Oyster Sauce, to serve with Fish, Boiled Poultry, &c.
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 dozen oysters
- 1/2 pint of melted butter, made with milk

## Directions

1. Open the oysters carefully, and save their liquor; strain it into a clean saucepan
2. Put in the oysters, and let them just come to the boiling-point, when they should look plump
3. Take them off the fire immediately, and put the whole into a basin
4. Strain the liquor from them, mix with it sufficient milk to make 1/2 pint altogether, and follow the directions for melted butter
5. When the melted butter is ready and very smooth, put in the oysters, which should be previously bearded, if you wish the sauce to be really nice
6. Set it by the side of the fire to get thoroughly hot, but do not allow it to boil, or the oysters will immediately harden
7. Using cream instead of milk makes this sauce extremely delicious
8. When liked, add a seasoning of cayenne, or anchovy sauce

title: Oyster Soup I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 dozen of oysters
- 2 quarts of white stock
- 1/2 pint of cream
- 2 oz. of butter
- 1-1/2 oz. of flour
- salt
- cayenne
- mace to taste

## Directions

1. Scald the oysters in their own liquor; take them out, beard them, and put them in a tureen.
2. Take a pint of the stock, put in the beards and the liquor, which must be carefully strained, and simmer for 1/2 an hour.
3. Take it off the fire, strain it again, and add the remainder of the stock with the seasoning and mace.
4. Bring it to a boil, add the thickening of butter and flour, simmer for 5 minutes, stir in the boiling cream, pour it over the oysters, and serve.

title: Oyster Soup II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 quarts of good mutton broth
- 6 dozen oysters
- 2 oz. butter
- 1 oz. of flour

## Directions

1. Beard the oysters, and scald them in their own liquor; then add it, well strained, to the broth; thicken with the butter and flour, and simmer for 1/4 of an hour.
2. Put in the oysters, stir well, but do not let it boil, and serve very hot.

title: Oysters Fried in Batter
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of oysters
- 2 eggs
- 1/2 pint of milk
- sufficient flour to make the batter
- pepper and salt to taste
- a little nutmeg
- hot lard

## Directions

1. Scald the oysters in their own liquor, beard them, and lay them on a cloth, to drain thoroughly.
2. Break the eggs into a basin, mix the flour with them, add the milk gradually, with nutmeg and seasoning.
3. Put the oysters in the batter.
4. Make some lard hot in a deep frying-pan, put in the oysters, one at a time.
5. When done, take them up with a sharp-pointed skewer, and dish them on a napkin.

title: Pan Kail
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of cabbage, or Savoy greens
- 1/4 lb. of butter or dripping
- salt and pepper to taste
- oatmeal for thickening
- 2 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Chop the cabbage very fine, thicken the water with oatmeal, put in the cabbage and butter, or dripping; season and simmer for 1-1/2 hour.
2. It can be made sooner by blanching and mashing the greens, adding any good liquor that a joint has been boiled in, and then further thicken with bread or pounded biscuit.

title: Panada
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The crumb of 2 penny rolls
- 4 tablespoonfuls of white stock, No. 107
- 1 oz. of butter
- 1 slice of ham
- 1 bay-leaf
- a little minced parsley
- 2 shalots
- 1 clove
- 2 blades of mace
- a few mushrooms
- butter
- yolks of 2 eggs

## Directions

1. Soak the crumb of the rolls in milk for about 1/2 hour
2. Take it out, and squeeze so as to press the milk from it
3. Put the soaked bread into a stewpan with the white stock, and set it on one side
4. In a separate stewpan put 1 oz. of butter, a slice of lean ham cut small, with a bay-leaf, herbs, mushrooms, spices, &c., and fry them gently over a slow fire
5. When done, moisten with 2 teacupfuls of white stock, boil for 20 minutes, and strain the whole through a sieve over the panada in the other stewpan
6. Place it over the fire, keep constantly stirring, to prevent its burning
7. When quite dry, put in a small piece of butter
8. Let this again dry up by stirring over the fire
9. Add the yolks of 2 eggs, mix well
10. Put the panada to cool on a clean plate, and use it when required

title: Paradise Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 eggs
- 3 apples
- 1/4 lb. of bread crumbs
- 3 oz. of sugar
- 3 oz. of currants
- salt and grated nutmeg to taste
- the rind of 1/2 lemon
- 1/2 wineglassful of brandy

## Directions

1. Pare, core, and mince the apples into small pieces, and mix them with the other dry ingredients
2. beat up the eggs, moisten the mixture with these, and beat it well
3. stir in the brandy, and put the pudding into a buttered mould
4. tie it down with a cloth, boil for 1-1/2 hour, and serve with sweet sauce

title: Parsley and Butter, to Serve with Calf's Head, Boiled Fowls, &c.
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 tablespoonfuls of minced parsley
- 1/2 pint of melted butter
- water, slightly salted

## Directions

1. Put into a saucepan a small quantity of water, slightly salted, and when it boils, throw in a good bunch of parsley which has been previously washed and tied together in a bunch
2. Let it boil for 5 minutes, drain it, mince the leaves very fine, and put the above quantity in a tureen
3. Pour over it 1/2 pint of smoothly-made melted butter
4. Stir once, that the ingredients may be thoroughly mixed, and serve

title: Parsnip Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of sliced parsnips
- 2 oz. of butter
- salt and cayenne to taste
- 1 quart of stock No. 106

## Directions

1. Put the parsnips into the stewpan with the butter, which has been previously melted, and simmer them till quite tender.
2. Then add nearly a pint of stock, and boil together for half an hour.
3. Pass all through a fine strainer, and put to it the remainder of the stock.
4. Season, boil, and serve immediately.

title: Partridge Pie
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 partridges
- pepper
- salt
- 1 teaspoonful of minced parsley
- mushrooms
- 3/4 lb. of veal cutlet
- a slice of ham
- 1/2 pint of stock
- puff paste
- egg yolk
- butter

## Directions

1. Line a pie-dish with a veal cutlet; over that place a slice of ham and a seasoning of pepper and salt.
2. Pluck, draw, and wipe the partridges; cut off the legs at the first joint, and season them inside with pepper, salt, minced parsley, and a small piece of butter.
3. Place them in the dish, and pour over the stock.
4. Line the edges of the dish with puff paste, cover with the same, brush it over with the yolk of an egg, and bake for 3/4 to 1 hour.

title: Partridge Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- partridges
- butter
- ham
- onions
- celery
- cold game remains
- stock
- sugar
- carrot
- turnip
- salt and seasoning

## Directions

1. Cut the partridges into pieces, and braise them in the butter and ham until quite tender
2. Take out the legs, wings, and breast, and set them by
3. Keep the backs and other trimmings in the braise, and add the onions and celery
4. Add any remains of cold game and 3 pints of stock
5. Simmer slowly for 1 hour, strain it, and skim the fat off as clean as possible
6. Put in the pieces that were taken out, give it one boil, and skim again to have it quite clear
7. Add the sugar and seasoning
8. Simmer the cut carrot and turnip in 1 pint of stock
9. When quite tender, put them to the partridges, and serve

title: Partridge Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 partridges
- 3 slices of lean ham
- 2 shred onions
- 1 head of celery
- 1 large carrot
- 1 turnip cut into any fanciful shapes
- 1 small lump of sugar
- 2 oz. of butter
- salt and pepper to taste
- 2 quarts of stock No. 105, or common, No. 106

## Directions

1. Cut the partridges into pieces, and braise them in the butter and ham until quite tender
2. then take out the legs, wings, and breast, and set them by
3. Keep the backs and other trimmings in the braise, and add the onions and celery; any remains of cold game can be put in, and 3 pints of stock
4. Simmer slowly for 1 hour, strain it, and skim the fat off as clean as possible
5. put in the pieces that were taken out, give it one boil, and skim again to have it quite clear, and add the sugar and seasoning
6. Now simmer the cut carrot and turnip in 1 pint of stock
7. when quite tender, put them to the partridges, and serve

title: Paste for Tarts
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/4 lb. of flour
- 1/2 lb. of butter
- rather more than 1/2 pint of water

## Directions

1. Rub the butter lightly into the flour
2. Mix it to a smooth paste with the water
3. Roll it out 2 or 3 times, and it will be ready for use

title: Pastry Ramakins, to Serve with the Cheese Course
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Any pieces of very good light puff-paste
- Cheshire, Parmesan, or Stilton cheese
- yolk of egg

## Directions

1. Gather up the pieces of paste, roll it out evenly, and sprinkle it with grated cheese of a nice flavour.
2. Fold the paste in three, roll it out again, and sprinkle more cheese over; fold the paste, roll it out, and with a paste-cutter shape it in any way that may be desired.
3. Brush the ramakins over with yolk of egg before they are placed in the oven.
4. Bake the ramakins in a brisk oven from 10 to 15 minutes, dish them on a hot napkin, and serve quickly.

title: Pastry Sandwiches
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Puff-paste
- jam of any kind
- white of an egg
- sifted sugar

## Directions

1. Roll the paste out thin; put half of it on a baking-sheet or tin, and spread equally over it apricot, greengage, or any preserve that may be preferred
2. Lay over this preserve another thin paste; press the edges together all round
3. Mark the paste in lines with a knife on the surface, to show where to cut it when baked
4. Bake from 20 minutes to 1/2 hour
5. A short time before being done, take the pastry out of the oven, brush it over with the white of an egg, sift over pounded sugar, and put it back in the oven to colour
6. When cold, cut it into strips
7. Pile these on a dish pyramidically, and serve

title: Pate Brisee, or French Crust for Raised Pies
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb flour
- 1/2 saltspoonful of salt
- 2 eggs
- 1/3 pint water
- 6 oz butter

## Directions

1. Spread the flour, which should be sifted and thoroughly dry, on the paste-board
2. Make a hole in the centre, into which put the butter
3. Work it lightly into the flour
4. When quite fine, add the salt
5. Work the whole into a smooth paste with the eggs (yolks and whites) and water
6. Make it very firm
7. Knead the paste well, and let it be rather stiff, that the sides of the pie may be easily raised, and that they do not afterwards tumble or shrink

title: Pea Soup (Green)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 pints of green peas
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 2 or three thin slices of ham
- 6 onions sliced
- 4 shredded lettuces
- the crumb of 2 French rolls
- 2 handfuls of spinach
- 1 lump of sugar
- 2 quarts of common stock

## Directions

1. Put the butter, ham, 1 quart of the peas, onions, and lettuces, to a pint of stock, and simmer for an hour; then add the remainder of the stock, with the crumb of the French rolls, and boil for another hour.
2. Now boil the spinach, and squeeze it very dry.
3. Rub the soup through a sieve, and the spinach with it, to colour it.
4. Have ready a pint of young peas boiled; add them to the soup, put in the sugar, give one boil, and serve.
5. If necessary, add salt.

title: Pea Soup (Inexpensive)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of onions
- 1/4 lb. of carrots
- 2 oz. of celery
- 3/4 lb. of split peas
- a little mint, shred fine
- 1 tablespoonful of coarse brown sugar
- salt and pepper to taste
- 4 quarts of water, or liquor in which a joint of meat has been boiled
- a little butter or dripping

## Directions

1. Fry the vegetables for 10 minutes in a little butter or dripping, previously cutting them up in small pieces
2. Pour the water on them, and when boiling add the peas
3. Let them simmer for nearly 3 hours, or until the peas are thoroughly done
4. Add the sugar, seasoning, and mint
5. Boil for 1/4 of an hour, and serve

title: Peach Fritters
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of flour
- 1/2 oz. of butter
- 1/2 saltspoonful of salt
- 2 eggs
- milk
- peaches
- hot lard or clarified dripping

## Directions

1. Make a nice smooth batter in the same manner as directed in recipe No. 1393, and skin, halve, and stone the peaches, which should be quite ripe.
2. Dip them in the batter, and fry the pieces in hot lard or clarified dripping, which should be brought to the boiling-point before the peaches are put in.
3. From 8 to 10 minutes will be required to fry them, and, when done, drain them before the fire, and dish them on a white d'oyley.
4. Strew over plenty of pounded sugar, and serve.

title: Peaches Preserved in Brandy
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of finely-pounded loaf sugar to every lb. of fruit weighed before being stoned
- brandy

## Directions

1. Let the fruit be gathered in dry weather; wipe and weigh it, and remove the stones as carefully as possible, without injuring the peaches much
2. Put them into a jar, sprinkle amongst them pounded loaf sugar in the above proportion, and pour brandy over the fruit
3. Cover the jar down closely, place it in a saucepan of boiling water over the fire, and bring the brandy to the simmering-point, but do not allow it to boil
4. Take the fruit out carefully, without breaking it; put it into small jars, pour over it the brandy, and, when cold, exclude the air by covering the jars with bladders, or tissue-paper brushed over on both sides with the white of an egg

title: Pears à l'Allemande
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 to 8 pears
- water
- sugar
- 2 oz. of butter
- the yolk of an egg
- 1/2 oz. of gelatine

## Directions

1. Peel and cut the pears into any form that may be preferred, and steep them in cold water to prevent them turning black.
2. Put them into a saucepan with sufficient cold water to cover them, and boil them with the butter and enough sugar to sweeten them nicely, until tender.
3. Brush the pears over with the yolk of an egg, sprinkle them with sifted sugar, and arrange them on a dish.
4. Add the gelatine to the syrup, boil it up quickly for about 5 minutes, strain it over the pears, and let it remain until set.

title: Pease Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 pint of split peas
- 2 oz. of butter
- 2 eggs
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Put the peas to soak over-night, in rain-water, and float off any that are wormeaten or discoloured
2. Tie them loosely in a clean cloth, leaving a little room for them to swell, and put them on to boil in cold rain-water, allowing 2-1/2 hours after the water has simmered up
3. When the peas are tender, take them up and drain; rub them through a colander with a wooden spoon
4. add the butter, eggs, pepper, and salt
5. beat all well together for a few minutes, until the ingredients are well incorporated
6. then tie them tightly in a floured cloth; boil the pudding for another hour, turn it on to the dish, and serve very hot

title: Perch Stewed with Wine
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Equal quantities of stock No. 105 and sherry
- 1 bay-leaf
- 1 clove of garlic
- a small bunch of parsley
- 2 cloves
- salt to taste
- thickening of butter and flour
- pepper
- grated nutmeg
- 1/2 teaspoonful of anchovy sauce

## Directions

1. Scale the fish and take out the gills, and clean them thoroughly.
2. Lay them in a stewpan with sufficient stock and sherry just to cover them.
3. Put in the bay-leaf, garlic, parsley, cloves, and salt, and simmer till tender.
4. When done, take out the fish, strain the liquor, add a thickening of butter and flour, the pepper, nutmeg, and the anchovy sauce.
5. Stir it over the fire until somewhat reduced, when pour over the fish, and serve.

title: Petites Bouchees
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of sweet almonds
- 1/4 lb. of sifted sugar
- rind of 1/2 lemon
- white of 1 egg
- puff-paste

## Directions

1. Blanch the almonds, and chop them fine
2. Rub the sugar on the lemon-rind, and pound it in a mortar
3. Mix this with the almonds and the white of the egg
4. Roll some puff-paste out; cut it in any shape that may be preferred, such as diamonds, rings, ovals, &c., and spread the above mixture over the paste
5. Bake the bouchées in an oven, not too hot, and serve cold

title: Pheasant Cutlets
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 or 3 pheasants
- egg and bread crumbs
- cayenne and salt to taste
- brown gravy
- stock
- herbs
- vegetables
- seasoning

## Directions

1. Procure 3 young pheasants that have been hung a few days
2. Pluck, draw, and wipe them inside
3. Cut them into joints
4. Remove the bones from the best of these
5. Put the backbones, trimmings, &c., into a stewpan, with a little stock, herbs, vegetables, seasoning, &c., to make the gravy
6. Flatten and trim the cutlets of a good shape
7. Egg and bread crumb them
8. Broil them over a clear fire
9. Pile them high in the dish
10. Pour under them the gravy made from the bones, which should be strained, flavoured, and thickened
11. Stick one of the small bones on the point of each cutlet

title: Pheasant Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 pheasants
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 2 slices of ham
- 2 large onions sliced
- 1/2 head of celery
- crumb of two French rolls
- yolks of 2 eggs boiled hard
- salt and cayenne to taste
- pounded mace
- 3 quarts of stock No. 105

## Directions

1. Cut up the pheasants, flour and braise them in the butter and ham till they are of a nice brown, but not burnt
2. Put them in a stewpan, with the onions, celery, and seasoning, and simmer for 2 hours
3. Strain the soup
4. Pound the breasts with the crumb of the roll previously soaked, and the yolks of the eggs
5. Put it to the soup, give one boil, and serve

title: Pickled Beetroot
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Sufficient vinegar to cover the beets
- 2 oz. of whole pepper
- 2 oz. of allspice to each gallon of vinegar

## Directions

1. Wash the beets free from dirt, and be very careful not to prick the outside skin, or they would lose their beautiful colour.
2. Put them into boiling water, let them simmer gently, and when about three parts done, which will be in 1-1/2 hour, take them out and let them cool.
3. Boil the vinegar with pepper and allspice, in the above proportion, for ten minutes, and when cold, pour it on the beets, which must be peeled and cut into slices about 1/2 inch thick.
4. Cover with bladder to exclude the air, and in a week they will be fit for use.

title: Pickled Capsicums
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Capsicum pods with stalks
- Vinegar
- 1/4 oz. of pounded mace per quart of vinegar
- 1/4 oz. of grated nutmeg per quart of vinegar
- Brine

## Directions

1. Gather the pods with the stalks on, before they turn red; slit them down the side with a small-pointed knife, and remove the seeds only.
2. Put them in a strong brine for 3 days, changing it every morning.
3. Take them out, lay them on a cloth, with another one over them, until they are perfectly free from moisture.
4. Boil sufficient vinegar to cover them, with mace and nutmeg in the above proportions.
5. Put the pods in a jar, pour over the vinegar when cold, and exclude them from the air by means of a wet bladder tied over.

title: Pickled Cucumbers
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 oz. of whole pepper
- 1 oz. of bruised ginger
- sufficient vinegar to cover the cucumbers
- salt

## Directions

1. Cut the cucumbers in thick slices
2. Sprinkle salt over them
3. Let them remain for 24 hours
4. The next day, drain them well for 6 hours
5. Put them into a jar
6. Pour boiling vinegar over them
7. Keep them in a warm place
8. In a short time, boil up the vinegar again
9. Add pepper and ginger in the above proportion
10. Instantly cover them up
11. Tie them down with bladder
12. In a few days they will be fit for use

title: Pickled Gherkins
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- salt and water
- 1 oz. of bruised ginger
- 1/2 oz. of whole black pepper
- 1/4 oz. of whole allspice
- 4 cloves
- 2 blades of mace
- a little horseradish
- 1 quart of vinegar

## Directions

1. Let the gherkins remain in salt and water for 3 or 4 days
2. Take them out and wipe perfectly dry
3. Put them into a stone jar
4. Boil sufficient vinegar to cover them with spices and pepper in the stated proportions for 10 minutes
5. Pour it, quite boiling, over the gherkins
6. Cover the jar with vine-leaves and put over them a plate
7. Set them near the fire where they must remain all night
8. Next day drain off the vinegar
9. Boil it up again and pour it hot over the gherkins
10. Cover up with fresh leaves and let the whole remain till quite cold
11. Tie down closely with bladder to exclude the air
12. In a month or two, they will be fit for use

title: Pickled Mackerel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 12 peppercorns
- 2 bay-leaves
- 1/2 pint of vinegar
- 4 mackerel

## Directions

1. Boil the mackerel as in the recipe No. 282, and lay them in a dish; take half the liquor they were boiled in; add as much vinegar, peppercorns, and bay-leaves; boil for 10 minutes, and when cold, pour over the fish.

title: Pickled Mushrooms
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Vinegar
- mushrooms
- 2 blades of pounded mace per quart of mushrooms
- 1 oz. of ground pepper per quart of mushrooms
- salt

## Directions

1. Choose some nice young button mushrooms for pickling, and rub off the skin with a piece of flannel and salt, and cut off the stalks.
2. If very large, take out the red inside, and reject the black ones, as they are too old.
3. Put them in a stewpan, sprinkle salt over them, with pounded mace and pepper in the above proportion.
4. Shake them well over a clear fire until the liquor flows, and keep them there until it is all dried up again.
5. Add as much vinegar as will cover them.
6. Just let it simmer for 1 minute, and store it away in stone jars for use.
7. When cold, tie down with bladder and keep in a dry place.

title: Pickled Nasturtiums
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- nasturtium-pods
- vinegar (1 pint per batch)
- salt (1 oz. per pint of vinegar)
- peppercorns (6 per pint of vinegar)

## Directions

1. Gather the nasturtium-pods on a dry day, and wipe them clean with a cloth
2. Put them in a dry glass bottle, with vinegar, salt, and pepper in the above proportion
3. If you cannot find enough ripe to fill a bottle, cork up what you have got until you have some more fit; they may be added from day to day
4. Bung up the bottles, and seal or rosin the tops
5. They will be fit for use in 10 or 12 months; the best way is to make them one season for the next

title: Pickled Onions
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Pickling onions
- vinegar (1 quart)
- allspice (2 teaspoonfuls per quart of vinegar)
- whole black pepper (2 teaspoonfuls per quart of vinegar)

## Directions

1. Have the onions gathered when quite dry and ripe, and, with the fingers, take off the thin outside skin
2. With a silver knife, remove one more skin, when the onion will look quite clear
3. Have ready some very dry bottles or jars, and as fast as they are peeled, put them in
4. Pour over sufficient cold vinegar to cover them, with pepper and allspice in the above proportions, taking care that each jar has its share of the latter ingredients
5. Tie down with bladder, and put them in a dry place, and in a fortnight they will be fit for use

title: Pickled Onions
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 gallon of pickling onions
- salt and water
- milk
- 1/2 gallon of vinegar
- 1 oz. of bruised ginger
- 1/4 teaspoonful of cayenne
- 1 oz. of allspice
- 1 oz. of whole black pepper
- 1/4 oz. of whole nutmeg bruised
- 8 cloves
- 1/4 oz. of mace
- 2 large spoonfuls of salt
- olive-oil

## Directions

1. Gather the onions, which should not be too small, when they are quite dry and ripe; wipe off the dirt, but do not pare them
2. Make a strong solution of salt and water, into which put the onions, and change this, morning and night, for 3 days, and save the last brine they were put in
3. Take the outside skin off, and put them into a tin saucepan capable of holding them all
4. Take equal quantities of milk and the last salt and water the onions were in, and pour this to them; to this add 2 large spoonfuls of salt, put them over the fire, and watch them very attentively
5. Keep constantly turning the onions about with a wooden skimmer, those at the bottom to the top, and vice versa; and let the milk and water run through the holes of the skimmer
6. Remember, the onions must never boil, or, if they do, they will be good for nothing; and they should be quite transparent
7. Keep the onions stirred for a few minutes, and, in stirring them, be particular not to break them
8. Turn the onions into a pan with a colander to drain, covering them with a cloth to keep in the steam
9. Place on a table an old cloth, 2 or 3 times double; put the onions on it when quite hot, and over them an old piece of blanket; cover this closely over them, to keep in the steam
10. Let them remain till the next day, when they will be quite cold, and look yellow and shrivelled; take off the shrivelled skins, when they should be as white as snow
11. Put them in a pan, make a pickle of vinegar and the remaining ingredients, boil all these up, and pour hot over the onions in the pan
12. Cover very closely to keep in all the steam, and let them stand till the following day, when they will be quite cold
13. Put them into jars or bottles well bunged, and a tablespoonful of the best olive-oil on the top of each jar or bottle
14. Tie them down with bladder, and let them stand in a cool place for a month or six weeks, when they will be fit for use

title: Pickled Oysters
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 100 oysters
- 1/2 pint of vinegar
- 1 blade of pounded mace
- 1 strip of lemon-peel
- 12 black peppercorns

## Directions

1. Get the oysters in good condition, open them, place them in a saucepan, and let them simmer in their own liquor for about 10 minutes, very gently
2. Take them out, one by one, and place them in a jar, and cover them, when cold, with a pickle made as follows
3. Measure the oyster-liquor; add to it the same quantity of vinegar, with mace, lemon-peel, and pepper in the above proportion, and boil it for 5 minutes
4. When cold, pour over the oysters, and tie them down very closely, as contact with the air spoils them
5. Put this pickle away in small jars; because directly one is opened, its contents should immediately be eaten, as they soon spoil

title: Pickled Red Cabbage
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Red cabbages
- salt
- water
- vinegar
- ginger well bruised (1/2 oz. to each quart of vinegar)
- whole black pepper (1 oz. to each quart of vinegar)
- cayenne (when liked, a little)
- bruised cochineal (optional)

## Directions

1. Take off the outside decayed leaves of a nice red cabbage, cut it in quarters, remove the stalks, and cut it across in very thin slices.
2. Lay these on a dish, and strew them plentifully with salt, covering them with another dish.
3. Let them remain for 24 hours, turn into a colander to drain, and, if necessary, wipe lightly with a clean soft cloth.
4. Put them in a jar; boil up the vinegar with spices in the above proportion, and, when cold, pour it over the cabbage.
5. Tie down with bladder, and keep in a dry place.
6. It will be fit for use in a week or two. To be really nice and crisp, and of a good red colour, it should be eaten almost immediately after it is made.

title: Pickled Salmon
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Salmon
- 1/2 oz. of whole pepper
- 1/2 oz. of whole allspice
- 1 teaspoonful of salt
- 2 bay-leaves
- equal quantities of vinegar and the liquor in which the fish was boiled

## Directions

1. After the fish comes from table, lay it in a nice dish with a cover to it, as it should be excluded from the air
2. take away the bone
3. boil the liquor and vinegar with the other ingredients for 10 minutes
4. let it stand to get cold
5. pour it over the salmon
6. in 12 hours this will be fit for the table

title: Pickled Walnuts
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 100 walnuts
- salt and water
- 2 oz. of whole black pepper per quart of vinegar
- 1 oz. of allspice per quart of vinegar
- 1 oz. of bruised ginger per quart of vinegar

## Directions

1. Procure the walnuts while young; be careful they are not woody, and prick them well with a fork
2. Prepare a strong brine of salt and water (4 lbs. of salt to each gallon of water), into which put the walnuts, letting them remain 9 days, and changing the brine every third day
3. Drain them off, put them on a dish, place it in the sun until they become perfectly black, which will be in 2 or 3 days
4. Have ready dry jars, into which place the walnuts, and do not quite fill the jars
5. Boil sufficient vinegar to cover them, for 10 minutes, with spices in the above proportion, and pour it hot over the walnuts, which must be quite covered with the pickle
6. Tie down with bladder, and keep in a dry place
7. They will be fit for use in a month, and will keep good 2 or 3 years

title: Pig's Liver (A Savoury and Economical Dish)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The liver and lights of a pig
- 6 or 7 slices of bacon
- Potatoes
- 1 large bunch of parsley
- 2 onions
- 2 sage-leaves
- Pepper and salt to taste
- A little broth or water

## Directions

1. Slice the liver and lights, and wash these perfectly clean
2. Parboil the potatoes
3. Mince the parsley and sage, and chop the onion rather small
4. Put the meat, potatoes, and bacon into a deep tin dish, in alternate layers, with a sprinkling of the herbs, and a seasoning of pepper and salt between each
5. Pour on a little water or broth
6. Bake in a moderately-heated oven for 2 hours

title: Pig's Pettitoes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A thin slice of bacon
- 1 onion
- 1 blade of mace
- 6 peppercorns
- 3 or 4 sprigs of thyme
- 1 pint of gravy
- Pepper and salt to taste
- Thickening of butter and flour

## Directions

1. Put the liver, heart, and pettitoes into a stewpan with the bacon, mace, peppercorns, thyme, onion, and gravy
2. Simmer these gently for 1/4 hour
3. Take out the heart and liver, and mince them very fine
4. Keep stewing the feet until quite tender, which will be in from 20 minutes to 1/2 hour, reckoning from the time that they boiled up first
5. Put back the minced liver
6. Thicken the gravy with a little butter and flour
7. Season with pepper and salt
8. Simmer over a gentle fire for 5 minutes, occasionally stirring the contents
9. Dish the mince, split the feet, and arrange them round alternately with sippets of toasted bread
10. Pour the gravy in the middle

title: Pigeon Pie (Epsom Grand-Stand Recipe)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 lb. of rump-steak
- 2 or 3 pigeons
- 3 slices of ham
- pepper and salt to taste
- 2 oz. of butter
- 4 eggs
- puff crust
- stock
- pounded mace

## Directions

1. Cut the steak into pieces about 3 inches square, and with it line the bottom of a pie-dish, seasoning it well with pepper and salt.
2. Clean the pigeons, rub them with pepper and salt inside and out, and put into the body of each rather more than 1/2 oz. of butter.
3. Lay them on the steak, and a piece of ham on each pigeon.
4. Add the yolks of 4 eggs, and half fill the dish with stock.
5. Place a border of puff paste round the edge of the dish, put on the cover, and ornament it in any way that may be preferred.
6. Clean three of the feet, and place them in a hole made in the crust at the top: this shows what kind of pie it is.
7. Glaze the crust,--that is to say, brush it over with the yolk of an egg.
8. Bake it in a well-heated oven for about 1-1/4 hour.
9. When liked, a seasoning of pounded mace may be added.

title: Pineapple Chips
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Pineapples
- sugar to taste

## Directions

1. Pare and slice the fruit thinly, put it on dishes, and strew over it plenty of pounded sugar.
2. Keep it in a hot closet, or very slow oven, 8 or 10 days, and turn the fruit every day until dry; then put the pieces of pine on tins, and place them in a quick oven for 10 minutes.
3. Let them cool, and store them away in dry boxes, with paper between each layer.

title: Pineapple Fritters
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A small pineapple
- a small wineglassful of brandy or liqueur
- 2 oz. of sifted sugar
- batter as for apple fritters

## Directions

1. Pare the pine with as little waste as possible
2. cut it into rather thin slices
3. soak these slices in the brandy or liqueur and pounded sugar for 4 hours
4. make a batter the same as for apple fritters, substituting cream for the milk, and using a smaller quantity of flour
5. dip in the pieces of pine, and fry them in boiling lard from 5 to 8 minutes
6. turn them when sufficiently brown on one side
7. drain them from the lard before the fire
8. dish them on a white d'oyley
9. strew over them sifted sugar
10. serve quickly

title: Plain Buns
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of flour
- 6 oz. of moist sugar
- 1/2 gill of yeast
- 1/2 pint of milk
- 1/2 lb. of butter
- warm milk

## Directions

1. Put the flour into a basin, mix the sugar well with it, make a hole in the centre, and stir in the yeast and milk (which should be lukewarm), with enough of the flour to make it the thickness of cream
2. Cover the basin over with a cloth, and let the sponge rise in a warm place, which will be accomplished in about 1-1/2 hour
3. Melt the butter, but do not allow it to oil
4. Stir it into the other ingredients, with enough warm milk to make the whole into a soft dough
5. Mould it into buns about the size of an egg
6. Lay them in rows quite 3 inches apart
7. Set them again in a warm place, until they have risen to double their size
8. Put them into a good brisk oven
9. Just before they are done, wash them over with a little milk
10. Bake for from 15 to 20 minutes

title: Plain Fritters
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 oz. of flour
- 3 eggs
- 1/3 pint of milk

## Directions

1. Mix the flour to a smooth batter with a small quantity of the milk
2. stir in the eggs, which should be well whisked, and then the remainder of the milk
3. beat the whole to a perfectly smooth batter, and should it be found not quite thin enough, add two or three tablespoonfuls more milk
4. Have ready a frying-pan, with plenty of boiling lard in it
5. drop in rather more than a tablespoonful at a time of the batter, and fry the fritters a nice brown, turning them when sufficiently cooked on one side
6. Drain them well from the greasy moisture by placing them upon a piece of blotting-paper before the fire
7. dish them on a white d'oyley
8. sprinkle over them sifted sugar
9. send to table with them a cut lemon and plenty of pounded sugar

title: Plain Lemon Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 lb. of flour
- 6 oz. of lard or dripping
- the juice of 1 large lemon
- 1 teaspoonful of flour
- sugar

## Directions

1. Make the above proportions of flour and lard into a smooth paste, and roll it out to the thickness of about 1/2 inch.
2. Squeeze the lemon-juice, strain it into a cup, stir the flour into it, and as much moist sugar as will make it into a stiff and thick paste; spread this mixture over the paste, roll it up, secure the ends, and tie the pudding in a floured cloth.
3. Boil for 2 hours.

title: Plain Sweet Omelet
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 eggs
- 4 oz. of butter
- 2 oz. of sifted sugar

## Directions

1. Break the eggs into a basin, omitting the whites of 3
2. Whisk them well, adding the sugar and 2 oz. of the butter, which should be broken into small pieces, and stir all these ingredients well together
3. Make the remainder of the butter quite hot in a small frying-pan, and when it commences to bubble, pour in the eggs
4. Keep stirring them until they begin to set
5. Turn the edges of the omelet over, to make it an oval shape, and finish cooking it
6. To brown the top, hold the pan before the fire, or use a salamander, and turn it carefully on to a very hot dish
7. Sprinkle sifted sugar over, and serve

title: Plum Jam
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- plums, weighed before being stoned
- 3/4 lb. of loaf sugar to every lb. of plums

## Directions

1. Divide the plums, take out the stones, and put them on to large dishes, with roughly-pounded sugar sprinkled over them in the above proportion, and let them remain for one day.
2. Put them into a preserving-pan, stand them by the side of the fire to simmer gently for about 1/2 hour, and then boil them rapidly for another 15 minutes.
3. The scum must be carefully removed as it rises, and the jam must be well stirred all the time, or it will burn at the bottom of the pan, and so spoil the colour and flavour of the preserve.
4. Some of the stones may be cracked, and a few kernels added to the jam just before it is done: these impart a very delicious flavour to the plums.

title: Plum Tart
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of good short crust No. 1211
- 1-1/2 pint of plums
- 1/4 lb. of moist sugar

## Directions

1. Line the edges of a deep tart-dish with crust made by recipe No. 1211; fill the dish with plums, and place a small cup or jar, upside down, in the midst of them.
2. Put in the sugar, cover the pie with crust, ornament the edges, and bake in a good oven from 1/2 to 3/4 hour.
3. When puff-crust is preferred to short crust, use that made by recipe No. 1206, and glaze the top by brushing it over with the white of an egg beaten to a stiff froth with a knife; sprinkle over a little sifted sugar, and put the pie in the oven to set the glaze.

title: Plum-Pudding of Fresh Fruit
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 lb. of suet crust No. 1
- 1-1/2 pint of Orleans or any other kind of plum
- 1/4 lb. of moist sugar

## Directions

1. Line a pudding-basin with suet crust rolled out to the thickness of about 1/2 inch; fill the basin with the fruit, put in the sugar, and cover with crust.
2. Fold the edges over, and pinch them together, to prevent the juice escaping.
3. Tie over a floured cloth, put the pudding into boiling water, and boil from 2 to 2-1/2 hours.
4. Turn it out of the basin, and serve quickly.

title: Plum-Pudding Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 wineglassful of brandy
- 2 oz. of very fresh butter
- 1 glass of Madeira
- pounded sugar to taste

## Directions

1. Put the pounded sugar in a basin, with part of the brandy and the butter.
2. Let it stand by the side of the fire until it is warm and the sugar and butter are dissolved.
3. Add the rest of the brandy, with the Madeira.
4. Either pour it over the pudding, or serve in a tureen.

title: Poached Eggs
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Eggs
- water
- 1 tablespoonful of vinegar to every pint of water

## Directions

1. Eggs for poaching should be perfectly fresh, but not quite new-laid; those that are about 36 hours old are the best for the purpose
2. Strain some boiling water into a deep clean frying-pan; break the egg into a cup without damaging the yolk
3. when the water boils, remove the pan to the side of the fire, and gently slip the egg into it
4. Place the pan over a gentle fire, and keep the water simmering until the white looks nicely set
5. Take it up gently with a slice, cut away the ragged edges of the white, and serve either on toasted bread or on slices of ham or bacon, or on spinach
6. When the egg is slipped into the water, the white should be gathered together, to keep it a little in form, or the cup should be turned over it for 1 minute
7. In poaching eggs in a frying-pan, never do more than four at a time

title: Poached Eggs, with Cream
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of water
- 1 teaspoonful of salt
- 4 teaspoonfuls of vinegar
- 4 fresh eggs
- 1/2 gill of cream
- salt, pepper, and pounded sugar to taste
- 1 oz. of butter

## Directions

1. Put the water, vinegar, and salt into a frying-pan, and break each egg into a separate cup
2. bring the water to boil, and slip the eggs gently into it without breaking the yolks
3. Simmer them from 3 to 4 minutes, but not longer
4. with a slice, lift them out on to a hot dish, and trim the edges
5. Empty the pan of its contents, put in the cream, add a seasoning to taste of pepper, salt, and pounded sugar
6. bring the whole to the boiling-point; then add the butter, broken into small pieces
7. toss the pan round and round till the butter is melted
8. pour it over the eggs, and serve

title: Polish for Bright Stoves and Steel Articles
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 tablespoonful of turpentine
- 1 tablespoonful of sweet oil
- emery powder

## Directions

1. Mix the turpentine and sweet oil together, stirring in sufficient emery powder to make the mixture of the thickness of cream.
2. Put it on the article with a piece of soft flannel, rub off quickly with another piece, then polish with a little dry emery powder and clean leather.

title: Polish Tartlets
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Puff-paste
- white of an egg
- pounded sugar
- apricot jam or marmalade or red-currant jelly

## Directions

1. Roll some good puff-paste out thin, and cut it into 2-1/2-inch squares
2. Brush each square over with the white of an egg, then fold down the corners, so that they all meet in the middle of each piece of paste
3. Slightly press the two pieces together, brush them over with the egg, sift over sugar, and bake in a nice quick oven for about 1/4 hour
4. When they are done, make a little hole in the middle of the paste, and fill it up with apricot jam, marmalade, or red-currant jelly
5. Pile them high in the centre of a dish, on a napkin, and garnish with the same preserve the tartlets are filled with

title: Pomade for the Hair
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of lard
- 2 pennyworth of castor-oil
- scent

## Directions

1. Let the lard be unsalted
2. Beat it up well
3. Add the castor-oil, and mix thoroughly together with a knife, adding a few drops of any scent that may be preferred
4. Put the pomatum into pots, which keep well covered to prevent it turning rancid

title: Pork Cheese (an Excellent Breakfast Dish)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of cold roast pork
- pepper
- salt
- 1 dessertspoonful of minced parsley
- 4 leaves of sage
- a very small bunch of savoury herbs
- 2 blades of pounded mace
- a little nutmeg
- 1/2 teaspoonful of minced lemon-peel
- good strong gravy

## Directions

1. Cut, but do not chop, the pork into fine pieces, and allow 1/4 lb. of fat to each pound of lean.
2. Season with pepper and salt.
3. Pound well the spices, and chop finely the parsley, sage, herbs, and lemon-peel, and mix the whole nicely together.
4. Put it into a mould, fill up with good strong well-flavoured gravy, and bake rather more than one hour.
5. When cold, turn it out of the mould.

title: Pork Cutlets (Cold Meat Cookery)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of cold roast loin of pork
- 1 oz. of butter
- 2 onions
- 1 dessertspoonful of flour
- 1/2 pint of gravy
- pepper and salt to taste
- 1 teaspoonful of vinegar and mustard

## Directions

1. Cut the pork into nice-sized cutlets, trim off most of the fat, and chop the onions.
2. Put the butter into a stewpan, lay in the cutlets and chopped onions, and fry a light brown.
3. Add the remaining ingredients, simmer gently for 5 or 7 minutes, and serve.

title: Pork Cutlets or Chops
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Loin of pork
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Cut the cutlets from a delicate loin of pork, bone and trim them neatly, and cut away the greater portion of the fat.
2. Season them with pepper.
3. Place the gridiron on the fire; when quite hot, lay on the chops and broil them for about 1/4 hour, turning them 3 or 4 times.
4. Be particular that they are thoroughly done, but not dry.
5. Dish them, sprinkle over a little fine salt, and serve plain, or with tomato sauce, sauce piquante, or pickled gherkins, a few of which should be laid round the dish as a garnish.

title: Pork Cutlets or Chops (Another Way)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Loin or fore-loin of pork
- egg
- bread crumbs
- salt
- pepper
- minced sage
- clarified butter
- lard

## Directions

1. Cut the cutlets from a loin, or fore-loin, of pork; trim them the same as mutton cutlets, and scrape the top part of the bone.
2. Brush them over with egg, sprinkle with bread crumbs, with which have been mixed minced sage and a seasoning of pepper and salt.
3. Drop a little clarified butter on them, and press the crumbs well down.
4. Put the frying-pan on the fire, put in some lard; when this is hot, lay in the cutlets, and fry them a light brown on both sides.
5. Take them out, put them before the fire to dry the greasy moisture from them, and dish them on mashed potatoes.
6. Serve with them any sauce that may be preferred; such as tomato sauce, sauce piquante, sauce Robert, or pickled gherkins.

title: Pork Pies (Warwickshire Recipe)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 5 lbs. of lard
- 14 lbs. of flour
- milk and water
- 3 lbs. of meat
- 1 oz. of salt
- 2-1/4 oz. of pepper
- a small quantity of cayenne
- 1 pint of water

## Directions

1. Rub into the flour a portion of the lard; the remainder put with sufficient milk and water to mix the crust, and boil this gently for 1/4 hour.
2. Pour it boiling on the flour, and knead and beat it till perfectly smooth.
3. Raise the crust in either a round or oval form.
4. Cut up the pork into pieces the size of a nut, season it in the above proportion, and press it compactly into the pie, in alternate layers of fat and lean, and pour in a small quantity of water.
5. Lay on the lid, cut the edges smoothly round, and pinch them together.
6. Bake in a brick oven, which should be slow, as the meat is very solid.
7. Keep the crust warm during the operation to prevent it from falling; a glass bottle or small jar may be placed in the middle of the paste, and the crust moulded on this.

title: Portable Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 knuckles of veal
- 3 shins of beef
- 1 large faggot of herbs
- 2 bay-leaves
- 2 heads of celery
- 3 onions
- 3 carrots
- 2 blades of mace
- 6 cloves
- a teaspoonful of salt
- sufficient water to cover all the ingredients

## Directions

1. Take the marrow from the bones
2. Put all the ingredients in a stock-pot, and simmer slowly for 12 hours, or more, if the meat be not done to rags
3. Strain it off, and put it in a very cool place
4. Take off all the fat, reduce the liquor in a shallow pan, by setting it over a sharp fire, but be particular that it does not burn
5. Boil it fast and uncovered for 8 hours, and keep it stirred
6. Put it into a deep dish, and set it by for a day
7. Have ready a stewpan of boiling water, place the dish in it, and keep it boiling
8. Stir occasionally, and when the soup is thick and ropy, it is done
9. Form it into little cakes by pouring a small quantity on to the bottom of cups or basins
10. When cold, turn them out on a flannel to dry
11. Keep them from the air in tin canisters

title: Potage Printanier, or Spring Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 a pint of green peas, if in season
- a little chervil
- 2 shredded lettuces
- 2 onions
- a very small bunch of parsley
- 2 oz. of butter
- the yolks of 3 eggs
- 1 pint of water
- seasoning to taste
- 2 quarts of stock No. 105

## Directions

1. Put in a very clean stewpan the chervil, lettuces, onions, parsley, and butter, to 1 pint of water, and let them simmer till tender
2. Season with salt and pepper
3. When done, strain off the vegetables, and put two-thirds of the liquor they were boiled in to the stock
4. Beat up the yolks of the eggs with the other third, give it a toss over the fire
5. At the moment of serving, add this, with the vegetables which you strained off, to the soup

title: Potato Fritters
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 large potatoes
- 4 eggs
- 2 tablespoonfuls of cream
- 2 tablespoonfuls of raisin or sweet wine
- 1 dessertspoonful of lemon-juice
- 4 teaspoonful of grated nutmeg
- hot lard

## Directions

1. Boil the potatoes, and beat them up lightly with a fork, but do not use a spoon, as that would make them heavy
2. Beat the eggs well, leaving out one of the whites
3. add the other ingredients, and beat all together for at least 20 minutes, or until the batter is extremely light
4. Put plenty of good lard into a frying-pan, and drop a tablespoonful of the batter at a time into it, and fry the fritters a nice brown
5. Serve them with the following sauce: A glass of sherry mixed with the strained juice of a lemon, and sufficient white sugar to sweeten the whole nicely
6. Warm these ingredients, and serve the sauce separately in a tureen
7. The fritters should be neatly dished on a white d'oyley, and pounded sugar sprinkled over them
8. they should be well drained on a piece of blotting-paper before the fire previously to being dished

title: Potato Pasty
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 lb. of rump-steak or mutton cutlets
- pepper and salt to taste
- 1/3 pint of weak broth or gravy
- 1 oz. of butter
- mashed potatoes
- a little milk

## Directions

1. Place the meat, cut in small pieces, at the bottom of the pan; season it with pepper and salt, and add the gravy and butter broken into small pieces.
2. Put on the perforated plate, with its valve-pipe screwed on, and fill up the whole space to the top of the tube with nicely-mashed potatoes mixed with a little milk, and finish the surface of them in any ornamental manner.
3. If carefully baked, the potatoes will be covered with a delicate brown crust, retaining all the savoury steam.

title: Potato Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of mashed potatoes
- 2 oz. of butter
- 2 eggs
- 1/4 pint of milk
- 3 tablespoonfuls of sherry
- 1/4 saltspoonful of salt
- the juice and rind of 1 small lemon
- 2 oz. of sugar

## Directions

1. Boil sufficient potatoes to make 1/2 lb. when mashed
2. Add to these the butter, eggs, milk, sherry, lemon-juice, and sugar
3. Mince the lemon-peel very finely, and beat all the ingredients well together
4. Put the pudding into a buttered pie-dish, and bake for rather more than 1/2 hour

title: Potato Rissoles
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Mashed potatoes
- salt and pepper to taste
- a very little minced parsley (when liked)
- egg
- bread crumbs
- hot lard

## Directions

1. Boil and mash the potatoes by recipe No. 1145
2. Add a seasoning of pepper and salt, and, when liked, a little minced parsley
3. Roll the potatoes into small balls
4. Cover them with egg and bread crumbs
5. Fry in hot lard for about 10 minutes
6. Let them drain before the fire
7. Dish them on a napkin
8. Serve

title: Potato Salad
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 10 or 12 cold boiled potatoes
- 4 tablespoonfuls of tarragon or plain vinegar
- 6 tablespoonfuls of salad-oil
- pepper and salt to taste
- 1 teaspoonful of minced parsley

## Directions

1. Cut the potatoes into slices about 1/2 inch in thickness
2. put these into a salad-bowl with oil and vinegar in the above proportion
3. season with pepper, salt, and a teaspoonful of minced parsley
4. stir the salad well, that all the ingredients may be thoroughly incorporated
5. This should be made two or three hours before it is wanted for table. Anchovies, olives, or pickles may be added to this salad, as also slices of cold beef, fowl, or turkey.

title: Potato Snow
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Large white potatoes
- salt
- water

## Directions

1. Choose large white potatoes, as free from spots as possible
2. Boil them in their skins in salt and water until perfectly tender
3. Drain and dry them thoroughly by the side of the fire
4. Peel them
5. Put a hot dish before the fire
6. Rub the potatoes through a coarse sieve on to this dish
7. Do not touch them afterwards, or the flakes will fall
8. Serve as hot as possible

title: Potato Soup I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 lbs. of mealy potatoes, boiled or steamed very dry
- pepper and salt to taste
- 2 quarts of stock No. 105

## Directions

1. When the potatoes are boiled, mash them smoothly, that no lumps remain
2. Gradually put them to the boiling stock
3. Pass it through a sieve, season, and simmer for 5 minutes
4. Skim well, and serve with fried bread

title: Potato Soup II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of shin of beef
- 1 lb. of potatoes
- 1 onion
- 1/2 a pint of peas
- 2 oz. of rice
- 2 heads of celery
- pepper and salt to taste
- 3 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Cut the beef into thin slices, chop the potatoes and onion, and put them in a stewpan with the water, peas, and rice
2. Stew gently till the gravy is drawn from the meat
3. Strain it off, take out the beef, and pulp the other ingredients through a coarse sieve
4. Put the pulp back in the soup, cut up the celery in it, and simmer till this is tender
5. Season, and serve with fried bread cut into it

title: Potato Soup III (Very Economical)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 middle-sized potatoes well pared
- a thick slice of bread
- 6 leeks peeled and cut into thin slices as far as the white extends upwards from the roots
- a teacupful of rice
- a teaspoonful of salt
- half that of pepper
- 2 quarts of water

## Directions

1. The water must be completely boiling before anything is put into it
2. Then add the whole of the ingredients at once, with the exception of the rice, the salt, and the pepper
3. Cover, and let these come to a brisk boil
