4. Put in the others, and let the whole boil slowly for an hour, or till all the ingredients are thoroughly done, and their several juices extracted and mixed

title: Potatoes a la Maitre d'Hotel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Potatoes
- salt and water
- 1 tablespoonful of minced parsley to every 6 potatoes
- 2 oz. of butter
- pepper and salt to taste
- 4 tablespoonfuls of gravy
- 2 tablespoonfuls of lemon-juice

## Directions

1. Wash the potatoes clean, and boil them in salt and water by recipe No. 1138
2. When they are done, drain them, let them cool
3. Peel and cut the potatoes into thick slices
4. Put the butter into a stewpan with the pepper, salt, gravy, and parsley; mix these ingredients well together
5. Put in the potatoes, shake them two or three times, that they may be well covered with the sauce
6. When quite hot through, squeeze in the lemon-juice, and serve

title: Potted Anchovies
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- anchovies
- pounded mace
- cayenne
- nutmeg

## Directions

1. Pound the anchovies in a mortar to a paste
2. Mix the butter gradually with them
3. Rub the whole through a sieve
4. Put it by in small pots for use
5. Carefully exclude the air with a bladder

title: Potted Beef (Cold Meat Cookery) II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of cold roast or boiled beef
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- cayenne to taste
- 2 blades of pounded mace

## Directions

1. Cut up the meat into small pieces and pound it well, with a little butter, in a mortar
2. Add a seasoning of cayenne and mace, and be very particular that the latter ingredient is reduced to the finest powder
3. When all the ingredients are thoroughly mixed, put it into glass or earthen potting-pots, and pour on the top a coating of clarified butter
4. If cold roast beef is used, remove all pieces of gristle and dry outside pieces, as these do not pound well

title: Potted Beef I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of lean beef
- 1 tablespoonful of water
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- salt to taste
- cayenne to taste
- pounded mace to taste
- black pepper to taste

## Directions

1. Procure a nice piece of lean beef, as free as possible from gristle, skin, &c., and put it into a jar (if at hand, one with a lid) with 1 tablespoonful of water
2. Cover it closely, and put the jar into a saucepan of boiling water, letting the water come within 2 inches of the top of the jar
3. Boil gently for 3-1/2 hours
4. Take the beef, chop it very small with a chopping-knife, and pound it thoroughly in a mortar
5. Mix with it by degrees all, or a portion, of the gravy that will have run from it, and a little clarified butter
6. Add the seasoning, put it in small pots for use, and cover with a little butter just warmed and poured over

title: Potted Chicken or Fowl
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- remains of cold roast chicken
- 1/4 lb. of fresh butter per lb. of meat
- salt and cayenne to taste
- 1 teaspoonful of pounded mace
- 1/4 small nutmeg

## Directions

1. Strip the meat from the bones of cold roast fowl
2. When it is freed from gristle and skin, weigh it
3. To every lb. of meat, allow the proportion of butter, seasoning, and spices
4. Cut the meat

title: Potted Chicken or Fowl
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of cold roast chicken
- 1/4 lb. of fresh butter per lb. of meat
- salt and cayenne to taste
- 1 teaspoonful of pounded mace
- 1/4 small nutmeg per lb. of meat
- clarified butter for covering
- 2 or 3 slices of ham, minced (optional improvement)

## Directions

1. Strip the meat from the bones of cold roast fowl
2. Free the meat from gristle and skin, and weigh it
3. To every lb. of meat, allow 1/4 lb. of fresh butter, salt and cayenne to taste, 1 teaspoonful of pounded mace, and 1/4 small nutmeg
4. Cut the meat into small pieces
5. Pound it well with the fresh butter
6. Sprinkle in the spices gradually, and keep pounding until reduced to a perfectly smooth paste
7. Put it into potting-pots for use
8. Cover it with clarified butter, about 1/4 inch in thickness
9. If to be kept for some time, tie over a bladder
10. Keep it in a dry place

title: Potted Crayfish
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 100 crayfish
- pounded mace
- pepper and salt to taste
- 2 oz. butter

## Directions

1. Boil the fish in salt and water; pick out all the meat and pound it in a mortar to a paste.
2. Whilst pounding, add the butter gradually, and mix in the spice and seasoning.
3. Put it in small pots, and pour over it clarified butter, carefully excluding the air.

title: Potted Ham I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 lbs. of lean ham
- 1 lb. of fat
- 2 teaspoonfuls of pounded mace
- 1/2 nutmeg grated
- rather more than 1/2 teaspoonful of cayenne
- clarified lard

## Directions

1. Mince the ham, fat and lean together in the above proportion.
2. Pound it well in a mortar, seasoning it with cayenne pepper, pounded mace, and nutmeg.
3. Put the mixture into a deep baking-dish, and bake for 1/2 hour.
4. Then press it well into a stone jar, fill up the jar with clarified lard, cover it closely, and paste over it a piece of thick paper.

title: Potted Ham II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of lean ham
- 1/2 lb. of fat
- 1 teaspoonful of pounded mace
- 1/2 teaspoonful of pounded allspice
- 1/2 nutmeg
- pepper to taste
- clarified butter

## Directions

1. Cut some slices from the remains of a cold ham and mince them small.
2. Pound the ham in a mortar to a fine paste, with the fat.
3. Gradually add the seasoning and spices, and be very particular that all the ingredients are well mixed and the spices well pounded.
4. Press the mixture into potting-pots, pour over clarified butter, and keep it in a cool place.

title: Potted Hare (a Luncheon or Breakfast Dish)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 hare
- a few slices of bacon
- a large bunch of savoury herbs
- 4 cloves
- 1/2 teaspoonful of whole allspice
- 2 carrots
- 2 onions
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 pint of water
- 2 glasses of sherry

## Directions

1. Skin, empty, and wash the hare; cut it down the middle, and put it into a stewpan, with a few slices of bacon under and over it.
2. Add the remaining ingredients, and stew very gently until the hare is tender, and the flesh will separate easily from the bones.
3. When done enough, take it up, remove the bones, and pound the meat, with the bacon, in a mortar, until reduced to a perfectly smooth paste.
4. Should it not be sufficiently seasoned, add a little cayenne, salt, and pounded mace, but be careful that these are well mixed with the other ingredients.
5. Press the meat into potting-pots, pour over clarified butter, and keep in a dry place.
6. The liquor that the hare was stewed in, should be saved for hashes, soups, &c. &c.

title: Potted Lobster
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lobsters
- nutmeg, pounded mace, white pepper, and salt to taste
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 3 or 4 bay-leaves
- clarified butter

## Directions

1. Take out the meat carefully from the shell, but do not cut it up
2. Put some butter at the bottom of a dish, lay in the lobster as evenly as possible, with the bay-leaves and seasoning between
3. Cover with butter, and bake for 3/4 hour in a gentle oven
4. When done, drain the whole on a sieve, and lay the pieces in potting-jars, with the seasoning about them
5. When cold, pour over it clarified butter

title: Potted Partridge
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Partridges
- mace
- allspice
- white pepper
- salt
- butter
- coarse paste

## Directions

1. Pluck and draw the birds, and wipe them inside with a damp cloth.
2. Pound well some mace, allspice, white pepper, and salt; mix together, and rub every part of the partridges with this.
3. Pack the birds as closely as possible in a baking-pan, with plenty of butter over them, and cover with a coarse flour and water crust.
4. Tie a paper over this, and bake for rather more than 1-1/2 hour; let the birds get cold, then cut them into pieces for keeping.
5. Pack them closely into a large potting-pot, and cover with clarified butter.
6. This should be kept in a cool dry place.

title: Potted Salmon
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Salmon
- pounded mace, cloves, and pepper to taste
- 3 bay-leaves
- 1/4 lb. butter
- salt

## Directions

1. Skin the salmon, and clean it thoroughly by wiping with a cloth (water would spoil it)
2. cut it into square pieces, which rub with salt
3. let them remain till thoroughly drained
4. lay them in a dish with the other ingredients, and bake
5. When quite done, drain them from the gravy, press into pots for use
6. when cold, pour over it clarified butter

title: Potted Shrimps
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of shelled shrimps
- 1/4 lb. of fresh butter
- 1 blade of pounded mace
- cayenne to taste
- nutmeg (optional, when liked)

## Directions

1. Have ready a pint of picked shrimps
2. put them, with the other ingredients, into a stewpan
3. let them heat gradually in the butter, but do not let it boil
4. Pour into small pots
5. when cold, cover

title: Potted Shrimps
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of shelled shrimps
- 1/4 lb. of fresh butter
- 1 blade of pounded mace
- cayenne to taste
- nutmeg (when liked)

## Directions

1. Have ready a pint of picked shrimps, and put them, with the other ingredients, into a stewpan
2. Let them heat gradually in the butter, but do not let it boil
3. Pour into small pots
4. When cold, cover with melted butter, and carefully exclude the air

title: Potted Veal
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- To every lb. of veal allow 1/4 lb. of ham
- cayenne and pounded mace to taste
- 6 oz. of fresh butter
- clarified butter

## Directions

1. Mince the veal and ham together as finely as possible, and pound well in a mortar, with cayenne, pounded mace, and fresh butter in the above proportion
2. When reduced to a perfectly smooth paste, press it into potting-pots, and cover with clarified butter
3. If kept in a cool place, it will remain good some days

title: Poulet A La Marengo
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 large fowl
- 4 tablespoonfuls of salad oil
- 1 tablespoonful of flour
- 1 pint of stock or water
- about 20 mushroom-buttons
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 teaspoonful of powdered sugar
- a very small piece of garlic

## Directions

1. Cut the fowl into 8 or 10 pieces
2. Put them with the oil into a stewpan, and brown them over a moderate fire
3. Dredge in the flour
4. When that is browned, pour in the stock or water
5. Let it simmer very slowly for rather more than 1/2 hour, and skim off the fat as it rises to the top
6. Add the mushrooms
7. Season with salt, pepper, garlic, and sugar
8. Take out the fowl, which arrange pyramidically on the dish, with the inferior joints at the bottom
9. Reduce the sauce by boiling it quickly over the fire, keeping it stirred until sufficiently thick to adhere to the back of a spoon
10. Pour over the fowl, and serve

title: Poulet aux Cressons
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A fowl
- a large bunch of water-cresses
- 3 tablespoonfuls of vinegar
- 1/4 pint of gravy
- salt

## Directions

1. Truss and roast a fowl by recipe No. 952, taking care that it is nicely frothed and brown.
2. Wash and dry the water-cresses, pick them nicely, and arrange them in a flat layer on a dish.
3. Sprinkle over a little salt and the above proportion of vinegar.
4. Place the fowl over these, and pour over it the gravy.
5. Serve with a little gravy in a tureen.
6. When not liked, the vinegar may be omitted.

title: Pound Cake
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of butter
- 1-1/4 lb. of flour
- 1 lb. of pounded loaf sugar
- 1 lb. of currants
- 9 eggs
- 2 oz. of candied peel
- 1/2 oz. of citron
- 1/2 oz. of sweet almonds
- a little pounded mace when liked

## Directions

1. Work the butter to a cream
2. Dredge in the flour
3. Add the sugar, currants, candied peel which should be cut into neat slices, and the almonds which should be blanched and chopped, and mix all these well together
4. Whisk the eggs, and let them be thoroughly blended with the dry ingredients
5. Beat the cake well for 20 minutes
6. Put it into a round tin, lined at the bottom and sides with a strip of white buttered paper
7. Bake it from 1-1/2 to 2 hours in a well heated oven

title: Pounded Cheese
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Cheese
- Fresh butter

## Directions

1. To pound cheese is an economical way of using it, if it has become dry; it is exceedingly good spread on bread, and is the best way of eating it for those whose digestion is weak.
2. Cut up the cheese into small pieces, and pound it smoothly in a mortar, adding butter in the proportion of 3 oz. of fresh butter to every lb. of cheese.
3. Press it down into a jar, cover with clarified butter, and it will keep for several days.
4. The flavour may be very much increased by adding mixed mustard (about a teaspoonful to every lb.), or cayenne, or pounded mace. Curry-powder is also not unfrequently mixed with it.

title: Prawn Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 quarts of fish stock or water
- 2 pints of prawns
- the crumbs of a French roll
- anchovy sauce or mushroom ketchup to taste
- 1 blade of mace
- 1 pint of vinegar
- a little lemon-juice

## Directions

1. Pick out the tails of the prawns, put the bodies in a stewpan with 1 blade of mace, 1/2 pint of vinegar, and the same quantity of water; stew them for 1/4 hour, and strain off the liquor.
2. Put the fish stock or water into a stewpan; add the strained liquor, pound the prawns with the crumb of a roll moistened with a little of the soup, rub them through a tammy, and mix them by degrees with the soup; add ketchup or anchovy sauce to taste, with a little lemon-juice.
3. When it is well cooked, put in a few picked prawns; let them get thoroughly hot, and serve.
4. If not thick enough, put in a little butter and flour.

title: Preserved Greengages in Syrup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. fruit
- 1 lb. loaf sugar
- 1/4 pint of water
- kernels from the stones, blanched

## Directions

1. Boil the sugar and water together for about 10 minutes.
2. Divide the greengages, take out the stones, put the fruit into the syrup, and let it simmer gently until nearly tender.
3. Take it off the fire, put it into a large pan, and, the next day, boil it up again for about 10 minutes with the kernels from the stones, which should be blanched.
4. Put the fruit carefully into jars, pour over it the syrup, and, when cold, cover down, so that the air is quite excluded.
5. Let the syrup be well skimmed both the first and second day of boiling, otherwise it will not be clear.

title: Preserved Mulberries
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of fruit
- 1 pint of juice
- 2-1/2 lbs. of loaf sugar

## Directions

1. Put some of the fruit into a preserving-pan, and simmer it gently until the juice is well drawn
2. Strain it through a bag, measure it, and to every pint allow the above proportion of sugar and fruit
3. Put the sugar into the preserving-pan, moisten it with the juice, boil it up, skim well, and then add the mulberries, which should be ripe, but not soft enough to break to a pulp
4. Let them stand in the syrup till warm through, then set them on the fire to boil gently
5. When half done, turn them carefully into an earthen pan, and let them remain till the next day
6. Then boil them as before, and when the syrup is thick, and becomes firm when cold, put the preserve into pots

title: Preserved Nectarines
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- sugar
- water
- nectarines

## Directions

1. Divide the nectarines in two, take out the stones, and make a strong syrup with sugar and water
2. Put in the nectarines, and boil them until they have thoroughly imbibed the sugar
3. Keep the fruit as whole as possible, and turn it carefully into a pan
4. The next day boil it again for a few minutes, take out the nectarines, put them into jars
5. Boil the syrup quickly for 5 minutes, pour it over the fruit, and, when cold, cover the preserve down

title: Preserved Pears
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Jargonelle pears
- to every lb. of sugar allow 1/2 pint of water

## Directions

1. Procure some Jargonelle pears, not too ripe; put them into a stewpan with sufficient water to cover them, and simmer them till rather tender, but do not allow them to break; then put them into cold water.
2. Boil the sugar and water together for 5 minutes, skim well, put in the pears, and simmer them gently for 5 minutes.
3. Repeat the simmering for 3 successive days, taking care not to let the fruit break.
4. The last time of boiling, the syrup should be made rather richer, and the fruit boiled for 10 minutes.
5. When the pears are done, drain them from the syrup, and dry them in the sun, or in a cool oven; or they may be kept in the syrup, and dried as they are wanted.

title: Preserved Pineapple
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- To every lb. of fruit, weighed after being pared, allow 1 lb. of loaf sugar
- 1/4 pint of water

## Directions

1. The pines for making this preserve should be perfectly sound but ripe.
2. Cut them into rather thick slices, as the fruit shrinks very much in the boiling.
3. Pare off the rind carefully, that none of the pine be wasted; and, in doing so, notch it in and out, as the edge cannot be smoothly cut without great waste.
4. Dissolve a portion of the sugar in a preserving-pan with 1/4 pint of water; when this is melted, gradually add the remainder of the sugar, and boil it until it forms a clear syrup, skimming well.
5. As soon as this is the case, put in the pieces of pine, and boil well for at least 1/2 hour, or until it looks nearly transparent.
6. Put it into pots, cover down when cold, and store away in a dry place.

title: Preserved Pineapple, for Present Use
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Pineapple
- sugar
- water

## Directions

1. Cut the pine into slices 1/4 inch in thickness; peel them, and remove the hard part from the middle.
2. Put the parings and hard pieces into a stewpan with sufficient water to cover them, and boil for 1/4 hour.
3. Strain the liquor, and put in the slices of pine.
4. Stew them for 10 minutes, add sufficient sugar to sweeten the whole nicely, and boil again for another 1/4 hour; skim well, and the preserve will be ready for use.
5. It must be eaten soon, as it will keep but a very short time.

title: Preserved Plums
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- fruit
- 3/4 lb. of loaf sugar to every lb. of fruit
- 1/4 lb. of sugar to each pint of water for the thin syrup

## Directions

1. Select large ripe plums; slightly prick them, to prevent them from bursting, and simmer them very gently in a syrup made with the above proportion of sugar and water.
2. Put them carefully into a pan, let the syrup cool, pour it over the plums, and allow them to remain for two days.
3. Having previously weighed the other sugar, dip the lumps quickly into water, and put them into a preserving-pan with no more water than hangs about them; and boil the sugar to a syrup, carefully skimming it.
4. Drain the plums from the first syrup; put them into the fresh syrup, and simmer them very gently until they are clear; lift them out singly into pots, pour the syrup over, and when cold, cover down to exclude the air.
5. This preserve will remain good some time, if kept in a dry place, and makes a very nice addition to a dessert.

title: Preserved Pumpkin
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- To each lb. of pumpkin allow 1 lb. of roughly pounded loaf sugar
- 1 gill of lemon-juice
- 1/4 pint of water to every 3 lbs. of sugar used

## Directions

1. Obtain a good sweet pumpkin; halve it, take out the seeds, and pare off the rind
2. Cut it into neat slices, or into pieces about the size of a five-shilling piece
3. Weigh the pumpkin, put the slices in a pan or deep dish in layers, with the sugar sprinkled between them
4. Pour the lemon-juice over the top, and let the whole remain for 2 or 3 days
5. Boil altogether, adding 1/4 pint of water to every 3 lbs. of sugar used until the pumpkin becomes tender
6. Turn the whole into a pan, where let it remain for a week
7. Drain off the syrup, boil it until it is quite thick; skim, and pour it, boiling, over the pumpkin

title: Preserved Strawberries in Wine
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 quart bottle of strawberries
- 1/4 lb. of finely-pounded loaf sugar
- sherry or Madeira

## Directions

1. Let the fruit be gathered in fine weather, and used as soon as picked
2. Have ready some perfectly dry glass bottles, and some nice soft corks or bungs
3. Pick the stalks from the strawberries, drop them into the bottles, sprinkling amongst them pounded sugar in the above proportion
4. When the fruit reaches to the neck of the bottle, fill up with sherry or Madeira
5. Cork the bottles down with new corks, and dip them into melted resin

title: Prince of Wales's Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 12 turnips
- 1 lump of sugar
- 2 spoonfuls of strong veal stock
- salt and white pepper to taste
- 2 quarts of very bright stock, No. 105
- bread

## Directions

1. Peel the turnips, and with a cutter cut them in balls as round as possible, but very small
2. Put them in the stock, which must be very bright, and simmer till tender
3. Add the veal stock and seasoning
4. Have little pieces of bread cut round, about the size of a shilling
5. Moisten them with stock
6. Put them into a tureen and pour the soup over without shaking, for fear of crumbling the bread, which would spoil the appearance of the soup, and make it look thick

title: Puits d'Amour, or Puff-Paste Rings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Puff-paste No. 1205
- the white of an egg
- sifted loaf sugar

## Directions

1. Make some good puff-paste by recipe No. 1205; roll it out to the thickness of about 1/4 inch, and, with a round fluted paste-cutter, stamp out as many pieces as may be required
2. work the paste up again, and roll it out to the same thickness, and with a smaller cutter, stamp out sufficient pieces to correspond with the larger ones
3. Again stamp out the centre of these smaller rings
4. brush over the others with the white of an egg, place a small ring on the top of every large circular piece of paste, egg over the tops, and bake from 15 to 20 minutes
5. Sift over sugar, put them back in the oven to colour them
6. fill the rings with preserve of any bright colour
7. Dish them high on a napkin, and serve

title: Puree de Pommes de Terre, or, Very Thin-mashed Potatoes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of mashed potatoes
- 1/4 pint of good broth or stock
- 2 oz. of butter

## Directions

1. Boil the potatoes, well drain them, and pound them smoothly in a mortar, or beat them up with a fork
2. Add the stock or broth, and rub the potatoes through a sieve
3. Put the puree into a very clean saucepan with the butter
4. Stir it well over the fire until thoroughly hot, and it will then be ready to serve

title: Queen-Cakes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of flour
- 1/2 lb. of butter
- 1/2 lb. of pounded loaf sugar
- 3 eggs
- 1 teacupful of cream
- 1/2 lb. of currants
- 1 teaspoonful of carbonate of soda
- essence of lemon, or almonds to taste

## Directions

1. Work the butter to a cream
2. Dredge in the flour, add the sugar and currants, and mix the ingredients well together
3. Whisk the eggs, mix them with the cream and flavouring, and stir these to the flour
4. Add the carbonate of soda
5. Beat the paste well for 10 minutes
6. Put it into small buttered pans
7. Bake the cake from 1/4 to 1/2 hour

title: Quenelles À Tortue
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- veal leg
- bread crumbs
- milk
- calf's udder
- butter
- egg yolks
- cayenne pepper
- salt
- spices
- eggs
- parsley

## Directions

1. Take out the fleshy part of a leg of veal about 1 lb.
2. Scrape off all the meat without leaving any sinews or fat
3. Soak in milk about the same quantity of crumbs of bread
4. When the bread is well soaked squeeze it and put it into a mortar with the veal
5. Add a small quantity of calf's udder a little butter the yolks of 4 eggs boiled hard a little cayenne pepper salt and spices
6. Pound the whole very fine
7. Thicken the mixture with 2 whole eggs and the yolk of another
8. Try this farce or stuffing in boiling-hot water to ascertain its consistency
9. If it is too thin add the yolk of an egg
10. Take half of it and put into it some chopped parsley
11. Let the whole cool in order to roll it of the size of the yolk of an egg
12. Poach it in salt and boiling water
13. When very hard drain on a sieve and put it into the turtle

title: Quickly-Made Puddings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 1/2 lb. of sifted sugar
- 1/4 lb. of flour
- 1 pint of milk
- 5 eggs
- a little grated lemon-rind

## Directions

1. Make the milk hot
2. Stir in the butter, and let it cool before the other ingredients are added to it
3. Stir in the sugar, flour, and eggs, which should be well whisked, and omit the whites of 2
4. Flavour with a little grated lemon-rind
5. Beat the mixture well
6. Butter some small cups, rather more than half fill them
7. Bake from 20 minutes to 1/2 hour, according to the size of the puddings
8. Serve with fruit, custard, or wine sauce, a little of which may be poured over them

title: Quin's Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of walnut pickle
- 1/2 pint of port wine
- 1 pint of mushroom ketchup
- 1 dozen anchovies
- 1 dozen shalots
- 1/4 pint of soy
- 1/2 teaspoonful of cayenne

## Directions

1. Put all the ingredients into a saucepan, having previously chopped the shalots and anchovies very small.
2. Simmer for 15 minutes, strain, and, when cold, bottle off for use.
3. The corks should be well sealed to exclude the air.

title: Rabbit A La Minute
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 rabbit
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- salt and pepper to taste
- 2 blades of pounded mace
- 3 dried mushrooms
- 2 tablespoonfuls of minced parsley
- 2 teaspoonfuls of flour
- 2 glasses of sherry
- 1 pint of water

## Directions

1. Empty, skin, and wash the rabbit thoroughly, and cut it into joints.
2. Put the butter into a stewpan with the pieces of rabbit; add salt, pepper, and pounded mace, and let it cook until three parts done.
3. Then put in the remaining ingredients, and boil for about 10 minutes: it will then be ready to serve.

title: Rabbit Pie
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 rabbit
- a few slices of ham
- salt and white pepper to taste
- 2 blades of pounded mace
- 1/2 teaspoonful of grated nutmeg
- a few forcemeat balls
- 3 hard-boiled eggs
- 1/2 pint of gravy
- puff crust
- water

## Directions

1. Cut up the rabbit (which should be young), remove the breastbone, and bone the legs.
2. Put the rabbit, slices of ham, forcemeat balls, and hard eggs, by turns, in layers, and season each layer with pepper, salt, pounded mace, and grated nutmeg.
3. Pour in about 1/2 pint of water, cover with crust, and bake in a well-heated oven for about 1-1/2 hour.
4. Should the crust acquire too much colour, place a piece of paper over it to prevent its burning.
5. When done, pour in at the top, by means of the hole in the middle of the crust, a little good gravy, which may be made of the breast- and leg-bones of the rabbit and 2 or 3 shank-bones, flavoured with onion, herbs, and spices.

title: Rabbit Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 large rabbits, or 3 small ones
- a faggot of savoury herbs
- 1/2 head of celery
- 2 carrots
- 1 onion
- 1 blade of mace
- salt and white pepper to taste
- pounded mace
- 1/2 pint of cream
- yolks of 2 eggs boiled hard
- crumb of a French roll
- nearly 3 quarts of water
- veal stock or water
- arrowroot or rice-flour
- vermicelli or pearl barley

## Directions

1. Make the soup with the legs and shoulders of the rabbit, and keep the nice pieces for a dish or entrée
2. Put them into warm water, and draw the blood
3. When quite clean, put them in a stewpan, with a faggot of herbs, and a teacupful, or rather more, of veal stock or water
4. Simmer slowly till done through, and add the 3 quarts of water, and boil for an hour
5. Take out the rabbit, pick the meat from the bones, covering it up to keep it white
6. Put the bones back in the liquor, add the vegetables, and simmer for 2 hours
7. Skim and strain, and let it cool
8. Pound the meat in a mortar, with the yolks of the eggs, and the crumb of the roll previously soaked
9. Rub it through a tammy, and gradually add it to the strained liquor, and simmer for 15 minutes
10. Mix arrowroot or rice-flour with the cream (say 2 dessert-spoonfuls), and stir in the soup
11. Bring it to a boil, and serve

title: Ragout of Cold Neck of Mutton
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- the remains of a cold neck or loin of mutton
- 2 oz. of butter
- a little flour
- 2 onions sliced
- 1/4 pint of water
- 2 small carrots
- 2 turnips
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Cut the mutton into small chops, and trim off the greater portion of the fat.
2. Put the butter into a stewpan, dredge in a little flour, add the sliced onions, and keep stirring till brown.
3. Then put in the meat.
4. When this is quite brown, add the water, and the carrots and turnips, which should be cut into very thin slices.
5. Season with pepper and salt, and stew till quite tender, which will be in about 3/4 hour.

title: Ragout of Cold Veal
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of cold veal
- 1 oz. of butter
- 1/2 pint of gravy
- thickening of butter and flour
- pepper and salt to taste
- 1 blade of pounded mace
- 1 tablespoonful of mushroom ketchup
- 1 tablespoonful of sherry
- 1 dessertspoonful of lemon-juice
- forcemeat balls

## Directions

1. Cut the meat into nice-looking pieces
2. put them in a stewpan with 1 oz. of butter, and fry a light brown
3. add the gravy (hot water may be substituted for this)
4. thicken with a little butter and flour
5. stew gently about 1/4 hour
6. season with pepper, salt, and pounded mace
7. add the ketchup, sherry, and lemon-juice
8. give one boil, and serve
9. Garnish the dish with forcemeat balls and fried rashers of bacon

title: Ragout of Fowl
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of cold roast fowls
- 3 shalots
- 2 blades of mace
- a faggot of savoury herbs
- 2 or three slices of lean ham
- 1 pint of stock or water
- pepper and salt to taste
- 1 onion
- 1 dessertspoonful of flour
- 1 tablespoonful of lemon-juice
- 1/2 teaspoonful of pounded sugar
- 1 oz. of butter

## Directions

1. Cut the fowls up into neat pieces, the same as for a fricassee
2. put the trimmings into a stewpan with the shalots, mace, herbs, ham, onion, and stock (water may be substituted for this)
3. Boil it slowly for 1 hour
4. strain the liquor, and put a small piece of butter into a stewpan
5. when melted, dredge in sufficient flour to dry up the butter, and stir it over the fire
6. Put in the strained liquor, boil for a few minutes, and strain it again over the pieces of fowl
7. Squeeze in the lemon-juice, add the sugar and a seasoning of pepper and salt, make it hot, but do not allow it to boil
8. lay the fowl neatly on the dish, and garnish with croûtons

title: Ragout Of Rabbit Or Hare
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 rabbit
- 3 teaspoonfuls of flour
- 3 sliced onions
- 2 oz. of butter
- a few thin slices of bacon
- pepper and salt to taste
- 2 slices of lemon
- 1 bay-leaf
- 1 glass of port wine

## Directions

1. Slice the onions, and put them into a stewpan with the flour and butter; place the pan near the fire, stir well as the butter melts, till the onions become a rich brown colour, and add, by degrees, a little water or gravy till the mixture is of the consistency of cream.
2. Cut some thin slices of bacon; lay in these with the rabbit, cut into neat joints; add a seasoning of pepper and salt, the lemon and bay-leaf, and let the whole simmer until tender.
3. Pour in the port wine, give one boil, and serve.

title: Raised Pie of Poultry or Game
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- To every lb. of flour allow 1/2 lb. of butter, 1/2 pint of water, the yolks of 2 eggs, 1/2 teaspoonful of salt
- 1 large fowl or pheasant
- a few slices of veal cutlet
- a few slices of dressed ham
- forcemeat
- seasoning of nutmeg, allspice, pepper and salt
- gravy

## Directions

1. Make a stiff short crust with the above proportion of butter, flour, water, and eggs, and work it up very smoothly; butter a raised-pie mould, as shown in No. 1190, and line it with the paste.
2. Previously to making the crust, bone the fowl, or whatever bird is intended to be used, lay it, breast downwards, upon a cloth, and season the inside well with pounded mace, allspice, pepper, and salt; then spread over it a layer of forcemeat, then a layer of seasoned veal, and then one of ham, and then another layer of forcemeat, and roll the fowl over, making the skin meet at the back.
3. Line the pie with forcemeat, put in the fowl, and fill up the cavities with slices of seasoned veal and ham and forcemeat; wet the edges of the pie, put on the cover, pinch the edges together with the paste-pincers, and decorate it with leaves.
4. Brush it over with beaten yolk of egg, and bake in a moderate oven for 4 hours.
5. In the mean time, make a good strong gravy from the bones, pour it through a funnel into the hole at the top; cover this hole with a small leaf, and the pie, when cold, will be ready for use.
6. Let it be remembered that the gravy must be considerably reduced before it is poured into the pie, as, when cold, it should form a firm jelly, and not be the least degree in a liquid state.
7. Truffles, mushrooms, &c., added to this pie, make it much nicer; and, to enrich it, lard the fleshy parts of the poultry or game with thin strips of bacon.

title: Raisin Cheese
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- To every lb. of raisins allow a lb. of loaf sugar
- pounded cinnamon and cloves to taste

## Directions

1. Stone the raisins
2. Put them into a stewpan with the sugar, cinnamon, and cloves, and let them boil for 1-1/2 hour, stirring all the time
3. Let the preparation cool a little, pour it into a glass dish, and garnish with strips of candied lemon-peel and citron

title: Ramakins
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of Cheshire cheese
- 1/4 lb. of Parmesan cheese
- 1/4 lb. of fresh butter
- 4 eggs
- The crumb of a small roll
- Pepper, salt, and pounded mace to taste

## Directions

1. Boil the crumb of the roll in milk for 5 minutes; strain, and put it into a mortar.
2. Add the cheese, which should be finely scraped, the butter, the yolks of the eggs, and seasoning, and pound these ingredients well together.
3. Whisk the whites of the eggs, mix them with the paste, and put it into small pans or saucers, which should not be more than half filled.
4. Bake them from 10 to 12 minutes, and serve them very hot and very quickly.

title: Raspberry and Currant, or any Fresh Fruit Salad
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- strawberries, raspberries, currants, or any of these fruits mixed, or peaches
- finely-pounded sugar
- wine or brandy, or liqueur
- pounded cinnamon

## Directions

1. Strip the fruit from the stalks, pile it on a dish, and sprinkle over it finely-pounded sugar
2. Pour about 6 large tablespoonfuls of wine or brandy, or 3 tablespoonfuls of liqueur, in the middle of the fruit
3. When the flavour is liked, a little pounded cinnamon may be added
4. When helping the fruit, lightly stir it, that the wine and sugar may be equally distributed

title: Raspberry Cream
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 pint of milk
- 3/4 pint of cream
- 1-1/2 oz. of isinglass
- raspberry jelly
- sugar to taste
- 2 tablespoonfuls of brandy

## Directions

1. Boil the milk, cream, and isinglass together for 1/4 hour, or until the latter is melted, and strain it through a hair sieve into a basin
2. Let it cool a little
3. then add to it sufficient raspberry jelly, which, when melted, would make 1/3 pint, and stir well till the ingredients are thoroughly mixed
4. If not sufficiently sweet, add a little pounded sugar with the brandy
5. whisk the mixture well until nearly cold
6. put it into a well-oiled mould, and set it in a cool place till perfectly set

title: Raspberry Jam
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- To every lb. of raspberries allow 1 lb. of sugar
- 1/4 pint of red-currant juice

## Directions

1. Let the fruit for this preserve be gathered in fine weather, and used as soon after it is picked as possible
2. Take off the stalks, put the raspberries into a preserving-pan, break them well with a wooden spoon, and let them boil for 1/4 hour, keeping them well stirred
3. Add the currant-juice and sugar, and boil again for 1/2 hour
4. Skim the jam well after the sugar is added

title: Raspberry Vinegar
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 pints of the best vinegar
- 4-1/2 pints of freshly-gathered raspberries
- 1 lb. of pounded loaf sugar per pint of liquor
- 1 wineglassful of brandy per pint of liquor

## Directions

1. Let the raspberries be freshly gathered; pick them from the stalks, and put 1-1/2 pint of them into a stone jar
2. Pour 3 pints of the best vinegar over them, and let them remain for 24 hours
3. Strain the liquor over another 1-1/2 pint of fresh raspberries
4. Let them remain another 24 hours, and the following day repeat the process for the third time
5. Drain off the liquor without pressing, and pass it through a jelly-bag (previously wetted with plain vinegar), into a stone jar
6. Add to every pint of the liquor 1 lb. of pounded loaf sugar; stir them together
7. When the sugar is dissolved, cover the jar; set it upon the fire in a saucepan of boiling water, and let it boil for an hour, removing the scum as fast as it rises
8. Add to each pint a glass of brandy, bottle it, and seal the corks

title: Ratafias
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of sweet almonds
- 1/4 lb. of bitter almonds
- 3/4 lb. of sifted loaf sugar
- the whites of 4 eggs
- cartridge paper

## Directions

1. Blanch, skin, and dry the almonds, and pound them in a mortar with the white of an egg
2. stir in the sugar, and gradually add the remaining whites of eggs, taking care that they are very thoroughly whisked
3. Drop the mixture through a small biscuit-syringe on to cartridge paper
4. bake the cakes from 10 to 12 minutes in rather a quicker oven than for macaroons

title: Ravigotte
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 teaspoonful of mushroom ketchup
- 1 teaspoonful of cavice
- 1 teaspoonful of Chili vinegar
- 1 teaspoonful of Reading sauce
- a piece of butter the size of an egg
- 3 tablespoonfuls of thick Béchamel
- 1 tablespoonful of minced parsley
- 3 tablespoonfuls of cream
- salt and pepper to taste

## Directions

1. Scald the parsley, mince the leaves very fine, and add it to all the other ingredients.
2. Mix the whole together thoroughly, and the sauce will be ready for use.

title: Reading Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2-1/2 pints of walnut pickle
- 1-1/2 oz. of shalots
- 1 quart of spring water
- 3/4 pint of Indian soy
- 1/2 oz. of bruised ginger
- 1/2 oz. of long pepper
- 1 oz. of mustard-seed
- 1 anchovy
- 1/2 oz. of cayenne
- 1/4 oz. of dried sweet bay-leaves

## Directions

1. Bruise the shalots in a mortar, and put them in a stone jar with the walnut-liquor.
2. Place it before the fire, and let it boil until reduced to 2 pints.
3. Into another jar, put all the ingredients except the bay-leaves, taking care that they are well bruised, so that the flavour may be thoroughly extracted.
4. Put this also before the fire, and let it boil for 1 hour, or rather more.
5. When the contents of both jars are sufficiently cooked, mix them together, stirring them well as you mix them, and submit them to a slow boiling for 1/2 hour.
6. Cover closely, and let them stand 24 hours in a cool place.
7. Then open the jar and add the bay-leaves.
8. Let it stand a week longer closed down, then strain through a flannel bag, and it will be ready for use.

title: Red Mullet
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Oiled paper
- thickening of butter and flour
- 1/2 teaspoonful of anchovy sauce
- 1 glass of sherry
- cayenne and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Clean the fish, take out the gills, but leave the inside, fold in oiled paper, and bake them gently.
2. When done, take the liquor that flows from the fish, add a thickening of butter kneaded with flour; put in the other ingredients, and let it boil for 2 minutes.
3. Serve the sauce in a tureen, and the fish, either with or without the paper cases.

title: Red-Currant and Raspberry Tart
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 pint of picked currants
- 1/2 pint of raspberries
- 3 heaped tablespoonfuls of moist sugar
- 1/2 lb. of short crust
- sifted sugar

## Directions

1. Strip the currants from the stalks, and put them into a deep pie-dish, with a small cup placed in the midst, bottom upwards
2. Add the raspberries and sugar
3. Place a border of paste round the edge of the dish, cover with crust, and ornament the edges
4. Bake from 1/2 to 3/4 hour
5. Strew some sifted sugar over before being sent to table

title: Red-Currant Jam
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of fruit
- 3/4 lb. of loaf sugar per lb. of fruit

## Directions

1. Let the fruit be gathered on a fine day
2. Weigh it, and then strip the currants from the stalks
3. Put them into a preserving-pan with sugar in the above proportion
4. Stir them, and boil them for about 3/4 hour
5. Carefully remove the scum as it rises
6. Put the jam into pots
7. When cold, cover with oiled papers
8. Over these put a piece of tissue-paper brushed over on both sides with the white of an egg
9. Press the paper round the top of the pot, and, when dry, the covering will be quite hard and air-tight

title: Red-Currant Jelly
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Red currants
- 3/4 lb. of loaf sugar per pint of juice

## Directions

1. Have the fruit gathered in fine weather; pick it from the stalks, put it into a jar, and place this jar in a saucepan of boiling water over the fire, and let it simmer gently until the juice is well drawn from the currants
2. Strain them through a jelly-bag or fine cloth, and if the jelly is wished very clear, do not squeeze them too much, as the skin and pulp from the fruit will be pressed through with the juice, and so make the jelly muddy
3. Measure the juice, and to each pint allow 3/4 lb. of loaf sugar; put these into a preserving-pan, set it over the fire, and keep stirring the jelly until it is done, carefully removing every particle of scum as it rises, using a wooden or silver spoon for the purpose
4. When it has boiled from 20 minutes to 1/2 hour, put a little of the jelly on a plate, and if firm when cool, it is done
5. Take it off the fire, pour it into small gallipots, cover each of the pots with an oiled paper, and then with a piece of tissue-paper brushed over on both sides with the white of an egg
6. Label the pots, adding the year when the jelly was made, and store it away in a dry place

title: Regency Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- bones and remains of cold game such as pheasants, partridges
- 2 carrots
- 2 small onions
- 1 head of celery
- 1 turnip
- 1/4 lb. of pearl barley
- yolks of 3 eggs boiled hard
- 1/4 pint of cream
- salt to taste
- 2 quarts of stock No. 105, or common stock No. 106

## Directions

1. Place the bones or remains of game in the stewpan, with the vegetables sliced
2. Pour over the stock, and simmer for 2 hours
3. Skim off all the fat, and strain it
4. Wash the barley, and boil it in 2 or 3 waters before putting it to the soup
5. Finish simmering in the soup, and when the barley is done, take out half, and pound the other half with the yolks of the eggs
6. When you have finished pounding, rub it through a clean tammy, add the cream, and salt if necessary
7. Give one boil, and serve

title: Remoulade
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 eggs
- 1/2 tablespoonful of made mustard
- salt and cayenne to taste
- 3 tablespoonfuls of olive-oil
- 1 tablespoonful of tarragon or plain vinegar

## Directions

1. Boil 3 eggs quite hard for about 1/4 hour, put them into cold water, and let them remain in it for a few minutes.
2. Strip off the shells, put the yolks in a mortar, and pound them very smoothly.
3. Add to them, very gradually, the mustard, seasoning, and vinegar, keeping all well stirred and rubbed down with the back of a wooden spoon.
4. Put in the oil drop by drop, and when this is thoroughly mixed with the other ingredients, add the yolk of a raw egg, and stir well, when it will be ready for use.
5. The quantities of oil and vinegar may be increased or diminished according to taste.

title: Rhubarb and Orange Jam, to Resemble Scotch Marmalade
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 quart of finely-cut rhubarb
- 6 oranges
- 1-1/2 lb. of loaf sugar

## Directions

1. Peel the oranges; remove as much of the white pith as possible, divide them, and take out the pips
2. Slice the pulp into a preserving-pan, add the rind of half the oranges cut into thin strips, and the loaf sugar, which should be broken small
3. Peel the rhubarb, cut it into thin pieces, put it to the oranges, and stir altogether over a gentle fire until the jam is done
4. Remove all the scum as it rises, put the preserve into pots, and, when cold, cover down
5. Should the rhubarb be very old, stew it alone for 1/4 hour before the other ingredients are added

title: Rhubarb Jam
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of rhubarb
- 1 lb. of loaf sugar
- rind of 1/2 lemon

## Directions

1. Wipe the rhubarb perfectly dry, take off the string or peel, and weigh it
2. Put it into a preserving-pan, with sugar in the above proportion
3. Mince the lemon-rind very finely, add it to the other ingredients, and place the preserving-pan by the side of the fire
4. Keep stirring to prevent the rhubarb from burning, and when the sugar is well dissolved, put the pan more over the fire, and let the jam boil until it is done, taking care to keep it well skimmed and stirred with a wooden or silver spoon
5. Pour it into pots, and cover down with oiled and egged papers

title: Rhubarb Tart
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of puff-paste No. 1206
- about 5 sticks of large rhubarb
- 1/4 lb. of moist sugar

## Directions

1. Make a puff-crust by recipe No. 1206; line the edges of a deep pie-dish with it, and wash, wipe, and cut the rhubarb into pieces about 1 inch long.
2. Should it be old and tough, string it, that is to say, pare off the outside skin.
3. Pile the fruit high in the dish, as it shrinks very much in the cooking; put in the sugar, cover with crust, ornament the edges, and bake the tart in a well-heated oven from 1/2 to 3/4 hour.
4. If wanted very nice, brush it over with the white of an egg beaten to a stiff froth, then sprinkle on it some sifted sugar, and put it in the oven just to set the glaze: this should be done when the tart is nearly baked.
5. A small quantity of lemon-juice, and a little of the peel minced, are by many persons considered an improvement to the flavour of rhubarb tart.

title: Rhubarb Wine
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 5 lbs. of rhubarb pulp
- 1 gallon of cold spring water
- 3 lbs. of loaf sugar per gallon of liquor
- 1/2 oz. of isinglass per gallon of liquor
- rind of 1 lemon per gallon of liquor

## Directions

1. Gather the rhubarb about the middle of May; wipe it with a wet cloth, and with a mallet, bruise it in a large wooden tub
2. When reduced to a pulp, weigh it, and to every 5 lbs. add 1 gallon of cold spring water
3. Let these remain for 3 days, stirring 3 or 4 times a day
4. On the fourth day, press the pulp through a hair sieve; put the liquor into a tub, and to every gallon put 3 lbs. of loaf sugar
5. Stir in the sugar until it is quite dissolved, and add the lemon-rind
6. Let the liquor remain, and in 4, 5, or 6 days, the fermentation will begin to subside, and a crust or head will be formed
7. Skim off the crust, or draw the liquor from it, when the crust begins to crack or separate
8. Put the wine into a cask, and if it ferments, rack it off into another cask, and in a fortnight stop it down
9. If the wine should have lost any of its original sweetness, add a little more loaf sugar, taking care that the cask is full
10. Bottle it off in February or March, and in the summer it should be fit to drink

title: Rib Of Beef Bones
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Rib of beef bones
- 1 onion chopped fine
- a few slices of carrot and turnip
- 1/4 pint of gravy
- pepper and salt

## Directions

1. The bones for this dish should have left on them a slight covering of meat; saw them into pieces 3 inches long
2. Season them with pepper and salt, and put them into a stewpan with the remaining ingredients
3. Stew gently, until the vegetables are tender, and serve

title: Rice Biscuits or Cakes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of rice-flour
- 1/4 lb. of pounded lump sugar
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 2 eggs

## Directions

1. Beat the butter to a cream
2. stir in the rice-flour and pounded sugar
3. moisten the whole with the eggs, which should be previously well beaten
4. Roll out the paste, shape it with a round paste-cutter into small cakes
5. bake them from 12 to 18 minutes in a very slow oven

title: Rice Blancmange
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of ground rice
- 3 oz. of loaf sugar
- 1 oz. of fresh butter
- 1 quart of milk
- flavouring of lemon-peel, essence of almonds or vanilla, or laurel-leaves

## Directions

1. Mix the rice to a smooth batter with about 1/2 pint of the milk, and the remainder put into a saucepan, with the sugar, butter, and whichever of the above flavourings may be preferred
2. bring the milk to the boiling-point, quickly stir in the rice, and let it boil for about 10 minutes, or until it comes easily away from the saucepan, keeping it well stirred the whole time
3. Grease a mould with pure salad-oil
4. pour in the rice, and let it get perfectly set, when it should turn out quite easily
5. garnish it with jam, or pour round a compôte of any kind of fruit, just before it is sent to table

title: Rice Bread
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of rice
- 4 lbs. of wheat flour
- nearly 3 tablespoonfuls of yeast
- 1/4 oz. of salt
- warm water

## Directions

1. Boil the rice in water until it is quite tender
2. Pour off the water, and put the rice, before it is cold, to the flour
3. Mix these well together with the yeast, salt, and sufficient warm water to make the whole into a smooth dough
4. Let it rise by the side of the fire
5. Form it into loaves, and bake them from 1-1/2 to 2 hours, according to their size

title: Rice Cake
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of ground rice
- 1/2 lb. of flour
- 1/2 lb. of loaf sugar
- 9 eggs
- 20 drops of essence of lemon, or the rind of 1 lemon
- 1/4 lb. of butter

## Directions

1. Separate the whites from the yolks of the eggs
2. Whisk them both well, and add to the latter the butter beaten to a cream
3. Stir in the flour, rice, and lemon (if the rind is used, it must be very finely minced), and beat the mixture well
4. Add the whites of the eggs
5. Beat the cake again for some time
6. Put it into a buttered mould or tin
7. Bake it for nearly 1-1/2 hour

title: Rice Croquettes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of rice
- 1 quart of milk
- 6 oz. of pounded sugar
- flavouring of vanilla, lemon-peel, or bitter almonds
- egg
- bread crumbs
- hot lard

## Directions

1. Put the rice, milk, and sugar into a saucepan, and let the former gradually swell over a gentle fire until all the milk is dried up
2. Just before the rice is done, stir in a few drops of essence of any of the above flavourings
3. Let the rice get cold
4. Form it into small round balls
5. Dip them into yolk of egg
6. Sprinkle them with bread crumbs
7. Fry them in boiling lard for about 10 minutes, turning them about, that they may get equally browned
8. Drain the greasy moisture from them, by placing them on a cloth in front of the fire for a minute or two
9. Pile them on a white d'oyley, and send them quickly to table
10. A small piece of jam is sometimes introduced into the middle of each croquette

title: Rice Fritters
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of rice
- 1 quart of milk
- 3 oz. of sugar
- 1 oz. of fresh butter
- 6 oz. of orange marmalade
- 4 eggs

## Directions

1. Swell the rice in the milk, with the sugar and butter, over a slow fire until it is perfectly tender, which will be in about 3/4 hour
2. When the rice is done, strain away the milk, should there be any left
3. Mix with it the marmalade and well-beaten eggs
4. Stir the whole over the fire until the eggs are set
5. Spread the mixture on a dish to the thickness of about 1/2 inch, or rather thicker
6. When it is perfectly cold, cut it into long strips
7. Dip them in a batter the same as for apple fritters
8. Fry them a nice brown
9. Dish them on a white d'oyley, strew sifted sugar over, and serve quickly

title: Rice Snowballs
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of rice
- 1 quart of milk
- flavouring of essence of almonds
- sugar to taste
- 1 pint of custard made by recipe No. 1423

## Directions

1. Boil the rice in the milk, with sugar and a flavouring of essence of almonds, until the former is tender, adding, if necessary, a little more milk, should it dry away too much
2. When the rice is quite soft, put it into teacups, or small round jars, and let it remain until cold
3. Turn the rice out on a deep glass dish
4. Pour over a custard made by recipe No. 1423
5. On the top of each ball place a small piece of bright-coloured preserve or jelly
6. Lemon-peel or vanilla may be boiled with the rice instead of the essence of almonds, when either of these is preferred, but the flavouring of the custard must correspond with that of the rice

title: Rice Souffle
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 tablespoonfuls of ground rice
- 1 pint of milk
- 5 eggs
- pounded sugar to taste
- flavouring of lemon-rind, vanilla, coffee, chocolate, or anything that may be preferred
- a piece of butter the size of a walnut

## Directions

1. Mix the ground rice with 6 tablespoonfuls of the milk quite smoothly
2. Put it into a saucepan with the remainder of the milk and butter
3. Keep stirring it over the fire for about 1/4 hour, or until the mixture thickens
4. Separate the yolks from the whites of the eggs
5. Beat the former in a basin
6. Stir to them the rice and sufficient pounded sugar to sweeten the soufflé
7. Add the sugar as sparingly as possible, as the less sugar there is used, the lighter will be the soufflé
8. Whisk the whites of the eggs to a stiff froth or snow
9. Mix them with the other preparation
10. Pour the whole into a soufflé-dish
11. Put it instantly into the oven
12. Bake it about 1/2 hour in a moderate oven
13. Take it out, hold a salamander or hot shovel over the top, sprinkle sifted sugar over it
14. Send the soufflé to table in the dish it was baked in, either with a napkin pinned round, or inclosed in a more ornamental dish

title: Rice Soufflé with Apple Marmalade
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of rice
- 1 quart of milk
- the rind of 1/2 lemon
- sugar to taste
- the yolks of 4 eggs
- the whites of 6 eggs
- 1-1/2 oz. of butter
- 4 tablespoonfuls of apple marmalade No. 1395

## Directions

1. Boil the milk with the lemon-peel until the former is well flavoured
2. Strain it, put in the rice, and let it gradually swell over a slow fire, adding sufficient sugar to sweeten it nicely
3. Crush the rice to a smooth pulp with the back of a wooden spoon
4. Line the bottom and sides of a round cake-tin with it, and put it into the oven to set
5. Turn it out of the tin carefully, and be careful that the border of rice is firm in every part
6. Mix with the marmalade the beaten yolks of eggs and the butter, and stir these over the fire until the mixture thickens
7. Take it off the fire
8. Add the whites of the eggs, which should be previously beaten to a strong froth
9. Stir all together, and put it into the rice border
10. Bake in a moderate oven for about 1/2 hour, or until the soufflé rises very light
11. Watch it, and serve instantly, or it will immediately fall after it is taken from the oven

title: Rice Soup I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 oz. of Patna rice
- salt
- cayenne
- mace
- 2 quarts of white stock

## Directions

1. Throw the rice into boiling water, and let it remain 5 minutes
2. Pour it into a sieve, and allow it to drain well
3. Add it to the stock boiling, and allow it to stew till it is quite tender
4. Season to taste
5. Serve quickly

title: Rice Soup II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of rice
- the yolks of 4 eggs
- 1/2 a pint of cream
- rather more than 2 quarts of stock No. 105

## Directions

1. Boil the rice in the stock, and rub half of it through a tammy
2. Put the stock in the stewpan, add all the rice, and simmer gently for 5 minutes
3. Beat the yolks of the eggs, mix them with the cream (previously boiled), and strain through a hair sieve
4. Take the soup off the fire, add the eggs and cream, stirring frequently
5. Heat it gradually, stirring all the time; but do not let it boil, or the eggs will curdle

title: Rice with Dried or Fresh Fruit
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of rice
- 1 pint of any kind of fresh fruit that may be preferred, or 1/2 lb. of raisins or currants

## Directions

1. Wash the rice, tie it in a cloth, allowing room for it to swell, and put it into a saucepan of cold water
2. Let it boil for an hour, then take it up, untie the cloth, stir in the fruit, and tie it up again tolerably tight
3. Put it into the water for the remainder of the time
4. Boil for another hour, or rather longer, and serve with sweet sauce, if made with dried fruit, and with plain sifted sugar and a little cream or milk, if made with fresh fruit

title: Rice-Milk
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 tablespoonfuls of rice
- 1 quart of milk
- sugar to taste
- when liked, a little grated nutmeg

## Directions

1. Well wash the rice
2. Put it into a saucepan with the milk
3. Simmer gently until the rice is tender
4. Stir it from time to time to prevent the milk from burning
5. Sweeten it
6. Add a little grated nutmeg
7. Serve

title: Rich Baked Apple Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of the pulp of apples
- 1/2 lb. of loaf sugar
- 6 oz. of butter
- the rind of 1 lemon
- 6 eggs
- puff-paste

## Directions

1. Peel, core, and cut the apples, as for sauce
2. Put them into a stewpan, with only just sufficient water to prevent them from burning
3. Let them stew until reduced to a pulp
4. Weigh the pulp
5. To every 1/2 lb. add sifted sugar, grated lemon-rind, and 6 well-beaten eggs
6. Beat these ingredients well together
7. Melt the butter
8. Stir it to the other things
9. Put a border of puff-paste round the dish
10. Bake for rather more than 1/2 hour

title: Rich Bride Or Christening Cake
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 5 lbs. of the finest flour
- 3 lbs. of fresh butter
- 5 lbs. of currants
- 2 lbs. of sifted loaf sugar
- 2 nutmegs
- 1/4 oz. of mace
- half 1/4 oz. of cloves
- 16 eggs
- 1 lb. of sweet almonds
- 1/2 lb. of candied citron
- 1/2 lb. each of candied orange and lemon peel
- 1 gill of wine
- 1 gill of brandy

## Directions

1. Let the flour be as fine as possible, and well dried and sifted
2. Have the currants washed, picked, and dried before the fire
3. Have the sugar well pounded and sifted
4. Have the nutmegs grated, the spices pounded
5. Have the eggs thoroughly whisked, whites and yolks separately
6. Have the almonds pounded with a little orange-flower water, and the candied peel cut in neat slices
7. Begin working the butter with the hand till it becomes of a cream-like consistency
8. Stir in the sugar
9. When the whites of the eggs are whisked to a solid froth, mix them with the butter and sugar
10. Well beat up the yolks for 10 minutes, and, adding them to the flour, nutmegs, mace, and cloves, continue beating the whole together for 1/2 hour or longer, till wanted for the oven
11. Mix in lightly the currants, almonds, and candied peel with the wine and brandy
12. Having lined a hoop with buttered paper, fill it with the mixture, and bake the cake in a tolerably quick oven, taking care, however, not to burn it
13. Cover the top of it with a sheet of paper to prevent burning
14. To ascertain whether the cake is done, plunge a clean knife into the middle of it, withdraw it directly, and if the blade is not sticky, and looks bright, the cake is sufficiently baked
15. Spread the cake with a thick layer of almond icing, and over that another layer of sugar icing, and afterwards ornament it

title: Rich Gravy For Hashes, Ragouts, &c.
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of shin of beef
- 1 large onion or a few shalots
- a little flour
- a bunch of savoury herbs
- 2 blades of mace
- 2 or 3 cloves
- 4 whole allspice
- 1/4 teaspoonful of whole pepper
- 1 slice of lean ham or bacon
- 1/2 a head of celery (when at hand)
- 2 pints of boiling water
- salt and cayenne to taste

## Directions

1. Cut the beef into thin slices, as also the onions
2. Dredge them with flour, and fry of a pale brown, but do not allow them to get black
3. Pour in the boiling water, let it boil up; and skim
4. Add the remaining ingredients, and simmer the whole very gently for 2 hours, or until all the juices are extracted from the meat
5. Put it by to get cold, when take off all the fat
6. Flavour with ketchup, store sauces, wine, or, in fact, anything that may give additional and suitable relish to the dish it is intended for

title: Rich Strong Stock
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 lbs. of shin of beef
- 4 lbs. of knuckle of veal
- 3/4 lb. of good lean ham
- any poultry trimmings
- 3 small onions
- 3 small carrots
- 3 turnips
- 1 head of celery
- a few chopped mushrooms, when obtainable
- 1 tomato
- a bunch of savoury herbs, not forgetting parsley
- 1-1/2 oz. of salt
- 12 white peppercorns
- 6 cloves
- 3 small blades of mace
- 4 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Line a delicately clean stewpan with the ham cut in thin broad slices, carefully trimming off all its rusty fat
2. Cut up the beef and veal in pieces about 3 inches square, and lay them on the ham
3. Set it on the stove, and draw it down, and stir frequently
4. When the meat is equally browned, put in the beef and veal bones, the poultry trimmings

title: Rich Sweet Apple Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of bread crumbs
- 1/2 lb. of suet
- 1/2 lb. of currants
- 1/2 lb. of apples
- 1/2 lb. of moist sugar
- 6 eggs
- 12 sweet almonds
- 1/2 saltspoonful of grated nutmeg
- 1 wineglassful of brandy

## Directions

1. Chop the suet very fine
2. Wash the currants, dry them, and pick away the stalks and pieces of grit
3. Pare, core, and chop the apple, and grate the bread into fine crumbs, and mince the almonds
4. Mix all these ingredients together, adding the sugar and nutmeg
5. Beat up the eggs, omitting the whites of three
6. Stir these to the pudding, and when all is well mixed, add the brandy
7. Put the pudding into a buttered mould
8. Tie down with a cloth, put it into boiling water, and let it boil for 3 hours

title: Rich Sweetmeat Gingerbread Nuts
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of treacle
- 1/4 lb. of clarified butter
- 1 lb. of coarse brown sugar
- 2 oz. of ground ginger
- 1 oz. of candied orange-peel
- 1 oz. of candied angelica
- 1/2 oz. of candied lemon-peel
- 1/2 oz. of coriander seeds
- 1/2 oz. of caraway seeds
- 1 egg
- flour

## Directions

1. Put the treacle into a basin
2. Pour over it the butter, melted so as not to oil, the sugar, and ginger
3. Stir these ingredients well together
4. Add the candied peel, which should be cut into very small pieces, but not bruised, and the caraway and coriander seeds, which should be pounded
5. Break in an egg
6. Work the whole up with as much fine flour as may be necessary to form a paste
7. Make this into nuts of any size
8. Put them on a tin plate
9. Bake in a slow oven from 1/4 to 1/2 hour

title: Richer Pancakes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 eggs
- 1 pint of cream
- 1/4 lb. of loaf sugar
- 1 glass of sherry
- 1/2 teaspoonful of grated nutmeg
- flour

## Directions

1. Ascertain that the eggs are extremely fresh, beat them well, strain and mix with them the cream, pounded sugar, wine, nutmeg, and as much flour as will make the batter nearly as thick as that for ordinary pancakes.
2. Make the frying-pan hot, wipe it with a clean cloth, pour in sufficient batter to make a thin pancake, and fry it for about 5 minutes.
3. Dish the pancakes piled one above the other, strew sifted sugar between each, and serve.

title: Roast Black-Cock
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Black-cock
- butter
- toast

## Directions

1. Let these birds hang for a few days, or they will be tough and tasteless, if not well kept.
2. Pluck and draw them, and wipe the insides and outsides with a damp cloth, as washing spoils the flavour.
3. Cut off the heads, and truss them, the same as a roast fowl, cutting off the toes, and scalding and peeling the feet.
4. Put them down to a brisk fire, well baste them with butter, and serve with a piece of toast under, and a good gravy and bread sauce.
5. After trussing, some cooks cover the breast with vine-leaves and slices of bacon, and then roast them.
6. They should be served in the same manner and with the same accompaniments as with the plainly-roasted birds.

title: Roast Breast of Veal
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal
- a little flour

## Directions

1. Wash the veal, well wipe it, and dredge it with flour; put it down to a bright fire, not too near, as it should not be scorched.
2. Baste it plentifully until done; dish it, pour over the meat some good melted butter, and send to table with it a piece of boiled bacon and a cut lemon.

title: Roast Ducks
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A couple of ducks
- sage-and-onion stuffing No. 504
- a little flour

## Directions

1. Choose ducks with plump bellies, and with thick and yellowish feet.
2. They should be trussed with the feet on, which should be scalded, and the skin peeled off, and then turned up close to the legs.
3. Run a skewer through the middle of each leg, after having drawn them as close as possible to the body, to plump up the breast, passing the same quite through the body.
4. Cut off the heads and necks, and the pinions at the first joint; bring these close to the sides, twist the feet round, and truss them at the back of the bird.
5. After the duck is stuffed, both ends should be secured with string, so as to keep in the seasoning.
6. To insure ducks being tender, never dress them the same day they are killed; and if the weather permits, they should hang a day or two.
7. Make a stuffing of sage and onion sufficient for one duck, and leave the other unseasoned, as the flavour is not liked by everybody.
8. Put them down to a brisk clear fire, and keep them well basted the whole of the time they are cooking.
9. A few minutes before serving, dredge them lightly with flour, to make them froth and look plump.
10. When the steam draws towards the fire, send them to table hot and quickly, with a good brown gravy poured round, but not over the ducks, and a little of the same in a tureen.
11. When in season, green peas should invariably accompany this dish.

title: Roast Fillet of Beef (Larded)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- About 4 lbs. of the inside fillet of the sirloin
- 1 onion
- a small bunch of parsley
- salt and pepper to taste
- sufficient vinegar to cover the meat
- glaze
- Spanish sauce, No. 411

## Directions

1. Lard the beef with bacon, and put it into a pan with sufficient vinegar to cover it, with an onion sliced, parsley, and seasoning.
2. Let it remain in this pickle for 12 hours.
3. Roast it before a nice clear fire for about 1-1/4 hour.
4. When done, glaze it.
5. Pour some Spanish sauce round the beef, and the remainder serve in a tureen.
6. It may be garnished with Spanish onions boiled and glazed.

title: Roast Fillet of Veal
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal
- forcemeat No. 417
- melted butter
- flour

## Directions

1. Have the fillet cut according to the size required; take out the bone, and after raising the skin from the meat, put under the flap a nice forcemeat, made by recipe No. 417.
2. Prepare sufficient of this, as there should be some left to eat cold, and to season and flavour a mince if required.
3. Skewer and bind the veal up in a round form; dredge well with flour, put it down at some distance from the fire at first, and baste continually.
4. About 1/2 hour before serving, draw it nearer the fire, that it may acquire more colour, as the outside should be of a rich brown, but not burnt.
5. Dish it, remove the skewers, which replace by a silver one; pour over the joint some good melted butter, and serve with either boiled ham, bacon, or pickled pork.
6. Never omit to send a cut lemon to table with roast veal.

title: Roast Fore-Quarter of Lamb
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Lamb
- a little salt

## Directions

1. Make up the fire in good time, that it may be clear and brisk when the joint is put down
2. Place it at a sufficient distance to prevent the fat from burning, and baste it constantly till the moment of serving
3. Lamb should be very thoroughly done without being dried up, and not the slightest appearance of red gravy should be visible
4. Serve with a little gravy made in the dripping-pan, the same as for other roasts, and send to table with it a tureen of mint sauce, No. 469, and a fresh salad
5. A cut lemon, a small piece of fresh butter, and a little cayenne, should also be placed on the table

title: Roast Fowl, Stuffed
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A large fowl
- forcemeat No. 417
- a little flour
- brown gravy
- bread sauce

## Directions

1. Select a large plump fowl and fill the breast with forcemeat, made by recipe No. 417.
2. Truss it firmly, the same as for a plain roast fowl.
3. Dredge it with flour, and put it down to a bright fire.
4. Roast it for nearly or quite an hour, should it be very large.
5. Remove the skewers, and serve with a good brown gravy and a tureen of bread sauce.

title: Roast Fowls
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A pair of fowls
- a little flour

## Directions

1. Fowls to be tender should be killed a couple of days before they are dressed
2. when the feathers come out easily, then let them be picked and cooked
3. In drawing them, be careful not to break the gall-bag
4. the liver and gizzard should also be preserved
5. After having carefully picked them, cut off the head, and skewer the skin of the neck down over the back
6. Cut off the claws
7. dip the legs in boiling water, and scrape them
8. turn the pinions under, run a skewer through them and the middle of the legs, which should be passed through the body to the pinion and leg on the other side, one skewer securing the limbs on both sides
9. The liver and gizzard should be placed in the wings, the liver on one side and the gizzard on the other
10. Tie the legs together by passing a trussing-needle, threaded with twine, through the backbone, and secure it on the other side
11. When firmly trussed, singe them all over
12. put them down to a bright clear fire, paper the breasts with a sheet of buttered paper, and keep the fowls well basted
13. Roast them for 3/4 hour, more or less, according to the size, and 10 minutes before serving, remove the paper, dredge the fowls with a little fine flour, put a piece of butter into the basting-ladle, and as it melts, baste the fowls with it
14. when nicely frothed and of a rich colour, serve with good brown gravy, a little of which should be poured over the fowls, and a tureen of well-made bread sauce
15. Mushroom, oyster, or egg sauce are very suitable accompaniments to roast fowl

title: Roast Goose
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Goose
- 4 large onions
- 10 sage-leaves
- 1/4 lb. of bread crumbs
- 1-1/2 oz. of butter
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 egg

## Directions

1. Select a goose with a clean white skin, plump breast, and yellow feet: if these latter are red, the bird is old. Should the weather permit, let it hang for a few days: by so doing, the flavour will be very much improved.
2. Pluck, singe, draw, and carefully wash and wipe the goose; cut off the neck close to the back, leaving the skin long enough to turn over; cut off the feet at the first joint, and separate the pinions at the first joint.
3. Beat the breast-bone flat with a rolling-pin, put a skewer through the under part of each wing, and having drawn up the legs closely, put a skewer into the middle of each, and pass the same quite through the body.
4. Insert another skewer into the small of the leg, bring it close down to the side bone, run it through, and do the same to the other side.
5. Cut off the end of the vent, and make a hole in the skin sufficiently large for the passage of the rump, in order to keep in the seasoning.
6. Make a sage-and-onion stuffing of the above ingredients, by recipe No. 504; put it into the body of the goose, and secure it firmly at both ends, by passing the rump through the hole made in the skin, and the other end by tying the skin of the neck to the back; by this means the seasoning will not escape.
7. Put it down to a brisk fire, keep it well basted, and roast from 1-1/2 to 2 hours, according to the size.
8. Remove the skewers, and serve with a tureen of good gravy, and one of well-made apple-sauce.
9. A ragoût, or pie, should be made of the giblets, or they may be stewed down to make gravy.
10. Be careful to serve the goose before the breast falls, or its appearance will be spoiled by coming flattened to table.
11. As this is rather a troublesome joint to carve, a large quantity of gravy should not be poured round the goose, but sent in a tureen.

title: Roast Griskin of Pork
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Pork
- powdered sage

## Directions

1. Put it down to a bright fire, and flour it.
2. About 10 minutes before taking it up, sprinkle over some powdered sage.
3. Make a little gravy in the dripping-pan, strain it over the meat, and serve with a tureen of apple sauce.
4. Take particular care that it is well basted to prevent it from becoming hard and dry.

title: Roast Grouse
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Grouse
- butter
- a thick slice of toasted bread

## Directions

1. Let the birds hang as long as possible.
2. Pluck and draw them.
3. Wipe, but do not wash them, inside and out.
4. Truss them without the head, the same as for a roast fowl.
5. Put them down to a sharp clear fire.
6. Keep them well basted the whole of the time they are cooking.
7. Serve them on a buttered toast, soaked in the dripping-pan, with a little melted butter poured over them, or with bread-sauce and gravy.

title: Roast Guinea-Fowl, Larded
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A Guinea-fowl
- lardoons
- flour
- salt
- gravy
- bread-sauce

## Directions

1. When this bird is larded, it should be trussed the same as a pheasant; if plainly roasted, truss it like a turkey.
2. After larding and trussing it, put it down to roast at a brisk fire.
3. Keep it well basted, and a short time before serving, dredge it with a little flour, and let it froth nicely.
4. The breast, if larded, should be covered with a piece of paper, and removed about 10 minutes before serving.
5. Serve with a little gravy in the dish, and a tureen of the same, and one of well-made bread-sauce.

title: Roast Hare
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Hare
- forcemeat No. 417
- a little milk
- butter

## Directions

1. Choose a young hare; which may be known by its smooth and sharp claws, and by the cleft in the lip not being much spread.
2. To be eaten in perfection, it must hang for some time; and, if properly taken care of, it may be kept for several days.
3. It is better to hang without being paunched; but should it be previously emptied, wipe the inside every day, and sprinkle over it a little pepper and ginger, to prevent the musty taste which long keeping in the damp occasions, and which also affects the stuffing.
4. After it is skinned, wash it well, and soak for an hour in warm water to draw out the blood; if old, let it lie in vinegar for a short time, but wash it well afterwards in several waters.
5. Make a forcemeat by recipe No. 417, wipe the hare dry, fill the belly with it, and sew it up.
6. Bring the hind and fore legs close to the body towards the head, run a skewer through each, fix the head between the shoulders by means of another skewer, and be careful to leave the ears on.
7. Pat a string round the body from skewer to skewer, and tie it above the back.
8. The hare should be kept at a distance from the fire when it is first laid down, or the outside will become dry and hard before the inside is done.
9. Baste it well with milk for a short time, and afterwards with butter; and particular attention must be paid to the basting, so as to preserve the meat on the back juicy and nutritive.
10. When it is almost roasted enough, flour the hare, and baste well with butter.
11. When nicely frothed, dish it, remove the skewers, and send it to table with a little gravy in the dish, and a tureen of the same.
12. Red-currant jelly must also not be forgotten, as this is an indispensable accompaniment to roast hare.
13. For economy, good beef dripping may be substituted for the milk and butter to baste with; but the basting, as we have before stated, must be continued without intermission.
14. If the liver is good, it maybe parboiled, minced, and mixed with the stuffing; but it should not be used unless quite fresh.

title: Roast Haunch of Mutton
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Haunch of mutton
- salt
- flour
- boiling water
- red-currant jelly

## Directions

1. Let this joint hang as long as possible without becoming tainted, and while hanging dust flour over it
2. Wash the outside well, lest it should have a bad flavour from keeping
3. Flour it and put it down to a nice brisk fire, at some distance, so that it may gradually warm through
4. Keep continually basting
5. About 1/2 hour before it is served, draw it nearer to the fire to get nicely brown
6. Sprinkle a little fine salt over the meat, pour off the dripping, add a little boiling water slightly salted, and strain this over the joint
7. Place a paper ruche on the bone, and send red-currant jelly and gravy in a tureen to table with it

title: Roast Haunch of Venison
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Venison
- coarse flour-and-water paste
- flour
- butter
- powdered ginger or pepper
- white paper
- twine
- currant jelly
- port wine
- gravy

## Directions
