1. Choose a haunch with clear, bright, and thick fat, and the cleft of the hoof smooth and close.
2. Ascertain how long it has been kept by running a sharp skewer into the meat close to the bone.
3. Keep it perfectly dry by wiping it with clean cloths till not the least damp remains.
4. Sprinkle over powdered ginger or pepper, as a preventative against the fly.
5. When required for use, wash it in warm water, and dry it well with a cloth.
6. Butter a sheet of white paper, put it over the fat.
7. Lay a coarse paste, about 1/2 inch in thickness, over this, and then a sheet or two of strong paper.
8. Tie the whole firmly on to the haunch with twine.
9. Put the joint down to a strong close fire.
10. Baste the venison immediately, to prevent the paper and string from burning.
11. Continue this operation without intermission the whole of the time it is cooking.
12. About 20 minutes before it is done, carefully remove the paste and paper.
13. Dredge the joint with flour, and baste well with butter until it is nicely frothed, and of a nice pale-brown colour.
14. Garnish the knuckle-bone with a frill of white paper.
15. Serve with a good, strong, but unflavoured gravy in a tureen, and currant jelly; or melt the jelly with a little port wine, and serve that also in a tureen.
16. Send very hot plates to table, as the venison fat so soon freezes.

title: Roast Landrail, or Corn-Crake
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 or 4 birds
- butter
- fried bread crumbs
- brown gravy
- bread-sauce

## Directions

1. Pluck and draw the birds, wipe them inside and out with damp cloths, and truss them in the following manner: Bring the head round under the wing, and the thighs close to the sides; pass a skewer through them and the body, and keep the legs straight.
2. Roast them before a clear fire, keep them well basted, and serve on fried bread crumbs, with a tureen of brown gravy.
3. When liked, bread-sauce may also be sent to table with them.

title: Roast Larks
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Larks
- egg
- bread crumbs
- fresh butter
- clarified butter
- slices of lemon

## Directions

1. Pick, gut, and clean them; when they are trussed, brush them over with the yolk of an egg.
2. Sprinkle with bread crumbs, and roast them before a quick fire.
3. Baste them continually with fresh butter, and keep sprinkling with the bread crumbs until the birds are well covered.
4. Dish them on bread crumbs fried in clarified butter, and garnish the dish with slices of lemon.

title: Roast Leg of Lamb
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Lamb
- a little salt

## Directions

1. Place the joint at a good distance from the fire at first, and baste well the whole time it is cooking
2. When nearly done, draw it nearer the fire to acquire a nice brown colour
3. Sprinkle a little fine salt over the meat, empty the dripping-pan of its contents
4. Pour in a little boiling water, and strain this over the meat
5. Serve with mint sauce and a fresh salad, and for vegetables send peas, spinach, or cauliflowers to table with it

title: Roast Leg of Mutton
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Leg of mutton
- salt
- flour
- boiling water

## Directions

1. Hang the mutton almost as long as it will keep
2. Flour it, and put it in a cool airy place for a few days, if the weather will permit
3. Wash off the flour and wipe it very dry
4. Cut off the shank-bone and put it down to a brisk clear fire
5. Dredge with flour, and keep continually basting the whole time it is cooking
6. About 20 minutes before serving, draw it near the fire to get nicely brown
7. Sprinkle over it a little salt, dish the meat, pour off the dripping
8. Add some boiling water slightly salted, strain it over the joint, and serve

title: Roast Leg of Pork
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Leg of pork
- a little oil for stuffing
- sage-and-onion stuffing (Recipe No. 504)
- salad-oil
- gravy made in the dripping-pan
- apple-sauce (No. 363)

## Directions

1. Choose a small leg of pork, and score the skin across in narrow strips, about 1/4 inch apart.
2. Cut a slit in the knuckle, loosen the skin, and fill it with a sage-and-onion stuffing, made by Recipe No. 504.
3. Brush the joint over with a little salad-oil (this makes the crackling crisper, and a better colour), and put it down to a bright, clear fire, not too near, as that would cause the skin to blister.
4. Baste it well, and serve with a little gravy made in the dripping-pan, and do not omit to send to table with it a tureen of well-made apple-sauce.

title: Roast Loin of Mutton
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Loin of mutton
- salt
- flour

## Directions

1. Cut and trim off the superfluous fat
2. See that the butcher joints the meat properly
3. Have ready a nice clear fire and put down the meat
4. Dredge with flour, and baste well until it is done
5. Make the gravy as for roast leg of mutton, and serve very hot

title: Roast Loin of Pork
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Pork
- salt
- salad oil

## Directions

1. Score the skin in strips rather more than 1/4 inch apart.
2. Place the joint at a good distance from the fire, on account of the crackling, which would harden before the meat would be heated through, were it placed too near.
3. If very lean, rub it over with a little salad oil, and keep it well basted all the time it is at the fire.
4. Pork should be very thoroughly cooked, but not dry; be careful never to send it to table the least underdone.
5. Serve with apple sauce and a little gravy made in the dripping-pan.
6. A stuffing of sage and onion may be made separately, and baked in a flat dish.

title: Roast Loin of Veal
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal
- melted butter

## Directions

1. Paper the kidney fat; roll in and skewer the flap, which makes the joint a good shape; dredge it well with flour, and put it down to a bright fire.
2. Should the loin be very large, skewer the kidney back for a time to roast thoroughly.
3. Keep it well basted, and a short time before serving, remove the paper from the kidney, and allow it to acquire a nice brown colour, but it should not be burnt.
4. Have ready some melted butter, put it into the dripping-pan after it is emptied of its contents, pour it over the veal, and serve.
5. Garnish the dish with slices of lemon and forcemeat balls, and send to table with it, boiled bacon, ham, pickled pork, or pig's cheek.

title: Roast Neck of Mutton
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Neck of mutton
- a little salt

## Directions

1. For roasting, choose the middle, or the best end, of the neck of mutton, and if there is a very large proportion of fat, trim off some of it, and save it for making into suet puddings.
2. Let the bones be cut short and see that it is properly jointed before it is laid down to the fire, as they will be more easily separated when they come to table.
3. Place the joint at a nice brisk fire, dredge it with flour, and keep continually basting until done.
4. A few minutes before serving, draw it nearer the fire to acquire a nice colour, sprinkle over it a little salt, pour off the dripping, add a little boiling water slightly salted, strain this over the meat and serve.
5. Red-currant jelly may be sent to table with it.

title: Roast Neck of Veal
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal
- melted butter
- forcemeat balls
- flour
- lemon

## Directions

1. Have the veal cut from the best end of the neck
2. Dredge it with flour, and put it down to a bright clear fire
3. Keep it well basted
4. Dish it, pour over it some melted butter, and garnish the dish with fried forcemeat balls
5. Send to table with a cut lemon
6. The scrag may be boiled or stewed in various ways, with rice, onion-sauce, or parsley and butter

title: Roast Neck of Veal
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal
- melted butter
- forcemeat balls

## Directions

1. Have the veal cut from the best end of the neck; dredge it with flour, and put it down to a bright clear fire; keep it well basted; dish it, pour over it some melted butter, and garnish the dish with fried forcemeat balls; send to table with a cut lemon.
2. The scrag may be boiled or stewed in various ways, with rice, onion-sauce, or parsley and butter.

title: Roast Or Baked Rabbit
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 rabbit
- forcemeat No. 417
- buttered paper
- sausage-meat
- minced liver

## Directions

1. Empty, skin, and thoroughly wash the rabbit; wipe it dry, line the inside with sausage-meat and forcemeat made by recipe No. 417, and to which has been added the minced liver.
2. Sew the stuffing inside, skewer back the head between the shoulders, cut off the fore-joints of the shoulders and legs, bring them close to the body, and secure them by means of a skewer.
3. Wrap the rabbit in buttered paper, and put it down to a bright clear fire; keep it well basted, and a few minutes before it is done remove the paper, flour and froth it, and let it acquire a nice brown colour.
4. Take out the skewers, and serve with brown gravy and red-currant jelly.
5. To bake the rabbit, proceed in the same manner as above; in a good oven, it will take about the same time as roasting.

title: Roast Partridge
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Partridge
- butter

## Directions

1. Choose young birds, with dark-coloured bills and yellowish legs, and let them hang a few days.
2. Pluck, draw, and wipe the partridge carefully inside and out.
3. Cut off the head, leaving sufficient skin on the neck to skewer back.
4. Bring the legs close to the breast, between it and the side-bones, and pass a skewer through the pinions and the thick part of the thighs.
5. When the bird is firmly and plumply trussed, roast it before a nice bright fire.
6. Keep it well basted, and a few minutes before serving, flour and froth it well.
7. Dish it, and serve with gravy and bread sauce, and send to table hot and quickly.
8. A little of the gravy should be poured over the bird.

title: Roast Pheasant
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Pheasant
- flour
- butter
- brown gravy
- bread sauce

## Directions

1. Pluck and draw the bird
2. Wipe the inside with a damp cloth
3. Truss it in the same manner as partridge, No. 1039
4. If the head is left on, bring it round under the wing, and fix it on to the point of the skewer
5. Roast it before a brisk fire
6. Keep it well basted
7. Flour and froth it nicely
8. Serve with brown gravy, a little of which should be poured round the bird, and a tureen of bread sauce

title: Roast Pigeon
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- pigeons
- pepper
- salt
- 3/4 oz. butter per bird
- fried parsley
- parsley and butter

## Directions

1. Wipe the birds very dry
2. Season them inside with pepper and salt
3. Put about 3/4 oz. of butter into the body of each
4. Put them down to a bright fire
5. Baste them well the whole of the time they are cooking
6. Cook from 20 to 30 minutes until done
7. Garnish with fried parsley
8. Serve with a tureen of parsley and butter
9. Serve with bread-sauce and gravy the same as for roast fowl, or egg-sauce

title: Roast Pigeons
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Pigeons
- 3 oz. of butter
- pepper and salt to taste
- fried parsley
- parsley and butter
- Bread-sauce
- gravy
- egg-sauce

## Directions

1. Cut off the heads and necks, truss the wings over the backs, and cut off the toes at the first joint.
2. Pigeons should be carefully cleaned, as no bird requires so much washing.
3. Wipe the birds very dry, season them inside with pepper and salt, and put about 3/4 oz. of butter into the body of each.
4. Put them down to a bright fire, and baste them well the whole of the time they are cooking (they will be done enough in from 20 to 30 minutes).
5. Garnish with fried parsley, and serve with a tureen of parsley and butter. Bread-sauce and gravy, the same as for roast fowl, are exceedingly nice accompaniments to roast pigeons, as also egg-sauce.

title: Roast Ribs of Beef
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Beef
- a little salt
- clean dripping
- flour
- fine salt
- boiling water

## Directions

1. Let the meat be well hung (should the weather permit), and cut off the thin ends of the bones, which should be salted for a few days, and then boiled
2. Put the meat down to a nice clear fire, put some clean dripping into the pan, dredge the joint with a little flour, and keep continually basting the whole time
3. Sprinkle some fine salt over it (this must never be done until the joint is dished, as it draws the juices from the meat)
4. Pour the dripping from the pan, put in a little boiling water slightly salted, and strain the gravy over the meat
5. Garnish with tufts of scraped horseradish, and send horseradish sauce to table with it

title: Roast Ribs of Beef, Boned and Rolled (a Very Convenient Joint for a Small Family)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 or 2 ribs of beef
- wooden skewers
- tape

## Directions

1. Choose a fine rib of beef, and have it cut according to the weight you require, either wide or narrow
2. Bone and roll the meat round, secure it with wooden skewers, and, if necessary, bind it round with a piece of tape
3. Spit the beef firmly, or, if a bottle-jack is used, put the joint on the hook, and place it near a nice clear fire
4. Let it remain so till the outside of the meat is set, when draw it to a distance, and keep continually basting until the meat is done, which can be ascertained by the steam from it drawing towards the fire
5. Remove the skewers, put in a plated or silver one, and send the joint to table with gravy in the dish, and garnish with tufts of horseradish

title: Roast Saddle of Lamb
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Lamb
- a little salt

## Directions

1. Have ready a clear brisk fire
2. Put down the joint at a little distance, to prevent the fat from scorching, and keep it well basted all the time it is cooking
3. Serve with mint sauce and a fresh salad, and send to table with it, either peas, cauliflowers, or spinach

title: Roast Shoulder of Lamb
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Lamb
- a little salt

## Directions

1. Have ready a clear brisk fire, and put down the joint at a sufficient distance from it, that the fat may not burn.
2. Keep constantly basting until done, and serve with a little gravy made in the dripping-pan, and send mint sauce to table with it.
3. Serve with peas, spinach, or cauliflowers, and also a fresh salad.

title: Roast Shoulder of Mutton
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Shoulder of mutton
- a little salt

## Directions

1. Put the joint down to a bright, clear fire; flour it well, and keep continually basting.
2. About 1/4 hour before serving, draw it near the fire, that the outside may acquire a nice brown colour, but not sufficiently near to blacken the fat.
3. Sprinkle a little fine salt over the meat, empty the dripping-pan of its contents, pour in a little boiling water slightly salted, and strain this over the joint.
4. Serve with onion sauce, or stewed Spanish onions, and sometimes baked potatoes.

title: Roast Sirloin of Beef
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Beef
- a little salt
- flour
- boiling water

## Directions

1. See that the fire is well made up about 3/4 hour before it is required, so that when the joint is put down, it is clear and bright
2. Choose a nice sirloin, the weight of which should not exceed 16 lbs., as the outside would be too much done, whilst the inside would not be done enough
3. Spit it or hook it on to the jack firmly, dredge it slightly with flour, and place it near the fire at first
4. Draw it to a distance, and keep continually basting until the meat is done
5. Sprinkle a small quantity of salt over it, empty the dripping-pan of all the dripping, pour in some boiling water slightly salted, stir it about, and strain over the meat
6. Garnish with tufts of horseradish

title: Roast Snipe
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Snipes
- butter
- flour
- buttered toast
- brown gravy

## Directions

1. Skin the snipe and the neck.
2. Twist the legs at the first joint, press the feet upon the thighs, and pass a skewer through these and the body.
3. Place four on a skewer, tie them on to the jack or spit.
4. Roast before a clear fire for about 1/4 hour.
5. Put some pieces of buttered toast into the dripping-pan to catch the trails.
6. Flour and froth the birds nicely.
7. Dish the pieces of toast with the snipes on them, and pour round, but not over them, a little good brown gravy.
8. Send to table very hot and expeditiously.

title: Roast Sturgeon
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal stuffing
- buttered paper
- the tail-end of a sturgeon

## Directions

1. Cleanse the fish, bone and skin it
2. Make a nice veal stuffing (see Forcemeats), and fill it with the part where the bones came from
3. Roll it in buttered paper
4. Bind it up firmly with tape, like a fillet of veal
5. Roast it in a Dutch oven before a clear fire
6. Serve with good brown gravy, or plain melted butter

title: Roast Sucking-Pig
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Pig
- 6 oz. of bread crumbs
- 16 sage-leaves
- pepper and salt to taste
- a piece of butter the size of an egg
- salad oil or butter to baste with
- about 1/2 pint of gravy
- 1 tablespoonful of lemon-juice

## Directions

1. After preparing the pig for cooking, stuff it with finely-grated bread crumbs, minced sage, pepper, salt, and a piece of butter the size of an egg, all of which should be well mixed together, and put into the body of the pig.
2. Sew up the slit neatly, and truss the legs back, to allow the inside to be roasted, and the under part to be crisp.
3. Put the pig down to a bright clear fire, not too near, and let it lay till thoroughly dry.
4. Have ready some butter tied up in a piece of thin cloth, and rub the pig with this in every part.
5. Keep it well rubbed with the butter the whole of the time it is roasting, and do not allow the crackling to become blistered or burnt.
6. When half-done, hang a pig-iron before the middle part (if this is not obtainable, use a flat iron), to prevent its being scorched and dried up before the ends are done.
7. Before it is taken from the fire, cut off the head, and part that and the body down the middle.
8. Chop the brains and mix them with the stuffing; add 1/2 pint of good gravy, a tablespoonful of lemon-juice, and the gravy that flowed from the pig.
9. Put a little of this on the dish with the pig, and send the remainder to table in a tureen.
10. Place the pig back to back in the dish, with one half of the head on each side, and one of the ears at each end, and send it to table as hot as possible.

title: Roast Teal
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Teal
- butter
- flour
- brown or orange gravy
- water-cresses
- lemon

## Directions

1. Choose fat plump birds, after the frost has set in.
2. Truss them in the same manner as wild duck, No. 1022.
3. Roast them before a brisk fire and keep them well basted.
4. Serve with brown or orange gravy, water-cresses, and a cut lemon.

title: Roast Turkey
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Turkey
- forcemeat No. 417
- sheet of buttered paper
- flour
- butter
- good brown gravy
- bread sauce

## Directions

1. Choose cock turkeys by their short spurs and black legs for young birds; avoid those with long spurs and pale rough legs as they are old
2. Carefully pluck the bird, singe it with white paper, and wipe it thoroughly with a cloth
3. Draw it, preserve the liver and gizzard, and be particular not to break the gall-bag
4. Wash it inside well, and wipe it thoroughly dry with a cloth; the outside merely requires nicely wiping
5. Cut off the neck close to the back, but leave enough of the crop-skin to turn over
6. Break the leg-bone close below the knee, draw out the strings from the thighs, and flatten the breastbone to make it look plump
7. Have ready a forcemeat made by recipe No. 417; fill the breast with this, and sew the neck over to the back with a trussing-needle or use a skewer
8. Run a skewer through the pinion and thigh into the body to the pinion and thigh on the other side, and press the legs as much as possible between the breast and the side bones
9. Put the liver under one pinion and the gizzard under the other
10. Pass a string across the back of the bird, catch it over the points of the skewer, tie it in the centre of the back, and ensure the turkey is very firmly trussed
11. Fasten a sheet of buttered paper on to the breast of the bird, put it down to a bright fire, at some little distance at first (afterwards draw it nearer), and keep it well basted the whole of the time it is cooking
12. About 1/4 hour before serving, remove the paper, dredge the turkey lightly with flour, and put a piece of butter into the basting-ladle
13. As the butter melts, baste the bird with it
14. When of a nice brown and well frothed, serve with a tureen of good brown gravy and one of bread sauce
15. Fried sausages may be used as a garnish, or forcemeat balls should be placed round the dish

title: Roast Turkey Poults
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Turkey poult
- butter
- flour
- water-cresses (optional)
- gravy

## Directions

1. Choose a plump bird, and truss it in the following manner: After it has been carefully plucked, drawn, and singed, skin the neck, and fasten the head under the wing; turn the legs at the first joint, and bring the feet close to the thighs, as a woodcock should be trussed, and do not stuff it.
2. Put it down to a bright fire, keep it well basted, and at first place a piece of paper on the breast to prevent its taking too much colour.
3. About 10 minutes before serving, dredge it lightly with flour, and baste well; when nicely frothed, send it to table immediately, with a little gravy in the dish, and some in a tureen.
4. If at hand, a few water-cresses may be placed round the turkey as a garnish, or it may be larded.
5. Time: About 1 hour. Sufficient for 6 or 7 persons.

title: Roast Widgeon
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Widgeons
- a little flour
- butter
- brown gravy or orange gravy
- a cut lemon

## Directions

1. Truss in the same manner as wild duck, No. 1022, but must not be kept so long before they are dressed.
2. Put them down to a brisk fire; flour, and baste them continually with butter.
3. When browned and nicely frothed, send them to table hot and quickly.
4. Serve with brown gravy, or orange gravy, No. 488, and a cut lemon.

title: Roast Wild Duck
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Wild duck
- flour
- butter
- lemon

## Directions

1. Carefully pluck and draw the duck.
2. Cut off the heads close to the necks, leaving sufficient skin to turn over, and do not cut off the feet.
3. Truss the birds as desired.
4. Roast the birds before a quick fire.
5. When first put down, let them remain for 5 minutes without basting to keep the gravy in.
6. Afterwards baste plentifully with butter.
7. A few minutes before serving dredge them lightly with flour and baste well.
8. Send them to table nicely frothed and full of gravy.
9. If the birds have a fishy taste, baste them for a few minutes with hot water to which have been added an onion and a little salt, then take away the pan and baste with butter.
10. Serve with a good gravy in the dish, or orange gravy, and send to table with them a cut lemon.

title: Roast Woodcock
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Woodcocks
- butter
- flour
- toast

## Directions

1. Woodcocks should not be drawn, as the trails are, by epicures, considered a great delicacy.
2. Pluck, and wipe them well outside; truss them with the legs close to the body, and the feet pressing upon the thighs; skin the neck and head, and bring the beak round under the wing.
3. Place some slices of toast in the dripping-pan to catch the trails, allowing a piece of toast for each bird.
4. Roast before a clear fire from 15 to 25 minutes; keep them well basted, and flour and froth them nicely.
5. When done, dish the pieces of toast with the birds upon them, and pour round a very little gravy; send some more to table in a tureen.

title: Rock Biscuits
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 eggs
- 1 lb. of sifted sugar
- 1/2 lb. of flour
- a few currants

## Directions

1. Break the eggs into a basin, beat them well until very light
2. add the pounded sugar
3. when this is well mixed with the eggs, dredge in the flour gradually, and add the currants
4. Mix all well together
5. put the dough, with a fork, on the tins, making it look as rough as possible
6. Bake the cakes in a moderate oven from 20 minutes to 1/2 hour
7. when they are done, allow them to get cool, and store them away in a tin canister, in a dry place

title: Rolled Beef, to Eat Like Hare
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- About 5 lbs. of the inside of the sirloin
- 2 glasses of port wine
- 2 glasses of vinegar
- a small quantity of forcemeat (No. 417)
- 1 teaspoonful of pounded allspice

## Directions

1. Take the inside of a large sirloin, soak it in 1 glass of port wine and 1 glass of vinegar, mixed, and let it remain for 2 days
2. Make a forcemeat by recipe No. 417, lay it on the meat, and bind it up securely
3. Roast it before a nice clear fire, and baste it with 1 glass each of port wine and vinegar, with which mix a teaspoonful of pounded allspice
4. Serve, with a good gravy in the dish, and send red-currant jelly to table with it

title: Rolled Loin of Mutton
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- About 6 lbs. of a loin of mutton
- 1/2 teaspoonful of pepper
- 1/4 teaspoonful of pounded allspice
- 1/4 teaspoonful of mace
- 1/4 teaspoonful of nutmeg
- 6 cloves
- forcemeat
- 1 glass of port wine
- 2 tablespoonfuls of mushroom ketchup
- red-currant jelly
- mushrooms

## Directions

1. Hang the mutton till tender and bone it
2. Sprinkle over it pepper, mace, cloves, allspice, and nutmeg, all of which must be pounded very fine
3. Let it remain for a day
4. Make a forcemeat by recipe No. 417 and cover the meat with it
5. Roll and bind it up firmly
6. Half bake it in a slow oven and let it grow cold
7. Take off the fat, and put the gravy into a stewpan
8. Flour the meat, put it in the gravy, and stew it till perfectly tender
9. Take out the meat and unbind it
10. Add to the gravy wine and ketchup, give one boil, and pour over the meat
11. Serve with red-currant jelly and, if obtainable, a few mushrooms stewed for a few minutes in the gravy

title: Rolled Treacle Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of suet crust
- 1 lb. of treacle
- 1/2 teaspoonful of grated ginger

## Directions

1. Make a suet crust with 1 lb. of flour by rolling it out to the thickness of 1/2 inch
2. Spread the treacle equally over it, leaving a small margin where the paste joins
3. Close the ends securely
4. Tie the pudding in a floured cloth
5. Plunge it into boiling water, and boil for 2 hours

title: Roly-Poly Jam Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 lb of suet-crust
- 3/4 lb. of any kind of jam

## Directions

1. Make a nice light suet-crust and roll it out to the thickness of about 1/2 inch
2. Spread the jam equally over it, leaving a small margin of paste without any, where the pudding joins
3. Roll it up, fasten the ends securely, and tie it in a floured cloth
4. Put the pudding into boiling water, and boil for 2 hours

title: Royal Coburg Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of new milk
- 6 oz. of flour
- 6 oz. of sugar
- 6 oz. of butter
- 6 oz. of currants
- 6 eggs
- brandy and grated nutmeg to taste

## Directions

1. Mix the flour to a smooth batter with the milk
2. Add the remaining ingredients gradually
3. When well mixed, put it into four basins or moulds half full
4. Bake for 3/4 hour
5. Turn the puddings out on a dish, and serve with wine sauce

title: Sage-and-Onion Stuffing
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 large onions
- 10 sage-leaves
- 1/4 lb. of bread crumbs
- 1-1/2 oz. of butter
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 egg

## Directions

1. Peel the onions, put them into boiling water, let them simmer for 5 minutes or rather longer, and, just before they are taken out, put in the sage-leaves for a minute or two to take off their rawness.
2. Chop both these very fine, add the bread, seasoning, and butter, and work the mixture together.

title: Sage-and-Onion Stuffing for Geese, Ducks, and Pork
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 large onions
- 10 sage-leaves
- 1/4 lb. of bread crumbs
- 1-1/2 oz. of butter
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 egg

## Directions

1. Peel the onions, put them into boiling water, let them simmer for 5 minutes or rather longer, and, just before they are taken out, put in the sage-leaves for a minute or two to take off their rawness.
2. Chop both these very fine, add the bread, seasoning, and butter, and work the whole together with the yolk of an egg, when the stuffing will be ready for use.
3. It should be rather highly seasoned, and the sage-leaves should be very finely chopped.

title: Sago Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 pint of milk
- 3 tablespoonfuls of sago
- rind of 1/2 lemon
- 3 oz. of sugar
- 4 eggs
- 1-1/2 oz. of butter
- grated nutmeg
- puff-paste

## Directions

1. Put the milk and lemon-rind into a stewpan, place it by the side of the fire, and let it remain until the milk is well flavoured with the lemon
2. Strain it, mix with it the sago and sugar
3. Simmer gently for about 15 minutes
4. Let the mixture cool a little
5. Stir to it the eggs, which should be well beaten, and the butter
6. Line the edges of a pie-dish with puff-paste
7. Pour in the pudding
8. Grate a little nutmeg over the top
9. Bake from 3/4 to 1 hour

title: Sago Sauce For Sweet Puddings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 tablespoonful of sago
- 1/3 pint of water
- 1/4 pint of port or sherry
- rind and juice of 1 small lemon
- sugar to taste
- a little pounded cinnamon when the flavour is liked

## Directions

1. Wash the sago in two or three waters
2. Put it into a saucepan, with the water and lemon-peel
3. Let it simmer gently by the side of the fire for 10 minutes
4. Take out the lemon-peel
5. Add the remaining ingredients
6. Give one boil, and serve
7. Be particular to strain the lemon-juice before adding it to the sauce

title: Sago Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 5 oz. of sago
- 2 quarts of stock No. 105

## Directions

1. Wash the sago in boiling water, and add it, by degrees, to the boiling stock, and simmer till the sago is entirely dissolved, and forms a sort of jelly

title: Salad Dressing (Excellent) I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 teaspoonful of mixed mustard
- 1 teaspoonful of pounded sugar
- 2 tablespoonfuls of salad oil
- 4 tablespoonfuls of milk
- 2 tablespoonfuls of vinegar
- cayenne and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Put the mixed mustard into a salad-bowl with the sugar, and add the oil drop by drop, carefully stirring and mixing all these ingredients well together.
2. Proceed in this manner with the milk and vinegar, which must be added very gradually, or the sauce will curdle.
3. Put in the seasoning, when the mixture will be ready for use.

title: Salad Dressing (Excellent) II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 eggs
- 1 teaspoonful of mixed mustard
- 1/4 teaspoonful of white pepper
- half that quantity of cayenne
- salt to taste
- 4 tablespoonfuls of cream
- vinegar

## Directions

1. Boil the eggs until hard, which will be in about 1/4 hour or 20 minutes.
2. Put them into cold water, take off the shells, and pound the yolks in a mortar to a smooth paste.
3. Add all the other ingredients, except the vinegar, and stir them well until the whole are thoroughly incorporated one with the other.
4. Pour in sufficient vinegar to make it of the consistency of cream, taking care to add but little at a time.
5. The mixture will then be ready for use.

title: Salad Dressing (Excellent) III
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 egg
- 1 teaspoonful of salad oil
- 1 teaspoonful of mixed mustard
- 1/4 teaspoonful of salt
- 1/2 teaspoonful of pounded sugar
- 2 tablespoonfuls of vinegar
- 6 tablespoonfuls of cream

## Directions

1. Prepare and mix the ingredients by the preceding recipe, and be very particular that the whole is well stirred.

title: Salmi de Perdrix, or Hashed Partridges
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 young partridges
- 3 shalots
- a slice of lean ham
- 1 carrot
- 3 or 4 mushrooms
- a bunch of savoury herbs
- 2 cloves
- 6 whole peppers
- 3/4 pint of stock
- 1 glass of sherry or Madeira
- a small lump of sugar
- butter

## Directions

1. After the partridges are plucked and drawn, roast them rather underdone, and cover them with paper, as they should not be browned.
2. Cut them into joints, take off the skin from the wings, legs, and breasts; put these into a stewpan, cover them up, and set by until the gravy is ready.
3. Cut a slice of ham into small pieces, and put them, with the carrots sliced, the shalots, mushrooms, herbs, cloves, and pepper, into a stewpan.
4. Fry them lightly in a little butter, pour in the stock, add the bones and trimming from the partridges, and simmer for 1/4 hour.
5. Strain the gravy, let it cool, and skim off every particle of fat.
6. Put it to the legs, wings, and breasts, add a glass of sherry or Madeira and a small lump of sugar.
7. Let all gradually warm through by the side of the fire, and when on the point of boiling, serve, and garnish the dish with croûtons.

title: Salmon and Caper Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 slices of salmon
- 1/4 lb. butter
- 1/2 teaspoonful of chopped parsley
- 1 shalot
- salt, pepper, and grated nutmeg to taste
- caper sauce

## Directions

1. Lay the salmon in a baking-dish, place pieces of butter over it, and add the other ingredients, rubbing a little of the seasoning into the fish
2. Baste it frequently
3. When done, take it out and drain for a minute or two
4. Lay it in a dish, pour caper sauce over it, and serve

title: Salmon Cutlets
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- slices of salmon, 1 inch thick
- pepper and salt
- butter
- white paper
- anchovy or caper sauce
- chopped herbs (optional)
- spice (optional)

## Directions

1. Cut the slices 1 inch thick, and season them with pepper and salt
2. butter a sheet of white paper, lay each slice on a separate piece, with their ends twisted
3. broil gently over a clear fire
4. serve with anchovy or caper sauce
5. When higher seasoning is required, add a few chopped herbs and a little spice

title: Salmon à la Genevese
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 slices of salmon
- 2 chopped shalots
- a little parsley
- a small bunch of herbs
- 2 bay-leaves
- 2 carrots
- pounded mace
- pepper and salt to taste
- 4 tablespoonfuls of Madeira
- 1/2 pint of white stock (No. 107)
- thickening of butter and flour
- 1 teaspoonful of essence of anchovies
- the juice of 1 lemon
- cayenne and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Rub the bottom of a stewpan over with butter, and put in the shalots, herbs, bay-leaves, carrots, mace, and seasoning
2. stir them for 10 minutes over a clear fire, and add the Madeira or sherry
3. simmer gently for 1/2 hour, and strain through a sieve over the fish
4. stew the fish in this gravy
5. As soon as the fish is sufficiently cooked, take away all the liquor, except a little to keep the salmon moist, and put it into another stewpan
6. add the stock, thicken with butter and flour
7. put in the anchovies, lemon-juice, cayenne, and salt
8. lay the salmon on a hot dish, pour over it part of the sauce, and serve the remainder in a tureen

title: Salt Cod, Commonly Called Salt-Fish
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Sufficient water to cover the fish
- 1/4 pint of vinegar
- Hard-boiled eggs

## Directions

1. Wash the fish, and lay it all night in water, with a 1/4 pint of vinegar.
2. When thoroughly soaked, take it out, see that it is perfectly clean, and put it in the fish-kettle with sufficient cold water to cover it.
3. Heat it gradually, but do not let it boil much, or the fish will be hard.
4. Skim well, and when done, drain the fish and put it on a napkin garnished with hard-boiled eggs cut in rings.

title: Sauce a l'Aurore, for Trout, Soles, &c.
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The spawn of 1 lobster
- 1 oz. of butter
- 1/2 pint of Béchamel
- the juice of 1/2 lemon
- salt and cayenne to taste

## Directions

1. Take the spawn and pound it in a mortar with the butter, until quite smooth, and work it through a hair sieve.
2. Put the Béchamel into a stewpan, add the pounded spawn, the lemon-juice, which must be strained, and a plentiful seasoning of cayenne and salt.
3. Let it just simmer, but do not allow it to boil, or the beautiful red colour of the sauce will be spoiled.
4. A small spoonful of anchovy essence may be added at pleasure.

title: Sauce a la Matelote, for Fish
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of Espagnole
- 3 onions
- 2 tablespoonfuls of mushroom ketchup
- 1/2 glass of port wine
- a bunch of sweet herbs
- 1/2 bay-leaf
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 clove
- 2 berries of allspice
- a little liquor in which the fish has been boiled
- lemon-juice
- anchovy sauce

## Directions

1. Slice and fry the onions of a nice brown colour, and put them into a stewpan with the Espagnole, ketchup, wine, and a little liquor in which the fish has been boiled.
2. Add the seasoning, herbs, and spices, and simmer gently for 10 minutes, stirring well the whole time.
3. Strain it through a fine hair sieve, put in the lemon-juice and anchovy sauce, and pour it over the fish.
4. This sauce may be very much enriched by adding a few small quenelles, or forcemeat balls made of fish, and also glazed onions or mushrooms. These, however, should not be added to the matelote till it is dished.

title: Sauce Allemande, or German Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of sauce tournée
- yolks of 2 eggs

## Directions

1. Put the sauce into a stewpan, heat it, and stir to it the beaten yolks of 2 eggs, which have been previously strained.
2. Let it just simmer, but not boil, or the eggs will curdle.
3. After the eggs are added to the sauce, it must be stirred without ceasing.

title: Sauce Aristocratique (a Store Sauce)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Green walnuts
- To every pint of juice, 1 lb. of anchovies
- 1 drachm of cloves
- 1 drachm of mace
- 1 drachm of Jamaica ginger bruised
- 8 shalots
- To every pint of the boiled liquor, 1/2 pint of vinegar
- 1/4 pint of port wine
- 2 tablespoonfuls of soy

## Directions

1. Pound the walnuts in a mortar, squeeze out the juice through a strainer, and let it stand to settle.
2. Pour off the clear juice, and to every pint of it, add anchovies, spices, and cloves in the above proportion.
3. Boil all these together till the anchovies are dissolved, then strain the juice again, put in the shalots (8 to every pint), and boil again.
4. To every pint of the boiled liquor add vinegar, wine, and soy, in the above quantities, and bottle off for use.
5. Cork well, and seal the corks.

title: Sauce for Wildfowl
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 glass of port wine
- 1 tablespoonful of Leamington sauce (No. 459)
- 1 tablespoonful of mushroom ketchup
- 1 tablespoonful of lemon-juice
- 1 slice of lemon-peel
- 1 large shalot cut in slices
- 1 blade of mace
- cayenne to taste

## Directions

1. Put all the ingredients into a stewpan, set it over the fire, and let it simmer for about 5 minutes; then strain and serve the sauce in a tureen.

title: Sauce Piquante, for Cutlets, Roast Meat, &c.
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. of butter
- 1 small carrot
- 6 shalots
- 1 small bunch of savoury herbs, including parsley
- 1/2 a bay-leaf
- 2 slices of lean ham
- 2 cloves
- 6 peppercorns
- 1 blade of mace
- 3 whole allspice
- 4 tablespoonfuls of vinegar
- 1/2 pint of stock
- 1 small lump of sugar
- 1/4 saltspoonful of cayenne
- salt to taste

## Directions

1. Put into a stewpan the butter, with the carrot and shalots, both of which must be cut into small slices
2. add the herbs, bay-leaf, spices, and ham (which must be minced rather finely), and let these ingredients simmer over a slow fire, until the bottom of the stewpan is covered with a brown glaze
3. Keep stirring with a wooden spoon, and put in the remaining ingredients
4. Simmer very gently for 1/4 hour
5. skim off every particle of fat
6. strain the sauce through a sieve, and serve very hot

title: Sauce Robert, for Steaks, &c.
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. of butter
- 3 onions
- 1 teaspoonful of flour
- 4 tablespoonfuls of gravy, or stock
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 teaspoonful of made mustard
- 1 teaspoonful of vinegar
- the juice of 1/2 lemon

## Directions

1. Put the butter into a stewpan, set it on the fire, and, when browning, throw in the onions, which must be cut into small slices
2. Fry them brown, but do not burn them
3. add the flour, shake the onions in it, and give the whole another fry
4. Put in the gravy and seasoning, and boil it gently for 10 minutes
5. skim off the fat
6. add the mustard, vinegar, and lemon-juice
7. give it one boil, and pour round the steaks, or whatever dish the sauce has been prepared for

title: Sauce Tournee
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of white stock
- thickening of flour and butter, or white roux
- a faggot of savoury herbs, including parsley
- 6 chopped mushrooms
- 6 green onions

## Directions

1. Put the stock into a stewpan with the herbs, onions, and mushrooms, and let it simmer very gently for about 1/2 hour
2. stir in sufficient thickening to make it of a proper consistency
3. let it boil for a few minutes, then skim off all the fat, strain and serve

title: Saucer-Cake For Tea
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of flour
- 1/4 lb. of tous-les-mois
- 1/4 lb. of pounded white sugar
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 2 eggs
- 1 oz. of candied orange or lemon-peel

## Directions

1. Mix the flour and tous-les-mois together
2. Add the sugar, the candied peel cut into thin slices, the butter beaten to a cream, and the eggs well whisked
3. Beat the mixture for 10 minutes
4. Put it into a buttered cake-tin or mould, or, if this is not obtainable, a soup-plate lined with a piece of buttered paper
5. Bake the cake in a moderate oven from 1 to 1-1/4 hour
6. When cold, put it away in a covered canister

title: Sausage-Meat Cakes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of lean pork
- 3/4 lb. of fat bacon
- 1/4 oz. of salt
- 1 saltspoonful of pepper
- 1/4 teaspoonful of grated nutmeg
- 1 teaspoonful of minced parsley

## Directions

1. Remove from the pork all skin, gristle, and bone, and chop it finely with the bacon.
2. Add the remaining ingredients, and carefully mix altogether.
3. Pound it well in a mortar, make it into convenient-sized cakes, flour these, and fry them a nice brown for about 10 minutes.

title: Sausage-Meat Stuffing for Turkey
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of lean pork
- 6 oz. of fat pork
- 2 oz. of bread crumbs
- 1 small tablespoonful of minced sage
- 1 blade of pounded mace
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 egg

## Directions

1. Chop the meat and fat very finely, mix with them the other ingredients, taking care that the whole is thoroughly incorporated.
2. Moisten with the egg, and the stuffing will be ready for use.

title: Savoury Beef Tea
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of solid beef
- 1 oz. of butter
- 1 clove
- 2 button onions or 1/2 a large one
- 1 saltspoonful of salt
- 1 quart of water

## Directions

1. Cut the beef into very small dice
2. Put it into a stewpan with the butter, clove, onion, and salt
3. Stir the meat round over the fire for a few minutes, until it produces a thin gravy
4. Add the water, and let it simmer gently from 1/2 to 3/4 hour, skimming off every particle of fat
5. When done, strain it through a sieve, and put it by in a cool place until required

title: Savoury Casserole of Rice
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 lb. of rice
- 3 pints of weak stock or broth
- 2 slices of fat ham
- 1 teaspoonful of salt
- clarified butter

## Directions

1. Wash the rice in two or three waters, drain it well, and put it into a stewpan with the stock, ham, and salt
2. Cover the pan closely, and let the rice gradually swell over a slow fire, occasionally stirring, to prevent its sticking
3. When it is quite soft, strain it, pick out the pieces of ham, and, with the back of a large wooden spoon, mash the rice to a perfectly smooth paste
4. Well grease a mould and turn it upside down for a minute or two, to drain away the fat, should there be too much
5. Put some rice all round the bottom and sides of it
6. Place a piece of soft bread in the middle, and cover it with rice
7. Press it in equally with the spoon, and let it cool
8. Dip the mould into hot water, turn the casserole carefully on to a dish, mark where the lid is to be formed on the top, by making an incision with the point of a knife about an inch from the edge all round
9. Put it into a very hot oven
10. Brush it over with a little clarified butter, and bake about 1/2 hour, or rather longer
11. Carefully remove the lid, which will be formed by the incision having been made all round, and remove the bread, in small pieces, with the point of a penknife, being careful not to injure the casserole
12. Fill the centre with the ragoût or fricassee, which should be made thick
13. Put on the cover, glaze it, place it in the oven to set the glaze, and serve as hot as possible

title: Savoury Jelly for Meat Pies
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 lbs. of shin of beef
- 1 calf's-foot
- 3 lbs. of knuckle of veal
- poultry trimmings (if for game pies, any game trimmings)
- 2 onions stuck with cloves
- 2 carrots
- 4 shalots
- a bunch of savoury herbs
- 2 bay-leaves
- 2 blades of mace and a little spice
- 2 slices of lean ham
- rather more than 2 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Cut up the meat and put it into a stewpan with all the ingredients except the water; set it over a slow fire to draw down, and, when the gravy ceases to flow from the meat, pour in the water.
2. Let it boil up, then carefully take away all scum from the top.
3. Cover the stewpan closely, and let the stock simmer very gently for 4 hours: if rapidly boiled, the jelly will not be clear.
4. When done, strain it through a fine sieve or flannel bag; and when cold, the jelly should be quite transparent.
5. If this is not the case, clarify it with the whites of eggs, as described in recipe No. 109.

title: Savoy Biscuits or Cakes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 eggs
- 6 oz. of pounded sugar
- the rind of 1 lemon
- 6 oz. of flour
- thick cartridge paper

## Directions

1. Break the eggs into a basin, separating the whites from the yolks
2. beat the yolks well, mix with them the pounded sugar and grated lemon-rind
3. beat these ingredients together for 1/4 hour
4. dredge in the flour gradually
5. when the whites of the eggs have been whisked to a solid froth, stir them to the flour, &c.
6. beat the mixture well for another 5 minutes
7. draw it along in strips upon thick cartridge paper to the proper size of the biscuit
8. bake them in rather a hot oven; but let them be carefully watched, as they are soon done, and a few seconds over the proper time will scorch and spoil them

title: Savoy Cake
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The weight of 4 eggs in pounded loaf sugar
- the weight of 7 in flour
- a little grated lemon-rind, or essence of almonds, or orange-flower water

## Directions

1. Break the 7 eggs, putting the yolks into one basin and the whites into another.
2. Whisk the former, and mix with them the sugar, the grated lemon-rind, or any other flavouring to taste; beat them well together, and add the whites of the eggs, whisked to a froth.
3. Put in the flour by degrees, continuing to beat the mixture for 1/4 hour, butter a mould, pour in the cake, and bake it from 1-1/4 to 1-1/2 hour.
4. This is a very nice cake for dessert, and may be iced for a supper-table, or cut into slices and spread with jam, which converts it into sandwiches.

title: Scalloped Fish
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Any cold fish
- 1 egg
- milk
- 1 large blade of pounded mace
- 1 tablespoonful of flour
- 1 teaspoonful of anchovy sauce
- pepper and salt to taste
- bread crumbs
- butter

## Directions

1. Pick the fish carefully from the bones, and moisten with milk and the egg
2. Add the other ingredients, and place in a deep dish or scallop shells
3. Cover with bread crumbs, butter the top, and brown before the fire
4. When quite hot, serve

title: Scalloped Oysters I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Oysters, say 1 pint
- 1 oz. butter
- flour
- 2 tablespoonfuls of white stock
- 2 tablespoonfuls of cream
- pepper and salt to taste
- bread crumbs
- oiled butter

## Directions

1. Scald the oysters in their own liquor, take them out, beard them, and strain the liquor free from grit.
2. Put 1 oz. of butter into a stewpan; when melted, dredge in sufficient flour to dry it up; add the stock, cream, and strained liquor, and give one boil.
3. Put in the oysters and seasoning; let them gradually heat through, but not boil.
4. Have ready the scallop-shells buttered; lay in the oysters, and as much of the liquid as they will hold; cover them over with bread crumbs, over which drop a little oiled butter.
5. Brown them in the oven, or before the fire, and serve quickly, and very hot.

title: Scalloped Oysters II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Oysters
- bread crumbs
- pepper
- salt
- grated nutmeg
- butter

## Directions

1. Prepare the oysters as in the preceding recipe, and put them in a scallop-shell or saucer, and between each layer sprinkle over a few bread crumbs, pepper, salt, and grated nutmeg; place small pieces of butter over, and bake before the fire in a Dutch oven.
2. Put sufficient bread crumbs on the top to make a smooth surface, as the oysters should not be seen.

title: Scotch Collops
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of cold roast veal
- a little butter
- flour
- 1/2 pint of water
- 1 onion
- 1 blade of pounded mace
- 1 tablespoonful of lemon-juice
- 1/2 teaspoonful of finely-minced lemon-peel
- 2 tablespoonfuls of sherry
- 1 tablespoonful of mushroom ketchup

## Directions

1. Cut the veal the same thickness as for cutlets, rather larger than a crown-piece
2. Flour the meat well
3. Fry a light brown in butter
4. Dredge again with flour
5. Add 1/2 pint of water, pouring it in by degrees
6. Set it on the fire
7. When it boils, add the onion and mace
8. Let it simmer very gently about 3/4 hour
9. Flavour the gravy with lemon-juice, peel, wine, and ketchup
10. Give one boil and serve

title: Scotch Collops, White
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of cold roast veal
- 1/2 teaspoonful of grated nutmeg
- 2 blades of pounded mace
- cayenne and salt to taste
- a little butter
- 1 dessertspoonful of flour
- 1/4 pint of water
- 1 teaspoonful of anchovy sauce
- 1 tablespoonful of lemon-juice
- 1 teaspoonful of lemon-peel
- 1 tablespoonful of mushroom ketchup
- 3 tablespoonfuls of cream
- 1 tablespoonful of sherry
- lemon
- slices of toasted bacon, rolled

## Directions

1. Cut the veal into thin slices about 3 inches in width
2. Hack them with a knife
3. Grate on them the nutmeg, mace, cayenne, and salt
4. Fry them in a little butter
5. Dish them
6. Make a gravy in the pan by putting in the remaining ingredients
7. Give one boil
8. Pour it over the collops
9. Garnish with lemon and slices of toasted bacon, rolled
10. If cream is not at hand, substitute the yolk of an egg beaten up well with a little milk

title: Scotch Eggs
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 eggs
- 6 tablespoonfuls of forcemeat No. 417
- hot lard
- 1/2 pint of good brown gravy

## Directions

1. Boil the eggs for 10 minutes; strip them from the shells, and cover them with forcemeat made by recipe No. 417; or substitute pounded anchovies for the ham
2. Fry the eggs a nice brown in boiling lard, drain them before the fire from their greasy moisture
3. dish them, and pour round from 1/4 to 1/2 pint of good brown gravy
4. To enhance the appearance of the eggs, they may be rolled in beaten egg and sprinkled with bread crumbs

title: Scotch Shortbread
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of flour
- 1 lb. of butter
- 1/4 lb. of pounded loaf sugar
- 1/2 oz. of caraway seeds
- 1 oz. of sweet almonds
- a few strips of candied orange-peel

## Directions

1. Beat the butter to a cream, gradually dredge in the flour, and add the sugar, caraway seeds, and sweet almonds, which should be blanched and cut into small pieces.
2. Work the paste until it is quite smooth, and divide it into six pieces.
3. Put each cake on a separate piece of paper, roll the paste out square to the thickness of about an inch, and pinch it upon all sides.
4. Prick it well, and ornament with one or two strips of candied orange-peel.
5. Put the cakes into a good oven, and bake them from 25 to 30 minutes.

title: Scotch Woodcock
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A few slices of hot buttered toast
- 1 anchovy to each slice
- 1/4 pint of cream
- yolks of 3 eggs

## Directions

1. Separate the yolks from the whites of the eggs; beat the former, stir to them the cream, and bring the sauce to the boiling-point, but do not allow it to boil, or it will curdle.
2. Have ready some hot buttered toast, spread with anchovies pounded to a paste; pour a little of the hot sauce on the top, and serve very hot and very quickly.

title: Scrap-Cakes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of leaf, or the inside fat of a pig
- 1-1/2 lb. of flour
- 1/4 lb. of moist sugar
- 1/2 lb. of currants
- 1 oz. of candied lemon-peel
- ground allspice to taste

## Directions

1. Cut the leaf into small pieces
2. Put it into a large dish and place in a quick oven
3. Be careful that it does not burn; in a short time it will be reduced to oil with the small pieces of leaf floating on the surface
4. Gather all the scraps together and put them into a basin with the flour
5. Rub them well together
6. Add the currants, sugar, candied peel cut into thin slices, and the ground allspice
7. When all ingredients are well mixed, moisten with sufficient cold water to make the whole into a nice paste
8. Roll it out thin and cut it into shapes
9. Bake the cakes in a quick oven from 15 to 20 minutes

title: Seed Biscuits
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of flour
- 1/4 lb. of sifted sugar
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 1/2 oz. of caraway seeds
- 3 eggs
- milk or the white of an egg

## Directions

1. Beat the butter to a cream
2. stir in the flour, sugar, and caraway seeds
3. when these ingredients are well mixed, add the eggs, which should be well whisked
4. Roll out the paste, with a round cutter shape out the biscuits
5. bake them in a moderate oven from 10 to 15 minutes
6. The tops of the biscuits may be brushed over with a little milk or the white of an egg, and then a little sugar strewn over

title: Semolina Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 5 oz. of semolina
- 2 quarts of boiling stock, No. 105, or 106

## Directions

1. Drop the semolina into the boiling stock, and keep stirring, to prevent its burning
2. Simmer gently for half an hour, and serve

title: Sheep's Brains, En Matelote (an Entree)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 sheep's brains
- vinegar
- salt
- a few slices of bacon
- 1 small onion
- 2 cloves
- a small bunch of parsley
- sufficient stock or weak broth to cover the brains
- 1 tablespoonful of lemon-juice
- matelote sauce, No. 512

## Directions

1. Detach the brains from the heads without breaking them, and put them into a pan of warm water; remove the skin, and let them remain for two hours.
2. Have ready a saucepan of boiling water, add a little vinegar and salt, and put in the brains.
3. When they are quite firm, take them out and put them into very cold water.
4. Place 2 or 3 slices of bacon in a stewpan, put in the brains, the onion stuck with 2 cloves, the parsley, and a good seasoning of pepper and salt; cover with stock, or weak broth, and boil them gently for about 25 minutes.
5. Have ready some croûtons; arrange these in the dish alternately with the brains, and cover with a matelote sauce, No. 512, to which has been added the lemon-juice.

title: Sheep's Feet or Trotters (Soyer's Recipe)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 12 feet
- 1/4 lb. of beef or mutton suet
- 2 onions
- 1 carrot
- 2 bay-leaves
- 2 sprigs of thyme
- 1 oz. of salt
- 1/4 oz. of pepper
- 2 tablespoonfuls of flour
- 2-1/2 quarts of water
- 1/4 lb. of fresh butter
- 1 teaspoonful of salt
- 1 teaspoonful of flour
- 3/4 teaspoonful of pepper
- a little grated nutmeg
- the juice of 1 lemon
- 1 gill of milk
- the yolks of 2 eggs

## Directions

1. Have the feet cleaned, and the long bone extracted from them.
2. Put the suet into a stewpan, with the onions and carrot sliced, the bay-leaves, thyme, salt, and pepper, and let these simmer for 5 minutes.
3. Add 2 tablespoonfuls of flour and the water, and keep stirring till it boils; then put in the feet.
4. Let these simmer for 3 hours, or until perfectly tender, and take them and lay them on a sieve.
5. Mix together, on a plate, with the back of a spoon, butter, salt, flour (1 teaspoonful), pepper, nutmeg, and lemon-juice, and put the feet, with a gill of milk, into a stewpan.
6. When very hot, add the butter mixture, and stir continually till melted.
7. Mix the yolks of 2 eggs with 5 tablespoonfuls of milk; stir this to the other ingredients, keep moving the pan over the fire continually for a minute or two, but do not allow it to boil after the eggs are added.
8. Serve in a very hot dish, and garnish with croûtons, or sippets of toasted bread.

title: Shoulder of Lamb Stuffed
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Shoulder of lamb
- forcemeat No. 417
- trimmings of veal or beef
- 2 onions
- 1/2 head of celery
- 1 faggot of savoury herbs
- a few slices of fat bacon
- 1 quart of stock No. 105

## Directions

1. Take the blade-bone out of a shoulder of lamb, fill up its place with forcemeat, and sew it up with coarse thread.
2. Put it into a stewpan with a few slices of bacon under and over the lamb, and add the remaining ingredients.
3. Stew very gently for rather more than 2 hours.
4. Reduce the gravy, with which glaze the meat, and serve with peas, stewed cucumbers, or sorrel sauce.

title: Shoulder of Veal, Stuffed and Stewed
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A shoulder of veal
- a few slices of ham or bacon
- forcemeat No. 417
- 3 carrots
- 2 onions
- salt and pepper to taste
- a faggot of savoury herbs
- 3 blades of pounded mace
- water
- thickening of butter and flour

## Directions

1. Bone the joint by carefully detaching the meat from the blade-bone on one side, and then on the other, being particular not to pierce the skin
2. cut the bone from the knuckle, and take it out
3. Fill the cavity whence the bone was taken with a forcemeat made by recipe No. 417
4. Roll and bind the veal up tightly
5. put it into a stew-pan with the carrots, onions, seasoning, herbs, and mace
6. pour in just sufficient water to cover it
7. let it stew very gently for about 5 hours
8. Before taking it up, try if it is properly done by thrusting a larding-needle in it: if it penetrates easily, it is sufficiently cooked
9. Strain and skim the gravy
10. thicken with butter and flour
11. give one boil, and pour it round the meat
12. A few young carrots may be boiled and placed round the dish as a garnish, and, when in season, green peas should always be served with this dish

title: Shrimp Sauce for Various Kinds of Fish
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/3 pint of melted butter (No. 376)
- 1/4 pint of picked shrimps
- cayenne to taste

## Directions

1. Make the melted butter very smoothly by recipe No. 376, shell the shrimps (sufficient to make 1/4 pint when picked), and put them into the butter; season with cayenne, and let the sauce just simmer, but do not allow it to boil.
2. When liked, a teaspoonful of anchovy sauce may be added.

title: Simple Hard Biscuits
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- flour
- butter
- skimmed milk

## Directions

1. Warm the butter in the milk until the former is dissolved, and then mix it with the flour into a very stiff paste
2. Beat it with a rolling-pin until the dough looks perfectly smooth
3. Roll it out thin
4. Cut it with the top of a glass into round biscuits
5. Prick them well, and bake them from 6 to 10 minutes

title: Skate with Caper Sauce (a la Francaise)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 or 3 slices of skate
- 1/2 pint of vinegar
- 2 oz. of salt
- 1/2 teaspoonful of pepper
- 1 sliced onion
- a small bunch of parsley
- 2 bay-leaves
- 2 or 3 sprigs of thyme
- sufficient water to cover the fish

## Directions

1. Put in a fish-kettle all the above ingredients
2. Simmer the skate in them till tender
3. When it is done, skin it neatly, and pour over it some of the liquor in which it has been boiling
4. Drain it, put it on a hot dish
5. Pour over it caper sauce, and send some of the latter to table in a tureen

title: Sliced and Broiled Beef
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A few slices of cold roast beef
- 4 or 5 potatoes
- a thin batter
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Pare the potatoes as you would peel an apple.
2. Fry the parings in a thin batter seasoned with salt and pepper, until they are of a light brown colour, and place them on a dish over some slices of beef, which should be nicely seasoned and broiled.

title: Sliced Carrots
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 5 or 6 large carrots
- a large lump of sugar
- 1 pint of weak stock
- 3 oz. of fresh butter
- salt to taste

## Directions

1. Scrape and wash the carrots, cut them into slices of an equal size
2. Boil them in salt and water, until half done
3. Drain them well
4. Put them into a stewpan with the sugar and stock
5. Let them boil over a brisk fire
6. When reduced to a glaze, add the fresh butter and a seasoning of salt
7. Shake the stewpan about well, and when the butter is well mixed with the carrots, serve

title: Small Almond Puddings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of sweet almonds
- 6 bitter almonds
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 4 eggs
- 2 tablespoonfuls of sifted sugar
- 2 tablespoonfuls of cream
- 1 tablespoonful of brandy
- a spoonful of water

## Directions

1. Blanch and pound the almonds to a smooth paste with a spoonful of water
2. Warm the butter, mix the almonds with this, and add the other ingredients, leaving out the whites of 2 eggs
3. Be particular that these are well beaten
4. Mix well, butter some cups, half fill them, and bake the puddings from 20 minutes to 1/2 hour
5. Turn them out on a dish, and serve with sweet sauce

title: Small Skate Fried
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Skate
- sufficient vinegar to cover them
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 sliced onion
- a small bunch of parsley
- the juice of 1/2 lemon
- hot dripping

## Directions

1. Cleanse the skate, lay them in a dish, with sufficient vinegar to cover them
2. Add the salt, pepper, onion, parsley, and lemon-juice
3. Let the fish remain in this pickle for 1-1/2 hour
4. Drain them well, flour them, and fry of a nice brown, in hot dripping

title: Snow Eggs, or Oeufs A La Neige
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 eggs
- 3/4 pint of milk
- pounded sugar to taste
- flavouring of vanilla, lemon-rind, or orange-flower water

## Directions

1. Put the milk into a saucepan with sufficient sugar to sweeten it nicely, and the rind of 1/2 lemon.
2. Let this steep by the side of the fire for 1/2 hour, when take out the peel.
3. Separate the whites from the yolks of the eggs, and whisk the former to a perfectly stiff froth, or until there is no liquid remaining.
4. Bring the milk to the boiling-point, and drop in the snow a tablespoonful at a time, and keep turning the eggs until sufficiently cooked.
5. Place them on a glass dish.
6. Beat up the yolks of the eggs, stir to them the milk, add a little more sugar, and strain this mixture into a jug.
7. Place the jug in a saucepan of boiling water, and stir it one way until the mixture thickens, but do not allow it to boil, or it will curdle.
8. Pour this custard over the eggs, when they should rise to the surface.
9. Put in a cold place after being made.

title: Snow-Cake
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. of tous-les-mois
- 1/4 lb. of white pounded sugar
- 1/4 lb. of fresh or washed salt butter
- 1 egg
- juice of 1 lemon

## Directions

1. Beat the butter to a cream
2. Add the egg, previously well beaten, and then the other ingredients
3. If the mixture is not light, add another egg, and beat for 1/4 hour, until it turns white and light
4. Line a flat tin with raised edges with a sheet of buttered paper
5. Pour in the cake and put it into the oven
6. Bake in a rather slow oven, 1 to 1-1/4 hour, and do not allow the cake to brown at all
7. Let it cool a few minutes
8. With a clean sharp knife cut it into small square pieces
9. Gently remove the pieces to a large flat dish to cool before putting away

title: Snow-Cake (A Genuine Scotch Recipe)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of arrowroot
- 1/2 lb. of pounded white sugar
- 1/2 lb. of butter
- whites of 6 eggs
- flavouring to taste, of essence of almonds, or vanilla, or lemon

## Directions

1. Beat the butter to a cream
2. Stir in the sugar and arrowroot gradually, at the same time beating the mixture
3. Whisk the whites of the eggs to a stiff froth
4. Add them to the other ingredients and beat well for 20 minutes
5. Put in whichever of the flavourings may be preferred
6. Pour the cake into a buttered mould or tin
7. Bake it in a moderate oven from 1 to 1-1/2 hour

title: Soda Biscuits
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of flour
- 1/2 lb. of pounded loaf sugar
- 1/4 lb. of fresh butter
- 2 eggs
- 1 small teaspoonful of carbonate of soda

## Directions

1. Put the flour (which should be perfectly dry) into a basin
2. Rub in the butter, add the sugar, and mix these ingredients well together
3. Whisk the eggs, stir them into the mixture, and beat it well, until everything is well incorporated
4. Quickly stir in the soda, roll the paste out until it is about 1/2 inch thick
5. Cut it into small round cakes with a tin cutter
6. Bake them from 12 to 18 minutes in rather a brisk oven

title: Soda Bread
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of flour
- 1 teaspoonful of tartaric acid
- 1 teaspoonful of salt
- 1 teaspoonful of carbonate of soda
- 2 breakfast-cupfuls of cold milk

## Directions

1. Let the tartaric acid and salt be reduced to the finest possible powder; then mix them well with the flour.
2. Dissolve the soda in the milk, and pour it several times from one basin to another, before adding it to the flour.
3. Work the whole quickly into a light dough, divide it into 2 loaves, and put them into a well-heated oven immediately, and bake for an hour.
4. Sour milk or buttermilk may be used, but then a little less acid will be needed.

title: Soda-Cake
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of butter
- 1 lb. of flour
- 1/2 lb. of currants
- 1/2 lb. of moist sugar
- 1 teacupful of milk
- 3 eggs
- 1 teaspoonful of carbonate of soda

## Directions

1. Rub the butter into the flour, add the currants and sugar, and mix these ingredients well together.
2. Whisk the eggs well, stir them to the flour, &c., with the milk, in which the soda should be previously dissolved, and beat the whole up together with a wooden spoon or beater.
3. Divide the dough into two pieces, put them into buttered moulds or cake-tins, and bake in a moderate oven for nearly an hour.
4. The mixture must be extremely well beaten up, and not allowed to stand after the soda is added to it, but must be placed in the oven immediately.
5. Great care must also be taken that the cakes are quite done through, which may be ascertained by thrusting a knife into the middle of them: if the blade looks bright when withdrawn, they are done.
6. If the tops acquire too much colour before the inside is sufficiently baked, cover them over with a piece of clean white paper, to prevent them from burning.

title: Sole or Cod Pie
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of cold boiled sole or cod
- pepper, salt, and pounded mace to taste
- 1 dozen oysters to each lb. of fish
- 3 tablespoonfuls of white stock
- 1 teacupful of cream thickened with flour
- puff paste

## Directions

1. Clear the fish from the bones, lay it in a pie-dish, and between each layer put a few oysters and a little seasoning.
2. Add the stock, and, when liked, a small quantity of butter.
3. Cover with puff paste, and bake for 1/2 hour.
4. Boil the cream with sufficient flour to thicken it; pour in the pie, and serve.

title: Soles With Cream Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 soles
- salt, cayenne, and pounded mace to taste
- the juice of 1/2 lemon
- salt and water
- 1/2 pint of cream

## Directions

1. Skin, wash, and fillet the soles, and divide each fillet in 2 pieces.
2. Lay them in cold salt and water, which bring gradually to a boil.
3. When the water boils, take out the fish, lay it in a delicately clean stewpan, and cover with the cream.
4. Add the seasoning, simmer very gently for ten minutes, and, just before serving, put in the lemon-juice.

title: Soles With Mushrooms
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of milk
- 1 pint of water
- 1 oz. butter
- 1 oz. salt
- a little lemon-juice
- 2 middling-sized soles
- good mushroom sauce

## Directions

1. Cleanse the soles, but do not skin them, and lay them in a fish-kettle, with the milk, water, butter, salt, and lemon-juice.
2. Bring them gradually to boil, and let them simmer very gently till done, which will be in about 7 minutes.
3. Take them up, drain them well on a cloth, put them on a hot dish, and pour over them a good mushroom sauce.

title: Somersetshire Puddings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 eggs
- flour equal in weight to the eggs
- pounded sugar equal in weight to the eggs
- butter equal in weight to the eggs
- flavouring of grated lemon-rind, bitter almonds, or essence of vanilla

## Directions

1. Carefully weigh the various ingredients, by placing on one side of the scales the eggs, and on the other the flour, then the sugar, and then the butter
2. Warm the butter, and with the hands beat it to a cream
3. Gradually dredge in the flour and pounded sugar, and keep stirring and beating the mixture without ceasing until it is perfectly smooth
4. Add the eggs, which should be well whisked, and either of the above flavourings that may be preferred
5. Butter some small cups, rather more than half-fill them
6. Bake in a brisk oven for about 1/2 hour
7. Turn them out, dish them on a napkin, and serve custard or wine-sauce with them

title: Soup a la Cantatrice
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 oz. of sago
- 1/2 pint of cream
- the yolks of 3 eggs
- 1 lump of sugar
- seasoning to taste
- 1 bay-leaf (if liked)
- 2 quarts of medium stock No. 105

## Directions

1. Having washed the sago in boiling water, let it be gradually added to the nearly boiling stock.
2. Simmer for 1/2 an hour, when it should be well dissolved.
3. Beat up the yolks of the eggs, add to them the boiling cream; stir these quickly in the soup, and serve immediately.
4. Do not let the soup boil, or the eggs will curdle.

title: Soup A La Crecy
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 carrots
- 2 sliced onions
- 1 cut lettuce
- chervil
- 2 oz. butter
- 1 pint of lentils
- the crumbs of 2 French rolls
- half a teacupful of rice
- 2 quarts of medium stock No. 105

## Directions

1. Put the vegetables with the butter in the stewpan, and let them simmer 5 minutes
2. Add the lentils and 1 pint of the stock, and stew gently for half an hour
3. Fill it up with the remainder of the stock, let it boil another hour, and put in the crumb of the rolls
4. When well soaked, rub all through a tammy
5. Have ready the rice boiled; pour the soup over this, and serve

title: Soup A La Flamande (Flemish)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 turnip
- 1 small carrot
- 1/2 head of celery
- 6 green onions shred very fine
- 1 lettuce cut small
- chervil
- 1/4 pint of asparagus cut small
- 1/4 pint of peas
- 2 oz. butter
- the yolks of 4 eggs
- 1/2 pint of cream
- salt to taste
- 1 lump of sugar
- 2 quarts of stock No. 105

## Directions

1. Put the vegetables in the butter to stew gently for an hour with a teacupful of stock
2. Add the remainder of the stock, and simmer for another hour
3. Beat the yolks of the eggs well, mix with the cream (previously boiled), and strain through a hair sieve
4. Take the soup off the fire, put the eggs, &c. to it, and keep stirring it well
5. Bring it to a boil, but do not leave off stirring, or the eggs will curdle
6. Season with salt, and add the sugar

title: Soup A La Julienne
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of carrots
- 1/2 pint of turnips
- 1/4 pint of onions
- 2 or 3 leeks
- 1/2 head of celery
- 1 lettuce
- a little sorrel and chervil, if liked
- 2 oz. of butter
- 2 quarts of stock No. 105

## Directions

1. Cut the vegetables into strips of about 1-1/4 inch long, and be particular they are all the same size, or some will be hard whilst the others will be done to a pulp.
2. Cut the lettuce, sorrel, and chervil into larger pieces; fry the carrots in the butter, and pour the stock boiling to them.
3. When this is done, add all the other vegetables, and herbs, and stew gently for at least an hour.
4. Skim off all the fat, pour the soup over thin slices of bread, cut round about the size of a shilling, and serve.

title: Soup a la Solferino
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 eggs
- 1/2 pint of cream
- 2 oz. of fresh butter
- salt and pepper to taste
- a little flour to thicken
- 2 quarts of bouillon, No. 105

## Directions

1. Beat the eggs, put them into a stewpan, and add the cream, butter, and seasoning
2. Stir in as much flour as will bring it to the consistency of dough
3. Make it into balls, either round or egg-shaped, and fry them in butter
4. Put them in the tureen, and pour the boiling bouillon over them

title: Soup Maigre (i.e. without Meat)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. butter
- 6 onions sliced
- 4 heads of celery
- 2 lettuces
- a small bunch of parsley
- 2 handfuls of spinach
- 3 pieces of bread-crust
- 2 blades of mace
- salt and pepper to taste
- the yolks of 2 eggs
- 3 teaspoonfuls of vinegar
- 2 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Melt the butter in a stewpan, and put in the onions to stew gently for 3 or 4 minutes.
2. Then add the celery, spinach, lettuces, and parsley, cut small.
3. Stir the ingredients well for 10 minutes.
4. Now put in the water, bread, seasoning, and mace.
5. Boil gently for 1-1/2 hour, and, at the moment of serving, beat in the yolks of the eggs and the vinegar, but do not let it boil, or the eggs will curdle.

title: Soup à la Reine
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 large fowl
- 1 oz. of sweet almonds
- crumb of 1 1/2 French roll
- 1/2 pint of cream
- salt to taste
- 1 small lump of sugar
- 2 quarts of good white veal stock

## Directions

1. Boil the fowl gently in the stock till quite tender, which will be in about an hour, or rather more
2. take out the fowl, pull the meat from the bones, and put it into a mortar with the almonds, and pound very fine
3. put the meat back in the stock, with the crumb of the rolls, and let it simmer for an hour
4. rub it through a tammy, add the sugar, 1/2 pint of cream that has boiled
5. if you prefer, cut the crust of the roll into small round pieces, and pour the soup over it, when you serve

title: Soup à la Reine (Economical)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- remains of roast chickens
- 1/2 teacupful of rice
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 quart of stock

## Directions

1. Take all the white meat and pound it with the rice, which has been slightly cooked, but not much
2. When it is all well pounded, dilute with the stock, and pass through a sieve

title: Soyer's Recipe for Forcemeats
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pound and a half of lean veal from the fillet
- 1 pound of good fresh beef suet
- 6 oz. of panada (bread soaked in milk and boiled till nearly dry)
- 1 teaspoonful of salt
- 1/4 teaspoonful of pepper
- 1/2 teaspoonful of nutmeg
- 4 eggs

## Directions

1. Cut the veal in long thin slices
2. Scrape with a knife till nothing but the fibre remains
3. Put it in a mortar and pound it 10 minutes, or until in a purée
4. Pass it through a wire sieve
5. Skin, shred, and chop the beef suet very fine
6. Put the suet in a mortar and pound it
7. Add the panada with the suet and pound them well together
8. Add the veal
9. Season with salt, pepper, and nutmeg
10. Work all well together
11. Add four eggs by degrees, continually pounding the contents of the mortar
12. Take a small piece in a spoon, and poach it in some boiling water to test if it is delicate, firm, and of a good flavour

title: Soyer's Recipe for Goose Stuffing
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 apples, peeled and cored
- 4 onions
- 4 leaves of sage
- 4 leaves of lemon thyme
- sufficient water
- sufficient pulp of mealy potatoes
- pepper and salt

## Directions

1. Boil the apples, onions, sage, and lemon thyme in a stewpan with sufficient water to cover them.
2. When done, pulp them through a sieve, removing the sage and thyme.
3. Add sufficient pulp of mealy potatoes to cause it to be sufficiently dry without sticking to the hand.
4. Add pepper and salt, and stuff the bird.

title: Soyer's Recipe for Puff-Paste
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of flour
- yolk of 1 egg
- juice of 1 lemon
- 1/2 saltspoonful of salt
- cold water
- 1 lb. of fresh butter

## Directions

1. Put the flour on to the paste-board and make a hole in the centre
2. Into this hole put the yolk of the egg, the lemon-juice, and salt
3. Mix the whole with cold water into a soft flexible paste, with the right hand, and handle it as little as possible
4. Squeeze all the buttermilk from the butter, wring it in a cloth, and roll out the paste
5. Place the butter on this, and fold the edges of the paste over, so as to hide it
6. Roll it out again to the thickness of 1/4 inch
7. Fold over one third, over which again pass the rolling-pin; then fold over the other third, thus forming a square
8. Place it with the ends, top, and bottom before you, shaking a little flour both under and over, and repeat the rolls and turns twice again
9. Flour a baking-sheet, put the paste on this, and let it remain on ice or in some cool place for 1/2 hour
10. Then roll twice more, turning it as before
11. Place it again upon the ice for 1/4 hour, give it 2 more rolls, making 7 in all, and it is ready for use when required

title: Soyer's Sauce for Plum-Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The yolks of 3 eggs
- 1 tablespoonful of powdered sugar
- 1 gill of milk
