9. Put the spinach into a clean stewpan, with the butter and a seasoning of pepper
10. stir the whole over the fire until quite hot
11. then put it on a hot dish, and garnish with sippets of toasted bread

title: To Boil Sugar To Caramel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of lump sugar
- 1 gill of spring water
- lemon-juice

## Directions

1. Boil the sugar and water together very quickly over a clear fire, skimming it very carefully as soon as it boils
2. Keep it boiling until the sugar snaps when a little of it is dropped in a pan of cold water
3. If it remains hard, the sugar has attained the right degree; then squeeze in a little lemon-juice, and let it remain an instant on the fire
4. Set the pan into another of cold water, and the caramel is then ready for use

title: To Boil Young Greens or Sprouts
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 gallon of water
- 1 heaped tablespoonful of salt
- a very small piece of soda
- greens or sprouts

## Directions

1. Pick away all the dead leaves and wash the greens well in cold water
2. Drain them in a colander
3. Put them into fast-boiling water, with salt and soda in the above proportion
4. Keep them boiling quickly, with the lid uncovered, until tender
5. The moment they are done, take them up to preserve their colour
6. When well drained, serve

title: To Bottle Fresh Fruit
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Fresh fruits, such as currants, raspberries, cherries, gooseberries, plums of all kinds, damsons
- Wide-mouthed glass bottles
- New corks to fit them tightly

## Directions

1. Let the fruit be full grown, but not too ripe, and gathered in dry weather
2. Pick it off the stalks without bruising or breaking the skin, and reject any that is at all blemished
3. Have ready some perfectly dry glass bottles, and some nice new soft corks or bungs
4. Burn a match in each bottle, to exhaust the air, and quickly place the fruit in to be preserved
5. Gently cork the bottles, and put them into a very cool oven, where let them remain until the fruit has shrunk away a fourth part
6. Take the bottles out; do not open them, but immediately beat the corks in tight, cut off the tops, and cover them with melted resin
7. If kept in a dry place, the fruit will remain good for months

title: To Bottle Fresh Fruit (II)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Any kind of fresh fruit, such as currants, cherries, gooseberries, all kinds of plums
- Wide-mouthed glass bottles
- New corks to fit them tightly

## Directions

1. The fruit must be full-grown, not too ripe, and gathered on a fine day
2. Let it be carefully picked and put into the bottles, which must be clean and perfectly dry
3. Tie over the tops of the bottles pieces of bladder
4. Stand the bottles in a large pot, copper, or boiler, with cold water to reach to their necks
5. Kindle a fire under, let the water boil, and as the bladders begin to rise and puff, prick them
6. As soon as the water boils, extinguish the fire, and let the bottles remain where they are, to become cold
7. The next day remove the bladders, and strew over the fruit a thick layer of pounded sugar
8. Fit the bottles with corks, and let each cork lie close at hand to its own bottle
9. Hold for a few moments, in the neck of the bottle, two or three lighted matches, and when they have filled the bottle neck with gas, and before they go out, remove them very quickly
10. Instantly cork the bottle closely, and dip it in bottle cement

title: To Bottle Fresh Fruit With Sugar
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Any kind of fresh fruit
- To each quart bottle allow 1/4 lb. of pounded sugar

## Directions

1. Let the fruit be gathered in dry weather
2. Pick it carefully, and drop it into clean and very dry quart glass bottles, sprinkling over it the above proportion of pounded sugar to each quart
3. Put the corks in the bottles, and place them in a copper of cold water up to their necks, with small hay-wisps round them, to prevent the bottles from knocking together
4. Light the fire under, bring the water gradually to boil, and let it simmer gently until the fruit in the bottles is reduced nearly one third
5. Extinguish the fire, and let the bottles remain in the water until it is perfectly cold
6. Take them out, make the corks secure, and cover them with melted resin or wax

title: To Clarify Stock
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The whites of 2 eggs
- 1/2 pint of water
- 2 quarts of stock

## Directions

1. Take the whites of 2 eggs, carefully separated from their yolks, whisk them well together with the water, and add gradually the 2 quarts of boiling stock, still whisking
2. Place the soup on the fire, and when boiling and well skimmed, whisk the eggs with it till nearly boiling again
3. Draw it from the fire, and let it settle, until the whites of the eggs become separated
4. Pass through a fine cloth, and the soup should be clear

title: To Clarify Sugar Or Syrup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of sugar
- 1/2 pint of water
- 1/2 the white of an egg

## Directions

1. Put the sugar, water, and the white of the egg, which should be well beaten, into a preserving-pan or lined saucepan
2. Do not put it on the fire till the sugar is dissolved
3. Place it on the fire, and when it boils, throw in a teacupful of cold water, and do not stir the sugar after this is added
4. Bring it to the boiling-point again, and then place the pan by the side of the fire, for the preparation to settle
5. Remove all the scum, and the sugar will be ready for use
6. The scum should be placed on a sieve, so that what syrup runs from it may be boiled up again; this must also be well skimmed

title: To Clarify Syrup For Jellies
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of loaf sugar to every quart of water
- the white of 1 egg

## Directions

1. Put the sugar and water into a stewpan
2. Set it on the fire, and when the sugar is dissolved, add the white of the egg, whipped up with a little water
3. Whisk the whole well together, and simmer very gently until it has thrown up all the scum
4. Take this off as it rises
5. Strain the syrup through a fine sieve or cloth into a basin, and keep it for use

title: To Clean Silk or Ribbons
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of gin
- 1/2 lb. of honey
- 1/2 lb. of soft soap
- 1/2 pint of water

## Directions

1. Mix the above ingredients together
2. Lay each breadth of silk upon a clean kitchen table or dresser, and scrub it well on the soiled side with the mixture
3. Have ready three vessels of cold water
4. Take each piece of silk at two corners, and dip it up and down in each vessel, but do not wring it
5. Take care that each breadth has one vessel of quite clean water for the last dip
6. Hang it up dripping for a minute or two
7. Dab it in a cloth
8. Iron it quickly with a very hot iron

title: To Cure a Cold
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 large teacupful of linseed
- 1/4 lb. of sun raisins
- 2 oz. of stick liquorice
- 2 quarts of soft water
- 1/4 lb. of pounded sugar-candy
- 1 tablespoonful of old rum
- 1 tablespoonful of best white-wine vinegar or lemon-juice

## Directions

1. Put linseed, sun raisins, and stick liquorice into soft water
2. Let simmer over a slow fire till reduced to one quart
3. Add pounded sugar-candy
4. Add rum and vinegar as the decoction is taken (not at first, as this makes it flat and less efficacious)
5. The dose is half a pint, made warm, on going to bed
6. A little may be taken whenever the cough is troublesome

title: To Cure Bacon in the Wiltshire Way
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 lb. of coarse sugar
- 1-1/2 lb. of bay-salt
- 6 oz. of saltpetre
- 1 lb. of common salt

## Directions

1. Sprinkle each flitch with salt, and let the blood drain off for 24 hours; then pound and mix the above ingredients well together and rub it well into the meat, which should be turned every day for a month; then hang it to dry, and afterwards smoke it for 10 days.

title: To Cure Bacon or Hams in the Devonshire Way
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- To every 14 lbs. of meat, 2 oz. of saltpetre
- 2 oz. of salt prunella
- 1 lb. of common salt
- 3 gallons of water
- 5 lbs. of common salt
- 7 lbs. of coarse sugar
- 3 lbs. of bay-salt
- dry sawdust

## Directions

1. Weigh the sides, hams, and cheeks, and to every 14 lb. allow 2 oz. of saltpetre, 2 oz. of salt prunella, and 1 lb. of common salt.
2. Pound and mix these together, and rub well into the meat.
3. Lay it in a stone trough or tub, rubbing it thoroughly, and turning it daily for 2 successive days.
4. At the end of the second day, pour on it a pickle made as follows: Put the ingredients into a saucepan, set it on the fire, and stir frequently.
5. Remove all the scum, allow it to boil for 1/4 hour, and pour it hot over the meat.
6. Let the hams, &c., be well rubbed and turned daily; if the meat is small, a fortnight will be sufficient for the sides and shoulders to remain in the pickle, and the hams 3 weeks; if from 30 lbs. and upwards, 3 weeks will be required for the sides, &c., and from 4 to 5 weeks for the hams.
7. On taking the pieces out, let them drain for an hour, cover with dry sawdust, and smoke from a fortnight to 3 weeks.
8. Boil and carefully skim the pickle after using, and it will keep good, closely corked, for 2 years.
9. When boiling it for use, add about 2 lbs. of common salt, and the same of treacle, to allow for waste.

title: To Cure Sweet Hams in the Westmoreland Way
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 lbs. of common salt
- 3 lbs. of coarse sugar
- 1 lb. of bay-salt
- 3 quarts of strong beer

## Directions

1. Before the hams are put into pickle, rub them the preceding day well with salt, and drain the brine well from them.
2. Put the above ingredients into a saucepan, and boil for 1/4 hour.
3. Pour over the hams, and let them remain a month in the pickle.
4. Rub and turn them every day, but do not take them out of the pickling-pan.
5. Have them smoked for a month.

title: To Cure Tongues (Method I)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 oz. of saltpetre
- 1/2 oz. of black pepper
- 4 oz. of sugar
- 3 oz. of juniper berries
- 6 oz. of salt

## Directions

1. Rub the above ingredients well into the tongue, and let it remain in the pickle for 10 days or a fortnight
2. Drain it, tie it up in brown paper, and have it smoked for about 20 days over a wood fire; or it may be boiled out of this pickle

title: To Cure Tongues (Method II)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 9 lbs. of salt
- 8 oz. of sugar
- 9 oz. of powdered saltpetre

## Directions

1. Rub the above ingredients well into the tongues, and keep them in this curing mixture for 2 months, turning them every day
2. Drain them from the pickle, cover with brown paper, and have them smoked for about 3 weeks

title: To Dress a Bullock's Heart
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 heart
- Stuffing of veal forcemeat, No. 417

## Directions

1. Put the heart into warm water to soak for 2 hours
2. Wipe it well with a cloth
3. Cut off the lobes
4. Stuff the inside with a highly-seasoned forcemeat (No. 417)
5. Fasten it in, by means of a needle and coarse thread
6. Tie the heart up in paper
7. Set it before a good fire, being very particular to keep it well basted, or it will eat dry, there being very little of its own fat
8. Two or three minutes before serving, remove the paper and baste well
9. Serve with good gravy and red-currant jelly or melted butter

title: To Dress A Green Goose
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Goose
- 3 oz. of butter
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Geese are called green till they are about four months old, and should not be stuffed.
2. After it has been singed and trussed, the same as in the preceding recipe, put into the body a seasoning of pepper and salt, and the butter to moisten it inside.
3. Roast before a clear fire for about 3/4 hour, froth and brown it nicely, and serve with a brown gravy, and, when liked, gooseberry-sauce.
4. This dish should be garnished with water-cresses.

title: To Dress a Leveret
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 leverets
- butter
- flour
- plain gravy
- red-currant jelly

## Directions

1. Leverets should be trussed in the same manner as a hare, but they do not require stuffing.
2. Roast them before a clear fire, and keep them well basted all the time they are cooking.
3. A few minutes before serving, dredge them lightly with flour, and froth them nicely.
4. Serve with plain gravy in the dish, and send to table red-currant jelly with them.

title: To Dress a Sheep's Head
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 sheep's head
- sufficient water to cover it
- 3 carrots
- 3 turnips
- 2 or 3 parsnips
- 3 onions
- a small bunch of parsley
- 1 teaspoonful of pepper
- 3 teaspoonfuls of salt
- 1/4 lb. of Scotch oatmeal

## Directions

1. Clean the head well, and let it soak in warm water for 2 hours, to get rid of the blood.
2. Put it into a saucepan, with sufficient cold water to cover it, and when it boils, add the vegetables, peeled and sliced, and the remaining ingredients.
3. Before adding the oatmeal, mix it to a smooth batter with a little of the liquor.
4. Keep stirring till it boils up; then shut the saucepan closely, and let it stew gently for 1-1/2 or 2 hours.
5. It may be thickened with rice or barley, but oatmeal is preferable.

title: To Dress Beef Kidney I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 kidney
- clarified butter
- pepper and salt to taste
- a small quantity of highly-seasoned gravy
- 1 tablespoonful of lemon-juice
- 1/4 teaspoonful of powdered sugar

## Directions

1. Cut the kidneys into neat slices, put them into warm water to soak for 2 hours, and change the water 2 or 3 times.
2. Put them on a clean cloth to dry the water from them, and lay them in a frying-pan with some clarified butter, and fry them of a nice brown.
3. Season each side with pepper and salt, put them round the dish, and the gravy in the middle.
4. Before pouring the gravy in the dish, add the lemon-juice and sugar.

title: To Dress Beef Kidney II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 kidney
- 1 dessertspoonful of minced parsley
- 1 teaspoonful of minced shalot
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1/4 pint of gravy
- 3 tablespoonfuls of sherry
- flour
- croûtons

## Directions

1. Take off a little of the kidney fat, mince it very fine, and put it in a frying-pan.
2. Slice the kidney, sprinkle over it parsley and shalots in the above proportion, add a seasoning of pepper and salt, and fry it of a nice brown.
3. When it is done enough, dredge over a little flour, and pour in the gravy and sherry.
4. Let it just simmer, but not boil any more, or the kidney would harden.
5. Serve very hot, and garnish with croûtons.

title: To Dress Beef Kidney III
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- kidney
- flour
- butter
- 1/4 pint of boiling water
- pepper and salt
- 1 tablespoonful of mushroom ketchup

## Directions

1. Cut the kidney into thin slices, flour them, and fry of a nice brown.
2. When done, make a gravy in the pan by pouring away the fat, putting in a small piece of butter, 1/4 pint of boiling water, pepper and salt, and a tablespoonful of mushroom ketchup.
3. Let the gravy just boil up, pour over the kidney, and serve.

title: To Dress Beef Palates (an Entree)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 palates
- sufficient gravy to cover them
- cayenne to taste
- 1 tablespoonful of mushroom ketchup
- 1 tablespoonful of pickled-onion liquor
- butter and flour for thickening

## Directions

1. Wash the palates, and put them into a stewpan, with sufficient water to cover them
2. Let them boil until perfectly tender, or until the upper skin may be easily peeled off
3. Have ready sufficient gravy to cover them; add a good seasoning of cayenne, and thicken with roux or a little butter kneaded with flour
4. Let it boil up, and skim
5. Cut the palates into square pieces, put them in the gravy, and let them simmer gently for 1/2 hour
6. Add ketchup and onion-liquor, give one boil, and serve

title: To Dress Carrots In The German Way
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 8 large carrots
- 3 oz. of butter
- salt to taste
- a very little grated nutmeg
- 1 tablespoonful of finely-minced parsley
- 1 dessertspoonful of minced onion
- rather more than 1 pint of weak stock or broth
- 1 tablespoonful of flour

## Directions

1. Wash and scrape the carrots
2. cut them into rings of about 1/4 inch in thickness
3. Put the butter into a stewpan
4. when it is melted, lay in the carrots, with salt, nutmeg, parsley, and onion in the above proportions
5. Toss the stewpan over the fire for a few minutes
6. when the carrots are well saturated with the butter, pour in the stock
7. simmer gently until they are nearly tender
8. Then put into another stewpan a small piece of butter
9. dredge in about a tablespoonful of flour
10. stir this over the fire
11. when of a nice brown colour, add the liquor that the carrots have been boiling in
12. let this just boil up
13. pour it over the carrots in the other stewpan
14. let them finish simmering until quite tender
15. Serve very hot

title: To Dress Crab
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 crab
- 2 tablespoonfuls of vinegar
- 1 tablespoonful of oil
- salt, white pepper, and cayenne, to taste

## Directions

1. Empty the shells, and thoroughly mix the meat with the above ingredients, and put it in the large shell.
2. Garnish with slices of cut lemon and parsley.
3. The quantity of oil may be increased when it is much liked.

title: To Dress Cucumbers
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 tablespoonfuls of salad-oil
- 4 tablespoonfuls of vinegar
- salt and pepper to taste
- cucumber

## Directions

1. Pare the cucumber, cut it equally into very thin slices, and commence cutting from the thick end; if commenced at the stalk, the cucumber will most likely have an exceedingly bitter taste, far from agreeable.
2. Put the slices into a dish, sprinkle over salt and pepper, and pour over oil and vinegar in the above proportion.
3. Turn the cucumber about, and it is ready to serve.

title: To Dress Lobsters
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- boiled lobster
- salad-oil
- parsley

## Directions

1. Rub the lobster over with a little salad-oil, which wipe off again
2. Separate the body from the tail
3. Break off the great claws, and crack them at the joints, without injuring the meat
4. Split the tail in halves, and arrange all neatly in a dish, with the body upright in the middle
5. Garnish with parsley

title: To Dress Pig's Fry
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 lb. of pig's fry
- 2 onions
- a few sage-leaves
- 3 lbs. of potatoes
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Put the lean fry at the bottom of a pie-dish, sprinkle over it some minced sage and onion, and a seasoning of pepper and salt.
2. Slice the potatoes; put a layer of these on the seasoning, then the fat fry, then more seasoning, and a layer of potatoes at the top.
3. Fill the dish with boiling water, and bake for 2 hours, or rather longer.

title: To Dress Plovers
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 plovers
- butter
- flour
- toasted bread

## Directions

1. Choose plovers that feel hard at the vent, as that shows their fatness.
2. Pluck off the feathers and wipe the outside of the birds with a damp cloth.
3. Do not draw them.
4. Truss with the head under the wing.
5. Put them down to a clear fire and lay slices of moistened toast in the dripping-pan to catch the trail.
6. Keep them well basted.
7. Dredge them lightly with flour a few minutes before they are done.
8. Let them be nicely frothed.
9. Dish them on the toasts, over which the trail should be equally spread.
10. Pour round the toast a little good gravy and send some to table in a tureen.

title: To Dress Prawns
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- prawns
- parsley

## Directions

1. Cover a dish with a large cup reversed, and over that lay a small white napkin.
2. Arrange the prawns on it in the form of a pyramid, and garnish with plenty of parsley.

title: To Dress Quails
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Quails
- butter
- toast

## Directions

1. Roast the birds without drawing.
2. Truss them in the same manner as woodcocks.
3. Roast them before a clear fire.
4. Keep them well basted.
5. Serve on toast.

title: To Dress Salsify
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Salsify
- water
- salt
- butter
- lemon-juice

## Directions

1. Scrape the roots gently, so as to strip them only of their outside peel
2. Cut them into pieces about 4 inches long
3. As they are peeled, throw them into water with which has been mixed a little lemon-juice, to prevent their discolouring
4. Put them into boiling water, with salt, butter, and lemon-juice in the above proportion
5. Let them boil rapidly until tender
6. Try them with a fork; and, when it penetrates easily, they are done
7. Drain the salsify
8. Serve with a good white sauce or French melted butter

title: To Dress Shad
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 shad
- oil
- pepper and salt
- caper sauce

## Directions

1. Scale, empty and wash the fish carefully
2. make two or three incisions across the back
3. Season it with pepper and salt
4. let it remain in oil for 1/2 hour
5. Broil it on both sides over a clear fire
6. serve with caper sauce

title: To Dress Snipes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Snipes
- butter
- flour
- toast

## Directions

1. Dress without being drawn.
2. Pluck and wipe them outside.
3. Truss them with the head under the wing, having previously skinned that and the neck.
4. Twist the legs at the first joint.
5. Press the feet upon the thighs and pass a skewer through these and the body.
6. Place four on a skewer and tie them on to the jack or spit.
7. Roast before a clear fire for about 1/4 hour.
8. Put some pieces of buttered toast into the dripping-pan to catch the trails.
9. Flour and froth the birds nicely.
10. Dish the pieces of toast with the snipes on them.
11. Pour round, but not over them, a little good brown gravy.
12. Send to table very hot and expeditiously.

title: To Dress The Ptarmigan
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 or 3 birds
- butter
- flour
- fried bread crumbs

## Directions

1. Pluck, draw, and truss them in the same manner as grouse.
2. Roast them before a brisk fire.
3. Flour and froth them nicely.
4. Serve on buttered toast with a tureen of brown gravy.
5. Bread sauce, when liked, may be sent to table with them, and fried bread crumbs substituted for the toasted bread.

title: To Dress Tripe
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Tripe
- onion sauce
- milk and water

## Directions

1. Ascertain that the tripe is quite fresh, and have it cleaned and dressed
2. Cut away the coarsest fat, and boil it in equal proportions of milk and water for 3/4 hour
3. Have ready some onion sauce made by recipe No. 484, dish the tripe, smother it with the sauce, and the remainder send to table in a tureen

title: To Dress Truffles With Champagne
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 12 fine black truffles
- a few slices of fat bacon
- 1 carrot
- 1 turnip
- 2 onions
- a bunch of savoury herbs, including parsley
- 1 bay-leaf
- 2 cloves
- 1 blade of pounded mace
- 2 glasses of champagne
- 1/2 pint of stock

## Directions

1. Carefully select the truffles, reject those that have a musty smell, and wash them well with a brush, in cold water only, until perfectly clean
2. Put the bacon into a stewpan, with the truffles and the remaining ingredients
3. Simmer these gently for an hour, and let the whole cool in the stewpan
4. When to be served, rewarm them, and drain them on a clean cloth
5. Arrange them on a delicately white napkin, that it may contrast as strongly as possible with the truffles, and serve

title: To Dress Wheatears
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Wheatears
- Fresh butter

## Directions

1. After the birds are picked, gutted, and cleaned, truss them like larks
2. Put them down to a quick fire
3. Baste them well with fresh butter
4. When done, which will be in about 20 minutes, dish them on fried bread crumbs
5. Garnish the dish with slices of lemon

title: To Dress Whitebait
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A little flour
- hot lard
- seasoning of salt

## Directions

1. This fish should be put into iced water as soon as bought, unless they are cooked immediately.
2. Drain them from the water in a colander, and have ready a nice clean dry cloth, over which put 2 good handfuls of flour.
3. Toss in the whitebait, shake them lightly in the cloth, and put them in a wicker sieve to take away the superfluous flour.
4. Throw them into a pan of boiling lard, very few at a time, and let them fry till of a whitey-brown colour.
5. Directly they are done, they must be taken out, and laid before the fire for a minute or two on a sieve reversed, covered with blotting-paper to absorb the fat.
6. Dish them on a hot napkin, arrange the fish very high in the centre, and sprinkle a little salt over the whole.

title: To Dry Pigs' Cheeks
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Salt
- 4 oz. of saltpetre
- 2 oz. of bay-salt
- 4 oz. of coarse sugar

## Directions

1. Cut out the snout, remove the brains, and split the head, taking off the upper bone to make the jowl a good shape
2. Rub it well with salt
3. Next day take away the brine, and salt it again the following day
4. Cover the head with saltpetre, bay-salt, and coarse sugar, in the above proportion, adding a little common salt
5. Let the head be often turned
6. When it has been in the pickle for 10 days, smoke it for a week or rather longer

title: To Frost Holly-Leaves
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Sprigs of holly
- Oiled butter
- Coarsely-powdered sugar

## Directions

1. Procure some nice sprigs of holly
2. Pick the leaves from the stalks, and wipe them with a clean cloth free from all moisture
3. Place them on a dish near the fire, to get thoroughly dry, but not too near to shrivel the leaves
4. Dip them into oiled butter, sprinkle over them some coarsely-powdered sugar, and dry them before the fire
5. Keep them in a dry place, as the least damp would spoil their appearance

title: To Fry Smelts
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Egg and bread crumbs
- a little flour
- boiling lard

## Directions

1. Smelts should be very fresh, and not washed more than is necessary to clean them.
2. Dry them in a cloth, lightly flour, dip them in egg, and sprinkle over with very fine bread crumbs, and put them into boiling lard.
3. Fry of a nice pale brown, and be careful not to take off the light roughness of the crumbs, or their beauty will be spoiled.
4. Dry them before the fire on a drainer, and serve with plain melted butter.

title: To Have Walnuts Fresh Throughout the Season
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- walnuts
- water
- salt

## Directions

1. To every pint of water allow 1 teaspoonful of salt.
2. Place the walnuts in the salt and water for 24 hours at least.
3. Take them out, and rub them dry.
4. Old nuts may be freshened in this manner; or walnuts, when first picked, may be put into an earthen pan with salt sprinkled amongst them, and with damped hay placed on the top of them, and then covered down with a lid.
5. They must be well wiped before they are put on table.

title: To Keep Eggs Fresh For Several Weeks
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- eggs
- sawdust
- butter or sweet oil
- bran

## Directions

1. Have ready a large saucepan, capable of holding 3 or 4 quarts, full of boiling water.
2. Put the eggs into a cabbage-net, say 20 at a time, and hold them in the water (which must be kept boiling) for 20 seconds.
3. Proceed in this manner till you have done as many eggs as you wish to preserve.
4. Pack them away in sawdust.
5. Alternatively, smear the shells with butter or sweet oil, pack in plenty of bran or sawdust, and ensure the eggs do not touch each other.
6. Or immerse them in lime-water soon after they have been laid, and put the vessel containing the lime-water in a cellar or cool outhouse.

title: To Keep Oysters
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- oysters
- salt
- water

## Directions

1. Put them in a tub, and cover them with salt and water.
2. Let them remain for 12 hours, when they are to be taken out, and allowed to stand for another 12 hours without water.
3. If left without water every alternate 12 hours, they will be much better than if constantly kept in it.
4. Never put the same water twice to them.

title: To Make a Fondue
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 eggs
- cheese (the weight of 2 eggs in Parmesan or good Cheshire cheese)
- butter (the weight of 2 eggs)
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Separate the yolks from the whites of the eggs
2. Beat the yolks in a basin
3. Grate the cheese, or cut it into very thin flakes
4. Break the butter into small pieces
5. Add the butter to the other ingredients, with sufficient pepper and salt to season nicely
6. Beat the mixture thoroughly
7. Well whisk the whites of the eggs
8. Stir them lightly in
9. Either bake the fondue in a soufflé-dish or small round cake-tin
10. Fill the dish only half full, as the fondue should rise very much
11. Pin a napkin round the tin or dish
12. Serve very hot and very quickly

title: To Make a Peck of Good Bread
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 lbs. of potatoes
- 6 pints of cold water
- 1/2 pint of good yeast
- a peck of flour
- 2 oz. of salt

## Directions

1. Peel and boil the potatoes
2. Beat them to a cream while warm
3. Add 1 pint of cold water, strain through a colander, and add to it 1/2 pint of good yeast, which should have been put in water over-night, to take off its bitterness
4. Stir all well together with a wooden spoon, and pour the mixture into the centre of the flour
5. Mix it to the substance of cream, cover it over closely, and let it remain near the fire for an hour
6. Add the 5 pints of water, milk-warm, with 2 oz. of salt; pour this in, and mix the whole to a nice light dough
7. Let it remain for about 2 hours
8. Make it into 7 loaves, and bake for about 1-1/2 hour in a good oven

title: To Make a Plain Omelet
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 eggs
- 1 saltspoonful of salt
- 1/3 saltspoonful of pepper
- 1/4 lb. of butter

## Directions

1. Break the eggs into a basin, omitting the whites of 3, and beat them up with the salt and pepper until extremely light
2. Add 2 oz. of the butter broken into small pieces, and stir this into the mixture
3. Put the other 2 oz. of butter into a frying-pan, make it quite hot
4. As soon as it begins to bubble, whisk the eggs, &c. very briskly for a minute or two, and pour them into the pan
5. Stir the omelet with a spoon one way until the mixture thickens and becomes firm
6. When the whole is set, fold the edges over, so that the omelet assumes an oval form
7. When it is nicely brown on one side, and quite firm, it is done
8. To take off the rawness on the upper side, hold the pan before the fire for a minute or two, and brown it with a salamander or hot shovel
9. Serve very expeditiously on a very hot dish

title: To Make A Souffle
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 heaped tablespoonfuls of potato-flour, rice-flour, arrowroot, or tapioca
- 1 pint of milk
- 5 eggs
- a piece of butter the size of a walnut
- sifted sugar to taste
- 1/4 saltspoonful of salt
- flavouring

## Directions

1. Mix the potato-flour, or whichever one of the above ingredients is used, with a little of the milk
2. Put it into a saucepan, with the remainder of the milk, the butter, salt, and sufficient pounded sugar to sweeten the whole nicely
3. Stir these ingredients over the fire until the mixture thickens
4. Take it off the fire, and let it cool a little
5. Separate the whites from the yolks of the eggs
6. Beat the latter, and stir them into the soufflé batter
7. Whisk the whites of the eggs to the firmest possible froth, for on this depends the excellence of the dish
8. Stir them to the other ingredients
9. Add a few drops of essence of any flavouring that may be preferred; such as vanilla, lemon, orange, ginger, &c.
10. Pour the batter into a soufflé-dish
11. Put it immediately into the oven, and bake for about 1/2 hour
12. Take it out, put the dish into another more ornamental one
13. Hold a salamander or hot shovel over the soufflé, strew it with sifted sugar, and send it instantly to table

title: To Make a Trifle
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of cream
- 3 oz. of pounded sugar
- the whites of 2 eggs
- a small glass of sherry or raisin wine
- 1 pint of custard, made with 8 eggs to a pint of milk
- 6 small sponge-cakes, or 6 slices of sponge-cake
- 12 macaroons
- 2 dozen ratafias
- 2 oz. of sweet almonds
- the grated rind of 1 lemon
- a layer of raspberry or strawberry jam
- 1/2 pint of sherry or sweet wine
- 6 tablespoonfuls of brandy

## Directions

1. The whip to lay over the top of the trifle should be made the day before it is required for table, as the flavour is better, and it is much more solid than when prepared the same day.
2. Put into a large bowl the pounded sugar, the whites of the eggs, which should be beaten to a stiff froth, a glass of sherry or sweet wine, and the cream.
3. Whisk these ingredients well in a cool place, and take off the froth with a skimmer as fast as it rises, and put it on a sieve to drain; continue the whisking till there is sufficient of the whip, which must be put away in a cool place to drain.
4. The next day, place the sponge-cakes, macaroons, and ratafias at the bottom of a trifle-dish.
5. Pour over them 1/2 pint of sherry or sweet wine, mixed with 6 tablespoonfuls of brandy, and, should this proportion of wine not be found quite sufficient, add a little more, as the cakes should be well soaked.
6. Over the cakes put the grated lemon-rind, the sweet almonds, blanched and cut into strips, and a layer of raspberry or strawberry jam.
7. Make a good custard by recipe No. 1423, using 8 instead of 5 eggs to the pint of milk, and let this cool a little; then pour it over the cakes, &c.
8. The whip being made the day previously, and the trifle prepared, there remains nothing to do now but heap the whip lightly over the top: this should stand as high as possible, and it may be garnished with strips of bright currant jelly, crystallized sweetmeats, or flowers; the small coloured comfits are sometimes used for the purpose of garnishing a trifle, but they are now considered rather old-fashioned.

title: TO MAKE ARROWROOT
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Two teaspoonfuls of arrowroot
- 3 tablespoonfuls of cold water
- 1/2 pint of boiling water
- lump sugar
- grated nutmeg or cinnamon, or a piece of lemon-peel, or when allowed, 3 tablespoonfuls of port or sherry

## Directions

1. Mix the arrowroot smoothly in a basin with the cold water, then pour on it the boiling water, stirring all the time.
2. The water must be boiling at the time it is poured on the mixture, or it will not thicken; if mixed with hot water only, it must be put into a clean saucepan, and boiled until it thickens.
3. Put the arrowroot into a tumbler, sweeten it with lump sugar, and flavour it with grated nutmeg or cinnamon, or a piece of lemon-peel, or, when allowed, 3 tablespoonfuls of port or sherry.
4. If arrowroot is made with milk instead of water, mix in the same manner with 3 tablespoonfuls of cold water, then pour on the boiling milk and stir well.
5. When made with milk, add no wine, but merely sugar and a little grated nutmeg or lemon-peel.

title: To Make Barley-Sugar
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Sugar, 1 lb.
- Water, 1/2 pint
- White of egg, 1/2
- Lemon-juice
- Essence of lemon

## Directions

1. Put the sugar into a well-tinned saucepan, with the water, and, when the former is dissolved, set it over a moderate fire, adding the well-beaten egg before the mixture gets warm, and stir it well together.
2. When it boils, remove the scum as it rises, and keep it boiling until no more appears, and the syrup looks perfectly clear; then strain it through a fine sieve or muslin bag, and put it back into the saucepan.
3. Boil it again like caramel, until it is brittle, when a little is dropped in a basin of cold water: it is then sufficiently boiled.
4. Add a little lemon-juice and a few drops of essence of lemon, and let it stand for a minute or two.
5. Have ready a marble slab or large dish, rubbed over with salad-oil; pour on it the sugar, and cut it into strips with a pair of scissors: these strips should then be twisted, and the barley-sugar stored away in a very dry place.
6. It may be formed into lozenges or drops, by dropping the sugar in a very small quantity at a time on to the oiled slab or dish.

title: To Make Barley-Water
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. of pearl barley
- 2 quarts of boiling water
- 1 pint of cold water

## Directions

1. Wash the barley in cold water
2. Put it into a saucepan with the above proportion of cold water
3. When it has boiled for about 1/4 hour, strain off the water, and add the 2 quarts of fresh boiling water
4. Boil it until the liquid is reduced one half
5. Strain it, and it will be ready for use
6. It may be flavoured with lemon-peel, after being sweetened, or a small piece may be simmered with the barley
7. When the invalid may take it, a little lemon-juice gives this pleasant drink in illness a very nice flavour

title: To Make Beef Tea
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of lean gravy-beef
- 1 quart of water
- 1 saltspoonful of salt

## Directions

1. Have the meat cut without fat and bone, and choose a nice fleshy piece
2. Cut it into small pieces about the size of dice, and put it into a clean saucepan
3. Add the water cold to it
4. Put it on the fire, and bring it to the boiling-point
5. Skim well
6. Put in the salt when the water boils, and simmer the beef tea gently from 1/2 to 3/4 hour, removing any more scum should it appear on the surface
7. Strain the tea through a hair sieve, and set it by in a cool place
8. When wanted for use, remove every particle of fat from the top
9. Warm up as much as may be required, adding, if necessary, a little more salt

title: To Make Cherry Brandy
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Morella cherries
- Good brandy
- Pounded sugar, 3 oz. to every lb. of cherries
- Peach or apricot kernels
- Blanched bitter almonds

## Directions

1. Have ready some glass bottles, which must be perfectly dry.
2. Ascertain that the cherries are not too ripe and are freshly gathered, and cut off about half of the stalks.
3. Put them into the bottles, with the above proportion of sugar to every lb. of fruit; strew this in between the cherries, and, when the bottles are nearly full, pour in sufficient brandy to reach just below the cork.
4. A few peach or apricot kernels will add much to their flavour, or a few blanched bitter almonds.
5. Put corks or bungs into the bottles, tie over them a piece of bladder, and store away in a dry place.

title: To Make Chocolate
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 oz. of chocolate per person
- 1/2 pint of water per oz. of chocolate
- 1/2 pint of milk per oz. of chocolate
- white sugar

## Directions

1. Make the milk-and-water hot
2. Scrape the chocolate into it
3. Stir the mixture constantly and quickly until the chocolate is dissolved
4. Bring it to the boiling-point
5. Stir it well
6. Serve directly with white sugar
7. Alternatively, using a mill: put in the scraped chocolate, pour over it the boiling milk-and-water, and mill it over the fire until hot and frothy

title: To Make Cocoa
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 teaspoonfuls of the prepared cocoa to 1 breakfast-cup
- boiling milk and boiling water

## Directions

1. Put the cocoa into a breakfast-cup
2. Pour over it sufficient boiling milk and boiling water

title: To Make Coffee
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 oz. or 1 tablespoonful of ground coffee per person
- 1/3 pint of water per oz. of coffee

## Directions

1. Warm the urn with boiling water
2. Remove the lid and movable filter and place the ground coffee at the bottom of the urn
3. Put the movable filter over this and screw the lid, inverted, tightly on the end of the centre pipe
4. Pour into the inverted lid the proportion of boiling water
5. When all the water has disappeared from the funnel and made its way down the centre pipe and up again through the ground coffee by hydrostatic pressure, unscrew the lid and cover the urn
6. Pour back direct into the urn, not through the funnel, one, two, or three cups according to the size of the percolator, in order to make the infusion of uniform strength
7. The contents will then be ready for use and should run from the tap strong, hot, and clear
8. Coffee should be served very hot in the same vessel in which it is made

title: To Make Essence of Coffee
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of ground coffee
- 1 small teaspoonful of powdered chicory per 1/4 lb. of coffee
- 3 small teacupfuls or 1 pint of water

## Directions

1. Let the coffee be freshly ground, and if possible, freshly roasted
2. Put it into a percolater or filter with the chicory
3. Pour slowly over it the boiling water
4. When it has all filtered through, warm the coffee sufficiently to bring it to the simmering-point, but do not allow it to boil
5. Filter it a second time
6. Put it into a clean and dry bottle
7. Cork it well, and it will remain good for several days
8. Use two tablespoonfuls of this essence for a breakfast-cupful of hot milk

title: To Make Fruit Ice-Creams
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of fruit-juice
- 1 pint of cream
- sugar to taste
- melted isinglass (optional, for moulding)

## Directions

1. Let the fruit be well ripened; pick it off the stalks, and put it into a large earthen pan.
2. Stir it about with a wooden spoon, breaking it until it is well mashed.
3. With the back of the spoon, rub it through a hair sieve.
4. Sweeten it nicely with pounded sugar.
5. Whip the cream for a few minutes, add it to the fruit, and whisk the whole again for another 5 minutes.
6. Put the mixture into the freezing-pot, and freeze in the same manner as directed for Ice Pudding, No. 1290, taking care to stir the cream, &c., two or three times, and to remove it from the sides of the vessel, that the mixture may be equally frozen and smooth.
7. If moulded, add a small quantity of melted isinglass to enable them to keep their shape.

title: To Make Fruit-Water Ices
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of fruit-juice
- 1 pint of syrup made by recipe No. 1513
- pounded sugar

## Directions

1. Select nice ripe fruit; pick off the stalks, and put it into a large earthen pan, with a little pounded sugar strewed over.
2. Stir it about with a wooden spoon until it is well broken, then rub it through a hair sieve.
3. Make the syrup by recipe No. 1513, omitting the white of the egg.
4. Let it cool, add the fruit-juice, mix well together, and put the mixture into the freezing-pot.
5. Proceed as directed for Ice Puddings, No. 1290, and when the mixture is equally frozen, put it into small glasses.

title: To Make Good Home-Made Bread
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 quartern of flour
- 1 large tablespoonful of solid brewer's yeast, or nearly 1 oz. of fresh German yeast
- 1-1/4 to 1-1/2 pint of warm milk-and-water

## Directions

1. Put the flour into a large earthenware bowl or deep pan
2. With a strong metal or wooden spoon, hollow out the middle; but do not clear it entirely away from the bottom of the pan
3. Take either a large tablespoonful of brewer's yeast which has been rendered solid by mixing it with plenty of cold water, and letting it afterwards stand to settle for a day and night; or nearly an ounce of German yeast
4. Put it into a large basin, and proceed to mix it, so that it shall be as smooth as cream, with 3/4 pint of warm milk-and-water, or with water only
5. Pour the yeast into the hole made in the flour, and stir into it as much of that which lies round it as will make a thick batter, in which there must be no lumps
6. Strew plenty of flour on the top
7. Throw a thick clean cloth over, and set it where the air is warm; but do not place it upon the kitchen fender
8. Look at it from time to time: when it has been laid for nearly an hour, and when the yeast has risen and broken through the flour, so that bubbles appear in it, you will know that it is ready to be made up into dough
9. Place the pan on a strong chair, or dresser, or table, of convenient height
10. Pour into the sponge the remainder of the warm milk-and-water
11. Stir into it as much of the flour as you can with the spoon
12. Wipe it out clean with your fingers, and lay it aside
13. Take plenty of the remaining flour, throw it on the top of the leaven, and begin, with the knuckles of both hands, to knead it well
14. When the flour is nearly all kneaded in, begin to draw the edges of the dough towards the middle, in order to mix the whole thoroughly
15. When it is free from flour and lumps and crumbs, and does not stick to the hands when touched, it will be done, and may again be covered with the cloth, and left to rise a second time
16. In 3/4 hour look at it, and should it have swollen very much, and begin to crack, it will be light enough to bake

title: To Make Good Plain Buns
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of flour
- 6 oz. of good butter
- 1/4 lb. of sugar
- 1 egg
- nearly 1/4 pint of milk
- 2 small teaspoonfuls of baking-powder
- a few drops of essence of lemon

## Directions

1. Warm the butter, without oiling it
2. Beat it with a wooden spoon
3. Stir the flour in gradually with the sugar, and mix these ingredients well together
4. Make the milk lukewarm, beat up with it the yolk of the egg and the essence of lemon
5. Stir these to the flour
6. Add the baking-powder
7. Beat the dough well for about 10 minutes
8. Divide it into 24 pieces
9. Put them into buttered tins or cups
10. Bake in a brisk oven from 20 to 30 minutes

title: To Make Gooseberry Fool
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Green gooseberries
- to every pint of pulp add 1 pint of milk, or 1/2 pint of cream and 1/2 pint of milk
- sugar to taste

## Directions

1. Cut the tops and tails off the gooseberries; put them into a jar, with 2 tablespoonfuls of water and a little good moist sugar
2. Set this jar in a saucepan of boiling water, and let it boil until the fruit is soft enough to mash
3. When done enough, beat it to a pulp, work this pulp through a colander, and stir to every pint the above proportion of milk, or equal quantities of milk and cream
4. Ascertain if the mixture is sweet enough, and put in plenty of sugar, or it will not be eatable
5. In mixing the milk and gooseberries, add the former very gradually to these
6. Serve in a glass dish, or in small glasses

title: To Make Gruel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 tablespoonful of Robinson's patent groats
- 2 tablespoonfuls of cold water
- 1 pint of boiling water

## Directions

1. Mix the prepared groats smoothly with the cold water in a basin
2. pour over them the boiling water, stirring it all the time
3. Put it into a very clean saucepan
4. boil the gruel for 10 minutes, keeping it well stirred
5. sweeten to taste, and serve
6. It may be flavoured with a small piece of lemon-peel, by boiling it in the gruel, or a little grated nutmeg may be put in
7. Pour the gruel in a tumbler and serve

title: To Make Hot Buttered Toast
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- a loaf of household bread about two days old
- good butter

## Directions

1. Cut as many nice even slices as may be required, rather more than 1/4 inch in thickness, and toast them before a very bright fire, without allowing the bread to blacken, which spoils the appearance and flavour of all toast.
2. When of a nice colour on both sides, put it on a hot plate; divide some good butter into small pieces, place them on the toast, set this before the fire, and when the butter is just beginning to melt, spread it lightly over the toast.
3. Trim off the crust and ragged edges, divide each round into 4 pieces, and send the toast quickly to table.
4. Some persons cut the slices of toast across from corner to corner, so making the pieces of a three-cornered shape.
5. Each slice should be cut into pieces as soon as it is buttered, and when all are ready, pile them lightly on the dish they are intended to be served on.

title: To Make Hot Punch
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of rum
- 1/2 pint of brandy
- 1/4 lb. of sugar
- 1 large lemon
- 1/2 teaspoonful of nutmeg
- 1 pint of boiling water

## Directions

1. Rub the sugar over the lemon until it has absorbed all the yellow part of the skin
2. Put the sugar into a punchbowl
3. Add the lemon-juice (free from pips)
4. Mix these two ingredients well together
5. Pour over them the boiling water
6. Stir well together
7. Add the rum, brandy, and nutmeg
8. Mix thoroughly

title: To Make Mutton Broth
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of the scrag end of the neck of mutton
- 1 onion
- a bunch of sweet herbs
- 4 turnip
- 1/2 pints of water
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Put the mutton into a stewpan
2. pour over the water cold and add the other ingredients
3. When it boils, skim it very carefully, cover the pan closely, and let it simmer very gently for an hour
4. strain it, let it cool, take off all the fat from the surface, and warm up as much as may be required, adding, if the patient be allowed to take it, a teaspoonful of minced parsley which has been previously scalded

title: To Make Negus
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- To every pint of port wine allow 1 quart of boiling water
- 1/4 lb. of sugar
- 1 lemon
- grated nutmeg to taste

## Directions

1. Put the wine into a jug, rub some lumps of sugar (equal to 1/4 lb.) on the lemon-rind until all the yellow part of the skin is absorbed, then squeeze the juice, and strain it.
2. Add the sugar and lemon-juice to the port wine, with the grated nutmeg; pour over it the boiling water, cover the jug, and, when the beverage has cooled a little, it will be fit for use.
3. Negus may also be made of sherry, or any other sweet white wine, but is more usually made of port than of any other beverage.

title: To Make Pancakes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- eggs
- flour
- milk
- salt
- butter

## Directions

1. Ascertain that the eggs are fresh and break each one separately in a cup
2. Whisk them well and put them into a basin with the flour, salt, and a few drops of milk
3. Beat the whole to a perfectly smooth batter
4. Add by degrees the remainder of the milk
5. The batter, when ready for frying, should be of the consistency of thick cream
6. Place a small frying-pan on the fire to get hot
7. Let it be delicately clean so the pancakes will not stick
8. When quite hot, put into it a small piece of butter, allowing about 1/2 oz. to each pancake
9. When it is melted, pour in the batter, about 1/2 teacupful to a pan 5 inches in diameter
10. Fry it for about 4 minutes, or until it is nicely brown on one side
11. By pouring in a small quantity of batter and so making the pancakes thin, the necessity of turning them is obviated

title: To Make Rusks
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of flour
- 2 oz. of butter
- 1/4 pint of milk
- 2 oz. of loaf sugar
- 3 eggs
- 1 tablespoonful of yeast

## Directions

1. Put the milk and butter into a saucepan, and keep shaking it round until the latter is melted.
2. Put the flour into a basin with the sugar, mix these well together, and beat the eggs.
3. Stir them with the yeast to the milk and butter, and with this liquid work the flour into a smooth dough.
4. Cover a cloth over the basin, and leave the dough to rise by the side of the fire.
5. Knead it, and divide it into 12 pieces.
6. Place them in a brisk oven, and bake for about 20 minutes.
7. Take the rusks out, break them in half, and then set them in the oven to get crisp on the other side.
8. When cold, they should be put into tin canisters to keep them dry.

title: To Make Sausages
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of pork, fat and lean, without skin or gristle
- 1 lb. of lean veal
- 1 lb. of beef suet
- 1/2 lb. of bread crumbs
- the rind of 1/2 lemon
- 1 small nutmeg
- 6 sage-leaves
- 1 teaspoonful of pepper
- 2 teaspoonfuls of salt
- 1/2 teaspoonful of savory
- 1/2 teaspoonful of marjoram

## Directions

1. Chop the pork, veal, and suet finely together.
2. Add the bread crumbs, lemon-peel (which should be well minced), and a small nutmeg grated.
3. Wash and chop the sage-leaves very finely.
4. Add these with the remaining ingredients to the sausage-meat, and when thoroughly mixed, either put the meat into skins, or, when wanted for table, form it into little cakes, which should be floured and fried.

title: To Make Small Sponge-Cakes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The weight of 5 eggs in flour
- the weight of 8 in pounded loaf sugar
- flavouring to taste

## Directions

1. Let the flour be perfectly dry, and the sugar well pounded and sifted
2. Separate the whites from the yolks of the eggs, and beat the latter up with the sugar
3. Whisk the whites until they become rather stiff, and mix them with the yolks, but do not stir them more than is just necessary to mingle the ingredients well together
4. Dredge in the flour by degrees, add the flavouring
5. Batter the tins well, pour in the batter, sift a little sugar over the cakes
6. Bake them in rather a quick oven, but do not allow them to take too much colour, as they should be rather pale
7. Remove them from the tins before they get cold, and turn them on their faces, where let them remain until quite cold
8. Store them away in a closed tin canister or wide-mouthed glass bottle

title: To Make Starch
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of cold water
- 1 quart of boiling water
- 2 tablespoonfuls of starch
- gum Arabic (optional, for extra stiffness)
- wax candle (optional, for shiny appearance)

## Directions

1. Put the starch into a tolerably large basin
2. Pour over it the cold water, and stir the mixture well with a wooden spoon until it is perfectly free from lumps, and quite smooth
3. Take the basin to the fire, and whilst the water is actually boiling in the kettle or boiler, pour it over the starch, stirring it the whole time
4. If the water not be boiling when added to the starch, put it into a clean saucepan, and stir over the fire until it boils
5. Take it off the fire, strain it into a clean basin
6. Cover it up to prevent a skin forming on the top
7. When sufficiently cool that the hand may be borne in it, starch the things
8. To give a shiny and smooth appearance to the linen when ironed, stir round two or three times in the starch a piece of wax candle, which also prevents the iron from sticking

title: To Make Syllabub
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of sherry or white wine
- 1/2 grated nutmeg
- sugar to taste
- 1-1/2 pint of milk
- clouted cream
- pounded cinnamon or nutmeg and sugar
- brandy

## Directions

1. Put the wine into a bowl, with the grated nutmeg and plenty of pounded sugar.
2. Milk into it the above proportion of milk frothed up.
3. Lay clouted cream on the top, with pounded cinnamon or nutmeg and sugar.
4. A little brandy may be added to the wine before the milk is put in.
5. In some counties, cider is substituted for the wine: when this is used, brandy must always be added.
6. Warm milk may be poured on from a spouted jug or teapot; but it must be held very high.

title: To Make Syrup For Compotes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of sugar
- 1-1/2 pint of water

## Directions

1. Boil the sugar and water together for 1/4 hour, carefully removing the scum as it rises
2. The syrup is then ready for the fruit

title: To Make Tea
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- tea leaves
- boiling water
- a few grains of carbonate of soda

## Directions

1. Warm the teapot with boiling water and let it remain for two or three minutes for the vessel to become thoroughly hot, then pour it away
2. Put in the tea, allowing a teaspoonful to each person and one over
3. Pour in from 1/2 to 3/4 pint of boiling water
4. Close the lid and let it stand for the tea to draw from 5 to 10 minutes
5. Fill up the pot with water
6. For very hard water, a few grains of carbonate of soda may be added before the boiling water is poured on the tea, but use sparingly to avoid a soapy taste
7. When more tea is required, empty the pot of the old leaves, scald it, and make fresh tea in the usual manner

title: To Make the Stock for Jelly, and to Clarify It
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 calf's feet
- 6 pints of water
- 6 oz. of loaf sugar
- shells and well-whisked whites of 5 eggs

## Directions

1. Procure 2 nice calf's feet from the butcher's
2. Scald them, to take off the hair
3. Slit them in two, remove the fat from between the claws, and wash the feet well in warm water
4. Put them into a stewpan, with cold water, bring it gradually to boil, and remove every particle of scum as it rises
5. When it is well skimmed, boil it very gently for 6 or 7 hours, or until the liquor is reduced rather more than half
6. Strain it through a sieve into a basin, and put it in a cool place to set
7. Measure the liquor to ascertain the proportion for the jelly
8. Carefully remove all the fat from the top, pour over a little warm water to wash away any that may remain, and wipe the jelly with a clean cloth
9. Remove the jelly from the sediment, put it into a saucepan, and add 6 oz. of loaf sugar, the shells and well-whisked whites of 5 eggs
10. Stir these ingredients together cold
11. Set the saucepan on the fire, but do not stir the jelly after it begins to warm
12. Let it boil about 10 minutes after it rises to a head, then throw in a teacupful of cold water
13. Let it boil 5 minutes longer, then take the saucepan off, cover it closely, and let it remain 1/2 hour near the fire
14. Dip the jelly-bag into hot water, wring it out quite dry, and fasten it on to a stand or the back of a chair, which must be placed near the fire
15. Place a basin underneath to receive the jelly
16. Pour it into the bag, and should it not be clear the first time, run it through the bag again

title: To Make Toast-and-Water
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A slice of bread
- 1 quart of boiling water

## Directions

1. Cut a slice from a stale loaf (a piece of hard crust is better than anything else for the purpose)
2. Toast it of a nice brown on every side, but do not allow it to burn or blacken
3. Put it into a jug
4. Pour the boiling water over it
5. Cover it closely
6. Let it remain until cold
7. When strained, it will be ready for use

title: To Make Yeast for Bread
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 oz. of hops
- 3 quarts of water
- 1 lb. of bruised malt
- 1/2 pint of yeast

## Directions

1. Boil the hops in the water for 20 minutes
2. Let it stand for about 5 minutes, then add it to 1 lb. of bruised malt prepared as for brewing
3. Let the mixture stand covered till about lukewarm
4. Put in not quite 1/2 pint of yeast
5. Keep it warm, and let it work 3 or 4 hours
6. Put it into small 1/2-pint bottles (ginger-beer bottles are the best for the purpose), cork them well, and tie them down
7. The yeast is now ready for use; it will keep good for a few weeks, and 1 bottle will be found sufficient for 18 lbs. of flour
8. When required for use, boil 3 lbs. of potatoes without salt, mash them in the same water in which they were boiled, and rub them through a colander
9. Stir in about 1/2 lb. of flour
10. Put in the yeast, pour it in the middle of the flour, and let it stand warm on the hearth all night
11. In the morning let it be quite warm when it is kneaded
12. Open the bottles of yeast very carefully, as it is generally exceedingly ripe

title: To Mull Wine
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of wine
- 1 large cupful of water
- sugar and spice to taste

## Directions

1. Boil the spice in the water until the flavour is extracted
2. Add the wine and sugar
3. Bring the whole to the boiling-point
4. Serve with strips of crisp dry toast, or with biscuits

title: To Pickle and Dress a Tongue to Eat Cold
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of salt
- 2 oz. of bay-salt
- 1 oz. of saltpetre
- 3 oz. of coarse sugar
- cloves, mace, and allspice to taste
- butter
- common crust of flour and water

## Directions

1. Lay the tongue for a fortnight in the above pickle, turn it every day, and be particular that the spices are well pounded
2. Put it into a small pan just large enough to hold it, place some pieces of butter on it, and cover with a common crust
3. Bake in a slow oven until so tender that a straw would penetrate it
4. Take off the skin, fasten it down to a piece of board by running a fork through the root and another through the tip, at the same time straightening it and putting it into shape
5. When cold, glaze it, put a paper ruche round the root, and garnish with tufts of parsley

title: To Pickle Eggs
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 16 eggs
- 1 quart of vinegar
- 1/2 oz. of Black pepper
- 1/2 oz. of Jamaica pepper
- 1/2 oz. of ginger

## Directions

1. Boil the eggs for 12 minutes, then dip them into cold water, and take off the shells.
2. Put the vinegar, with the pepper and ginger, into a stewpan, and let it simmer for 10 minutes.
3. Place the eggs in a jar, pour over them the vinegar, &c., boiling hot, and, when cold, tie them down with bladder to exclude the air.
4. This pickle will be ready for use in a month.

title: To Pickle Hams (Suffolk Recipe)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of coarse sugar
- 3/4 lb. of salt
- 1 oz. of saltpetre
- 1/2 a teacupful of vinegar

## Directions

1. Rub the hams well with common salt, and leave them for a day or two to drain.
2. Then rub well in, the above proportion of sugar, salt, saltpetre, and vinegar, and turn them every other day.
3. Keep them in the pickle 1 month, drain them, and send them to be smoked over a wood fire for 3 weeks or a month.

title: To Pickle Lemons With The Peel On
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 lemons
- 2 quarts of boiling water
- 2 quarts of vinegar
- 1 oz. of cloves per quart of vinegar
- 1/2 oz. of white pepper per quart of vinegar
- 1 oz. of bruised ginger per quart of vinegar
- 1/4 oz. of mace and chilies per quart of vinegar
- 1 oz. of mustard-seed per quart of vinegar
- 1/2 stick of sliced horseradish per quart of vinegar
- a few cloves of garlic per quart of vinegar

## Directions

1. Put the lemons into a brine that will bear an egg
2. Let them remain in it 6 days, stirring them every day
3. Have ready 2 quarts of boiling water, put in the lemons, and allow them to boil for 1/4 hour
4. Take them out, and let them lie in a cloth until perfectly dry and cold
5. Boil up sufficient vinegar to cover the lemons, with all the above ingredients, allowing the same proportion as stated to each quart of vinegar
6. Pack the lemons in a jar, pour over the vinegar boiling hot, and tie down with a bladder
7. They will be fit for use in about 12 months, or rather sooner

title: To Pickle Lemons Without The Peel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 lemons
- 1 lb. of fine salt
- vinegar
- 1 oz. of cloves per quart of vinegar
- 1/2 oz. of white pepper per quart of vinegar
- 1 oz. of bruised ginger per quart of vinegar
- 1/4 oz. of mace and chilies per quart of vinegar
- 1 oz. of mustard-seed per quart of vinegar
- 1/2 stick of sliced horseradish per quart of vinegar
- a few cloves of garlic per quart of vinegar

## Directions

1. Peel the lemons, slit each one down 3 times, so as not to divide them
2. Rub the salt well into the divisions
3. Place them in a pan, where they must remain for a week, turning them every other day
4. Put them in a Dutch oven before a clear fire until the salt has become perfectly dry
5. Arrange them in a jar
6. Pour over sufficient boiling vinegar to cover them, to which have been added the ingredients mentioned in the foregoing recipe
7. Tie down closely, and in about 9 months they will be fit for use

title: To Pickle Part of a Round of Beef for Hanging
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 14 lbs. of a round of beef
- 1-1/2 lb. of salt
- 1/2 oz. of powdered saltpetre
- coarse linen tape

## Directions

1. Rub in, and sprinkle the mixture of salt and saltpetre on 14 lbs. of meat
2. Keep it in an earthenware pan, or a deep wooden tray, and turn twice a week during 3 weeks
3. Bind up the beef tightly with coarse linen tape, and hang it in a kitchen in which a fire is constantly kept, for 3 weeks

title: To Pickle Part of a Round of Beef for Hanging
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 14 lbs. of a round of beef
- 1-1/2 lb. of salt
- 1/2 oz. of powdered saltpetre
- or 1 lb. of salt
- 1/2 lb. of sugar
- 4 oz. of powdered saltpetre

## Directions

1. Rub in, and sprinkle either of the above mixtures on 14 lbs. of meat
2. Keep it in an earthenware pan, or a deep wooden tray, and turn twice a week during 3 weeks
3. Bind up the beef tightly with coarse linen tape, and hang it in a kitchen in which a fire is constantly kept, for 3 weeks

title: To Pickle Pork
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of saltpetre
- Salt

## Directions

1. Cut the pork into pieces of a suitable size as soon as the pig is cold
2. Rub the pieces of pork well with salt, and put them into a pan with a sprinkling of it between each piece
3. As it melts on the top, strew on more
4. Lay a coarse cloth over the pan, a board over that, and a weight on the board, to keep the pork down in the brine

title: To Preserve and Dry Greengages
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. fruit
- 1 lb. sugar
- 1/4 pint of water

## Directions

1. For this purpose, the fruit must be used before it is quite ripe, and part of the stalk must be left on.
2. Weigh the fruit, rejecting all that is in the least degree blemished, and put it into a lined saucepan with the sugar and water, which should have been previously boiled together to a rich syrup.
3. Boil the fruit in this for 10 minutes, remove it from the fire, and drain the greengages.
4. The next day, boil up the syrup and put in the fruit again, and let it simmer for 3 minutes, and drain the syrup away.
5. Continue this process for 5 or 6 days, and the last time place the greengages, when drained, on a hair sieve, and put them in an oven or warm spot to dry.
6. Keep them in a box, with paper between each layer, in a place free from damp.

title: To Preserve Apples in Quarters, in Imitation of Ginger
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- apples
- 3/4 lb. of sugar per lb. of apples
- 1-1/2 oz. of the best white ginger per lb. of apples
- 1 oz. of ginger per 1/2 pint of water

## Directions

1. Peel, core, and quarter the apples, and put the fruit, sugar, and ginger in layers into a wide-mouthed jar, and let them remain for 2 days.
2. Infuse 1 oz. of ginger in 1/2 pint of boiling water, and cover it closely, and let it remain for 1 day.
3. Put the apples, &c., into a preserving-pan with the water strained from the ginger, and boil till the apples look clear and the syrup is rich, which will be in about an hour.
4. The rind of a lemon may be added just before the apples have finished boiling; and great care must be taken not to break the pieces of apple in putting them into the jars.
5. Serve on glass dishes for dessert.

title: To Preserve Cherries in Syrup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 lbs. of cherries
- 3 lbs. of sugar
- 1 pint of white-currant juice

## Directions

1. Let the cherries be as clear and as transparent as possible, and perfectly ripe
2. Pick off the stalks, and remove the stones, damaging the fruit as little as you can
3. Make a syrup with the sugar by the proportions given
4. Mix the cherries with it, and boil them for about 15 minutes, carefully skimming them
5. Turn them gently into a pan, and let them remain till the next day
6. Drain the cherries on a sieve, and put the syrup and white-currant juice into the preserving-pan again
7. Boil these together until the syrup is somewhat reduced and rather thick
8. Put in the cherries, and let them boil for about 5 minutes
9. Take them off the fire, skim the syrup
10. Put the cherries into small pots or wide-mouthed bottles
11. Pour the syrup over, and when quite cold, tie them down carefully, so that the air is quite excluded

title: To Preserve Damsons, Or Any Kind Of Plums
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Damsons or plums
- Boiling water

## Directions

1. Pick the fruit into clean dry stone jars, taking care to leave out all that are broken or blemished
2. When full, pour boiling water on the plums, until it stands one inch above the fruit
3. Cut a piece of paper to fit the inside of the jar, over which pour melted mutton-suet
4. Cover down with brown paper, and keep the jars in a dry cool place
5. When used, the suet should be removed, the water poured off, and the jelly at the bottom of the jar used and mixed with the fruit

title: To Preserve Morello Cherries
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- cherries
- sugar
- water

## Directions

1. Select ripe cherries; pick off the stalks, and reject all that have any blemishes
2. Boil the sugar and water together for 5 minutes
3. Put in the cherries, and boil them for 10 minutes, removing the scum as it rises
4. Turn the fruit into a pan, and let it remain until the next day
5. Boil it all again for another 10 minutes, and, if necessary, skim well
6. Put the cherries into small pots; pour over them the syrup, and, when cold, cover down with oiled papers, and the tops of the jars with tissue-paper brushed over on both sides with the white of an egg, and keep in a dry place

title: To Preserve Mushrooms
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 oz. of butter per quart of mushrooms
- pepper and salt to taste
- juice of 1 lemon
- clarified butter

## Directions

1. Peel the mushrooms, put them into cold water, with a little lemon-juice
2. Take them out and dry them very carefully in a cloth
3. Put the butter into a stewpan capable of holding the mushrooms
4. When it is melted, add the mushrooms, lemon-juice, and a seasoning of pepper and salt
5. Draw them down over a slow fire, and let them remain until their liquor is boiled away, and they have become quite dry, but be careful in not allowing them to stick to the bottom of the stewpan
6. When done, put them into pots, and pour over the top clarified butter
7. To re-warm them, put the mushrooms into a stewpan, strain the butter from them, and they will be ready for use

title: To Preserve Oranges
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Oranges
- 2 lbs. of loaf sugar to every lb. of juice and pulp
- 1/2 lb. of loaf sugar to every pint of water

## Directions

1. Wholly grate or peel the oranges, taking off only the thin outside portion of the rind
2. Make a small incision where the stalk is taken out, squeeze out as much of the juice as can be obtained, and preserve it in a basin with the pulp that accompanies it
3. Put the oranges into cold water; let them stand for 3 days, changing the water twice
4. Boil them in fresh water till they are very tender, and put them to drain
5. Make a syrup with the above proportion of sugar and water, sufficient to cover the oranges; let them stand in it for 2 or 3 days; then drain them well
6. Weigh the juice and pulp, allow double their weight of sugar, and boil them together until the scum ceases to rise, which must all be carefully removed
7. Put in the oranges, boil them for 10 minutes, place them in jars, pour over them the syrup, and, when cold, cover down

title: To Preserve Parsley Through the Winter
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- freshly-gathered parsley
- boiling water, slightly salted

## Directions

1. Use freshly-gathered parsley for keeping, and wash it perfectly free from grit and dirt
2. Put it into boiling water which has been slightly salted and well skimmed, and then let it boil for 2 or 3 minutes
3. Take it out, let it drain, and lay it on a sieve in front of the fire, when it should be dried as expeditiously as possible
4. Store it away in a very dry place in bottles, and when wanted for use, pour over it a little warm water, and let it stand for about 5 minutes

title: To Preserve Plums Dry
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- plums
- sugar
- 1/4 pint of water to every lb. of sugar

## Directions

1. Gather the plums when they are full-grown and just turning colour; prick them, put them into a saucepan of cold water, and set them on the fire until the water is on the point of boiling.
2. Then take them out, drain them, and boil them gently in syrup made with the above proportion of sugar and water; and if the plums shrink, and will not take the sugar, prick them as they lie in the pan; give them another boil, skim, and set them by.
3. The next day add some more sugar, boiled almost to candy, to the fruit and syrup; put all together into a wide-mouthed jar, and place them in a cool oven for 2 nights; then drain the plums from the syrup, sprinkle a little powdered sugar over, and dry them in a cool oven.

title: To Preserve Strawberries Whole
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of strawberries
- 1-1/2 lb. of good loaf sugar
- 1 pint of red-currant juice

## Directions

1. Choose the strawberries not too ripe, of a fine large sort and of a good colour
2. Pick off the stalks, lay the strawberries in a dish, and sprinkle over them half the quantity of sugar, which must be finely pounded
3. Shake the dish gently, that the sugar may be equally distributed and touch the under-side of the fruit, and let it remain for 1 day
4. Have ready the currant-juice, drawn as for red-currant jelly; boil it with the remainder of the sugar until it forms a thin syrup
5. In this syrup simmer the strawberries and sugar, until the whole is sufficiently jellied
6. Take great care not to stir the fruit roughly, as it should be preserved as whole as possible

title: To Promote the Growth of Hair
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Equal quantities of olive-oil and spirit of rosemary
- a few drops of oil of nutmeg

## Directions

1. Mix the ingredients together
2. Rub the roots of the hair every night with a little of this liniment, and the growth of it will very soon sensibly increase

title: To Ragout A Duck Whole
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 large duck
- pepper and salt to taste
- good beef gravy
- 2 onions sliced
- 4 sage-leaves
- a few leaves of lemon thyme
- thickening of butter and flour
- about 1-1/2 pint of young green peas (when in season)

## Directions

1. After having emptied and singed the duck, season it inside with pepper and salt, and truss it.
2. Roast it before a clear fire for about 20 minutes, and let it acquire a nice brown colour.
3. Put it into a stewpan with sufficient well-seasoned beef gravy to cover it.
4. Slice and fry the onions, and add these, with the sage-leaves and lemon thyme, both of which should be finely minced, to the stock.
5. Simmer gently until the duck is tender.
6. Strain, skim, and thicken the gravy with a little butter and flour.
7. Boil it up, pour over the duck, and serve.
8. When in season, about 1-1/2 pint of young green peas, boiled separately, and put in the ragoût, very much improve this dish.

title: To Ragout a Knuckle of Veal
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Knuckle of veal
- pepper and salt to taste
- flour
- 1 onion
- 1 head of celery, or a little celery-seed
- a faggot of savoury herbs
- 2 blades of pounded mace
- butter and flour for thickening
- a few young carrots
- 1 tablespoonful of ketchup
- 1 tablespoonful of tomato sauce
- 3 tablespoonfuls of sherry
- the juice of 1/4 lemon
- hot water

## Directions

1. Cut the meat from a knuckle of veal into neat slices, season with pepper and salt, and dredge them with flour
2. Fry them in a little butter of a pale brown, and put them into a stewpan with the bone (which should be chopped in several places)
3. Add the celery, herbs, mace, and carrots
4. Pour over all about 1 pint of hot water, and let it simmer very gently for 2 hours, over a slow but clear fire
5. Take out the slices of meat and carrots, strain and thicken the gravy with a little butter rolled in flour
6. Add the remaining ingredients, give one boil, put back the meat and carrots, let these get hot through, and serve

title: To Restore Whiteness to Scorched Linen
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of vinegar
- 2 oz. of fuller's-earth
- 1 oz. of dried fowls' dung
- 1/2 oz. of soap
- the juice of 2 large onions

## Directions

1. Boil all these ingredients together to the consistency of paste
2. Spread the composition thickly over the damaged part
3. Allow it to dry on
4. Wash the place once or twice, and every trace of scorching will disappear

title: To Roast Coffee
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 lbs. of coffee
- a piece of butter the size of a nut
- a dessert-spoonful of powdered sugar

## Directions

1. Before roasting, add to every 3 lbs. of coffee a piece of butter the size of a nut and a dessert-spoonful of powdered sugar
2. Roast in the usual manner
3. The addition of the butter and sugar develops the flavour and aroma of the berry; the quality of the butter must be of the very best description

title: To Salt Beef
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 round of beef
- 4 oz. of sugar
- 1 oz. of powdered saltpetre
- 2 oz. of black pepper
- 1/4 lb. of bay-salt
- 1/2 lb. of common salt

## Directions

1. Rub the meat well with salt, and let it remain for a day, to disgorge and clear it from slime.
2. The next day, rub it well with the above ingredients on every side, and let it remain in the pickle for about a fortnight, turning it every day.
3. It may be boiled fresh from the pickle, or smoked.

title: To Salt Two Hams
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of treacle
- 1/2 lb. of saltpetre
- 1 lb. of bay-salt
- 2 pounds of common salt

## Directions

1. Two days before they are put into pickle, rub the hams well with salt, to draw away all slime and blood.
2. Throw what comes from them away, and then rub them with treacle, saltpetre, and salt.
3. Lay them in a deep pan, and let them remain one day.
4. Boil the above proportion of treacle, saltpetre, bay-salt, and common salt for 1/4 hour, and pour this pickle boiling hot over the hams: there should be sufficient of it to cover them.
5. For a day or two rub them well with it; afterwards they will only require turning.
6. They ought to remain in this pickle for 3 weeks or a month, and then be sent to be smoked, which will take nearly or quite a month to do.

title: To Smoke Hams and Fish at Home
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- old hogshead
- cross-stick
- iron pan
- sawdust
- small pieces of green wood
- red-hot iron

## Directions
