1. Take an old hogshead, stop up all the crevices, and fix a place to put a cross-stick near the bottom, to hang the articles to be smoked on.
2. In the side, cut a hole near the top, to introduce an iron pan filled with sawdust and small pieces of green wood.
3. Turn the tub upside down, hang the articles upon the cross-stick, introduce the iron pan in the opening, and place a piece of red-hot iron in the pan.
4. Cover it with sawdust.
5. Let a large ham remain 40 hours, and keep up a good smoke.

title: To Steam Potatoes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Potatoes
- Boiling water

## Directions

1. Pare the potatoes, throw them into cold water as they are peeled.
2. Put them into a steamer.
3. Place the steamer over a saucepan of boiling water, and steam the potatoes from 20 to 40 minutes, according to the size and sort.
4. When a fork goes easily through them, they are done.
5. Take them up, dish, and serve very quickly.

title: To Toast Tea-Cakes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- tea-cakes
- butter

## Directions

1. Cut each tea-cake into three or four slices, according to its thickness
2. Toast them on both sides before a nice clear fire
3. As each slice is done, spread it with butter on both sides
4. When a cake is toasted, pile the slices one on the top of the other
5. Cut them into quarters, put them on a very hot plate
6. Send the cakes immediately to table
7. As they are wanted, send them in hot, one or two at a time

title: Toad-in-the-Hole
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 lb. of rump-steak
- 1 sheep's kidney
- pepper and salt to taste
- 3 eggs
- 1 pint of milk
- 4 tablespoonfuls of flour
- 1/2 saltspoonful of salt

## Directions

1. Cut up the steak and kidney into convenient-sized pieces, and put them into a pie-dish, with a good seasoning of salt and pepper
2. Mix the flour with a small quantity of milk at first, to prevent its being lumpy
3. Add the remainder of the milk and the 3 eggs, which should be well beaten
4. Put in the salt, stir the batter for about 5 minutes, and pour it over the steak
5. Place it in a tolerably brisk oven immediately, and bake for 1-1/2 hour

title: Toad-in-the-Hole (Cold Meat Cookery)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of flour
- 1 pint of milk
- 3 eggs
- butter
- a few slices of cold mutton
- pepper and salt to taste
- 2 kidneys

## Directions

1. Make a smooth batter of flour, milk, and eggs in the above proportion
2. butter a baking-dish, and pour in the batter
3. Into this place a few slices of cold mutton, previously well seasoned, and the kidneys, which should be cut into rather small pieces
4. bake about 1 hour, or rather longer, and send it to table in the dish it was baked in
5. Oysters or mushrooms may be substituted for the kidneys, and will be found exceedingly good

title: Toast Sandwiches
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Thin cold toast
- Thin slices of bread-and-butter
- pepper and salt to taste

## Directions

1. Place a very thin piece of cold toast between 2 slices of thin bread-and-butter in the form of a sandwich
2. Add a seasoning of pepper and salt
3. This sandwich may be varied by adding a little pulled meat, or very fine slices of cold meat, to the toast

title: Toasted Cheese, or Scotch Rare-Bit
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A few slices of rich cheese
- toast
- mustard
- pepper

## Directions

1. Cut some nice rich sound cheese into rather thin slices; melt it in a cheese-toaster on a hot plate, or over steam.
2. When melted, add a small quantity of mixed mustard and a seasoning of pepper; stir the cheese until it is completely dissolved.
3. Brown it before the fire, or with a salamander.
4. Fill the bottom of the cheese-toaster with hot water, and serve with dry or buttered toasts, whichever may be preferred.
5. If the cheese be not very rich, a few pieces of butter may be mixed with it to great advantage.
6. Serve the mixture very hot, and very quickly send to table.

title: Toasted Cheese, or Welsh Rare-Bit
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Slices of bread
- butter
- Cheshire or Gloucester cheese
- mustard
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Cut the bread into slices about 1/2 inch in thickness; pare off the crust, toast the bread slightly without hardening or burning it, and spread it with butter.
2. Cut some slices, not quite so large as the bread, from a good rich fat cheese; lay them on the toasted bread in a cheese-toaster.
3. Be careful that the cheese does not burn, and let it be equally melted.
4. Spread over the top a little made mustard and a seasoning of pepper, and serve very hot, with very hot plates.
5. To facilitate the melting of the cheese, it may be cut into thin flakes or toasted on one side before it is laid on the bread.
6. Should the cheese be dry, a little butter mixed with it will be an improvement.

title: Tomato Sauce for Keeping (Excellent) I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- To every quart of tomato-pulp: 1 pint of cayenne vinegar
- 3/4 oz. of shalots
- 3/4 oz. of garlic, peeled and cut in slices
- salt to taste
- To every six quarts of liquor: 1 pint of soy
- 1 pint of anchovy sauce

## Directions

1. Gather the tomatoes quite ripe
2. Bake them in a slow oven till tender
3. Rub them through a sieve
4. To every quart of pulp add cayenne vinegar, shalots, garlic, and salt in the above proportion
5. Boil the whole together till the garlic and shalots are quite soft
6. Rub it through a sieve
7. Put it again into a saucepan
8. To every six quarts of the liquor, add 1 pint of soy and the same quantity of anchovy sauce
9. Boil altogether for about 20 minutes
10. Bottle off for use and carefully seal or rosin the corks

title: Tomato Sauce for Keeping (Excellent) II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 dozen tomatoes
- 2 teaspoonfuls of the best powdered ginger
- 1 dessertspoonful of salt
- 1 head of garlic chopped fine
- 2 tablespoonfuls of vinegar
- 1 dessertspoonful of Chili vinegar

## Directions

1. Choose ripe tomatoes
2. Put them into a stone jar and stand them in a cool oven until quite tender
3. When cold, take the skins and stalks from them
4. Mix the pulp with the liquor which is in the jar, but do not strain it
5. Add all the other ingredients and mix well together
6. Put it into well-sealed bottles
7. Store away in a cool dry place

title: Tomato Sauce for Keeping (Excellent) III
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 dozen tomatoes
- To every pound of tomato-pulp: 1 pint of Chili vinegar
- 1 oz. of garlic
- 1 oz. of shalot
- 2 oz. of salt
- 1 large green capsicum
- 1/2 teaspoonful of cayenne
- 2 pickled gherkins
- 6 pickled onions
- 1 pint of common vinegar
- the juice of 6 lemons

## Directions

1. Choose the tomatoes when quite ripe and red
2. Put them in a jar with a cover to it and bake them till tender
3. The better way is to put them in the oven overnight when it will not be too hot
4. Examine them in the morning to see if they are tender
5. Do not allow them to remain in the oven long enough to break them
6. They should be sufficiently soft to skin nicely and rub through the sieve
7. Measure the pulp
8. To each pound of pulp add the above proportion of vinegar and other ingredients
9. Chop very fine the garlic, shalot, capsicum, onion, and gherkins
10. Boil the whole together till everything is tender
11. Rub it through a sieve again and add the lemon-juice
12. Boil the whole again till it becomes as thick as cream, keeping it continually stirred
13. Bottle it when quite cold, cork well, and seal the corks

title: Tomato Sauce--Hot, to Serve with Cutlets, Roast Meats, &c.
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 tomatoes
- 2 shalots
- 1 clove
- 1 blade of mace
- salt and cayenne to taste
- 1/4 pint of gravy or stock

## Directions

1. Cut the tomatoes in two and squeeze the juice and seeds out
2. Put them in a stewpan with all the ingredients
3. Let them simmer gently until the tomatoes are tender enough to pulp
4. Rub the whole through a sieve
5. Boil it for a few minutes and serve

title: Truffles a l'Italienne
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 10 truffles
- 1 tablespoonful of minced parsley
- 1 minced shalot
- salt and pepper to taste
- 2 oz. of butter
- 2 tablespoonfuls of good brown gravy
- the juice of 1/2 lemon
- cayenne to taste

## Directions

1. Wash the truffles and cut them into slices about the size of a penny-piece; put them into a sauté pan, with the parsley, shalot, salt, pepper, and 1 oz. of butter; stir them over the fire, that they may all be equally done, which will be in about 10 minutes, and drain off some of the butter.
2. Then add a little more fresh butter, 2 tablespoonfuls of good gravy, the juice of 1/2 lemon, and a little cayenne; stir over the fire until the whole is on the point of boiling, when serve.

title: Truffles au Naturel
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Truffles
- buttered paper

## Directions

1. Select some fine truffles
2. Cleanse them, by washing them in several waters with a brush, until not a particle of sand or grit remains on them
3. Wrap each truffle in buttered paper
4. Bake in a hot oven for quite an hour
5. Take off the paper, wipe the truffles, and serve them in a hot napkin

title: Turbot a la Creme
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Remains of cold turbot
- 2 oz. of butter
- 4 tablespoonfuls of cream
- Salt
- Cayenne
- Pounded mace

## Directions

1. Clear away all skin and bone from the flesh of the turbot
2. Cut the flesh into nice square pieces, as equally as possible
3. Put into a stewpan the butter, let it melt, and add the cream and seasoning
4. Let it just simmer for one minute, but not boil
5. Lay in the fish to warm, and serve it garnished with croutons or a paste border

title: Turbot au Gratin
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Remains of cold turbot
- Béchamel
- Bread crumbs
- Butter
- 4 or 5 tablespoonfuls of béchamel

## Directions

1. Cut the flesh of the turbot into small dice, carefully freeing it from all skin and bone
2. Put them into a stewpan, and moisten with 4 or 5 tablespoonfuls of béchamel
3. Let it get thoroughly hot, but do not allow it to boil
4. Spread the mixture on a dish, cover with finely-grated bread crumbs, and place small pieces of butter over the top
5. Brown it in the oven, or with a salamander

title: Turkey Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 quarts of medium stock
- remains of a cold roast turkey
- 2 oz. of rice-flour or arrowroot
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 tablespoonful of Harvey's sauce or mushroom ketchup

## Directions

1. Cut up the turkey in small pieces, and put it in the stock
2. let it simmer slowly until the bones are quite clean
3. Take the bones out, and work the soup through a sieve
4. when cool, skim well
5. Mix the rice-flour or arrowroot to a batter with a little of the soup
6. add it with the seasoning and sauce, or ketchup
7. Give one boil, and serve

title: Turnip Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 oz. of butter
- 9 good-sized turnips
- 4 onions
- 2 quarts of stock No. 106
- seasoning to taste

## Directions

1. Melt the butter in the stewpan, but do not let it boil
2. Wash, drain, and slice the turnips and onions very thin
3. Put them in the butter, with a teacupful of stock
4. Stew very gently for an hour
5. Add the remainder of the stock
6. Simmer another hour
7. Rub it through a tammy
8. Put it back into the stewpan, but do not let it boil
9. Serve very hot

title: Turnips in White Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 7 or 8 turnips
- 1 oz. of butter
- 1/2 pint of white sauce
- a little butter
- a small lump of sugar

## Directions

1. Peel and cut the turnips in the shape of pears or marbles
2. Boil them in salt and water, to which has been added a little butter, until tender
3. Take them out, drain, arrange them on a dish
4. Pour over the white sauce made by recipe No. 538 or 539, and to which has been added a small lump of sugar
5. When approved, a little mustard may be added to the sauce

title: Turtle Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- ham slices
- knuckles of veal
- turtle flesh and members
- sweet basil
- sweet marjoram
- lemon thyme
- winter savory
- bay-leaves
- common thyme
- parsley
- green onions
- large onion
- cloves
- flour
- butter
- Madeira
- sugar
- mace
- calf's udder
- egg yolks
- cayenne pepper
- salt
- spices
- bread crumbs
- milk
- eggs
- lemon juice

## Directions

1. Cut off the head of the turtle the preceding day
2. In the morning open the turtle by leaning heavily with a knife on the shell of the animal's back and cut this off all round
3. Turn it upright on its end that all the water may run out
4. Cut the flesh off along the spine with the knife sloping towards the bones to avoid touching the gall
5. Wash the flesh clean and let them drain
6. Have ready a large vessel full of boiling water and put the shells into it
7. When the shells come easily off take them out of the water and prick them all including those of the back belly fins and head
8. Boil the back and belly till the bones can be taken off without allowing the softer parts to be sufficiently done as they will be boiled again in the soup
9. Lay the slices of ham on the bottom of a very large stewpan over them the knuckles of veal then the inside flesh of the turtle and over the whole the members
10. Moisten with the water in which you are boiling the shell and draw it down thoroughly
11. Ascertain if it be thoroughly done by thrusting a knife into the fleshy part of the meat
12. When no blood appears moisten it again with the liquor in which the bones have been boiling
13. Put in a large bunch of sweet herbs--sweet basil sweet marjoram lemon thyme winter savory 2 or 3 bay-leaves common thyme a handful of parsley and green onions and a large onion stuck with 6 cloves
14. Let the whole be thoroughly done
15. Probe the members to see whether they are done and if so drain and send them to the larder
16. When the flesh is completely done strain it through a silk sieve
17. Make a very thin white roux with flour
18. Moisten the roux with the liquor keeping it over the fire till it boils
19. Ascertain that the sauce is neither too thick nor too thin
20. Draw the stewpan on the side of the stove to skim off the white scum and all the fat and oil that rise to the surface
21. Cut the softer parts to about the size of one or two inches square and throw into the soup
22. Let the soup simmer gently
23. Skim off all the fat and froth
24. Take all the leaves of the herbs from the stock and put these in a stewpan with about 1/4 lb. of fresh butter
25. Let it simmer on a slow fire till quite melted
26. Pour in 1 bottle of good Madeira adding a small bit of sugar and let it boil gently for 1 hour
27. Rub it through a tammy and add it to the soup
28. Let the soup boil till no white scum rises
29. Take with a skimmer all the bits of turtle out of the sauce and put them in a clean stewpan
30. Pour the soup over the bits of turtle through a tammy
31. Make quenelles à tortue by taking the fleshy part of a leg of veal about 1 lb. scraping off all the meat without leaving any sinews or fat
32. Soak in milk about the same quantity of crumbs of bread
33. Squeeze the bread and put it into a mortar with the veal a small quantity of calf's udder a little butter the yolks of 4 eggs boiled hard a little cayenne pepper salt and spices
34. Pound the whole very fine
35. Thicken the mixture with 2 whole eggs and the yolk of another
36. Try this farce or stuffing in boiling-hot water to ascertain its consistency
37. If it is too thin add the yolk of an egg
38. Take half of the farce and put into it some chopped parsley
39. Let the whole cool in order to roll it of the size of the yolk of an egg
40. Poach it in salt and boiling water and when very hard drain on a sieve and put it into the turtle
41. Before you send up squeeze the juice of 2 or 3 lemons with a little cayenne pepper and pour that into the soup
42. When either lemon-juice or cayenne pepper has been introduced no boiling must take place

title: Universal Pickle
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 quarts of vinegar
- 1 lb. of salt
- 1/4 lb. of ginger
- 1 oz. of mace
- 1/2 lb. of shalots
- 1 tablespoonful of cayenne
- 2 oz. of mustard-seed
- 1-1/2 oz. of turmeric
- radish-pods
- French beans
- cauliflowers
- gherkins

## Directions

1. Boil all the ingredients together for about 20 minutes
2. When cold, put them into a jar with whatever vegetables you choose, such as radish-pods, French beans, cauliflowers, gherkins, &c. &c., as these come into season
3. Put them in fresh as you gather them, having previously wiped them perfectly free from moisture and grit
4. This pickle will be fit for use in about 8 or 9 months

title: Useful Soup for Benevolent Purposes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- An ox-cheek
- any pieces of trimmings of beef, which may be bought very cheaply (say 4 lbs.)
- a few bones
- any pot-liquor the larder may furnish
- 1/4 peck of onions
- 6 leeks
- a large bunch of herbs
- 1/2 lb. of celery (the outside pieces, or green tops, do very well)
- 1/2 lb. of carrots
- 1/2 lb. of turnips
- 1/2 lb. of coarse brown sugar
- 1/2 a pint of beer
- 4 lbs. of common rice, or pearl barley
- 1/2 lb. of salt
- 1 oz. of black pepper
- a few raspings
- 10 gallons of water

## Directions

1. Cut up the meat in small pieces, break the bones, put them in a copper, with the 10 gallons of water, and stew for 1/2 an hour
2. Cut up the vegetables, put them in with the sugar and beer, and boil for 4 hours
3. Two hours before the soup is wanted, add the rice and raspings, and keep stirring till it is well mixed in the soup, which simmer gently
4. If the liquor reduces too much, fill up with water

title: Vanilla Cream
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of milk
- the yolks of 8 eggs
- 6 oz. of sugar
- 1 oz. of isinglass
- essence of vanilla to taste

## Directions

1. Put the milk and sugar into a saucepan, and let it get hot over a slow fire.
2. Beat up the yolks of the eggs, to which add gradually the sweetened milk.
3. Flavour the whole with essence of vanilla, put the mixture into a jug, and place this jug in a saucepan of boiling water.
4. Stir the contents with a wooden spoon one way until the mixture thickens, but do not allow it to boil, or it will be full of lumps.
5. Take it off the fire; stir in the isinglass, which should be previously dissolved in about 1/4 pint of water, and boiled for 2 or 3 minutes.
6. Pour the cream into an oiled mould, put it in a cool place to set, and turn it out carefully on a dish.
7. Instead of using the essence of vanilla, a pod may be boiled in the milk instead, until the flavour is well extracted. A pod, or a pod and a half, will be found sufficient for the above proportion of ingredients.

title: Vanilla Custard Sauce, to serve with Puddings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of milk
- 2 eggs
- 2 oz. of sugar
- 10 drops of essence of vanilla

## Directions

1. Beat the eggs, sweeten the milk; stir these ingredients well together, and flavour them with essence of vanilla, regulating the proportion of this latter ingredient by the strength of the essence, the size of the eggs, &c.
2. Put the mixture into a small jug, place this jug in a saucepan of boiling water, and stir the sauce one way until it thickens; but do not allow it to boil, or it will instantly curdle.
3. Serve in a boat or tureen separately, with plum, bread, or any kind of dry pudding. Essence of bitter almonds or lemon-rind may be substituted for the vanilla, when they are more in accordance with the flavouring of the pudding with which the sauce is intended to be served.

title: Veal a la Bourgeoise
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 to 3 lbs. of the loin or neck of veal
- 10 or 12 young carrots
- a bunch of green onions
- 2 slices of lean bacon
- 2 blades of pounded mace
- 1 bunch of savoury herbs
- pepper and salt to taste
- a few new potatoes
- 1 pint of green peas
- butter
- tomato sauce and ketchup

## Directions

1. Cut the veal into cutlets and trim them
2. Put the trimmings into a stewpan with a little butter
3. Lay in the cutlets and fry them a nice brown colour on both sides
4. Add the bacon, carrots, onions, spice, herbs, and seasoning
5. Pour in about a pint of boiling water
6. Stew gently for 2 hours on a very slow fire
7. When done, skim off the fat
8. Take out the herbs
9. Flavour the gravy with a little tomato sauce and ketchup
10. Have ready the peas and potatoes, boiled separately
11. Put them with the veal and serve

title: Veal and Bacon Cakes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Equal quantities of fat bacon and lean veal
- 1 teaspoonful of minced sage per lb. of meat
- Salt and pepper to taste

## Directions

1. Chop the meat and bacon finely
2. To every lb. allow minced sage
3. Add a seasoning of pepper and salt
4. Mix the whole well together
5. Make it into flat cakes
6. Fry a nice brown

title: Veal and Ham Pie
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of veal cutlets
- 1/2 lb. of boiled ham
- 2 tablespoonfuls of minced savoury herbs
- 1/4 teaspoonful of grated nutmeg
- 2 blades of pounded mace
- pepper and salt to taste
- a strip of lemon-peel finely minced
- the yolks of 2 hard-boiled eggs
- 1/2 pint of water
- nearly 1/2 pint of good strong gravy
- puff-crust
- egg yolk

## Directions

1. Cut the veal into nice square pieces, and put a layer of them at the bottom of a pie-dish
2. Sprinkle over these a portion of the herbs, spices, seasoning, lemon-peel, and the yolks of the eggs cut in slices
3. Cut the ham very thin, and put a layer of this in
4. Proceed in this manner until the dish is full, so arranging it that the ham comes at the top
5. Lay a puff-paste on the edge of the dish, and pour in about 1/2 pint of water
6. Cover with crust, ornament it with leaves, brush it over with the yolk of an egg, and bake in a well-heated oven for 1 to 1-1/2 hour, or longer, should the pie be very large
7. When it is taken out of the oven, pour in at the top, through a funnel, nearly 1/2 pint of strong gravy: this should be made sufficiently good that, when cold, it may cut in a firm jelly
8. This pie may be very much enriched by adding a few mushrooms, oysters, or sweetbreads; but it will be found very good without any of the last-named additions

title: Veal Cake (a Convenient Dish for a Picnic)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A few slices of cold roast veal
- a few slices of cold ham
- 2 hard-boiled eggs
- 2 tablespoonfuls of minced parsley
- a little pepper
- good gravy

## Directions

1. Cut off all the brown outside from the veal, and cut the eggs into slices.
2. Procure a pretty mould; lay veal, ham, eggs, and parsley in layers, with a little pepper between each.
3. When the mould is full, get some strong stock, and fill up the shape.
4. Bake for 1/2 hour, and when cold, turn it out.

title: Veal Collops (an Entree)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- About 2 lbs. of the prime part of the leg of veal
- a few slices of bacon
- forcemeat No. 417
- cayenne to taste
- egg and bread crumbs
- gravy
- butter
- flour
- 1/4 pint of water
- 2 tablespoonfuls of lemon-juice
- salt
- pepper
- pounded mace

## Directions

1. Cut the veal into long thin collops, flatten them, and lay on each a piece of thin bacon of the same size
2. Spread forcemeat over the bacon, sprinkle over all a little cayenne, roll them up tightly, and do not let them be more than 2 inches long
3. Skewer each one firmly, egg and bread crumb them, and fry them a nice brown in a little butter, turning them occasionally, and shaking the pan about
4. When done, place them on a dish before the fire
5. Put a small piece of butter in the pan, dredge in a little flour, add 1/4 pint of water, 2 tablespoonfuls of lemon-juice, a seasoning of salt, pepper, and pounded mace
6. Let the whole boil up, and pour it over the collops

title: Veal Cutlets
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- About 3 lbs. of the prime part of the leg of veal
- egg
- bread crumbs
- 3 tablespoonfuls of minced savoury herbs
- salt and pepper to taste
- a small piece of butter
- fresh lard or butter
- flour
- a piece of butter the size of a walnut
- boiling water
- lemon-juice
- broiled bacon
- forcemeat balls

## Directions

1. Have the veal cut into slices about 3/4 of an inch in thickness, and, if not cut perfectly even, level the meat with a cutlet-bat or rolling-pin.
2. Shape and trim the cutlets, and brush them over with egg.
3. Sprinkle with bread crumbs, with which have been mixed minced herbs and a seasoning of pepper and salt, and press the crumbs down.
4. Fry them of a delicate brown in fresh lard or butter, and be careful not to burn them.
5. They should be very thoroughly done, but not dry.
6. If the cutlets be thick, keep the pan covered for a few minutes at a good distance from the fire, after they have acquired a good colour: by this means, the meat will be done through.
7. Lay the cutlets in a dish, keep them hot, and make a gravy in the pan as follows: Dredge in a little flour, add a piece of butter the size of a walnut, brown it, then pour as much boiling water as is required over it, season with pepper and salt, add a little lemon-juice, give one boil, and pour it over the cutlets.
8. They should be garnished with slices of broiled bacon, and a few forcemeat balls will be found a very excellent addition to this dish.

title: Veal Cutlets a la Maintenon
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 or 3 lbs. of veal cutlets
- egg and bread crumbs
- 2 tablespoonfuls of minced savoury herbs
- salt and pepper to taste
- a little grated nutmeg
- buttered paper
- melted butter or a good gravy

## Directions

1. Cut the cutlets about 3/4 inch in thickness
2. Flatten them
3. Brush them over with the yolk of an egg
4. Dip them into bread crumbs and minced herbs
5. Season with pepper and salt and grated nutmeg
6. Fold each cutlet in a piece of buttered paper
7. Broil them
8. Send them to table with melted butter or a good gravy

title: Veal Gravy for White Sauces, Fricassees, &c.
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 slices of nicely flavoured lean ham
- any poultry trimmings
- 3 lbs. of lean veal
- a faggot of savoury herbs, including parsley
- a few green onions (or 1 large onion may be substituted for these)
- a few mushrooms, when obtainable
- 1 blade of mace
- salt to taste
- 3 pints of water

## Directions

1. Cut up the ham and veal into small square pieces, put these in a stewpan, moistening them with a small quantity of water
2. Place them over the fire to draw down
3. When the bottom of the stewpan becomes covered with a white glaze, fill up with water in the above proportion
4. Add the remaining ingredients, stew very slowly for 3 or 4 hours, and do not forget to skim well the moment it boils
5. Put it by, and, when cold, take off all the fat
6. This may be used for Béchamel, sauce tournée, and many other white sauces

title: Veal Olive Pie
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A few thin slices of cold fillet of veal
- A few thin slices of bacon
- forcemeat No. 417
- a cupful of gravy
- 4 tablespoonfuls of cream
- puff-crust
- shalot
- cayenne

## Directions

1. Cut thin slices from a fillet of veal, place on them thin slices of bacon, and over them a layer of forcemeat, made by recipe No. 417, with an additional seasoning of shalot and cayenne
2. Roll them tightly, and fill up a pie-dish with them
3. Add the gravy and cream
4. Cover with a puff-crust, and bake for 1 to 1-1/2 hour: should the pie be very large, allow 2 hours
5. The pieces of rolled veal should be about 3 inches in length, and about 3 inches round

title: Veal Pie
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. of veal cutlets
- 1 or 2 slices of lean bacon or ham
- pepper and salt to taste
- 2 tablespoonfuls of minced savoury herbs
- 2 blades of pounded mace
- crust
- 1 teacupful of gravy
- water
- egg yolk

## Directions

1. Cut the cutlets into square pieces, and season them with pepper, salt, and pounded mace
2. Put them in a pie-dish with the savoury herbs sprinkled over, and 1 or 2 slices of lean bacon or ham placed at the top: if possible, this should be previously cooked, as undressed bacon makes the veal red, and spoils its appearance
3. Pour in a little water
4. Cover with crust, ornament it in any way that is approved
5. Brush it over with the yolk of an egg, and bake in a well-heated oven for about 1-1/2 hour
6. Pour in a good gravy after baking, which is done by removing the top ornament, and replacing it after the gravy is added

title: Veal Quenelles
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Equal quantities of veal, panada (No. 420), and calf's udder (No. 421)
- 2 eggs
- pepper
- salt
- pounded mace, or grated nutmeg
- a little flour

## Directions

1. Take the fleshy part of veal, scrape it with a knife, till all the meat is separated from the sinews, allowing about 1/2 lb. for an entrée
2. Chop the meat, and pound it in a mortar till reduced to a paste
3. Roll it into a ball
4. Make another ball of panada (No. 420), the same size
5. Make another ball of udder (No. 421), taking care that these three balls be of the same size
6. Pound them altogether in a mortar for some time
7. Moisten with the eggs, whites and yolks, and continue pounding
8. Add a seasoning of pepper, spices, &c.
9. Mould it into balls, or whatever shape is intended
10. Roll them in flour
11. Poach in boiling water, to which a little salt should have been added
12. If the quenelles are not firm enough, add the yolk of another egg, but omit the white

title: Veal Rissoles
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- A few slices of cold roast veal
- a few slices of ham or bacon
- 1 tablespoonful of minced parsley
- 1 tablespoonful of minced savoury herbs
- 1 blade of pounded mace
- a very little grated nutmeg
- cayenne and salt to taste
- 2 eggs well beaten
- bread crumbs

## Directions

1. Mince the veal very finely with a little ham or bacon
2. add the parsley, herbs, spices, and seasoning
3. mix into a paste with an egg
4. form into balls or cones
5. brush these over with egg
6. sprinkle with bread crumbs
7. fry a rich brown
8. Serve with brown gravy, and garnish the dish with fried parsley

title: Veal Rolls
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- The remains of a cold fillet of veal
- egg and bread crumbs
- a few slices of fat bacon
- forcemeat No. 417

## Directions

1. Cut a few slices from a cold fillet of veal 1/2 inch thick
2. rub them over with egg
3. lay a thin slice of fat bacon over each piece of veal
4. brush these with the egg
5. spread the forcemeat thinly over this
6. roll up each piece tightly
7. egg and bread crumb them
8. fry them a rich brown
9. Serve with mushroom sauce or brown gravy

title: Veal Sausages
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Equal quantities of fat bacon and lean veal
- to every lb. of meat, allow 1 teaspoonful of minced sage
- salt and pepper to taste

## Directions

1. Chop the meat and bacon finely
2. to every lb. allow the above proportion of very finely-minced sage
3. add a seasoning of pepper and salt
4. mix the whole well together
5. make it into flat cakes
6. fry a nice brown

title: Vegetable Marrows in White Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 or 5 moderate-sized marrows
- 1/2 pint of white sauce

## Directions

1. Pare the marrows
2. Cut them in halves, and shape each half at the top in a point, leaving the bottom end flat for it to stand upright in the dish
3. Boil the marrows in salt and water until tender
4. Take them up very carefully, and arrange them on a hot dish
5. Have ready 1/2 pint of white sauce, made by recipe No. 539
6. Pour this over the marrows, and serve

title: Vegetable Soup I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 7 oz. of carrot
- 10 oz. of parsnip
- 10 oz. of potato, cut into thin slices
- 1-1/4 oz. of butter
- 5 teaspoonfuls of flour
- a teaspoonful of made mustard
- salt and pepper to taste
- the yolks of 2 eggs
- rather more than 2 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Boil the vegetables in the water 2-1/2 hours
2. Stir them often, and if the water boils away too quickly, add more, as there should be 2 quarts of soup when done
3. Mix up in a basin the butter and flour, mustard, salt, and pepper, with a teacupful of cold water
4. Stir in the soup
5. Boil 10 minutes
6. Have ready the yolks of the eggs in the tureen
7. Pour on, stir well, and serve

title: Vegetable Soup II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Equal quantities of onions, carrots, turnips
- 1/4 lb. of butter
- a crust of toasted bread
- 1 head of celery
- a faggot of herbs
- salt and pepper to taste
- 1 teaspoonful of powdered sugar
- 2 quarts of common stock or boiling water
- Allow 3/4 lb. of vegetables to 2 quarts of stock, No. 105

## Directions

1. Cut up the onions, carrots, and turnips
2. Wash and drain them well
3. Put them in the stewpan with the butter and powdered sugar
4. Toss the whole over a sharp fire for 10 minutes, but do not let them brown
5. Pour the stock or boiling water on them
6. Add the bread, celery, herbs, and seasoning
7. Stew for 3 hours
8. Skim well and strain it off
9. When ready to serve, add a little sliced carrot, celery, and turnip
10. Flavour with a spoonful of Harvey's sauce, or a little ketchup

title: Vegetable Soup III (Good and Cheap, made without Meat)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 potatoes
- 4 turnips, or 2 if very large
- 2 carrots
- 2 onions
- 2 mushrooms, if obtainable
- 1 head of celery
- 1 large slice of bread
- 1 small saltspoonful of salt
- 1/4 saltspoonful of ground black pepper
- 2 teaspoonfuls of Harvey's sauce
- 6 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Peel the vegetables, and cut them up into small pieces
2. Toast the bread rather brown
3. Put all into a stewpan with the water and seasoning
4. Simmer gently for 3 hours, or until all is reduced to a pulp
5. Pass it through a sieve in the same way as pea-soup

title: Vegetable-Marrow Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 young vegetable marrows, or more, if very small
- 1/2 pint of cream
- salt and white pepper to taste
- 2 quarts of white stock, No. 107

## Directions

1. Pare and slice the marrows
2. Put them in the stock boiling
3. When done almost to a mash, press them through a sieve
4. At the moment of serving, add the boiling cream and seasoning

title: Vermicelli Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 oz. of vermicelli
- 1-1/2 pint of milk
- 1/2 pint of cream
- 3 oz. of butter
- 3 oz. of sugar
- 4 eggs

## Directions

1. Boil the vermicelli in the milk until it is tender.
2. Stir in the remaining ingredients, omitting the cream, if not obtainable.
3. Flavour the mixture with grated lemon-rind, essence of bitter almonds, or vanilla.
4. Butter a pie-dish and line the edges with puff-paste.
5. Put in the pudding, and bake in a moderate oven for about 3/4 hour.

title: Vermicelli Soup I
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 lb. of bacon, stuck with cloves
- 1/2 oz. of butter, worked up in flour
- 1 small fowl, trussed for boiling
- 2 oz. of vermicelli
- 2 quarts of white stock, No. 107

## Directions

1. Put the stock, bacon, butter, and fowl into the stewpan, and stew for 3/4 of an hour
2. Take the vermicelli, add it to a little of the stock, and set it on the fire, till it is quite tender
3. When the soup is ready, take out the fowl and bacon, and put the bacon on a dish
4. Skim the soup as clean as possible; pour it, with the vermicelli, over the fowl
5. Cut some bread thin, put in the soup, and serve

title: Vermicelli Soup II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of vermicelli
- 2 quarts of clear gravy stock, No. 169

## Directions

1. Put the vermicelli in the soup, boiling
2. Simmer very gently for 1/2 an hour, and stir frequently

title: Very Good Puff-Paste
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. flour
- 1 lb. butter
- not quite 1/2 pint water

## Directions

1. Carefully weigh the flour and butter, and have the exact proportion
2. Squeeze the butter well, to extract the water from it, and afterwards wring it in a clean cloth, that no moisture may remain
3. Sift the flour; see that it is perfectly dry
4. Work the whole into a smooth paste, with not quite 1/2 pint of water, using a knife to mix it with
5. Roll it out until it is of an equal thickness of about an inch
6. Break 4 oz. of the butter into small pieces; place these on the paste, sift over it a little flour, fold it over, roll out again, and put another 4 oz. of butter
7. Repeat the rolling and buttering until the paste has been rolled out 4 times, or equal quantities of flour and butter have been used
8. Every time the paste is rolled out, dredge a little flour over that and the rolling-pin, to prevent both from sticking
9. Handle the paste as lightly as possible, and do not press heavily upon it with the rolling-pin
10. Brush the paste as often as rolled out, and the pieces of butter placed thereon, with the white of an egg, to assist it to rise in leaves or flakes
11. Put it into the oven when it is sufficiently hot to raise the paste

title: Very Good Short Crust for Fruit Tarts
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of flour
- 3/4 lb. of butter
- 1 tablespoonful of sifted sugar
- 1/3 pint of water

## Directions

1. Rub the butter into the flour, after having ascertained that the latter is perfectly dry
2. Add the sugar, and mix the whole into a stiff paste, with about 1/3 pint of water
3. Roll it out two or three times, folding the paste over each time, and it will be ready for use

title: Very Plain Bread Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Odd pieces of crust or crumb of bread
- 1/2 teaspoonful of salt per quart
- 1 teaspoonful of grated nutmeg per quart
- 3 oz. of moist sugar per quart
- 1/2 lb. of currants per quart
- 1-1/4 oz. of butter per quart

## Directions

1. Break the bread into small pieces, and pour on them as much boiling water as will soak them well
2. Let these stand till the water is cool
3. then press it out, and mash the bread with a fork until it is quite free from lumps
4. Measure this pulp, and to every quart stir in salt, nutmeg, sugar, and currants in the above proportion
5. mix all well together, and put it into a well-buttered pie-dish
6. Smooth the surface with the back of a spoon, and place the butter in small pieces over the top
7. bake in a moderate oven for 1-1/2 hour, and serve very hot

title: Vicarage Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of flour
- 1/4 lb. of chopped suet
- 1/4 lb. of currants
- 1/4 lb. of raisins
- 1 tablespoonful of moist sugar
- 1/2 teaspoonful of ground ginger
- 1/2 saltspoonful of salt

## Directions

1. Put all the ingredients into a basin, having previously stoned the raisins, and washed, picked, and dried the currants.
2. Mix well with a clean knife.
3. Dip the pudding-cloth into boiling water, wring it out, and put in the mixture.
4. Have ready a saucepan of boiling water, plunge in the pudding, and boil for 3 hours.
5. Turn it out on the dish, and serve with sifted sugar.

title: Victoria Buns
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. of pounded loaf sugar
- 1 egg
- 1-1/2 oz. of ground rice
- 2 oz. of butter
- 1-1/2 oz. of currants
- a few thin slices of candied peel
- flour

## Directions

1. Whisk the egg, stir in the sugar, and beat these ingredients well together
2. Beat the butter to a cream
3. Stir in the ground rice, currants, and candied peel, and as much flour as will make it of such a consistency that it may be rolled into 7 or 8 balls
4. Put these on to a buttered tin
5. Bake them from 1/2 to 3/4 hour
6. Put them into the oven immediately, or they will become heavy

title: Victoria Sandwiches
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 eggs
- pounded sugar equal in weight to the eggs
- butter equal in weight to the eggs
- flour equal in weight to the eggs
- 1/4 saltspoonful of salt
- layer of jam or marmalade

## Directions

1. Beat the butter to a cream
2. Dredge in the flour and pounded sugar
3. Stir these ingredients well together
4. Add the eggs, which should be previously thoroughly whisked
5. Beat the mixture well for about 10 minutes
6. Butter a Yorkshire-pudding tin and pour in the batter
7. Bake it in a moderate oven for 20 minutes
8. Let it cool
9. Spread one half of the cake with a layer of nice preserve
10. Place the other half of the cake over it
11. Press the pieces slightly together
12. Cut it into long finger-pieces
13. Pile them in crossbars on a glass dish and serve

title: Vol-au-Vent (an Entree)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 to 1 lb. of puff-paste No. 1208
- fricasseed chickens, rabbits, ragouts, or the remains of cold fish, flaked and warmed in thick white sauce

## Directions

1. Make from 3/4 to 1 lb. of puff-paste by recipe No. 1208, taking care that it is very evenly rolled out each time, to insure its rising properly.
2. Roll out the paste to the thickness of about 1-1/2 inch, and, with a fluted cutter, stamp it out to the desired shape, either round or oval.
3. With the point of a small knife, make a slight incision in the paste all round the top, about an inch from the edge, which, when baked, forms the lid.
4. Put the vol-au-vent into a good brisk oven, and keep the door shut for a few minutes after it is put in.
5. When of a nice colour, without being scorched, withdraw it from the oven, instantly remove the cover where it was marked, and detach all the soft crumb from the centre.
6. Be careful not to break the edges of the vol-au-vent; but should they look thin in places, stop them with small flakes of the inside paste, stuck on with the white of an egg.
7. Fill the vol-au-vent with a rich mince, or fricassee, or ragoût, or the remains of cold fish flaked and warmed in a good white sauce.
8. Do not make them very liquid, for fear of the gravy bursting the crust.
9. Replace the lid, and serve.
10. To improve the appearance of the crust, brush it over with the yolk of an egg after it has risen properly.

title: Vol-au-Vent of Fresh Strawberries with Whipped Cream
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 lb. of puff-paste No. 1208
- 1 pint of freshly-gathered strawberries
- sugar to taste
- a plateful of whipped cream

## Directions

1. Make a vol-au-vent case by recipe No. 1379, only not quite so large nor so high as for a savoury one.
2. When nearly done, brush the paste over with the white of an egg, then sprinkle on it some pounded sugar, and put it back in the oven to set the glaze.
3. Remove the interior, or soft crumb, and, at the moment of serving, fill it with the strawberries, which should be picked, and broken up with sufficient sugar to sweeten them nicely.
4. Place a few spoonfuls of whipped cream on the top, and serve.

title: Walnut Ketchup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 100 walnuts
- 1 handful of salt
- 1 quart of vinegar
- 1/4 oz. of mace
- 1/4 oz. of nutmeg
- 1/4 oz. of cloves
- 1/4 oz. of ginger
- 1/4 oz. of whole black pepper
- a small piece of horseradish
- 20 shalots
- 1/4 lb. of anchovies
- 1 pint of port wine

## Directions

1. Procure the walnuts at the time you can run a pin through them, slightly bruise, and put them into a jar with the salt and vinegar
2. Let them stand 8 days, stirring every day
3. Then drain the liquor from them, and boil it, with the above ingredients, for about 1/2 hour
4. It may be strained or not, as preferred, and, if required, a little more vinegar or wine can be added, according to taste
5. When bottled well, seal the corks

title: Walnut Ketchup II
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 sieve of walnut-shells
- 2 quarts of water
- salt
- 1/2 lb. of shalots
- 1 oz. of cloves
- 1 oz. of mace
- 1 oz. of whole pepper
- 1 oz. of garlic

## Directions

1. Put the walnut-shells into a pan, with the water, and a large quantity of salt
2. Let them stand for 10 days, then break the shells up in the water, and let it drain through a sieve, putting a heavy weight on the top to express the juice
3. Place it on the fire, and remove all scum that may arise
4. Now boil the liquor with the shalots, cloves, mace, pepper, and garlic, and let all simmer till the shalots sink
5. Put the liquor into a pan, and, when cold, bottle, and cork closely
6. It should stand 6 months before using: should it ferment during that time, it must be again boiled and skimmed

title: Water Souchy
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Perch, tench, soles, eels, or flounders
- water
- bunch of chopped parsley
- roots
- salt
- finely-chopped parsley
- roots cut into slices
- brown bread and butter

## Directions

1. Put some water into a stewpan with a bunch of chopped parsley, some roots, and sufficient salt to make it brackish
2. Let these simmer for 1 hour
3. Stew the fish in this water
4. When they are done, take them out to drain
5. Have ready some finely-chopped parsley, and a few roots cut into slices of about one inch thick and an inch in length
6. Put the fish in a tureen or deep dish, strain the liquor over them, and add the minced parsley and roots
7. Serve with brown bread and butter

title: Welsh Nectar
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of raisins
- 3 lemons
- 2 lbs. of loaf sugar
- 2 gallons of boiling water

## Directions

1. Cut the peel of the lemons very thin, pour upon it the boiling water
2. When cool, add the strained juice of the lemons, the sugar, and the raisins, stoned and chopped very fine
3. Let it stand 4 or 5 days, stirring it every day
4. Strain it through a jelly-bag, and bottle it for present use

title: West-Indian Pudding
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of cream
- 1/2 lb. of loaf-sugar
- 1/2 lb. of Savoy or sponge-cakes
- 8 eggs
- 3 oz. of preserved green ginger

## Directions

1. Crumble down the cakes, put them into a basin, and pour over them the cream, which should be previously sweetened and brought to the boiling-point
2. Cover the basin, well beat the eggs, and when the cream is soaked up, stir them in
3. Butter a mould, arrange the ginger round it, pour in the pudding carefully, and tie it down with a cloth
4. Steam or boil it slowly for 1-1/2 hour, and serve with the syrup from the ginger, which should be warmed, and poured over the pudding

title: Whipped Cream
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of cream
- 3 oz. of pounded sugar
- 1 glass of sherry or sweet white wine
- rind of 1/2 lemon
- white of 1 egg

## Directions

1. Rub the sugar on the lemon-rind and pound it in a mortar until quite fine
2. Beat up the white of the egg until quite stiff
3. Put the cream into a large bowl with the sugar, wine, and beaten egg
4. Whip it to a froth
5. As fast as the froth rises, take it off with a skimmer and put it on a sieve to drain in a cool place
6. Whip the cream in a cool place, and in summer, over ice if obtainable
7. Make this the day before it is wanted
8. Serve plain whipped cream on a glass dish and garnish with strips of angelica, pastry leaves, or pieces of bright-coloured jelly

title: Whipped Syllabubs
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of cream
- 1/4 pint of sherry
- 1/8 pint of brandy
- juice of 1/2 lemon
- grated nutmeg
- 3 oz. of pounded sugar
- whipped cream

## Directions

1. Mix all the ingredients together
2. Put the syllabub into glasses
3. Heap a little whipped cream made in the same manner on the top of them

title: Whiskey Cordial
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of ripe white currants
- the rind of 2 lemons
- 1/4 oz. of grated ginger
- 1 quart of whiskey
- 1 lb. of lump sugar

## Directions

1. Strip the currants from the stalks
2. Put them into a large jug
3. Add the lemon-rind, ginger, and whiskey
4. Cover the jug closely
5. Let it remain covered for 24 hours
6. Strain through a hair sieve
7. Add the lump sugar
8. Let it stand 12 hours longer
9. Bottle and cork well

title: White Gingerbread
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of flour
- 1/2 lb. of butter
- 1/2 lb. of loaf sugar
- the rind of 1 lemon
- 1 oz. of ground ginger
- 1 nutmeg grated
- 1/2 teaspoonful of carbonate of soda
- 1 gill of milk

## Directions

1. Rub the butter into the flour
2. Add the sugar, which should be finely pounded and sifted, and the minced lemon-rind, ginger, and nutmeg
3. Mix these well together
4. Make the milk just warm
5. Stir in the soda
6. Work the whole into a nice smooth paste
7. Roll it out
8. Cut it into cakes
9. Bake in a moderate oven from 15 to 20 minutes

title: White Mushroom Sauce, a More Simple Method
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of melted butter, made with milk
- 1/2 pint of button mushrooms
- 1 dessertspoonful of mushroom ketchup
- cayenne
- salt

## Directions

1. Make the melted butter by recipe No. 380, and add to it the mushrooms, which must be nicely cleaned, and free from grit, and the stalks cut off.
2. Let them simmer gently for about 10 minutes, or until they are quite tender.
3. Put in the seasoning and ketchup; let it just boil, when serve.

title: White Mushroom Sauce, to Serve with Boiled Fowls, Cutlets, &c.
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Rather more than 1/2 pint of button mushrooms
- lemon-juice
- water
- 1 oz. of butter
- 1/2 pint of Béchamel
- 1/4 teaspoonful of pounded sugar

## Directions

1. Turn the mushrooms white by putting them into lemon-juice and water, having previously cut off the stalks and wiped them perfectly free from grit.
2. Chop them, and put them in a stewpan with the butter.
3. When the mushrooms are softened, add the Béchamel, and simmer for about 5 minutes; should they, however, not be done enough, allow rather more time.
4. They should not boil longer than necessary, as they would then lose their colour and flavour.
5. Rub the whole through a tammy, and serve very hot.
6. After this, it should be warmed in a bain marie.

title: White Onion Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 9 large onions, or 12 middling-sized ones
- 1 pint of melted butter made with milk
- 1/2 teaspoonful of salt, or rather more
- salt (for soaking water)

## Directions

1. Peel the onions and put them into water to which a little salt has been added, to preserve their whiteness, and let them remain for 1/4 hour
2. Then put them in a stewpan, cover them with water, and let them boil until tender; if the onions should be very strong, change the water after they have been boiling for 1/4 hour
3. Drain them thoroughly, chop them, and rub them through a tammy or sieve
4. Make 1 pint of melted butter, and when that boils, put in the onions, with a seasoning of salt
5. Stir it till it simmers, when it will be ready to serve

title: White or Green Gooseberry Jam
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- white or green gooseberries
- sugar (equal weight to fruit)
- water (1/2 pint per lb. of sugar)
- oiled paper
- tissue-paper
- unbeaten white of an egg

## Directions

1. Select the gooseberries not very ripe, either white or green, and top and tail them
2. Boil the sugar with water (allowing 1/2 pint to every lb.) for about 1/4 hour, carefully removing the scum as it rises
3. Put in the gooseberries, and simmer gently till clear and firm
4. Try a little of the jam on a plate; if it jellies when cold, it is done
5. Pour into pots. When cold, cover with oiled paper, and tissue-paper brushed over on both sides with the unbeaten white of an egg
6. Store away in a dry place

title: White Roux, for Thickening White Sauces
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. of butter
- 9 oz. of flour

## Directions

1. Allow the same proportions of butter and flour as in the brown roux recipe
2. Proceed in the same manner as for brown roux, but do not keep it on the fire too long
3. Take care not to let it colour
4. Pour it into a jar to use when wanted

title: White Sauce
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of white stock
- 1/2 pint of cream
- 1 dessertspoonful of flour
- salt to taste

## Directions

1. Have ready a delicately-clean saucepan, into which put the stock, which should be well flavoured with vegetables, and rather savoury
2. Mix the flour smoothly with the cream, add it to the stock
3. Season with a little salt, and boil all these ingredients very gently for about 10 minutes, keeping them well stirred the whole time, as this sauce is very liable to burn

title: White Sauce (A Very Simple and Inexpensive Method)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 pint of milk
- 1-1/2 oz. of rice
- 1 strip of lemon-peel
- 1 small blade of pounded mace
- salt and cayenne to taste

## Directions

1. Boil the milk with the lemon-peel and rice until the latter is perfectly tender
2. Take out the lemon-peel and pound the milk and rice together
3. Put it back into the stewpan to warm
4. Add the mace and seasoning
5. Give it one boil and serve
6. This sauce should be of the consistency of thick cream

title: White Sauce, Made Without Meat
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. of butter
- 2 small onions
- 1 carrot
- 1/2 a small teacupful of flour
- 1 pint of new milk
- salt and cayenne to taste

## Directions

1. Cut up the onions and carrot very small, and put them into a stewpan with the butter
2. Simmer them till the butter is nearly dried up
3. Then stir in the flour, and add the milk
4. Boil the whole gently until it thickens
5. Strain it, season with salt and cayenne, and it will be ready to serve

title: White Soup
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. of sweet almonds
- 1/4 lb. of cold veal or poultry
- a thick slice of stale bread
- a piece of fresh lemon-peel
- 1 blade of mace, pounded
- 3/4 pint of cream
- the yolks of 2 hard-boiled eggs
- 2 quarts of white stock, No. 107

## Directions

1. Reduce the almonds in a mortar to a paste, with a spoonful of water, and add to them the meat, which should be previously pounded with the bread
2. Beat all together, and add the lemon-peel, very finely chopped, and the mace
3. Pour the boiling stock on the whole, and simmer for an hour
4. Rub the eggs in the cream, put in the soup, bring it to a boil, and serve immediately

title: White Stock
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 lbs. of knuckle of veal
- any poultry trimmings
- 4 slices of lean ham
- 1 carrot
- 2 onions
- 1 head of celery
- 12 white peppercorns
- 1 oz. of salt
- 1 blade of mace
- 1 oz. butter
- 4 quarts of water

## Directions

1. Cut up the veal, and put it with the bones and trimmings of poultry, and the ham, into the stewpan, which has been rubbed with the butter
2. Moisten with 1/2 a pint of water, and simmer till the gravy begins to flow
3. Then add the 4 quarts of water and the remainder of the ingredients; simmer for 5 hours
4. After skimming and straining it carefully through a very fine hair sieve, it will be ready for use

title: White-Currant Jelly
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- White currants
- 3/4 lb. of good loaf sugar per pint of juice

## Directions

1. Pick the currants from the stalks, and put them into a jar
2. Place this jar in a saucepan of boiling water, and simmer until the juice is well drawn from the fruit, which will be in from 3/4 to 1 hour
3. Strain the currants through a fine cloth or jelly-bag; do not squeeze them too much, or the jelly will not be clear
4. Put the juice into a very clean preserving-pan, with the sugar
5. Let this simmer gently over a clear fire until it is firm, and keep stirring and skimming until it is done
6. Then pour it into small pots, cover them, and store away in a dry place

title: Whiting Au Gratin, or Baked Whiting
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 whiting
- butter
- 1 tablespoonful of minced parsley
- a few chopped mushrooms when obtainable
- pepper
- salt
- grated nutmeg to taste
- 2 glasses of sherry or Madeira
- bread crumbs

## Directions

1. Grease the bottom of a baking-dish with butter, and over it, strew some minced parsley and mushrooms.
2. Scale, empty, and wash the whitings, and wipe them thoroughly dry, carefully preserving the livers.
3. Lay them in the dish, sprinkle them with bread crumbs and seasoning, adding a little grated nutmeg, and also a little more minced parsley and mushrooms.
4. Place small pieces of butter over the whiting, moisten with the wine, and bake for 20 minutes in a hot oven.
5. If there should be too much sauce, reduce it by boiling over a sharp fire for a few minutes, and pour under the fish.
6. Serve with a cut lemon, and no other sauce.

title: Whiting Aux Fine Herbes
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 bunch of sweet herbs chopped very fine
- butter

## Directions

1. Clean and skin the fish, fasten the tails in the mouths; and lay them in a baking-dish.
2. Mince the herbs very fine, strew them over the fish, and place small pieces of butter over.
3. Cover with another dish, and let them simmer in a Dutch oven for 1/4 hour or 20 minutes.
4. Turn the fish once or twice, and serve with the sauce poured over.

title: Wine or Brandy Sauce for Puddings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of melted butter No. 377
- 3 heaped teaspoonfuls of pounded sugar
- 1 large wineglassful of port or sherry, or 3/4 of a small glassful of brandy

## Directions

1. Make 1/2 pint of melted butter by recipe No. 377, omitting the salt; then stir in the sugar and wine or spirit in the above proportion, and bring the sauce to the point of boiling.
2. Serve in a boat or tureen separately, and, if liked, pour a little of it over the pudding. To convert this into punch sauce, add to the sherry and brandy a small wineglassful of rum and the juice and grated rind of 1/2 lemon. Liqueurs, such as Maraschino or Curaçoa substituted for the brandy, make excellent sauces.

title: Wine Sauce For Puddings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint of sherry
- 1/4 pint of water
- yolks of 6 eggs
- 2 oz. of pounded sugar
- 1/2 teaspoonful of minced lemon-peel
- a few pieces of candied citron cut thin

## Directions

1. Separate the yolks from the whites of 5 eggs; beat them, and put them into a very clean saucepan (if at hand, a lined one is best)
2. Add all the other ingredients
3. Place them over a sharp fire, and keep stirring until the sauce begins to thicken
4. Take it off and serve
5. Note: If it is allowed to boil, it will be spoiled, as it will immediately curdle

title: Winter Pea Soup (Yellow)
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 quart of split peas
- 2 lbs. of shin of beef
- trimmings of meat or poultry
- a slice of bacon
- 2 large carrots
- 2 turnips
- 5 large onions
- 1 head of celery
- seasoning to taste
- 2 quarts of soft water
- any bones left from roast meat
- 2 quarts of common stock, or liquor in which a joint of meat has been boiled

## Directions

1. Put the peas to soak over-night in soft water, and float off such as rise to the top.
2. Boil them in the water till tender enough to pulp; then add the ingredients mentioned above, and simmer for 2 hours, stirring it occasionally.
3. Pass the whole through a sieve, skim well, season, and serve with toasted bread cut in dice.

title: Winter Salad
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- Endive
- mustard-and-cress
- boiled beetroot
- 3 or 4 hard-boiled eggs
- celery

## Directions

1. Shred the celery into thin pieces, after having carefully washed and cut away all wormeaten pieces.
2. Cleanse the endive and mustard-and-cress free from grit, and arrange these high in the centre of a salad-bowl or dish.
3. Garnish with the hard-boiled eggs and beetroot, both of which should be cut in slices.
4. Pour into the dish, but not over the salad, either of the sauces No. 506, 507, or 508.
5. Never dress a salad long before it is required for table, as, by standing, it loses its freshness and pretty crisp and light appearance; the sauce, however, may always be prepared a few hours beforehand, and when required for use, the herbs laid lightly over it.

title: Yeast Dumplings
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 quartern of dough
- boiling water
- milk

## Directions

1. Make a very light dough as for bread, using to mix it, milk, instead of water
2. Divide it into 7 or 8 dumplings
3. Plunge them into boiling water, and boil them for 20 minutes
4. Serve the instant they are taken up, as they spoil directly, by falling and becoming heavy
5. In eating them do not touch them with a knife, but tear them apart with two forks
6. They may be eaten with meat gravy, or cold butter and sugar

title: Yorkshire Pudding, to Serve with Hot Roast Beef
author: Isabella Beeton
body:
## Ingredients

- 1-1/2 pint of milk
- 6 large tablespoonfuls of flour
- 3 eggs
- 1 saltspoonful of salt
- beef dripping

## Directions

1. Put the flour into a basin with the salt, and stir gradually to this enough milk to make it into a stiff batter
2. When this is perfectly smooth, and all the lumps are well rubbed down, add the remainder of the milk and the eggs, which should be well beaten
3. Beat the mixture for a few minutes, and pour it into a shallow tin, which has been previously well rubbed with beef dripping
4. Put the pudding into the oven, and bake it for an hour
5. Then, for another 1/2 hour, place it under the meat, to catch a little of the gravy that flows from it
6. Cut the pudding into small square pieces, put them on a hot dish, and serve
